name: ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS

topics.title: Vehicle Network Systems - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Network Systems - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Network Systems (In-Vehicle Networking / IVN)** are standardized, multiplexed digital communication architectures that interconnect various Electronic Control Units (ECUs), sensors, actuators, and infotainment subsystems within an automobile through a **shared serial bus medium**, thereby replacing the traditional point-to-point wiring harness with a unified, intelligent, and fault-tolerant data exchange framework governed by protocols such as **CAN (Controller Area Network)**, **LIN (Local Interconnect Network)**, **FlexRay**, **MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport)**, and **Automotive Ethernet**.

In a modern premium vehicle, there can be **70–150 ECUs** communicating over multiple bus segments. Without a network, the wiring harness of a luxury car would exceed **2 km in length** and weigh over **60 kg**, making the architecture physically impossible to manufacture, diagnose, or maintain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multilingual Office"

Imagine a **large corporate office** with 100 employees (ECUs) sitting in different rooms. In the old days (point-to-point wiring), every employee had a dedicated **direct telephone line** to every other employee they ever needed to talk to. If employee A needed to speak to B, C, D, E, and F, he needed **5 separate physical wires**. With 100 employees, this becomes **100 × 99 / 2 = 4,950 wires** — chaos.

Now imagine a **single intercom channel** (the bus) where everyone is "plugged in." If Employee A wants to talk to Employee F, he simply **broadcasts a labeled message on the common channel** (with F's address), and only F listens and acts. The medium is shared, the wiring is minimal, and the system is flexible. **This is exactly what a vehicle network does.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A vehicle network is essentially a **shared digital highway** inside the car that allows dozens of microcontrollers to talk to each other using **only two wires** (in the case of CAN), instead of running a dedicated wire between every pair of devices.

### 1.3 The Three Fundamental Problems a Network Solves

1. **Wiring Complexity Problem** – Reducing the wire count and weight of the harness.
2. **Data Sharing Problem** – Allowing one sensor reading (e.g., vehicle speed) to be used by multiple ECUs (ABS, transmission, instrument cluster) without duplication.
3. **Diagnostic Problem** – Providing a single standardized port (OBD-II) through which a scan tool can query any ECU on the vehicle.

### 1.4 Standard Terminology (KTU-Aligned)

| Term | Expansion | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **ECU** | Electronic Control Unit | The microcontroller-based "brain" of any subsystem |
| **Bus** | Shared Communication Line | The single pair of wires carrying all messages |
| **Node** | Bus Participant | Any ECU connected to the bus |
| **Message / Frame** | Data Packet | A structured bundle of bits carrying information |
| **Protocol** | Communication Language | Rules governing who may speak, when, and how |
| **Multiplexing** | Time/Event Sharing | Allowing multiple signals to share one channel |
| **Bit Rate** | Baud Rate | Number of bits transmitted per second (**bps**) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution — From Point-to-Point to Bus Architecture

**Stage 1 — Discrete Wiring (Pre-1980s):**
Each switch directly drove its load. A rear window defogger switch ran a heavy-gauge wire to the defogger grid. Every device needed its own wire pair from the battery/ground.

**Stage 2 — Switched / Relay-Based Wiring (1980s):**
Relays centralized high-current switching. Still point-to-point, but load current was separated from control current.

**Stage 3 — Multiplexed Bus Architecture (1990s onwards):**
A **single twisted pair** carries all control messages. A switch is no longer a current-carrying device; it is an **input to an ECU**, which then transmits a digital "switch pressed" message on the bus. The load-driving ECU receives the message and activates the actuator. This is the **multiplexing principle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Multiplexing Principle:** Instead of switching raw current, we switch *information*. Power Electronics handle the actual load switching at the actuator end, while a low-current data bus carries the "ON/OFF" command.

### 2.2 Why a Network is Needed — The Engineering Drivers

1. **Reduction in Wiring Harness Mass and Volume** – Up to **30–50% weight reduction** in harness mass.
2. **Real-Time Data Sharing** – A single wheel-speed sensor feeds ABS, ESP, transmission, instrument cluster, and adaptive cruise control simultaneously.
3. **Modular ECU Architecture** – Adding a new feature (e.g., lane-keep assist) requires only software addition, not new wiring.
4. **Centralized Diagnostics (OBD-II)** – Standardized on **CAN** since 2008 (US/EU mandate for all light-duty vehicles).
5. **Functional Safety (ISO 26262)** – Distributed ECUs allow redundant computation for safety-critical functions like braking and steering.

### 2.3 Classification of In-Vehicle Networks (The Five-Class Model)

Modern vehicles typically use **multiple bus classes** matched to the bandwidth and criticality of each subsystem. This is the canonical classification taught at KTU level.

| Class | Protocol | Typical Bit Rate | Application Domain | Example Subsystems |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | **LIN** | 1 – 20 kbps | Low-speed body electronics | Window lifts, mirror adjust, seat controls, rain sensor |
| **Class B** | **CAN (Low Speed)** | 50 – 125 kbps | Comfort & convenience | Instrument cluster, HVAC, central locking |
| **Class C** | **CAN (High Speed)** | 250 kbps – 1 Mbps | Real-time powertrain & chassis | Engine, ABS, ESP, transmission, airbags |
| **Class D** | **FlexRay / CAN-FD** | 1 – 10 Mbps | High-bandwidth, deterministic, safety-critical | Drive-by-wire, ADAS sensor fusion, active suspension |
| **Class E** | **MOST / Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1)** | 25 – 1000+ Mbps | Infotainment & ADAS | Telematics, camera/video, rear-seat entertainment, V2X |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Point:** A modern vehicle is a **multi-bus** system, not a single-bus system. A gateway ECU bridges between the high-speed powertrain CAN and the low-speed body CAN, translating messages between protocols.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol | Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit Time | $T_b$ | seconds | Time duration of one bit on the bus |
| 2 | Bit Rate | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | bps (bits per second) | Inverse of bit time |
| 3 | Bus Utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Used Bandwidth}}{\text{Total Bandwidth}} \times 100\%$ | % | Load on the bus |
| 4 | Worst-Case Latency (CAN) | $L_{max} \le (5 \cdot f_{prop} + 10 \cdot T_b)$ | seconds | Time from request to reception, including arbitration |
| 5 | Frame Transmission Time | $T_{frame} = (N_{bits} \cdot T_b)$ | seconds | Time to push one full message |
| 6 | Throughput Efficiency (CAN) | $\eta = \dfrac{D}{D + O}$ | dimensionless | Useful data bits / total bits |
| 7 | Maximum Cable Length (CAN) | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{50 \text{ m}}{R_b \text{ (in Mbps)}}$ | meters | Trade-off between speed and distance |
| 8 | Number of Nodes (CAN) | $N_{max}$ | **30 (typ), 110 (theoretical)** | Bus driver fan-out limited |
| 9 | Data Field Size (Classical CAN) | $D$ | **8 bytes** | Hard upper limit per frame |
| 10 | Data Field Size (CAN-FD) | $D$ | **64 bytes** | Extended frame format |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** Classical CAN = **1 Mbps max, 8 bytes payload, 11-bit or 29-bit ID**. CAN-FD = **5 Mbps (data phase), 64 bytes payload, same arbitration**. LIN = **single-wire, master-slave, max 20 kbps**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs (Tata, Maruti, Toyota, BMW)** use CAN as the default backbone; you will encounter it in every service manual.
- **EVs (Tata Nexon EV, Tesla Model 3)** use **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)** for ADAS camera and sensor data, as CAN's 1 Mbps is grossly insufficient for 4-camera surround-view systems producing **~3 Gbps raw data**.
- **FAME-II / AIS standards** in India mandate **OBD-II over CAN** for all petrol cars (since 2017) and diesel cars (since 2018).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Bus Length vs. Bit Rate in CAN

The Controller Area Network's standard **ISO 11898** specifies a relationship between the maximum allowed bus length and the chosen bit rate. The constraint comes from **propagation delay**: a bit must remain on the bus long enough for the signal to reach the farthest node and return before the next bit begins. The standard rule is empirically:

$$L_{max} \, (\text{metres}) = \dfrac{50}{R_b \, (\text{Mbps})}$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Identify the governing constraint.**
A recessive bit (logic '1' on the CAN bus) must propagate to the farthest node, be reflected back, and the total round-trip propagation delay must not exceed **half the nominal bit time**. This ensures the bus arbitration logic functions correctly.

**Step 2 — Express the propagation time constraint.**
Signal propagation speed in an automotive twisted pair is approximately $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (≈ 2/3 the speed of light in vacuum, due to the dielectric properties of the cable). The one-way propagation delay for a cable of length $L$ is:

$$t_{prop} = \dfrac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the ISO 11898 limit.**
For a CAN network to function reliably, the one-way propagation delay must satisfy:

$$t_{prop} \le \dfrac{T_b}{2}$$

where $T_b$ is the nominal bit time in seconds.

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve for L.**
Substituting $T_b = 1/R_b$ and $t_{prop} = L/v$:

$$\dfrac{L}{v} \le \dfrac{1}{2 R_b}$$

$$L \le \dfrac{v}{2 R_b}$$

**Step 5 — Insert the empirical automotive values.**
With $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s:

$$L \le \dfrac{2 \times 10^8}{2 R_b} = \dfrac{10^8}{R_b} \;\; \text{(in metres, with } R_b \text{ in Hz)}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify to the practical engineering rule.**
Expressing $R_b$ in Mbps, this reduces to the field-engineering rule of thumb used universally:

$$\boxed{L_{max} \, (\text{metres}) = \dfrac{50}{R_b \, (\text{Mbps})}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical validation examples.**

| $R_b$ | Calculated $L_{max}$ | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 Mbps | 50 m | Powertrain CAN backbone |
| 500 kbps | 100 m | Chassis CAN in large SUVs |
| 250 kbps | 200 m | Body CAN, comfort electronics |
| 125 kbps | 400 m | Low-speed CAN, diagnostic extension |

> [!NOTE]
> The inverse linear relationship explains why a high-speed CAN at **1 Mbps is limited to 40 m** in some OEM specifications (a safety margin is subtracted from the theoretical 50 m).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bus Utilization and Bottleneck Analysis

When a designer adds ECUs to a CAN bus, the bus utilization must remain under a threshold to guarantee message latency. The standard SAE analysis defines:

$$U = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( f_i \cdot T_{frame,i} \right)}{T_{obs}}$$

where:
- $N$ = number of periodic messages on the bus
- $f_i$ = transmission frequency of message $i$ (in Hz)
- $T_{frame,i}$ = transmission time of message $i$ (in seconds)
- $T_{obs}$ = total observation window (e.g., 1 second)

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**

A CAN bus runs at **500 kbps** (so $T_b = 2 \, \mu s$). Three periodic messages are scheduled:

| Message | Data Length (bytes) | Frequency (Hz) | Bits/Frame (with overhead) | Frame Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M1 (Engine RPM) | 2 | 100 | $47 \times 8 = 47$ bits* | $T_{M1}$ |
| M2 (Wheel Speed) | 4 | 50 | $63 \times 8 = 63$ bits* | $T_{M2}$ |
| M3 (Brake Status) | 1 | 20 | $43 \times 8 = 43$ bits* | $T_{M3}$ |

*(Note: Classical CAN frame has ~47 bits overhead + data. Approximation used here: total bits ≈ 47 + 8×data_bytes.)*

**Step 1 — Compute each frame time.**

$$T_{M1} = 47 \times 2 \, \mu s = 94 \, \mu s$$

$$T_{M2} = (47 + 32) \times 2 \, \mu s = 79 \times 2 \, \mu s = 158 \, \mu s$$

$$T_{M3} = (47 + 8) \times 2 \, \mu s = 55 \times 2 \, \mu s = 110 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Compute bus usage per second.**

$$U = (100)(94\mu s) + (50)(158\mu s) + (20)(110\mu s)$$

$$U = 9{,}400 + 7{,}900 + 2{,}200 = 19{,}500 \, \mu s / s$$

$$U = 0.0195 = 1.95\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is far below the **30% practical ceiling** recommended for real-time CAN networks (above 30%, latency jitter becomes non-deterministic and safety-critical messages may be delayed). This is why designers use **bus load analysis tools** like CANalyzer or Vector CANoe during ECU development.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Bus

| Design Requirement | Recommended Bus | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Mirror fold/unfold | **LIN** | Low cost, single-wire, < 1 ms latency tolerable |
| Engine RPM broadcast (10 ms) | **High-Speed CAN** | Deterministic, 1 Mbps, robust EMC |
| Drive-by-wire braking | **FlexRay** | 10 Mbps, dual-channel redundancy, deterministic TDMA |
| 360° surround-view camera (4× MP) | **Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1)** | Multi-Gbps required, no equivalent legacy bus |
| Airbag deployment trigger | **High-Speed CAN with priority 0** | Sub-millisecond, highest priority via arbitration |
| Audio streaming to 12 speakers | **MOST150** | 150 Mbps, isochronous, designed for media |
| Telematics (4G/5G cloud) | **Ethernet + LTE/5G modem** | High throughput to back-end |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Multi-Bus Vehicle Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PC["POWERTRAIN DOMAIN Class C 500 kbps CAN"]
        ECU_EMS[Engine ECU EMS]
        ECU_TCU[Transmission ECU TCU]
        ECU_ABS[ABS ECU]
        ECU_ESP[ESP ECU]
    end

    subgraph CH["CHASSIS DOMAIN Class C 500 kbps CAN"]
        ECU_SUSP[Active Suspension ECU]
        ECU_SB[Steer by Wire ECU]
    end

    subgraph BD["BODY DOMAIN Class B 125 kbps CAN"]
        ECU_BCM[Body Control Module BCM]
        ECU_HVAC[HVAC ECU]
        ECU_IC[Instrument Cluster]
        ECU_OBII[OBD II Port]
    end

    subgraph COMF["COMFORT DOMAIN Class A LIN Master"]
        ECU_LINM[LIN Master in BCM]
        NODE_DR[Door Module LIN]
        NODE_MIR[Mirror LIN]
        NODE_SEAT[Seat Module LIN]
        NODE_RAIN[Rain Sensor LIN]
    end

    subgraph ADAS["ADAS DOMAIN Class D FlexRay or CAN FD"]
        ECU_RADAR[Front Radar ECU]
        ECU_CAM[Front Camera ECU]
        ECU_LIDAR[Lidar ECU]
    end

    subgraph IF["INFOTAINMENT DOMAIN Ethernet 1000BASE T1"]
        ECU_HU[Head Unit HU]
        ECU_TEL[Telematics ECU]
        ECU_AMP[Amplifier MOST150]
        ECU_DIAG[Diagnostic Gateway]
    end

    CGW[Central Gateway ECU]:::gateway

    ECU_EMS --- CGW
    ECU_TCU --- CGW
    ECU_ABS --- CGW
    ECU_ESP --- CGW
    ECU_SUSP --- CGW
    ECU_SB --- CGW
    ECU_BCM --- CGW
    ECU_HVAC --- CGW
    ECU_IC --- CGW
    ECU_OBII --- CGW
    ECU_RADAR --- CGW
    ECU_CAM --- CGW
    ECU_LIDAR --- CGW
    ECU_HU --- CGW
    ECU_TEL --- CGW
    ECU_AMP --- CGW
    ECU_DIAG --- CGW

    ECU_LINM --- ECU_BCM
    ECU_LINM --- NODE_DR
    ECU_LINM --- NODE_MIR
    ECU_LINM --- NODE_SEAT
    ECU_LINM --- NODE_RAIN

    classDef gateway fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Central Gateway ECU** is the routing hub bridging all bus domains. It translates messages between dissimilar protocols (e.g., CAN ↔ Ethernet) and enforces firewall rules.
- Each rectangular subgraph is a **distinct bus domain** operating at its own protocol and bit rate.
- The **LIN Master** (in the BCM) controls four LIN slave nodes — typical of a body-comfort subsystem.

---

### 4.2 Multiplexing Concept — Signal Sharing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SS[Vehicle Speed Sensor VSS] -->|Analog Pulse| ECU_VSS[ECU A Powertrain]
    ECU_VSS -->|CAN Frame ID 0x100| BUS[CAN Bus Twisted Pair]
    BUS --> ECU_ABS_NODE[ECU B ABS Reads ID 0x100]
    BUS --> ECU_TCU_NODE[ECU C TCU Reads ID 0x100]
    BUS --> ECU_IC_NODE[ECU D Instrument Cluster Reads ID 0x100]
    BUS --> ECU_ESP_NODE[ECU E ESP Reads ID 0x100]

    ECU_ABS_NODE --> ABS_OUT[ABS Actuation]
    ECU_TCU_NODE --> GEAR[Gear Shift Logic]
    ECU_IC_NODE --> DIAL[Speedometer Dial]
    ECU_ESP_NODE --> ESP_OUT[Stability Control]
```

**Insight:** A **single physical sensor** generates **one bus message** that is consumed by **four downstream ECUs** simultaneously. The bus is the "single source of truth," and the harness complexity drops from 4 separate signal wires to 2 bus wires (twisted pair).

---

### 4.3 Communication Protocol Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L7["Application Layer"]
        A1[Diagnostic UDS over CAN]
        A2[CANopen CiA 401]
    end

    subgraph L2["Data Link Layer MAC"]
        D1[CAN ISO 11898]
        D2[FlexRay ISO 17458]
        D3[LIN ISO 17987]
    end

    subgraph L1["Physical Layer"]
        P1[5V Diff Twisted Pair 120 ohm]
        P2[Optical Fiber MOST]
        P3[Single Wire 12V LIN]
    end

    L7 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Multiplexing" as applied to automotive electrical systems. List any two advantages of using a multiplexed bus over conventional point-to-point wiring.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Multiplexing in an automotive context refers to the technique of transmitting **multiple independent signals or data streams over a single shared communication medium** (typically a twisted-pair bus) using time-division or event-driven protocols, rather than running a dedicated wire for each signal.

**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Reduction in wiring harness mass and complexity** — A single twisted pair replaces dozens of point-to-point wires, reducing vehicle weight and improving fuel efficiency.
2. **Real-time data sharing across multiple ECUs** — One sensor reading (e.g., wheel speed) is simultaneously available to ABS, ESP, transmission, and instrument cluster without duplication.
3. **Centralized diagnostics via standardized OBD-II port** — A single scan tool can interrogate every ECU on the network.
4. **Easier feature addition and modularity** — New ECUs can be added to the bus with only software-level addressing changes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Class A, Class B, and Class C automotive networks with one example protocol and one application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Class | Bit Rate | Protocol Example | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Class A** | Up to 20 kbps | **LIN** | Power window, mirror, seat controls |
| **Class B** | 50 – 125 kbps | **Low-speed CAN** | Instrument cluster, HVAC, central locking |
| **Class C** | 125 kbps – 1 Mbps | **High-speed CAN** | Engine management, ABS, airbag, transmission |

Class A is **low-cost, low-speed** and uses **single-wire** physical media. Class B is **medium-speed** for **comfort and convenience** functions. Class C is **high-speed, deterministic** and is **safety-critical** for powertrain and chassis domains.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a typical multi-bus in-vehicle network. With a neat block diagram, show how a **Central Gateway ECU** interconnects the **Powertrain CAN**, **Body CAN**, **LIN Sub-Bus**, and **Infotainment Ethernet** domains. State the function of the gateway in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a numerical example, derive the relationship between the **maximum allowable bus length** and the **bit rate** in a CAN network. For a 500 kbps CAN bus, calculate the maximum bus length.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Multi-Bus Architecture and Gateway Function [7 marks]**

A modern automobile does not use a single bus; instead, it employs a **multi-domain** architecture in which subsystems are grouped by **function, safety criticality, and bandwidth requirement**, and connected to a **Central Gateway ECU**.

**The four typical domains are:**

1. **Powertrain / Chassis Domain (High-Speed CAN @ 500 kbps – 1 Mbps):** Hosts the Engine ECU, Transmission ECU, ABS ECU, and ESP ECU. Latency must be **< 5 ms**; messages are safety-critical.

2. **Body / Comfort Domain (Low-Speed CAN @ 125 kbps):** Hosts the Body Control Module (BCM), HVAC, instrument cluster, and central locking. Latency tolerance ~ 50–100 ms.

3. **LIN Sub-Bus (Class A @ 19.2 kbps, single-wire):** The BCM acts as a **LIN Master**, with door modules, mirror modules, seat modules, and rain/light sensors as **LIN Slaves**. LIN is used where cost dominates over speed.

4. **Infotainment Domain (Automotive Ethernet 100/1000BASE-T1):** Hosts the Head Unit, telematics module, and rear-seat entertainment. Throughput is in hundreds of Mbps.

**Function of the Central Gateway ECU:**

- **Protocol Translation** – Converts messages between dissimilar protocols (e.g., CAN frame → Ethernet UDP packet).
- **Message Routing** – Filters and forwards relevant messages from one bus to another (e.g., engine RPM from powertrain CAN to instrument cluster on body CAN).
- **Network Management** – Performs sleep/wake-up coordination, ECU presence detection, and bus-off recovery.
- **Firewall / Security** – In connected vehicles, it isolates the safety-critical powertrain CAN from external Ethernet attack surfaces (per ISO 21434 cybersecurity).
- **Diagnostic Aggregation** – Collects DTCs (Diagnostic Trouble Codes) from all ECUs and presents them over the OBD-II port.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the equivalent Mermaid block diagram; a hand-drawn equivalent in the exam would carry full marks.)*

---

**(b) Derivation of $L_{max}$ vs. $R_b$ [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Governing Principle (1 mark):**
The signal on a CAN bus must travel to the farthest node and back within the **bit time $T_b$** to ensure correct arbitration and acknowledgement sampling.

**Step 2 — Propagation Speed (1 mark):**
Signal velocity in an automotive twisted pair:
$$v \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Constraint Equation (2 marks):**
$$\dfrac{L}{v} \le \dfrac{T_b}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2 R_b}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for L (1 mark):**
$$L \le \dfrac{v}{2 R_b}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify to engineering rule (1 mark):**
With $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s and $R_b$ in Mbps:
$$L_{max} \, (\text{m}) = \dfrac{50}{R_b \, (\text{Mbps})}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical calculation (1 mark):**
For $R_b = 500 \text{ kbps} = 0.5 \text{ Mbps}$:
$$L_{max} = \dfrac{50}{0.5} = 100 \text{ m}$$

**Answer: The maximum bus length for a 500 kbps CAN network is approximately 100 metres.**

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative Internal Choice Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **CAN**, **LIN**, and **FlexRay** protocols in a tabular form covering **at least 6 parameters**: physical medium, bit rate, arbitration method, typical application, cost, and fault tolerance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CAN bus operating at **250 kbps** carries three periodic messages. Message M1 has **2 data bytes** and is sent at **100 Hz**. Message M2 has **6 data bytes** at **50 Hz**. Message M3 has **1 data byte** at **20 Hz**. Calculate the **bus utilization** over 1 second. State whether the bus load is healthy (use 30% as the practical ceiling).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparative Table [7 marks]**

| Parameter | **CAN** | **LIN** | **FlexRay** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical Medium** | Twisted pair, differential | Single wire | Twisted pair, dual channel |
| **Bit Rate** | Up to 1 Mbps (CAN-FD: 5 Mbps data) | 1 – 20 kbps | Up to 10 Mbps |
| **Arbitration** | CSMA/CA + non-destructive bit-wise priority | Master-driven schedule (TDMA) | TDMA + FTDMA, deterministic |
| **Typical Application** | Powertrain, chassis, body | Window, mirror, seat | Drive-by-wire, ADAS, active suspension |
| **Cost per Node** | Medium | Very low | High |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Good (CAN-FD has CRC improvements) | Basic (single-wire) | Excellent (dual-redundant channels) |
| **Max Payload** | 8 bytes (64 bytes in CAN-FD) | 8 bytes | 254 bytes |

---

**(b) Bus Utilization Calculation [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Bit time at 250 kbps (1 mark):**
$$T_b = \dfrac{1}{250{,}000} = 4 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2 — Frame size in bits (using ~47-bit overhead for classical CAN) (2 marks):**
- M1: $47 + (2 \times 8) = 63$ bits
- M2: $47 + (6 \times 8) = 95$ bits
- M3: $47 + (1 \times 8) = 55$ bits

**Step 3 — Frame transmission times (1 mark):**
- $T_{M1} = 63 \times 4 = 252 \, \mu s$
- $T_{M2} = 95 \times 4 = 380 \, \mu s$
- $T_{M3} = 55 \times 4 = 220 \, \mu s$

**Step 4 — Total bus time used per second (2 marks):**
$$U = (100 \times 252) + (50 \times 380) + (20 \times 220)$$
$$U = 25{,}200 + 19{,}000 + 4{,}400 = 48{,}600 \, \mu s / s$$
$$U = 0.0486 = 4.86\%$$

**Step 5 — Health check (1 mark):**
Since **4.86% < 30%**, the bus is **healthy**, with ample headroom for additional messages, event-driven (non-periodic) traffic, and error frames.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Confusing bit stuffing in CAN** — Students forget that CAN inserts a *stuff bit* after every 5 consecutive identical bits. This adds **~10–15% extra bits** to the actual on-wire frame length, and ignoring it makes bus-utilization answers optimistic (over-estimate headroom, may fail safety certification).
> 2. **Mixing up LIN's master-slave concept with CAN's multi-master** — LIN has exactly **one master** controlling all bus access; CAN is **multi-master** with priority-based contention. Many students wrongly call LIN "multi-master."
> 3. **Forgetting the terminating resistor** — A CAN bus **must** have a 120 Ω resistor at **each end** of the twisted pair to suppress reflections. No mention → −1 to −2 marks in bus architecture questions.
> 4. **Writing "CAN" without version context** — Always specify *Classical CAN 2.0A/B* vs *CAN-FD* in 14-mark answers; the bit rate and payload are different, and examiners explicitly check this.
> 5. **Mixing up "bit time" and "baud rate" units** — Baud rate is **symbols per second**; on CAN, 1 symbol = 1 bit, so baud = bps, but the exam may penalize unit confusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** A vehicle network is a **multiplexed digital bus** interconnecting ECUs via a shared protocol (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, MOST, Ethernet).
- **Why?** Reduces wiring mass, enables real-time data sharing, supports OBD-II diagnostics, enables modular feature addition.
- **The Multiplexing Principle:** Switch *information*, not *current*. ECU outputs logic levels; load switching happens at the actuator end via power drivers.
- **Five bus classes** are universally accepted (A = LIN, B = LS-CAN, C = HS-CAN, D = FlexRay/CAN-FD, E = MOST/Ethernet).
- **CAN key facts:** 1 Mbps max (classical), 8-byte payload, 11/29-bit ID, CSMA/CA + non-destructive arbitration, differential twisted pair, 120 Ω termination at each end.
- **CAN-FD improvement:** Up to 5 Mbps in data phase, 64-byte payload — addresses the bandwidth crunch.
- **LIN key facts:** Single-wire, master-slave, ≤ 20 kbps, 1 master + up to 16 slaves, used for low-cost body electronics.
- **FlexRay key facts:** 10 Mbps, dual-channel redundancy, TDMA/FTDMA, deterministic — used in drive-by-wire and ADAS.
- **MOST key facts:** 25/50/150 Mbps, optical fiber, isochronous — for infotainment.
- **Automotive Ethernet key facts:** 100/1000BASE-T1, single twisted pair, full duplex, replacing MOST — used for ADAS cameras, OTA updates, V2X.
- **Central Gateway ECU** is the heart of the multi-bus architecture — performs protocol translation, message routing, network management, and cybersecurity firewalling.
- **Bus length vs. bit rate:** $L_{max} = 50 / R_b$ (metres / Mbps).
- **Practical bus utilization ceiling:** 30% — beyond this, latency becomes non-deterministic and safety messages may be delayed.
- **OBD-II mandate:** Since 2008 globally, since 2017/2018 in India, all ECUs must be accessible over CAN.
- **Layered architecture:** Every bus follows a 7-layer ISO-OSI-like model, but in practice only the **physical** and **data-link** layers are standardised; application layer varies by OEM.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Architecture - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Architecture - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

In the context of modern automotive engineering, **Network Architecture** refers to the systematic topological arrangement and logical interconnection of Electronic Control Units (ECUs), sensors, actuators, and communication buses within a vehicle's electrical and electronic (E/E) system. As per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, it defines *how data is physically routed, electrically transmitted, and logically addressed* between distributed automotive nodes to achieve coordinated vehicle functions such as powertrain control, chassis management, body electronics, infotainment, and Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Network Architecture is the foundational enabler of all in-vehicle communication. Without a well-defined architecture, ECUs cannot communicate deterministically, safely, or in real-time.

## Network Architecture — The Main Topological Types

| # | Architecture Type | Core Description | Typical Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bus (Linear / Backbone)** | All ECUs share a single common twisted-pair cable; data is broadcast to all nodes | CAN, LIN, CAN FD |
| 2 | **Star** | One central master node (gateway/hub) connects all peripheral ECUs | MOST, Automotive Ethernet with switch |
| 3 | **Ring** | Each ECU connects to exactly two neighbors forming a closed loop | MOST (legacy), FlexRay dual-channel |
| 4 | **Tree / Hierarchical** | Multiple subnetworks (sub-buses) connected through a central gateway | Modern zonal E/E architectures |
| 5 | **Mesh** | Every ECU has multiple redundant paths to every other ECU | Autonomous driving (L4/L5), fail-operational ADAS |
| 6 | **Hybrid (Point-to-Point + Bus)** | Combination of point-to-point links and shared buses via gateways | Modern luxury vehicles |

## Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a **hospital emergency ward**. Doctors, nurses, and lab technicians all need to share patient information instantly.
>
> - **Bus topology** is like a single **public address system** in a hallway — anyone can speak, and everyone hears, but only one person can talk at a time. *(This is how CAN works.)*
> - **Star topology** is like a **switchboard operator** — every call must go through the central receptionist. *(This is how MOST and switched Ethernet work.)*
> - **Ring topology** is like a **conveyor belt with notes** — a message passes from one person to the next in a circle until it reaches the right recipient. *(This is how FlexRay and legacy token-ring networks work.)*
> - **Mesh topology** is like a **team chat app where everyone can DM everyone** — multiple paths exist, so if one person is offline, the message still gets through. *(This is mandatory for autonomous vehicles where failure is not an option.)*

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in Automotive Networks

- **CAN bus speed:** Up to **1 Mbit/s** (Classical CAN)
- **CAN FD speed:** Up to **5 Mbit/s**
- **CAN XL speed:** Up to **10 Mbit/s**
- **LIN bus speed:** Up to **19.2 kbit/s**
- **FlexRay speed:** Up to **10 Mbit/s** per channel
- **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1):** **100 Mbit/s** full-duplex on a single twisted pair
- **Automotive Ethernet (1000BASE-T1):** **1 Gbit/s** full-duplex on a single twisted pair
- **MOST speed:** Up to **150 Mbit/s** (MOST150)
- **Standard battery voltage:** **12 V** (conventional ICE) or **48 V** (mild-hybrid architectures)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These bandwidth numbers are **hard physical limits** defined by ISO standards (ISO 11898 for CAN, ISO 17458 for FlexRay, ISO 21111 for Automotive Ethernet).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bus Topology — Detailed Operational Logic

The **bus topology** is the most prevalent architecture in current passenger vehicles. All ECUs tap into a single pair of wires (CAN_H and CAN_L) terminated at both ends with **120 Ω** resistors.

**How it works (Step-by-step logic):**

1. The bus is **multi-master**, meaning any ECU can initiate a transmission when the bus is detected idle.
2. Messages are **broadcast** — every connected ECU receives every frame, but each ECU filters messages by its unique **CAN ID (11-bit or 29-bit)**.
3. **Non-Destructive Bitwise Arbitration** resolves collisions — the ECU transmitting the lowest ID wins the bus without losing data.
4. Physical signaling uses **differential voltage**:
   - Dominant bit (logic 0): CAN_H ≈ **3.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **1.5 V** → differential ≈ **2 V**
   - Recessive bit (logic 1): CAN_H ≈ **2.5 V**, CAN_L ≈ **2.5 V** → differential ≈ **0 V**

**Bus Topology Variants in Automotive:**

| Variant | Standard | Bit Rate | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Classical CAN | ISO 11898-1 | up to 1 Mbit/s | Powertrain, Chassis |
| CAN FD | ISO 11898-1:2015 | up to 5 Mbit/s | ECU flashing, ADAS sensors |
| CAN XL | ISO 11898-1:2024 | up to 10 Mbit/s | High-bandwidth backbone |
| LIN | ISO 17987 | up to 19.2 kbit/s | Low-cost body electronics (mirrors, seats) |

## 2.2 Star Topology — Detailed Operational Logic

A **central node (master/switch/gateway)** sits at the hub. All peripheral ECUs connect point-to-point to this center. The central node handles **all message routing**, **bandwidth allocation**, and **fault isolation**.

**Why use star in automotive?**
- **Fault isolation:** A broken link to one ECU does not bring down the entire network.
- **Bandwidth scalability:** Modern **Automotive Ethernet** uses full-duplex switched star topology — every link gets dedicated bandwidth (no collision).
- Used in **MOST networks** for infotainment and in **zonal architectures** where a central zone controller acts as the star master.

## 2.3 Ring Topology — Detailed Operational Logic

Each node has exactly **two neighbors**, forming a closed loop. A token (or time-slot) circulates around the ring; only the holder may transmit.

**Automotive relevance:**
- **FlexRay** uses a *dual-channel* ring-like configuration for redundancy (Channel A and Channel B).
- **MOST** historically used a plastic optical fiber (POF) ring.
- **Disadvantage:** A single break in the ring disables the entire network unless dual-ring redundancy is used.

## 2.4 Mesh Topology — Detailed Operational Logic

In a **full mesh**, every ECU connects directly to every other ECU. In a **partial mesh**, only critical nodes have multiple paths. Used in **fail-operational** systems (autonomous driving, brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire) where any single link failure must not cause loss of function.

## 2.5 Tree / Hierarchical (Zonal) Topology — Modern Trend

The **zonal architecture** is the industry's direction. The vehicle is divided into physical zones (Front-Left, Front-Right, Rear-Left, Rear-Right, Central). Each zone has a **zonal controller** that aggregates local ECUs and connects to a **central high-performance computer** via Automotive Ethernet.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all KTU-examinable formulas and metrics for Network Architecture.

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Description |
|---|---|---|
| CAN bus differential voltage (dominant) | $V_{\text{diff}} = V_{CAN\_H} - V_{CAN\_L} = 3.5 - 1.5 = 2.0\ \text{V}$ | Active "0" bit |
| CAN bus differential voltage (recessive) | $V_{\text{diff}} = V_{CAN\_H} - V_{CAN\_L} = 2.5 - 2.5 = 0.0\ \text{V}$ | Idle "1" bit |
| Maximum CAN bus length (at 1 Mbit/s) | $L_{\max} \approx 40\ \text{m}$ | Per ISO 11898 |
| CAN bit time | $t_{\text{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{baud}}}$ | Inverse of baud rate |
| CAN arbitration priority | Lower ID = Higher priority | Dominant bit wins |
| FlexRay cycle time | $T_{\text{cycle}} = T_{\text{static}} + T_{\text{dynamic}} + T_{\text{symbol}}$ | Up to 5 ms |
| Ethernet link utilization (full-duplex) | $U = \dfrac{\text{Data Transferred}}{\text{Max Throughput}} \times 100\%$ | No collisions in full-duplex |
| MOST bandwidth allocation | Bandwidth reserved by **streaming connection** | Synchronous channels |
| Termination resistor (CAN) | $R_{\text{term}} = 120\ \Omega$ | Two at bus ends |

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bus topology** reduces wiring harness weight — replacing hundreds of point-to-point wires with two wires saved **~50 kg** in modern luxury cars.
- **Star topology with Ethernet** enables OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware updates across multiple zones.
- **Mesh topology** is the safety backbone for **Level 4 and Level 5 autonomous vehicles** where ISO 26262 mandates ASIL-D redundancy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — CAN Bus Arbitration Resolution

We will mathematically demonstrate **why the lowest CAN ID wins arbitration** without any data loss.

**Setup:**
- Node A transmits CAN ID = `0x100` (binary: `001 0000 0000`)
- Node B transmits CAN ID = `0x1FF` (binary: `001 1111 1111`)
- Both nodes start transmitting simultaneously at bit position 1 (MSB first).

**Step 1 — Synchronization to bit 1:**
Both IDs have MSB = `0` (dominant). Both nodes transmit the same level. No arbitration conflict yet.

**Step 2 — Bit position 2:**
Both IDs have bit = `0` (dominant). No conflict.

**Step 3 — Continue until bit 6:**
A: bit 6 = `1` (recessive)
B: bit 6 = `1` (recessive)
Still no conflict — both nodes "agree" the bus is recessive.

**Step 4 — Critical bit 7:**
A: bit 7 = `0` (dominant)
B: bit 7 = `0` (dominant)
No conflict yet. But now suppose we change B's ID to `0x0FF`:

B at bit 7 = `1` (recessive).

A drives dominant (`0`), B drives recessive (`1`).
The **bus becomes dominant** because of the wired-AND behavior.

**Step 5 — B detects mismatch:**
B monitors the bus while transmitting. B sees a dominant bit where it tried to send recessive. B concludes: *"I have lost arbitration."* B immediately stops transmitting and switches to **receiver mode**.

**Step 6 — A continues:**
A wins the bus and completes its full message transmission. **No data is lost.** B will retry in the next bus idle period.

**Mathematical summary:**

$$
\text{Priority} = f(\text{CAN ID}), \quad \text{where } \min(\text{ID}) \Rightarrow \max(\text{Priority})
$$

$$
\text{Arbitration winner} = \arg\min_{i \in \text{transmitters}} \text{ID}_i
$$

**Wired-AND logical model:**

$$
V_{\text{bus}} = \min(V_{A}, V_{B}) \quad \text{(in voltage terms, dominant overrides recessive)}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — KTU Board Favourite

This matrix is the **most exam-relevant analytical tool** for the topic. KTU examiners expect students to compare topologies across multiple engineering parameters.

| Parameter | Bus (CAN/LIN) | Star (Ethernet) | Ring (FlexRay/MOST) | Mesh |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cabling complexity | **Low** | Medium | Medium | High |
| Fault tolerance | Low (single break affects all) | **High** (fault isolated) | Low (single break disables ring) | **Very High** (redundant paths) |
| Bandwidth scalability | Limited (~5 Mbit/s) | **Excellent** (up to 10 Gbit/s) | Good (10 Mbit/s) | Excellent |
| Real-time determinism | Good (CSMA/CR) | Excellent (full-duplex switch) | **Excellent** (TDMA) | Excellent |
| Cost per node | **Lowest** | Medium-High | High | Very High |
| Maximum nodes (typical) | ~30 (CAN), ~16 (LIN) | Limited by switch ports | ~22 (FlexRay) | Application-dependent |
| Wiring harness weight | **Lowest** | Medium | Medium | Highest |
| Automotive use case | Body & Powertrain | ADAS, backbone | Chassis (X-by-Wire) | Autonomous L4/L5 |
| Standard | ISO 11898 | IEEE 802.3bw/bp | ISO 17458 | Custom / 100BASE-T1 |

## 3.3 Derivation — Ethernet vs CAN Throughput Trade-off

The **effective bandwidth** of a CAN bus under worst-case loading (all nodes transmitting) is reduced due to arbitration and stuffing.

$$
\text{CAN useful load} = f_{\text{baud}} \times \frac{\text{payload bits}}{\text{total frame bits}}
$$

For an 8-byte CAN frame at 500 kbit/s:

$$
\text{Total frame bits} = 1_{\text{SOF}} + 11_{\text{ID}} + 1_{\text{RTR}} + 6_{\text{control}} + 64_{\text{data}} + 16_{\text{CRC}} + 2_{\text{ACK}} + 7_{\text{EOF}} = 108\ \text{bits}
$$

$$
\text{Frame time} = \frac{108\ \text{bits}}{500{,}000\ \text{bit/s}} = 216\ \mu s
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{64\ \text{bits}}{216\ \mu s} = 296{,}296\ \text{bit/s} \approx 296\ \text{kbit/s}
$$

For 100BASE-T1 Ethernet (100 Mbit/s full-duplex):

$$
\text{Throughput}_{\text{Ethernet}} \approx 100\ \text{Mbit/s}\ (\text{per direction})
$$

This is why modern vehicles migrate to Ethernet for high-bandwidth applications like camera and radar data.

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm — Choosing the Right Topology

```
START
  │
  ├─ Is the application safety-critical (ASIL-D)?
  │     ├─ YES → Mesh or Redundant Star (Ethernet)
  │     └─ NO  ↓
  │
  ├─ Is real-time determinism mandatory (X-by-Wire)?
  │     ├─ YES → FlexRay (Ring) or TSN Ethernet
  │     └─ NO  ↓
  │
  ├─ Is data bandwidth > 5 Mbit/s?
  │     ├─ YES → Automotive Ethernet (Star/Switched)
  │     └─ NO  ↓
  │
  ├─ Is cost the dominant constraint (mirror, seat)?
  │     ├─ YES → LIN (Bus, single-master)
  │     └─ NO  ↓
  │
  └─ DEFAULT → Classical CAN or CAN FD (Bus)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bus Topology — CAN Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    ECU1[ECU1\nPowertrain]:::node
    ECU2[ECU2\nABS]:::node
    ECU3[ECU3\nBody Control]:::node
    ECU4[ECU4\nInstrument Cluster]:::node
    TERM1[R = 120 Ohm]:::term
    TERM2[R = 120 Ohm]:::term
    BUS((CAN BUS\nTwisted Pair)):::bus

    ECU1 --- BUS
    ECU2 --- BUS
    ECU3 --- BUS
    ECU4 --- BUS
    BUS --- TERM1
    BUS --- TERM2

    classDef node fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef term fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Star Topology — Ethernet Switched Network

```mermaid
graph TD
    SW[Central Ethernet Switch\n100BASE-T1]:::switch
    Z1[Zone Controller FL\nFront Left]:::zone
    Z2[Zone Controller FR\nFront Right]:::zone
    Z3[Zone Controller RL\nRear Left]:::zone
    Z4[Zone Controller RR\nRear Right]:::zone
    CP[Central Compute\nAD/ADAS]:::compute
    HUD[Infotainment HUD]:::hud

    Z1 ---|Full Duplex| SW
    Z2 ---|Full Duplex| SW
    Z3 ---|Full Duplex| SW
    Z4 ---|Full Duplex| SW
    CP ---|1 Gbit/s| SW
    HUD ---|100 Mbit/s| SW

    classDef switch fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef zone fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef compute fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef hud fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Ring Topology — FlexRay Dual-Channel

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[ECU1\nEngine]:::ringnode
    N2[ECU2\nBrake]:::ringnode
    N3[ECU3\nSteer]:::ringnode
    N4[ECU4\nStability]:::ringnode
    CHA[Channel A\n10 Mbit/s]:::channel
    CHB[Channel B\n10 Mbit/s Redundant]:::channel

    N1 ---|CHA| N2
    N2 ---|CHA| N3
    N3 ---|CHA| N4
    N4 ---|CHA| N1

    N1 ---|CHB| N4
    N4 ---|CHB| N3
    N3 ---|CHB| N2
    N2 ---|CHB| N1

    classDef ringnode fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Mesh Topology — Autonomous Vehicle Backbone

```mermaid
graph TD
    CC[Central Compute L4]:::core
    ADAS1[ADAS Sensor 1\nLidar Front]:::sensor
    ADAS2[ADAS Sensor 2\nCamera Rear]:::sensor
    ADAS3[ADAS Sensor 3\nRadar]:::sensor
    ADAS4[ADAS Sensor 4\nUltrasonic]:::sensor
    BRK[Brake by Wire\nRedundant ECU]:::brake
    STR[Steer by Wire\nRedundant ECU]:::steer

    CC ---|path1| ADAS1
    CC ---|path2| ADAS2
    CC ---|path3| ADAS3
    CC ---|path4| ADAS4
    CC ---|path5| BRK
    CC ---|path6| STR

    ADAS1 ---|redundant| BRK
    ADAS2 ---|redundant| STR
    BRK ---|cross link| STR

    classDef core fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef sensor fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef brake fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef steer fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Modern Zonal (Tree) Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    HCC[High Performance\nCentral Computer]:::core
    GATE[Central Gateway]:::gw
    ZF[Zone Front]:::zone
    ZR[Zone Rear]:::zone
    ZL[Zone Left]:::zone
    ZRT[Zone Right]:::zone
    ECU1[ECU Headlight]:::ecu
    ECU2[ECU Radar]:::ecu
    ECU3[ECU Taillight]:::ecu
    ECU4[ECU Speaker]:::ecu
    ECU5[ECU Mirror]:::ecu
    ECU6[ECU Door]:::ecu
    LIN1[LIN Sub-bus]:::linbus
    LIN2[LIN Sub-bus]:::linbus

    HCC ---|1 Gbit/s Ethernet| GATE
    GATE --- ZF
    GATE --- ZR
    GATE --- ZL
    GATE --- ZRT
    ZF --- ECU1
    ZF --- ECU2
    ZR --- ECU3
    ZR --- ECU4
    ZL --- ECU5
    ZRT --- ECU6
    ECU1 --- LIN1
    ECU5 --- LIN2

    classDef core fill:#DC143C,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    classDef gw fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef zone fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef ecu fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef linbus fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice in the zonal architecture diagram that **LIN sub-buses hang off the zonal ECUs** — this is the *hybrid tree* topology in practice. It combines the cost-efficiency of LIN (bus) with the scalability of Ethernet (star/tree).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define network architecture in the context of automotive E/E systems. List any four types of in-vehicle network topologies.**

**Model Answer:**
Network architecture in automotive E/E systems refers to the structured topological arrangement of ECUs, sensors, actuators, and communication buses that enables data exchange for coordinated vehicle functions. The four major types are:
1. Bus topology (CAN, LIN)
2. Star topology (MOST, Automotive Ethernet)
3. Ring topology (FlexRay, MOST legacy)
4. Mesh topology (autonomous driving)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **topology** with **protocol**. Topology = physical/logical layout. Protocol = communication rules (CAN, Ethernet). Don't write "CAN is a topology" — write "CAN is a protocol that typically uses bus topology."

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Compare bus and star topologies with respect to fault tolerance and bandwidth scalability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Bus | Star |
|---|---|---|
| Fault tolerance | Low — a single short-circuit can bring down the entire bus | High — failure of one link only isolates that ECU |
| Bandwidth scalability | Limited (~5 Mbit/s for CAN FD) | Excellent (up to 1 Gbit/s and beyond per link) |
| Collision handling | Non-destructive bitwise arbitration | No collisions — full-duplex switched |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **CAN bus topology** in detail. Discuss how non-destructive bitwise arbitration ensures priority-based message transmission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **star topology** used in Automotive Ethernet networks. Explain why modern vehicles are migrating from CAN to Ethernet-based zonal architectures. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**CAN Bus Topology — Explanation:**

The CAN bus uses a **linear bus topology** where all ECUs tap into a single twisted-pair cable consisting of **CAN_H** and **CAN_L** lines. The cable is terminated at both ends with **120 Ω resistors** to prevent signal reflections.

**Physical Signaling:**

- **Dominant bit (logic 0):** CAN_H ≈ 3.5 V, CAN_L ≈ 1.5 V → differential ≈ **2 V**
- **Recessive bit (logic 1):** CAN_H ≈ 2.5 V, CAN_L ≈ 2.5 V → differential ≈ **0 V**

**Non-Destructive Bitwise Arbitration:**

When two ECUs transmit simultaneously, they arbitrate bit-by-bit starting from the MSB of the CAN ID. The ECU transmitting the **lower (dominant) ID wins** because of the wired-AND electrical behavior.

The losing ECU detects the mismatch by monitoring the bus while transmitting, immediately stops, and switches to receiver mode. **No data is destroyed** — hence "non-destructive."

The arbitration guarantees bounded latency for high-priority messages (e.g., brake-by-wire signals), making CAN suitable for real-time powertrain and chassis applications.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Star Topology with Automotive Ethernet:**

A **central switch** sits at the hub. Each ECU connects point-to-point to the switch via a single twisted-pair cable. The switch uses **MAC address tables** to forward Ethernet frames only to the correct destination port.

**Full-duplex operation** means each link can send and receive simultaneously — no collisions.

**Why migration to Ethernet?**

1. **Bandwidth:** 100 Mbit/s to 1 Gbit/s vs 1 Mbit/s for CAN — essential for cameras, radar, lidar.
2. **Weight:** Ethernet's star with zonal aggregation replaces kilometers of CAN wiring → weight reduction.
3. **OTA updates:** Ethernet's high bandwidth enables fast firmware flashing.
4. **Standardization:** Reuses IEEE 802.3 IP stack — easier integration with cloud and V2X.
5. **Zonal scalability:** New ECUs are added by connecting to the nearest zone controller without redesigning the whole bus.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]

**(a)** Explain **FlexRay's ring/dual-channel topology**. Why is it preferred for X-by-Wire applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain **mesh topology** in autonomous vehicles. Discuss why fail-operational systems demand redundant communication paths. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a):

**FlexRay Ring Topology:**

FlexRay uses a **dual-channel ring-like topology**. Each ECU connects to two neighbors, and messages travel along **Channel A** and a redundant **Channel B**. Either channel can carry the full traffic, providing fault tolerance.

**Why FlexRay for X-by-Wire:**

1. **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access):** Deterministic time slots ensure messages arrive within bounded latency (microsecond-level).
2. **Redundancy:** Channel B acts as a hot standby if Channel A fails.
3. **Bandwidth:** 10 Mbit/s per channel — far higher than CAN.
4. **Synchronization:** Global time base ensures all ECUs act on a synchronized clock — critical for coordinated braking and steering.
5. **Flexibility:** Supports both time-triggered (static) and event-triggered (dynamic) segments.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Mesh Topology in Autonomous Vehicles:**

In a full mesh, every ECU has a direct link to every other ECU. In a partial mesh (more common), only critical ECUs (brake-by-wire, steer-by-wire, ADAS compute) have multiple redundant paths.

**Fail-Operational Requirement:**

A **fail-operational** system must continue functioning even after a single fault. ISO 26262 ASIL-D mandates this for Level 3+ autonomous driving.

**Why mesh?**

- A single broken link does not isolate any ECU — alternative paths exist.
- Latency is reduced because data can take the shortest path.
- Critical ECUs (e.g., brake controller) can cross-check with another ECU for plausibility.

In the diagram shown in Section 4.4, the central compute unit has six independent paths to sensors and actuators. If any one fails, the others continue to deliver data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "protocol" with "topology" — CAN is a protocol that commonly uses bus topology.
> 2. **Always** state the standard (ISO number) when discussing any network: ISO 11898 for CAN, ISO 17458 for FlexRay, IEEE 802.3bw for Automotive Ethernet.
> 3. **Numericals:** If asked to compute CAN throughput, show the bit-by-bit frame breakdown (SOF, ID, RTR, control, data, CRC, ACK, EOF). Marks are awarded for **each step**.
> 4. **Diagrams:** Always draw termination resistors (120 Ω) at both ends of the CAN bus. Forgetting them costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Comparisons:** Use a **table** for comparison questions — never write paragraphs. Tables earn full marks faster in KTU valuation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Network Architecture** = topological layout + communication protocol + addressing scheme.
- **Bus topology** is the most widely used today (CAN, LIN, CAN FD) — simple, cheap, low wiring weight.
- **Star topology** dominates modern Ethernet-based zonal architectures — scalable, fault-isolated.
- **Ring topology** (FlexRay dual-channel) is used in safety-critical X-by-Wire with deterministic TDMA.
- **Mesh topology** is mandatory for L4/L5 autonomous vehicles — fail-operational, redundant.
- **Zonal/Tree topology** is the industry trend — central compute + zone controllers connected via Ethernet.
- CAN bus termination is **120 Ω at both ends** — never forget.
- CAN differential voltage: **2 V (dominant)** and **0 V (recessive)**.
- **Lower CAN ID = higher priority** in arbitration — non-destructive.
- **FlexRay** provides **10 Mbit/s per channel × 2 channels** = high bandwidth + redundancy.
- **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** uses a **single twisted pair** for full-duplex — saves weight vs traditional Ethernet (2 pairs).
- **LIN** is the lowest-cost sub-bus — used for mirrors, seats, window lifters.
- **MOST** (legacy infotainment ring) is being phased out in favor of Ethernet AVB/TSN.
- Always remember the **standards**: ISO 11898 (CAN), ISO 17458 (FlexRay), ISO 17987 (LIN), IEEE 802.3bw (100BASE-T1), IEEE 802.3bp (1000BASE-T1).
- For KTU board exams, **draw diagrams with termination resistors, channel labels, and ECU names** — visual clarity earns easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Automotive Network Communication** refers to the systematic exchange of digital data between multiple Electronic Control Units (ECUs), sensors, actuators, and diagnostic interfaces within a vehicle using standardized serial bus protocols. It replaces the traditional point-to-point wiring harness with a shared, multi-drop communication medium that enables real-time, deterministic, and noise-immune data transfer across powertrain, chassis, body, infotainment, and ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems) domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the PEAUT862 course outcomes, students must classify automotive communication protocols (CAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet), explain their frame structures, arbitration mechanisms, and physical layer signaling, and select appropriate buses for specific vehicle subsystems.

The term *Network* here is borrowed from data communication theory and adapted to the harsh electromagnetic, thermal, and vibration environment of a vehicle. Each protocol defines:
- **Physical Layer** — voltage levels, bit timing, connector pins
- **Data Link Layer** — framing, arbitration, error detection (CRC)
- **Application Layer** — message identifiers, signal interpretation (e.g., CAN 2.0A, CAN 2.0B, J1939)

The four dominant protocols assessed in KTU examinations are listed in **Table 1**.

| Protocol | Full Form | Standard Body | Typical Bandwidth |
|----------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **CAN** | Controller Area Network | Bosch / ISO 11898 | **1 Mbit/s** |
| **LIN** | Local Interconnect Network | LIN Consortium | **19.2 kbit/s** |
| **FlexRay** | FlexRay Consortium | ISO 17458 | **10 Mbit/s** |
| **Automotive Ethernet** | 100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1 | IEEE 802.3bw / 802.3bp | **100 Mbit/s – 1 Gbit/s** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Conference Table Meeting":**
> Imagine 30 engineers sitting around a single long conference table. Instead of shouting at each other across the room (which causes chaos), each engineer holds a small sign with a unique priority number. When two engineers try to speak at the same instant, the one with the **lower priority number wins** and finishes his message. The other waits silently and retries. The table itself is the **bus**. The signs are **message identifiers**. This is precisely how **CAN arbitration** works — a non-destructive, priority-based bitwise arbitration that makes CAN ideal for safety-critical vehicle networks.

A more granular analogy: think of the car as a hospital. The **CAN bus** is the ICU intercom system where critical life-support signals (airbag, ABS, engine RPM) must never collide. The **LIN bus** is the patient room call-button system — slow, cheap, and adequate for non-critical comfort functions (window lift, mirror adjust). The **FlexRay bus** is the operation theatre network — deterministic, redundant, time-triggered. The **Automotive Ethernet** is the hospital's high-speed imaging backbone carrying X-rays and 4K endoscopic video.

### 1.3 Why Automotive Networking is Necessary

A modern premium vehicle contains **> 100 ECUs** connected by **> 3 km of wiring** if wired point-to-point. Network communication solves four engineering problems:

1. **Weight Reduction** — a single twisted pair replaces dozens of dedicated wires (≈ 30% harness weight reduction).
2. **Scalability** — adding a new ECU requires only a tap on the bus, not new wiring.
3. **Data Sharing** — one sensor (e.g., wheel speed) is read by ABS, ESC, and TC simultaneously.
4. **Diagnostics** — a single OBD-II port can poll every ECU through the gateway.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **CAN High-Speed Signaling:** Differential voltage $V_{diff} = V_{CANH} - V_{CANL}$ where **Dominant bit = 2 V**, **Recessive bit = 0 V**.
- **CAN Bus Termination Resistance:** **120 Ω** at each end of the bus (characteristic impedance matching).
- **LIN Bus Voltage:** **12 V** single-wire (VBAT level).
- **FlexRay Bit Time:** derived from a **10 µs** macrotick at 10 Mbit/s.
- **Automotive Ethernet:** **PAM-3** (100BASE-T1) or **PAM-2** (1000BASE-T1) over a single twisted pair.
- **OBD-II Connector:** **SAE J1962** — 16-pin trapezoidal, 500 kbit/s CAN.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 OSI Layer Mapping in Automotive Networks

The ISO/OSI 7-layer model is collapsed in automotive networks because ECUs have limited memory and real-time constraints. Each protocol maps as follows:

| OSI Layer | CAN | LIN | FlexRay | Auto Ethernet |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------|---------------|
| 7 — Application | CANopen, J1939 | LIN Description File (LDF) | AUTOSAR | SOME/IP, DoIP |
| 2 — Data Link | CSMA/CR + Arbitration | Master-Slave Polling | TDMA + FTDMA | Full MAC |
| 1 — Physical | Differential 2-wire | Single-wire 12 V | Differential 2-wire or optical | Single twisted pair |

### 2.2 CAN Protocol — Detailed Mechanism

#### 2.2.1 CAN Frame Structure (Standard 2.0A, 11-bit ID)

A CAN data frame contains the following bit fields in order:
1. **SOF (Start of Frame)** — 1 dominant bit
2. **Arbitration Field** — 11-bit ID + 1-bit RTR (Remote Transmission Request)
3. **Control Field** — IDE, r0, DLC (4 bits)
4. **Data Field** — 0 to 8 bytes
5. **CRC Field** — 15-bit CRC + 1-bit delimiter
6. **ACK Field** — 2 bits (any receiving node flips recessive to dominant to acknowledge)
7. **EOF (End of Frame)** — 7 recessive bits
8. **IFS (Inter-Frame Space)** — 3 recessive bits

#### 2.2.2 Bitwise Arbitration — The Core Innovation

CAN uses a **CSMA/CD + AMP** (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection and Arbitration on Message Priority) scheme. Every transmitting node monitors the bus bit-by-bit. When two nodes transmit simultaneously:

- A node transmitting a **recessive (1)** bit that reads back a **dominant (0)** bit immediately **loses arbitration** and becomes a receiver.
- The node transmitting the dominant bit continues without any data loss.

This is why lower numerical IDs have higher priority. A frame with ID = 0x000 wins arbitration over a frame with ID = 0x7FF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Point:** CAN arbitration is called **non-destructive** because the higher-priority message is transmitted without delay or corruption. The losing node automatically retries once the bus becomes idle.

#### 2.2.3 Bit Stuffing

After every 5 consecutive identical bits, the transmitter inserts a stuff bit of opposite polarity. Receivers de-stuff to recover the original bit stream. This guarantees a sufficient number of edges for clock synchronization.

### 2.3 LIN Protocol

LIN is a **single-wire, master-slave, UART-based** protocol used for low-speed body electronics. The master sends a **header** (break, sync, identifier), and one of up to **16 slaves** responds with the **response** (data + checksum).

- **Schedule Table** — the master defines when each frame is sent.
- **Baud rate** — typically **19200 bit/s**, derived from the master's clock.
- **Cost** — uses the existing UART hardware of microcontrollers, no dedicated controller required.

### 2.4 FlexRay Protocol

FlexRay was designed for **x-by-wire** systems (steer-by-wire, brake-by-wire) requiring deterministic, fault-tolerant communication. Key features:

- **Dual-channel architecture** — Channel A and Channel B for redundancy.
- **Communication Cycle** — fixed duration of **5 ms** (configurable), divided into:
  - **Static Segment** — TDMA, fixed time slots, deterministic latency.
  - **Dynamic Segment** — FTDMA (Flexible Time Division), mini-slot based.
  - **Symbol Window** — for wake-up and special symbols.
  - **Network Idle Time (NIT)** — clock correction interval.
- **Bus Guardian** — hardware that prevents a faulty node from monopolizing the bus.

### 2.5 Automotive Ethernet

For ADAS cameras, LiDAR, and infotainment, CAN bandwidth is insufficient. Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1) provides:

- **Full-duplex** communication over a **single twisted pair**.
- **PAM-3** signaling (100BASE-T1) or **PAM-2** (1000BASE-T1).
- **Audio Video Bridging (AVB)** / **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** for synchronized, low-latency streaming.
- Protocols like **SOME/IP** (Scalable service-Oriented Middleware over IP) and **DoIP** (Diagnostics over IP) for OTA updates.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Symbol | Expression | Description / Unit |
|------------------|------------|--------------------|
| Bus Length vs Bit Rate (CAN) | $L_{max} \approx \dfrac{40\,m \cdot \text{Mbit/s}}{R_{bit}}$ | Maximum stub length trade-off; $L_{max}$ in m, $R_{bit}$ in Mbit/s |
| Bit Time | $t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bit}}$ | e.g., 1 µs at 1 Mbit/s, 4 µs at 250 kbit/s |
| Nominal Bit Time Parts | $t_{bit} = t_{SyncSeg} + t_{PropSeg} + t_{PhaseSeg1} + t_{PhaseSeg2}$ | CAN bit timing (in Time Quanta, $t_q$) |
| Time Quantum | $t_q = \dfrac{2 \cdot t_{CLK}}{BRP}$ | BRP = Baud Rate Prescaler (integer 1–64) |
| CRC Polynomial (CAN) | $g(x) = x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^8 + x^7 + x^4 + x^3 + 1$ | 15-bit CRC |
| Bus Termination | $R_{term} = 120\,\Omega$ | Two resistors at bus ends |
| Differential Voltage | $V_{diff} = V_{CANH} - V_{CANL}$ | Dominant ≈ 2 V, Recessive ≈ 0 V |
| FlexRay Macrotick | $MT = n \cdot gt$ | $gt$ = global time, $n$ = integer |
| Ethernet Coding Efficiency (100BASE-T1) | $\eta = \dfrac{100\,\text{Mbit/s}}{\text{symbol rate}}$ | 66.67% (4B/3B → PAM-3) |
| LIN Maximum Nodes | $N_{slave} \le 16$ | Limited by 6-bit ID |
| OBD-II Baud | $R_{OBD} = 500\,\text{kbit/s}$ | ISO 15765 (CAN-based) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CAN FD (Flexible Data-rate)** — extends data field to 64 bytes and bit rate to 5 Mbit/s during the data phase. Used in modern BMW, VW, and Tata passenger cars.
- **CAN XL** — the third generation, supports up to **2048-byte** payloads and **10 Mbit/s**, bridging the gap to Automotive Ethernet.
- **Gateway ECUs** route and translate frames between CAN, LIN, FlexRay, and Ethernet domains (e.g., the Volkswagen central gateway).
- **Functional Safety** — ISO 26262 mandates ASIL-D communication for steering and braking, driving the adoption of FlexRay and Ethernet TSN.
- **Cybersecurity** — CAN lacks authentication; modern vehicles use **SecOC** (Secure Onboard Communication) with MAC authentication for safety-critical messages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Maximum CAN Bus Length

The CAN standard (ISO 11898) specifies that the propagation delay across the bus must be less than the bit time, otherwise arbitration will fail. We derive the maximum cable length from this constraint.

**Step 1 — Define signal propagation speed.**

In a twisted pair cable, the signal travels at roughly **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (about 2/3 the speed of light due to dielectric constant ≈ 1.5 for PVC insulation).

**Step 2 — Define one-way propagation delay per meter.**

$$
t_{pd} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s/m} = 5\,\text{ns/m}
$$

**Step 3 — Define bit time at chosen baud rate.**

For a CAN network at **1 Mbit/s**:

$$
t_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{R_{bit}} = \dfrac{1}{10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 1000\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Allocate a portion of bit time for propagation.**

The CAN specification requires that round-trip propagation be completed within the **Propagation Segment (PropSeg)**. Empirically, PropSeg is approximately **40% of one bit time** for safety margin.

$$
t_{PropSeg} \approx 0.4 \times t_{bit} = 0.4 \times 1000 = 400\,\text{ns}
$$

**Step 5 — Account for two-way (round-trip) propagation.**

During arbitration, the dominant bit must propagate from the farthest node to all other nodes and the recessive bit from the loser must propagate back. This is a **round-trip** delay:

$$
t_{round-trip} = 2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot L_{max}
$$

**Step 6 — Equate round-trip to PropSeg and solve.**

$$
2 \cdot t_{pd} \cdot L_{max} \leq t_{PropSeg}
$$

$$
L_{max} \leq \dfrac{t_{PropSeg}}{2 \cdot t_{pd}}
$$

$$
L_{max} \leq \dfrac{400 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 5 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
L_{max} \leq \dfrac{400}{10} = 40\,\text{m}
$$

**Step 7 — General formula.**

For any baud rate $R_{bit}$ (in bit/s) and propagation delay $t_{pd}$ (in s/m):

$$
L_{max} = \dfrac{0.4}{2 \cdot R_{bit} \cdot t_{pd}} = \dfrac{0.2}{R_{bit} \cdot t_{pd}}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Bit Time from Time Quanta (CAN Bit Timing)

The CAN controller's clock oscillator runs at $f_{CLK}$. The Baud Rate Prescaler (BRP) divides it down to yield the Time Quantum $t_q$.

**Step 1 — Define oscillator period.**

$$
T_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Time Quantum.**

$$
t_q = \dfrac{2 \cdot T_{CLK}}{BRP} = \dfrac{2}{f_{CLK} \cdot BRP}
$$

**Step 3 — Express each segment in quanta.**

- SyncSeg = 1 $t_q$ (always)
- PropSeg = $T_{PropSeg} / t_q$ quanta
- PhaseSeg1 = $T_{PS1} / t_q$ quanta
- PhaseSeg2 = $T_{PS2} / t_q$ quanta

**Step 4 — Sum to find total Number of Time Quanta per Bit (NBT).**

$$
NBT = 1 + T_{PropSeg} + T_{PS1} + T_{PS2}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the actual bit rate.**

Since $t_{bit} = NBT \cdot t_q$:

$$
t_{bit} = NBT \cdot \dfrac{2}{f_{CLK} \cdot BRP}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $R_{bit}$.**

$$
R_{bit} = \dfrac{1}{t_{bit}} = \dfrac{f_{CLK} \cdot BRP}{2 \cdot NBT}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

Suppose $f_{CLK} = 16\,\text{MHz}$, $BRP = 4$, $NBT = 16$, $T_{PropSeg} = 3$, $T_{PS1} = 7$, $T_{PS2} = 5$ quanta.

$$
R_{bit} = \dfrac{16 \times 10^{6} \times 4}{2 \times 16} = \dfrac{64 \times 10^{6}}{32} = 2 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bit/s}
$$

Result: $R_{bit} = \mathbf{2\,\text{Mbit/s}}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of CRC Residual for CAN

The CAN CRC polynomial is:

$$
g(x) = x^{15} + x^{14} + x^{10} + x^{8} + x^{7} + x^{4} + x^{3} + 1
$$

The transmitting node computes CRC over the **SOF, Arbitration, Control, and Data fields** (a total of $k$ bits). Let the polynomial of the message be $M(x)$.

**Step 1 — Multiply message polynomial by $x^{15}$.**

$$
M'(x) = x^{15} \cdot M(x)
$$

This appends 15 zero bits as placeholder for the CRC.

**Step 2 — Perform modulo-2 polynomial division.**

$$
R(x) = M'(x) \mod g(x)
$$

$R(x)$ is a 15-bit remainder.

**Step 3 — Transmit frame.**

The transmitted bit stream is $T(x) = x^{15} \cdot M(x) + R(x)$, which is exactly divisible by $g(x)$.

**Step 4 — Receiver verification.**

The receiver computes:

$$
T(x) \mod g(x) = (x^{15} M(x) + R(x)) \mod g(x) = 0
$$

If the remainder is **non-zero**, an error is flagged and the frame is destroyed by sending an **Error Flag** (6 dominant bits).

**Step 5 — Bit Error Probability.**

For a 15-bit CRC, the probability of an undetected error is approximately $2^{-15} \approx 3.05 \times 10^{-5}$, which is sufficient for CAN's automotive safety requirements.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Bus

| Criterion | LIN | CAN | FlexRay | Auto Ethernet |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------|---------------|
| Bandwidth | 19.2 kbit/s | 1 Mbit/s | 10 Mbit/s | 100 Mbit/s – 1 Gbit/s |
| Wires | 1 | 2 | 2 or 4 | 1 (twisted pair) |
| Cost per node | Very Low | Low | High | Medium |
| Determinism | Yes (master) | Best-effort | Yes (TDMA) | Yes (TSN) |
| Redundancy | No | No | Yes (dual ch.) | Optional |
| Application | Window, seat, mirror | Engine, ABS, airbag | Steer/Brake-by-wire | Camera, radar, OTA |
| Typical OEM Tier | Body | Powertrain + Chassis | Chassis (legacy) | ADAS + Infotainment |
| ISO Standard | ISO 17987 | ISO 11898 | ISO 17458 | IEEE 802.3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Vehicle Network Architecture (Domain-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PowerTrain["POWERTRAIN DOMAIN\nEngine ECU, Transmission ECU\nCAN HS 500 kbit/s"]
    Chassis["CHASSIS DOMAIN\nABS, ESC, EPS\nCAN HS 500 kbit/s / FlexRay"]
    Body["BODY DOMAIN\nBCM, Lights, Locks\nCAN LS 125 kbit/s / LIN"]
    ADAS["ADAS DOMAIN\nCamera, Radar, LiDAR\nAutomotive Ethernet 1 Gbit/s"]
    Info["INFOTAINMENT\nHead Unit, HUDS\nMOST / Ethernet 100 Mbit/s"]
    Diag["OBD-II PORT\nJ1962 Connector\nCAN 500 kbit/s"]
    Gateway["CENTRAL GATEWAY ECU\nRoutes across domains\nCAN, LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet"]
    Cloud["TELEMATICS / OTA\nLTE 5G Modem\nEthernet to Cloud"]

    PowerTrain --- Gateway
    Chassis --- Gateway
    Body --- Gateway
    ADAS --- Gateway
    Info --- Gateway
    Gateway --- Diag
    Gateway --- Cloud
```

### 4.2 CAN Bit Arbitration — Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Node A and Node B\nstart transmission\nat same instant"]
    TxA["Node A transmits\nID bit: DOMINANT 0"]
    TxB["Node B transmits\nID bit: RECESSIVE 1"]
    Bus["Bus level becomes\nDOMINANT 0 due to AND logic"]
    ReadA["Node A reads back DOMINANT\nMatches expected — CONTINUES"]
    ReadB["Node B reads back DOMINANT\nExpected RECESSIVE — LOSES"]
    Win["Node A wins arbitration\ncompletes transmission"]
    Retry["Node B becomes receiver\nretries after bus idle"]

    Start --> TxA
    Start --> TxB
    TxA --> Bus
    TxB --> Bus
    Bus --> ReadA
    Bus --> ReadB
    ReadA --> Win
    ReadB --> Retry
```

### 4.3 CAN Data Frame — Field Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOF["SOF\n1 bit"]
    Arb["ARBITRATION\n11 bit ID + RTR"]
    Ctrl["CONTROL\nIDE + r0 + DLC"]
    Data["DATA\n0 to 8 bytes"]
    CRC["CRC\n15 bit + delim"]
    ACK["ACK\n2 bits"]
    EOF["EOF\n7 bits"]
    IFS["IFS\n3 bits"]

    SOF --> Arb --> Ctrl --> Data --> CRC --> ACK --> EOF --> IFS
```

### 4.4 FlexRay Communication Cycle — Time Partitioning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Cycle["FlexRay Cycle\n5 ms total"]
    Static["STATIC SEGMENT\nTDMA\nDeterministic"]
    Dynamic["DYNAMIC SEGMENT\nFTDMA\nEvent-driven"]
    Symbol["SYMBOL WINDOW\nWake-up"]
    NIT["NETWORK IDLE TIME\nClock sync"]

    Cycle --> Static --> Dynamic --> Symbol --> NIT
```

### 4.5 Signal Level Schematic — CAN Differential Voltage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CAN_BUS["CAN BUS PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        V1["V_CANH = 3.5 V (Dominant)"]
        V2["V_CANH = 2.5 V (Recessive)"]
        V3["V_CANL = 1.5 V (Dominant)"]
        V4["V_CANL = 2.5 V (Recessive)"]
        DiffD["V_diff = 2.0 V → Dominant 0"]
        DiffR["V_diff = 0.0 V → Recessive 1"]
    end

    V1 --> DiffD
    V3 --> DiffD
    V2 --> DiffR
    V4 --> DiffR
```

### 4.6 OSI Layer Mapping Across Protocols

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    App["LAYER 7 APPLICATION\nCANopen / J1939 / SOME-IP"]
    DL["LAYER 2 DATA LINK\nCSMA-CR / TDMA / MAC"]
    Phy["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL\nTwisted pair / Single wire / Optical"]

    subgraph CAN_NODE["CAN NODE"]
        App
        DL
        Phy
    end

    subgraph LIN_NODE["LIN SLAVE"]
        LApp["LIN LDF"]
        LDL["UART Polling"]
        LPhy["Single wire 12 V"]
        LApp --> LDL --> LPhy
    end

    subgraph ETH_NODE["AUTO ETHERNET"]
        EApp["DoIP / SOME-IP"]
        EDL["Full MAC CSMA-CD"]
        EPhy["PAM-3 / PAM-2"]
        EApp --> EDL --> EPhy
    end

    Phy --- Bus["SHARED VEHICLE BUS INFRASTRUCTURE"]
    LPhy --- Bus
    EPhy --- Bus
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the Controller Area Network. List its key features.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Controller Area Network (CAN) is a multi-master, message-oriented serial bus protocol standardized by **ISO 11898**, developed by **Bosch** in 1986 for in-vehicle networking.

**Key features (any three, 1 mark each):**

1. **Multi-master** — any node can initiate transmission when the bus is idle.
2. **Non-destructive bitwise arbitration** — the highest-priority ID wins without data loss.
3. **Differential signaling** — $V_{diff} = V_{CANH} - V_{CANL}$, providing strong EMI immunity.
4. **Built-in error detection** — CRC, bit stuffing, ACK, bit monitoring, and error confinement.
5. **Data payload** — 0 to 8 bytes per frame (CAN 2.0A and 2.0B).
6. **Maximum bandwidth** — 1 Mbit/s in standard CAN; up to 5 Mbit/s in CAN FD.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Compare CAN and LIN protocols in terms of speed, cost, and typical applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | CAN | LIN |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| **Speed** | up to 1 Mbit/s (CAN 2.0) or 5 Mbit/s (CAN FD) | 19.2 kbit/s |
| **Cost** | Requires dedicated CAN controller IC (~₹80) | Uses existing UART — almost zero added cost |
| **Topology** | Multi-master, differential 2-wire | Master-slave, single-wire |
| **Typical Application** | Engine, ABS, airbag, transmission | Window lift, mirror adjust, seat control, roof module |

LIN is used as a **sub-bus** under a CAN master for low-bandwidth body electronics, reducing system cost.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Focus on CAN Protocol

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** 

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **CAN data frame format** as per CAN 2.0A. Discuss the role of **bit stuffing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **CSMA/CD + AMP arbitration** with a suitable example of two ECUs transmitting simultaneously. Show which node wins and why. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CAN Data Frame Format (7 marks):**

The CAN 2.0A data frame consists of the following bit fields, transmitted MSB-first on the bus.

| Field | Bits | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| **SOF** | 1 | Start of Frame — single dominant bit synchronizes all nodes |
| **Arbitration** | 11 + 1 | 11-bit Identifier + 1-bit RTR (Remote Transmission Request) |
| **Control** | 6 | IDE (1), r0 (1), DLC (4) — Data Length Code (0–8 bytes) |
| **Data** | 0–64 | Payload, 0 to 8 bytes |
| **CRC** | 16 | 15-bit CRC + 1-bit delimiter |
| **ACK** | 2 | Any receiver overwrites recessive to dominant to acknowledge |
| **EOF** | 7 | End of Frame — 7 recessive bits |
| **IFS** | 3 | Inter-Frame Space — bus must remain idle before next frame |

**Bit Stuffing (3 marks of part a):**

After every **5 consecutive identical bits**, the transmitter automatically inserts a **stuff bit** of opposite polarity. The receiver de-stuffs by removing these bits. This guarantees a minimum edge density for clock resynchronization. Example:

Original: `00000000` (8 recessive bits)
Stuffed: `00000` + `1` + `000` (stuff bit inserted after 5 zeros)

If 6 dominant bits are detected in a row, an error flag is raised.

**(b) CSMA/CD + AMP Arbitration (7 marks):**

CAN uses **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection and Arbitration on Message Priority (CSMA/CD + AMP)**.

**Procedure:**
1. Every node monitors the bus; if idle, any node may transmit.
2. As each bit is transmitted, the node reads back the actual bus level.
3. If a node transmits a recessive (1) bit but reads a dominant (0) bit, it has lost arbitration and immediately becomes a receiver.
4. The winning node continues uninterrupted.

**Worked Example:**

Consider two ECUs:

| ECU | Message ID (binary) |
|-----|---------------------|
| ECU_A — Brake controller | `001 1010 1000` |
| ECU_B — Wiper controller | `010 1110 0100` |

Both start transmission at the same instant.

- **Bit 0 (MSB):** ECU_A transmits `0` (dominant), ECU_B transmits `0` (dominant). Bus = dominant. Both read dominant, both continue.
- **Bit 1:** ECU_A = `0` (dominant), ECU_B = `1` (recessive). Bus = dominant. ECU_A reads dominant (OK). ECU_B reads dominant (mismatch — expected recessive), so **ECU_B loses arbitration**.

**ECU_A wins** and completes its brake message. ECU_B will retry once the bus is idle. The lower numerical ID = higher priority.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Focus on LIN, FlexRay, Ethernet

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **LIN protocol architecture**, including master-slave communication, frame structure, and schedule table. State two applications. **(7 marks)**
