# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Automotive Ethernet. List any two IEEE standards associated with it.**

**Model Answer:**

> Automotive Ethernet refers to the use of IEEE 802.3 Ethernet technology for in-vehicle networking, optimized to operate over a single unshielded twisted pair (UTP) with full-duplex communication, providing high bandwidth and reduced wiring weight.
>
> Two IEEE standards:
> 1. **IEEE 802.3bw (100BASE-T1)** – 100 Mbps over a single pair.
> 2. **IEEE 802.3bp (1000BASE-T1)** – 1 Gbps over a single pair.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for standards with brief explanation.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is a Cyber-Physical System (CPS)? How does an autonomous vehicle qualify as a CPS?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** is a system in which computational (cyber) processes and physical processes are tightly integrated through sensors, actuators, and feedback loops, with the cyber layer influencing the behavior of the physical layer in real time.
>
> An autonomous vehicle is a CPS because:
> 1. **Perception (Physical):** Sensors (camera, radar, lidar) measure the environment.
> 2. **Decision (Cyber):** AI algorithms perform object detection and path planning.
> 3. **Actuation (Physical):** Steering, brake, and throttle execute the decision.
> 4. **Feedback:** Continuous closed-loop operation at ~10–100 Hz.
>
> *(1 mark definition, 2 marks for autonomous vehicle justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain with neat block diagrams the architecture of an **Automotive Ethernet 100BASE-T1 PHY layer**. Discuss the role of **PAM-3 modulation** and **echo cancellation** in enabling full-duplex over a single twisted pair.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a derivation, show that a single **4K ADAS camera (3840×2160, 30 fps, 12-bit raw)** generates approximately **3 Gbps** of data. Explain why this bandwidth requirement makes **Automotive Ethernet** mandatory in modern vehicles.

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** Draw the MII → PCS → PAM-3 Encoder → Echo Canceller → TX Driver → UTP Cable → RX Equalizer → PAM-3 Decoder → MII chain (refer to SECTION 4.2).

2. **PAM-3 Modulation (2 marks):** Each symbol can take **3 amplitude levels** ($+1$, $0$, $-1$), encoding $\log_2(3) \approx 1.585$ bits per symbol. This packs more data per unit bandwidth compared to NRZ, enabling **100 Mbps over a single pair** with a 66.67 MHz symbol clock.

3. **Echo Cancellation (2 marks):** Since both ends transmit simultaneously on the same pair, the PHY must subtract its own transmitted signal from the received signal. The **echo canceller** uses adaptive filters (LMS or similar) trained during link startup. This is what makes **full-duplex over one pair** physically possible.

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **Formula (2 marks):**
$$
R_{cam} = W \times H \times f \times b
$$

2. **Substitution (2 marks):**
$$
R_{cam} = 3840 \times 2160 \times 30 \times 12 = 2{,}985{,}984{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 2.99 \text{ Gbps}
$$

3. **Comparison (2 marks):**

| Bus | Bandwidth | Sufficient for 3 Gbps? |
|---|---|---|
| CAN | 1 Mbps | ❌ No |
| CAN-FD | 5 Mbps | ❌ No |
| FlexRay | 10 Mbps | ❌ No |
| 100BASE-T1 | 100 Mbps | ❌ No |
| **1000BASE-T1** | **1 Gbps** | **Partial – needs compression** |
| **2.5G/5G/10G** | **≥ 2.5 Gbps** | **✅ Yes** |

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Therefore, **multi-gig Automotive Ethernet** is mandatory for modern ADAS camera pipelines, justifying its adoption over legacy buses.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **four functional layers of a Connected Car Cyber-Physical System (Perception, Communication, Decision, Actuation)**. With a block diagram, show the closed-loop feedback operation.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Explain the various **V2X communication modes** (V2V, V2I, V2N, V2P, V2G). Compare **DSRC** and **C-V2X (5G)** technologies with respect to latency, range, and standard.

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Layer Descriptions (4 marks):**
   - **Perception Layer:** Cameras, radar, lidar, GPS/IMU collect raw environmental data.
   - **Communication Layer:** Automotive Ethernet (in-vehicle) and C-V2X/DSRC (external) transmit data.
   - **Decision Layer (Cyber):** Sensor fusion, AI/ML, path planning on domain controllers.
   - **Actuation Layer (Physical):** Steering, brake, throttle execute the decision.

2. **Closed-Loop Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the CPS loop — Sensors → Network → Compute → Actuators → Vehicle Dynamics → Sensors (refer to SECTION 4.1). Emphasize the **feedback path** that distinguishes a CPS from a simple embedded system.

#### Model Solution to (b):

1. **V2X Modes (3 marks):** Briefly define each — V2V (vehicle to vehicle collision avoidance), V2I (traffic infrastructure), V2N (cloud), V2P (pedestrians), V2G (grid/EV).

2. **Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X 5G (3GPP Rel. 16) |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | < 5 ms | < 5 ms (URLLC mode) |
| **Range** | ~300 m | ~1 km |
| **Spectrum** | 5.9 GHz ITS band | 5.9 GHz + cellular bands |
| **Standard Maturity** | Mature, deployed (US) | Emerging, global trend |
| **Network Coverage** | No (ad-hoc only) | Yes (cellular network) |

3. **Conclusion (notional):** 5G C-V2X is the future trend because of its integration with cellular infrastructure and ultra-reliable low-latency communication (URLLC) profile.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing IEEE Standards**: Students often write *"IEEE 802.3"* alone for Automotive Ethernet. The examiner expects the **specific sub-standard** (e.g., **802.3bw for 100BASE-T1, 802.3bp for 1000BASE-T1**). Lose 1 mark if not specified.
> 2. **Conflating CAN and CAN-FD**: Mentioning only CAN (1 Mbps) for ADAS shows outdated knowledge. Use **CAN-FD (5 Mbps)** as the legacy baseline.
> 3. **Missing the "Cyber" in CPS**: A common error is to describe a connected car as just a "networked car" or "IoT device". A CPS **must** highlight the **closed-loop feedback** between cyber computation and physical actuation — this is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 4. **PAM-3 vs. NRZ confusion**: Writing *"PAM-3 uses 2 levels"* is wrong — it specifically uses **3 amplitude levels**. This is a 1-mark trap.
> 5. **V2X – Forgetting V2G**: In Kerala, with growing EV adoption, V2G is increasingly relevant. Listing only V2V, V2I, V2N is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Automotive Ethernet** replaces legacy CAN/LIN/FlexRay for **high-bandwidth** in-vehicle networking using a **single UTP** pair.
- Key standards: **IEEE 802.3bw (100BASE-T1 @ 100 Mbps)** and **IEEE 802.3bp (1000BASE-T1 @ 1 Gbps)**.
- **PAM-3 modulation** and **echo cancellation** enable **full-duplex** over a single twisted pair.
- The **bandwidth motivation** comes from a single 4K camera producing **~3 Gbps** of raw data.
- A **Connected Car** is a CPS integrating perception, communication, decision, and actuation with **V2X** connectivity.
- **V2X** includes **V2V, V2I, V2N, V2P, V2G, V2D** — knowing all six is expected at KTU.
- **DSRC** uses IEEE 802.11p; **C-V2X (5G)** uses 3GPP Release 16/17 with **URLLC** profile for sub-5 ms latency.
- The **CPS control loop latency budget** for safety systems (AEB, lane keep) must be **< 50 ms**, with **automotive Ethernet TSN** providing the deterministic **< 1 ms** communication slice.
- **Zonal architecture** (front, rear, left, right zones + central computer) is the future trend, replacing legacy domain architecture.
- **Cybersecurity** is integral to the connected car CPS — the automotive industry uses standards like **ISO/SAE 21434** and **UNECE WP.29** for secure OTA and V2X.
- **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** is the Ethernet profile that adds **determinism, time synchronization (IEEE 802.1AS), and traffic shaping** for safety-critical automotive traffic.
- **OTA (Over-the-Air) updates** depend on the connected car architecture and require cybersecurity hardening to prevent unauthorized firmware flashing.
- **Multi-Gig Automotive Ethernet (2.5G/5G/10G)** is the next step to support **uncompressed high-resolution sensor data** for L3+ autonomous driving.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip for KTU 2024 Scheme:** When asked about *connected cars*, always frame your answer as a **Cyber-Physical System** with a **closed-loop feedback structure**. Simply listing "Wi-Fi, GPS, 5G" will only earn partial marks. The CPS framing is the **2-mark differentiator** in a 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Sensor Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wireless Sensor Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** in the automotive context is a spatially distributed autonomous network of spatially dispersed and dedicated sensors that cooperatively monitor physical or environmental conditions of a vehicle, such as temperature, pressure, vibration, tire pressure, proximity, and engine parameters, and wirelessly transmit the collected data through the network to a main location (Electronic Control Unit / Central Gateway).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examinable):**
> A Wireless Sensor Network (WSN) consists of a large number of low-cost, low-power, multifunctional **sensor nodes** that are densely deployed in a vehicle (or around it) to sense, compute, and communicate data wirelessly over short distances to perform intelligent monitoring, control, and actuation functions.

## 1.2 Key Components of an Automotive WSN

| Component | Function in Vehicle |
|---|---|
| **Sensor Node (Mote)** | Sensing + Processing + Transmitting (e.g., TPMS sensor) |
| **Base Station / Gateway** | ECU or Central Gateway that collects data from all nodes |
| **Radio Transceiver** | Wireless communication medium (RF, ZigBee, BLE, UWB) |
| **Power Source** | Battery / Energy harvesting (vibration, heat) |
| **Actuator (optional)** | Performs action (e.g., airbag deployment, brake actuation) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Human Nervous System"**
> 
> Imagine your body. Your skin has millions of tiny touch sensors (pressure, temperature, pain). Each sensor does not run a wire to the brain — instead, it sends a wireless-like electrochemical signal through nerves to the spinal cord and brain, which then decides what to do (move your hand away from fire). 
> 
> A **Vehicle WSN works exactly the same way**:
> - **Sensor nodes** = Nerves in your skin (TPMS sensor in tire, oxygen sensor in exhaust)
> - **Wireless Channel** = Spinal cord (RF signals)
> - **ECU/Gateway** = Brain (decides to deploy airbag, adjust fuel injection, etc.)

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & WSN Characteristics (KTU High-Yield)

- **Node Density:** Typically 10–100 nodes per vehicle in modern premium cars.
- **Operating Frequency Bands:** **2.4 GHz (ISM band)**, 433 MHz, 5.8 GHz (UWB for radar).
- **Communication Range:** **0.1 m to 100 m** (intra-vehicle < 5 m, V2X up to 1000 m).
- **Power Budget per Node:** **< 100 mW** (battery lifetime target: 10 years for TPMS).
- **Data Rate:** 20–250 kbps (sufficient for most sensor telemetry).
- **Standard Protocol:** **IEEE 802.15.4** (basis for ZigBee, 6LoWPAN, WirelessHART).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Wireless Sensor Node (Stack View)

A sensor node in an automotive WSN is a **5-layer micro-system**:

1. **Sensing Layer (Hardware Transducer)** — Converts physical quantity → electrical signal.
   - Examples: MEMS accelerometer, thermistor, piezoelectric pressure sensor, Hall-effect sensor.
2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC Layer** — Amplifies, filters, and digitizes the analog signal.
3. **Processing Layer (Microcontroller/DSP)** — Local computation, data aggregation, encryption.
4. **Communication Layer (Radio Transceiver)** — Handles MAC protocol, packetization, RF transmission.
5. **Power Management Layer** — DC-DC conversion, sleep modes, energy harvesting circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Each sensor node has **three major subsystems**: *Sensing*, *Processing*, and *Communication* — and one *Power Supply*. The energy consumption follows the order: **Communication > Processing > Sensing**.

## 2.2 Network Topologies in Automotive WSN

| Topology | Description | Automotive Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Star** | All nodes communicate directly with central ECU | TPMS (each tire → central receiver) |
| **Mesh** | Nodes can route data through neighbours | Interior cabin sensors (lighting, climate) |
| **Cluster / Tree (Hybrid)** | Nodes form clusters with cluster heads | Body Control Module sub-networks |
| **P2P (Peer-to-Peer)** | Direct node-to-node communication | V2V safety messaging (DSRC) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mesh is preferred in vehicles:** Redundancy. If one node fails, data reroutes automatically — critical for safety systems like brake-by-wire or steer-by-wire.

## 2.3 Wireless Communication Protocols Used in Vehicles

| Protocol | Frequency | Data Rate | Range | Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | 250 kbps | 10–100 m | Cabin monitoring, RKE |
| **Bluetooth / BLE** | 2.4 GHz | 1–2 Mbps | 10 m | Infotainment, diagnostics (OBD-II) |
| **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11p/DSRC)** | 5.9 GHz | 6–27 Mbps | 1000 m | V2X, V2V, V2I |
| **UWB (IEEE 802.15.4z)** | 3.1–10.6 GHz | 110 Mbps | 10 m | Keyless entry, in-cabin radar |
| **RFID (Passive)** | 125 kHz–13.56 MHz | — | < 1 m | Tire tagging, immobilizer |
| **Cellular V2X (C-V2X)** | 1.9 GHz / 5.9 GHz | 100+ Mbps | km | Connected car, OTA updates |

## 2.4 Energy Consumption Model (Fundamental Formula)

The energy consumed to transmit **k** bits over distance **d** using the radio model:

$$E_{Tx}(k,d) = \begin{cases} k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}, & \text{if } d < d_0 \quad \text{(Free-space)} \\ k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4}, & \text{if } d \ge d_0 \quad \text{(Multipath)} \end{cases}$$

$$E_{Rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$$

The energy to receive k bits is purely electronic, while transmission has an additional path-loss term.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Unit | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_{Tx}(k,d) = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{fs} d^{2}$ | Transmit energy (free space) | Joules | $d < d_0$ |
| $E_{Tx}(k,d) = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d^{4}$ | Transmit energy (multipath) | Joules | $d \ge d_0$ |
| $E_{Rx}(k) = k E_{elec}$ | Receive energy | Joules | Constant per bit |
| $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ | Crossover distance | metres | $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit |
| $S/N = P_{rx}/N$ | Signal-to-Noise ratio | dimensionless | Use Shannon: $C = B \log_2(1+S/N)$ |
| $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^{2}$ | Friis free-space path loss | Watts | **Bold** link budget |
| $T_{sleep} / T_{total}$ | Duty cycle ratio | — | Lower duty cycle → longer battery life |
| $N_{cluster} = k_{opt}$ | Optimal cluster size | nodes | $k_{opt} = \sqrt{N/\pi} \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` (LaTeX) or `abs()` instead of `|` in exams when writing the absolute value of a variable — otherwise KTU online portal / answer scripts will deduct marks for ambiguous notation.**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a modern luxury car (e.g., Tesla Model S, BMW 7 Series), **over 150 sensors** are deployed. WSN technology eliminates **> 2 km of copper wiring**, reducing:
- Vehicle mass by **~ 30–50 kg** (improving fuel economy by 5–8%).
- Assembly time and harness cost.
- Failure points (no physical connectors to corrode).

This is the foundation of **x-by-wire systems** and the **Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Clusters in a WSN (LEACH Model)

### Problem Statement
Given **N** sensor nodes randomly distributed over a region of area **A** (m²), and a sink located at the centre, derive the expression for the **optimal number of clusters** $k_{opt}$ that minimizes total network energy consumption.

### Assumptions
1. All nodes transmit to the cluster head (CH) over distance $d_{to\_CH}$.
2. All CHs transmit aggregated data to the sink over distance $d_{to\_BS}$.
3. Energy for data aggregation: $E_{DA} = 5$ nJ/bit/signal.

### Step 1: Total Energy of One Cluster (Non-CH nodes)

Each non-CH node transmits **k** bits to the CH.

$$
E_{nonCH} = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{fs} \cdot d_{toCH}^{2}
$$

### Step 2: Energy of the Cluster Head

The CH receives $N/k - 1$ frames, aggregates them, and transmits to the sink.

$$
E_{CH} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) k \cdot E_{elec} + \frac{N}{k} k \cdot E_{DA} + k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{mp} \cdot d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

### Step 3: Expected Square Distance from Node to CH (for uniformly distributed nodes)

$$
E[d_{toCH}^{2}] = \frac{A}{2\pi k}
$$

### Step 4: Total Energy per Cluster

$$
E_{cluster} = \left(\frac{N}{k} - 1\right) \cdot E_{nonCH} + E_{CH}
$$

### Step 5: Substituting and Simplifying

$$
E_{cluster} \approx \left(\frac{N}{k}\right) k E_{elec} + \left(\frac{N}{k}\right) k \varepsilon_{fs} \frac{A}{2\pi k} + E_{elec} \frac{N}{k} k + \frac{N}{k} k E_{DA} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

$$
E_{cluster} = 2 N E_{elec} + \frac{N A \varepsilon_{fs}}{2\pi k} + N E_{DA} + k \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

### Step 6: Total Network Energy

$$
E_{total} = k \cdot E_{cluster} = 2 N k E_{elec} + \frac{N A \varepsilon_{fs}}{2\pi} + N k E_{DA} + k^{2} \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4}
$$

### Step 7: Differentiate with respect to k and set to zero

$$
\frac{d E_{total}}{d k} = 2 N E_{elec} + N E_{DA} + 2 k \varepsilon_{mp} d_{toBS}^{4} = 0
$$

Solving for k:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{elec} + E_{DA}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{1}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

For $E_{elec} = E_{DA}$, this simplifies to the well-known LEACH result:

$$
\boxed{\,k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{1}{d_{toBS}^{2}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The optimal cluster count is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance to the base station**. A vehicle ECU placed in the centre of the body minimizes the optimal cluster count needed — this is why automotive ECUs are mounted centrally.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: WSN vs Wired Bus in Vehicles

| Parameter | WSN (Wireless) | Wired Bus (CAN/LIN) |
|---|---|---|
| Weight | **Light** (no harness) | Heavy (cable harness) |
| Cost | Moderate (RF modules) | High (copper + connectors) |
| Latency | 1–50 ms (protocol dependent) | < 1 ms (CAN) |
| Reliability | Susceptible to EMI | Highly reliable |
| Security | Vulnerable to spoofing/jamming | Physical access needed |
| Scalability | Easy (add a node) | Requires harness rework |
| Power | Battery-limited per node | Powered by bus |
| Best for | Body, comfort, TPMS | Powertrain, chassis control |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Modern vehicles use a **hybrid architecture** — wired CAN/LIN for **safety-critical** systems (braking, steering) and WSN for **comfort and monitoring** (TPMS, cabin lighting, seat occupancy, climate control).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Architecture of a Wireless Sensor Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Quantity] --> B[Sensor / Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning]
    C --> D[ADC Conversion]
    D --> E[Microcontroller Unit]
    E --> F[Memory and Processing]
    E --> G[Radio Transceiver]
    G --> H[Antenna]
    H --> I[Wireless Channel]
    I --> J[Gateway or ECU]
    J --> K[Decision and Actuation]
    K --> L[Vehicle Control Action]
    P[Power Source Battery or Harvested] -.-> B
    P -.-> C
    P -.-> D
    P -.-> E
    P -.-> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: In-Vehicle WSN Network Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ClusterA[Tire Pressure Cluster]
        T1[Tire FL Sensor]
        T2[Tire FR Sensor]
        T3[Tire RL Sensor]
        T4[Tire RR Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ClusterB[Body and Cabin Cluster]
        S1[Seat Occupancy Sensor]
        S2[Climate Sensor]
        S3[Ambient Light Sensor]
        S4[Door Position Sensor]
    end
    subgraph ClusterC[Engine and Drivetrain Cluster]
        E1[Oxygen Sensor]
        E2[Knock Sensor]
        E3[Vibration Sensor]
        E4[Oil Quality Sensor]
    end
    ClusterA --> GW[Central Gateway ECU]
    ClusterB --> GW
    ClusterC --> GW
    GW --> CAN[CAN Bus to Powertrain]
    GW --> Display[Driver HMI Display]
    GW --> Cloud[Cloud V2X via Cellular]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Protocol Stack for Automotive WSN

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer TPMS Climate Diagnostics]
    L2[Network Layer IPv6 6LoWPAN RPL Routing]
    L3[Transport Layer UDP CoAP]
    L4[MAC Layer IEEE 802.15.4 CSMA-CA]
    L5[Physical Layer 2.4 GHz O-QPSK DSSS]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: WSN Energy Flow in a Sensor Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Power Source Battery] --> PMU[Power Management Unit]
    PMU --> SLP[Sleep Mode Controller]
    SLP -->|Wake Event| SEN[Sensing Circuit]
    SLP -->|Active| PROC[Processor]
    SLP -->|Active| TX[Radio Transmitter]
    SEN --> PROC --> TX
    TX --> ANT[Antenna to Wireless Channel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When asked to "draw the architecture of a WSN node" in exams, always show **five blocks** (Sensing, ADC, Processing, Transceiver, Power) and indicate arrows for **data flow** and **dashed arrows** for **power supply** — examiners specifically look for this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Q: Define a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN). List any four applications of WSN in automobiles.**

**Model Answer:**
A Wireless Sensor Network is a network of spatially distributed autonomous sensor nodes that monitor physical or environmental conditions and cooperatively pass data wirelessly through the network to a central station (ECU).
> [!NOTE]
> **Automotive applications of WSN:**
> 1. **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** — pressure/temperature sensors in each tyre.
> 2. **Climate control** — distributed temperature and humidity sensors in the cabin.
> 3. **Occupant detection** — seat occupancy sensors for airbag deployment logic.
> 4. **Engine health monitoring** — vibration, knock, and oil-quality sensors.
> 5. **Parking assist** — ultrasonic/radar sensors around the vehicle perimeter.
> 6. **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) safety messaging** using DSRC.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing applications with 1-line explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**Q: Differentiate between ZigBee and Bluetooth in the context of in-vehicle WSNs.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | ZigBee (IEEE 802.15.4) | Bluetooth / BLE |
|---|---|---|
| Data Rate | 250 kbps | 1 Mbps (Classic) / 2 Mbps (BLE 5) |
| Power Consumption | Very low (~30 mA active) | Higher (~50 mA active) |
| Network Topology | Mesh, Star, Tree | Star (Piconet) |
| Typical Use in Vehicle | Body sensors, RKE, lighting | Infotainment, OBD-II diagnostics |
| Range | 10–100 m | ~10 m |
| Nodes per Network | 65,000 | 8 active (Classic) |

*(3 marks: Comparison table or 3 distinct points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a sensor node in an automotive WSN. Discuss the functions of each block and state the role of the power management unit in extending battery life.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the five functional blocks of a sensor node.**

**Model Solution:**

A sensor node in an automotive WSN consists of the following five functional units:

1. **Sensing Unit (Transducer):** Converts physical phenomena (pressure, temperature, vibration) into electrical signals. In a TPMS, this is a MEMS pressure sensor.

2. **Processing Unit (Microcontroller / DSP):** Performs local data processing — filtering, thresholding, data aggregation, and encryption. Uses an ultra-low-power MCU (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0+).

3. **Transceiver Unit (Radio):** Handles wireless transmission and reception. Uses ISM 2.4 GHz band. Implements MAC protocols (CSMA-CA) and packet framing.

4. **Power Supply Unit:** Provides regulated DC power; typically a coin-cell Li battery (3 V, 220 mAh) for TPMS, or energy-harvested power (vibration, thermal) for cabin sensors.

5. **Memory Unit:** Stores firmware, calibration constants, and buffered data (e.g., 8 KB flash + 2 KB RAM).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the role of power management in extending battery life.**

**Model Solution:**

Power management is critical because battery replacement in deployed nodes (especially TPMS) is impractical. Key techniques include:

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{active} + T_{sleep}}
$$

A TPMS sensor spends ~99.9 % of its life in **sleep mode**, waking for < 10 ms every 30 s.

**Power-saving techniques:**

- **Sleep / Wake cycling** — MCU draws < 1 µA in sleep vs. ~ 5 mA active.
- **Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS):** Reduces $V_{dd}$ during low computation.
- **Radio duty cycling:** Transceiver OFF except during TX/RX windows.
- **Energy harvesting:** Piezoelectric (vibration), thermoelectric (exhaust heat), or photovoltaic.

**Numerical example (TPMS):**
Battery energy: $E = 3 \text{ V} \times 220 \text{ mAh} = 660 \text{ mWh}$.
Average current: $1 \text{ mA}$ (with duty cycling).
Lifetime:

$$
L = \frac{660 \text{ mWh}}{3 \text{ V} \times 1 \text{ mA}} = 220 \text{ hours} \quad \text{(continuous)} \rightarrow \sim 5 \text{ years with 0.05\% duty cycle.}
$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q: Compare the star, mesh, and cluster (hybrid) topologies used in automotive WSNs. For a vehicle with 100 sensor nodes uniformly distributed over an area of 100 m² with the sink at the centre, compute the optimal number of clusters. Assume $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013$ pJ/bit/m⁴, and $d_{toBS} = 10$ m.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Comparison of Topologies (tabular form):**

| Parameter | Star | Mesh | Cluster (Hybrid) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Structure | All nodes → central hub | Nodes interconnected, multi-hop | Groups + cluster heads |
| Reliability | Low (single point of failure) | High (redundant paths) | Medium-High |
| Energy Efficiency | Poor for distant nodes | High | Very High (LEACH) |
| Latency | Low (direct) | Higher (multi-hop) | Medium |
| Scalability | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| Complexity | Low | High | Medium |
| Automotive Use | TPMS (4 wheels → 1 ECU) | Cabin/body sensors | Body + powertrain sensors |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Numerical Problem — Optimal Clusters:**

Given:
- $N = 100$ nodes
- $A = 100 \text{ m}^2$
- $d_{toBS} = 10 \text{ m}$
- $\varepsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$
- $\varepsilon_{mp} = 0.0013 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^4$

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
k_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \frac{1}{d_{toBS}^{2}}
$$

**Step 1:** Compute the first term.

$$
\sqrt{\frac{100}{2\pi}} = \sqrt{15.915} = 3.989
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the second term.

$$
\sqrt{\frac{10}{0.0013}} = \sqrt{7692.31} = 87.71
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the third term.

$$
\frac{1}{d_{toBS}^{2}} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply all three.

$$
k_{opt} = 3.989 \times 87.71 \times 0.01
$$

$$
k_{opt} = 3.498 \approx 3.5 \approx 4 \text{ clusters}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Approximately **4 clusters** should be formed to minimize total network energy. This is a classic KTU-style numerical — practice similar problems from past papers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Marks Deduction:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing WSN with Ad-hoc Networks:** WSNs are *data-centric* (query-based), whereas ad-hoc networks are *address-centric*. Always mention this distinction in definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Marks are deducted if $k_{opt}$ is reported without "clusters" or energy without "Joules".
> 3. **Drawing only 4 blocks instead of 5** when asked for sensor node architecture — examiners expect **Sensing, Processing, Transceiver, Power, Memory**.
> 4. **Mixing up IEEE standards:** ZigBee = 802.15.4; Wi-Fi = 802.11; Bluetooth = 802.15.1; WAVE/DSRC = 802.11p. Tabulate these clearly.
> 5. **Ignoring the dashed vs. solid line convention** in block diagrams (data flow = solid, power = dashed). This is a recurring KTU mark-loser.
> 6. **Using $|x|$ in a markdown table** — this breaks the table layout. Always use $\vert x \vert$ or `abs(x)` in KTU answer scripts and online submissions.
> 7. **Skipping assumptions** before derivations: the LEACH derivation requires the *uniform distribution* and *equal-energy nodes* assumptions — state them first.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wireless Sensor Networks**

- **Definition:** A WSN is a *spatially distributed*, *self-organizing*, *multi-hop* network of low-power sensor nodes communicating wirelessly.
- **Core Functions of a node:** **Sense → Process → Communicate.**
- **Five architectural blocks:** Sensing, ADC, Processing, Transceiver, Power (sometimes Memory listed separately).
- **Topologies:** Star (TPMS), Mesh (cabin), Cluster/Tree (hybrid body + powertrain).
- **Key Protocols:** IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee), BLE, DSRC (802.11p), UWB, C-V2X.
- **Energy Model:** $E_{Tx} = k E_{elec} + k \varepsilon d^n$ with $n = 2$ (free-space) or $n = 4$ (multipath).
- **Optimal clusters (LEACH):** $k_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{N}{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_{fs}}{\varepsilon_{mp}}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{d_{toBS}^2}$.
- **Crossover distance:** $d_0 = \sqrt{\varepsilon_{fs}/\varepsilon_{mp}}$.
- **Duty cycling** is the primary method of power saving in WSN nodes.
- **Automotive use cases:** TPMS, climate control, occupant detection, parking assist, V2V/V2X, engine health, lighting, RKE, keyless entry.
- **Modern trend:** Software-Defined Vehicle (SDV) + OTA updates + V2X rely on in-vehicle WSN.
- **Key challenges:** Limited battery, harsh EMI environment, latency for safety, cybersecurity (spoofing/jamming), and reliability over the vehicle's 10–15 year lifespan.
- **Numbers to memorise:** 2.4 GHz ISM band, 250 kbps (ZigBee), 100 mW node power, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Telematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Telematics** is defined as the synergistic integration of **telecommunications** and **informatics** (computer science) specifically applied to the acquisition, processing, storage, and bidirectional transmission of vehicle and driver-related data over long distances. In the KTU 2024 automotive context, telematics encompasses the onboard **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)**, wireless communication transceivers, satellite positioning modules, and the cloud-based back-end infrastructure that collectively enable services such as **eCall**, **fleet management**, **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI)**, **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication, **Over-the-Air (OTA)** updates, and remote diagnostics.

The term itself is a portmanteau coined by combining the French *télécommunications* and *informatique*, and was popularized in the automotive domain during the late 1990s with the introduction of services like **OnStar (1996)** and **eCall (EU mandate 2018 – Regulation (EU) 2015/758)**.

### 1.1.1 Sub-Domains Inside Automotive Telematics

| Sub-Domain | Primary Function |
|------------|------------------|
| **Vehicle Telematics** | Real-time tracking, speed, geofencing |
| **GPS/GNSS Telematics** | Positioning using satellites (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo) |
| **Insurance Telematics** | Driver Behaviour Scoring (DBS) for UBI premiums |
| **Mobile Resource Management (MRM)** | Fleet and asset tracking |
| **Connected Car Services** | Infotainment, weather, concierge, OTA |
| **V2X Telematics** | Communication between vehicles and infrastructure |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Always-On Digital Co-Pilot"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students**: Think of automotive telematics as a *smartphone permanently bolted inside your car* that **never sleeps, never forgets, and is always whispering to a control tower**.

- **Smartphone analogy**: Just as your phone knows your location (via GPS), sends WhatsApp messages (via cellular network), and syncs photos to the cloud (via Wi-Fi/4G), the car's TCU knows the vehicle's location, transmits engine parameters, and pushes diagnostic logs to the manufacturer's cloud.
- **Control Tower analogy**: Just like an air traffic controller monitors every aircraft on radar, a fleet manager monitors every truck on a digital dashboard.
- **Black Box analogy**: Like the Flight Data Recorder in an aircraft, the TCU continuously records *speed*, *braking*, *acceleration*, *fuel level*, *crash pulse (Δv)*, and *seatbelt status* — but unlike a passive black box, it actively *transmits* this data in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single biggest conceptual distinction you must remember for KTU exams:
> **Telematics ≠ Infotainment**. Infotainment (music, video, navigation display) is the *passenger-facing entertainment layer*, while Telematics is the *data-acquisition-and-communication layer* that operates silently in the background, often without the driver's awareness.

---

## 1.3 Why Telematics is a High-Yield KTU Topic

The EU eCall mandate (April 2018) made telematics **mandatory** in all new passenger cars sold in the European Union. India's **AIS-140 standard** (Automotive Industry Standards, by ARAI) made it mandatory for all public transport vehicles (buses, taxis, ambulances) from April 2018 onward. These regulatory pushes have made telematics an essential topic for any Indian B.Tech Automobile / Electrical / Electronics graduate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard to Remember**: **AIS-140** mandates that every public service vehicle in India must have a vehicle location tracking device, an emergency button, and a 4G/5G communication module connected to a government-mandated backend server.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Four-Layer Model

Automotive telematics operates as a hierarchical four-layer architecture. Each layer has distinct responsibilities, and questions in KTU 2024 frequently test your understanding of these layers.

### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensor Layer (The "Senses")
- GNSS receiver (GPS L1/L5, GLONASS, Galileo, IRNSS-NAVIC for India)
- Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU): 3-axis accelerometer + 3-axis gyroscope
- Wheel-speed sensors, yaw-rate sensor
- OBD-II / CAN bus interface reading ECU data
- Crash sensor (high-G accelerometer ≥ ±24 g) for eCall trigger
- Microphone (for hands-free emergency voice call)
- Temperature, fuel-level, door-state sensors

### Layer 2 — Processing / Edge Layer (The "Brain")
- **Telematics Control Unit (TCU)** — typically a 32-bit automotive-grade microcontroller (e.g., NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850, Qualcomm MDM9x15)
- Runs a real-time operating system (RTOS) like AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive, FreeRTOS, or QNX Neutrino
- Performs data fusion (sensor fusion of GPS + IMU via Kalman Filter)
- Stores events in non-volatile memory (FRAM, NAND Flash)
- Triggers emergency calls on crash detection
- Handles OTA update verification (cryptographic signature check using RSA-2048 or ECDSA)

### Layer 3 — Communication / Network Layer (The "Voice")
- Cellular modem: **2G/3G/4G LTE** (and emerging **5G NR-V2X**)
- Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax) for high-bandwidth OTA downloads
- Bluetooth / BLE for smartphone tethering
- DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications, IEEE 802.11p) for V2X
- C-V2X (Cellular V2X, 3GPP Release 14+) — increasingly preferred over DSRC
- Satellite communication (Iridium, Inmarsat) for remote / off-grid vehicles
- CAN / LIN / Ethernet (100BASE-T1, 1000BASE-T1) for in-vehicle networking

### Layer 4 — Cloud / Application Layer (The "Memory & Intelligence")
- Telematics Service Provider (TSP) servers
- Big-data analytics (Apache Kafka, Spark)
- Driver behaviour scoring engines
- Fleet management dashboards
- Government regulatory backends (e.g., NIC backend for AIS-140)
- API gateways exposing data to third-party apps

## 2.2 High-Yield Formula Sheet (KTU 2024)

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | GPS Pseudorange | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | metres (m) | c ≈ 3×10⁸ m/s |
| 2 | 3D Position Fix (Trilateration) | $\sqrt{(x-x_s)^2+(y-y_s)^2+(z-z_s)^2} = \rho_i$ | m | Needs ≥4 satellites for (x,y,z,t) |
| 3 | Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | d in km, f in MHz |
| 4 | Link Budget | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - L_{total}$ | dBm | Must exceed receiver sensitivity |
| 5 | Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_{2}(1 + \mathrm{SNR})$ | bits/s | B = bandwidth, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio |
| 6 | Doppler Shift (Satellite) | $f_d = \dfrac{v_{rel} \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Hz | $v_{rel}$ ≈ 928 m/s for GPS L1 |
| 7 | Crank-Nicolson Update (1D Kalman) | $x_{k+1} = \Phi x_k + \Gamma w_k$ | — | $\Phi$ = state transition matrix |
| 8 | Acceleration Event Detection | $\Delta v = \int a(t) \, dt$ | m/s | Used by eCall for crash severity |
| 9 | Packet Transmission Time | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{N_{bits}}{R_{bitrate}}$ | seconds | $N_{bits}$ = frame size |
| 10 | Battery Standby (TCU) | $E = V \cdot I_{sleep} \cdot t$ | Wh | Typically $I_{sleep} \leq 100 \mu A$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip**: In KTU 2024 papers, **FSPL** and **Shannon's capacity** are the two most-tested formulas in any wireless/communication module. Memorize the constant **32.44** (which arises because $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) = 32.44$ when $c$ is in m/s, $f$ in Hz, $d$ in metres — but the form given above uses km and MHz for cleaner numbers).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fleet operators** (Tata Motors, Ashok Leyland, Amazon Logistics) reduce fuel costs by 8–15% using telematics-driven route optimization.
- **Insurance companies** (Acko, ICICI Lombard in India) offer 20–30% premium discounts for drivers who install telematics dongles that prove safe driving.
- **Stolen vehicle recovery** success rates exceed **80%** when a covert TCU is fitted (LoJack, OnStar).
- **Predictive maintenance** using telematics data reduces unscheduled downtime by up to **45%** in commercial trucking.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation)

The Friis transmission equation in isotropic form is:

$$
P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

where $P_t$ = transmitted power, $P_r$ = received power, $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains, $\lambda$ = wavelength, $d$ = distance.

**Step 1** — Convert to decibel form by taking $10\log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$
10\log_{10}(P_r) = 10\log_{10}(P_t) + 10\log_{10}(G_t) + 10\log_{10}(G_r) + 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 2** — Apply the identity $10\log_{10}(X^2) = 20\log_{10}(X)$ and pull out the negative sign:

$$
P_r\,[\mathrm{dB}] = P_t\,[\mathrm{dB}] + G_t\,[\mathrm{dBi}] + G_r\,[\mathrm{dBi}] - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
$$

**Step 3** — Identify the bracketed term as the **Free-Space Path Loss** (FSPL):

$$
FSPL = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)
$$

**Step 4** — Substitute $\lambda = c / f$ to convert wavelength into frequency:

$$
FSPL = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{f}\right)
$$

**Step 5** — Simplify $-20\log_{10}(c/f) = 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(c)$. With $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s and expressing $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz, the constants algebraically collapse to:

$$
\boxed{\;FSPL\,[\mathrm{dB}] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d\,[\mathrm{km}]) + 20\log_{10}(f\,[\mathrm{MHz}])\;}
$$

This is the exact form KTU examiners expect you to reproduce.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — GPS Position Fix Using Trilateration

Consider 4 satellites at known positions $S_1(x_1, y_1, z_1)$, $S_2(x_2, y_2, z_2)$, $S_3(x_3, y_3, z_3)$, $S_4(x_4, y_4, z_4)$. The receiver is at unknown position $(x, y, z)$ with unknown clock bias $b$ (multiplied by $c$ to convert to distance). The pseudorange to the $i$-th satellite is:

$$
\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2 + (z-z_i)^2} + c \cdot b
$$

Writing out four such equations (one per satellite) yields a non-linear system. Linearize about an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$:

$$
\rho_i \approx \rho_{i,0} + \frac{(x_0-x_i)}{\rho_{i,0}}\Delta x + \frac{(y_0-y_i)}{\rho_{i,0}}\Delta y + \frac{(z_0-z_i)}{\rho_{i,0}}\Delta z + c\,\Delta b
$$

where $\rho_{i,0} = \sqrt{(x_0-x_i)^2+(y_0-y_i)^2+(z_0-z_i)^2}$. In matrix form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{x_0-x_1}{\rho_{1,0}} & \frac{y_0-y_1}{\rho_{1,0}} & \frac{z_0-z_1}{\rho_{1,0}} & 1 \\
\frac{x_0-x_2}{\rho_{2,0}} & \frac{y_0-y_2}{\rho_{2,0}} & \frac{z_0-z_2}{\rho_{2,0}} & 1 \\
\frac{x_0-x_3}{\rho_{3,0}} & \frac{y_0-y_3}{\rho_{3,0}} & \frac{z_0-z_3}{\rho_{3,0}} & 1 \\
\frac{x_0-x_4}{\rho_{4,0}} & \frac{y_0-y_4}{\rho_{4,0}} & \frac{z_0-z_4}{\rho_{4,0}} & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ c\,\Delta b \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Letting $\mathbf{H}$ denote the geometry matrix (the $4 \times 4$ matrix above), $\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho}$ the residual measurement vector, and $\Delta\mathbf{x}$ the unknown correction vector:

$$
\Delta\boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H}\,\Delta\mathbf{x}
$$

The Least-Squares solution is:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta\mathbf{x} = \left(\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta\boldsymbol{\rho}\;}
$$

The corrected state becomes $(x_0+\Delta x,\ y_0+\Delta y,\ z_0+\Delta z,\ b_0+\Delta b)$, and the iteration is repeated until convergence (typically 5–10 iterations give sub-metre accuracy).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Capacity for OTA Update Bandwidth Planning

A vehicle must download a **2 GB firmware update** over a **4G LTE** link with bandwidth $B = 10$ MHz and measured $\mathrm{SNR} = 15$ dB. The maximum theoretical data rate is:

$$
\mathrm{SNR_{linear}} = 10^{15/10} = 31.623
$$

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SNR}) = 10 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(32.623)
$$

$$
\log_2(32.623) = \frac{\ln(32.623)}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.485}{0.693} \approx 5.027\ \text{bits/s/Hz}
$$

$$
C = 10^{7} \cdot 5.027 = 5.027 \times 10^7\ \text{bits/s} \approx 50.27\ \text{Mbps}
$$

Time to download 2 GB:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{2 \times 10^9 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{5.027 \times 10^7 \text{ bits/s}} \\
t &= \frac{1.6 \times 10^{10}}{5.027 \times 10^7} \\
t &\approx 318.3\ \text{seconds} \approx 5.3\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

In real-world deployments with overhead, TCP retransmissions, and signal fading, the practical throughput is **30–40% of the Shannon limit**, so realistic time ≈ **12–18 minutes**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Link Budget for TCU-to-Server

A TCU transmits at $P_t = +23$ dBm on a 4G LTE band at $f = 1800$ MHz. The distance to the nearest cell tower is $d = 3$ km. The TCU antenna gain is $G_t = +2$ dBi and the tower antenna gain is $G_r = +18$ dBi. Compute the received power.

**Step 1** — Free-space path loss:

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(3) + 20\log_{10}(1800)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(3) = 20 \times 0.4771 = 9.542
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(1800) = 20 \times 3.2553 = 65.106
$$

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 9.542 + 65.106 = 107.09\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 2** — Received power:

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL = 23 + 2 + 18 - 107.09 = -64.09\ \text{dBm}
$$

A typical LTE base-station receiver sensitivity is around **−101 dBm**, so a received power of **−64 dBm** leaves about **37 dB of link margin** — a healthy margin that can absorb vehicle body blockage, rain attenuation, and multi-path fading.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 32.44 magic number** is the conversion constant that arises when you express the Friis equation with $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz: $20\log_{10}(4\pi \times 10^3 / 3\times 10^2) = 32.44$. KTU examiners love this number; commit it to memory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define automotive telematics. List any four of its major applications in modern vehicles.**

**Model Answer**:
> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Automotive telematics is the integrated use of telecommunications and informatics to acquire, process, and transmit vehicle data over wireless networks between the vehicle, the cloud, and end-user applications.

**Four major applications (any 4 × 0.5 mark = 2 marks)**:
1. **Emergency Call (eCall)** — automatically contacts emergency services on crash detection.
2. **Fleet Management** — real-time vehicle tracking, route optimization, fuel monitoring.
3. **Usage-Based Insurance (UBI)** — calculates premium based on actual driver behaviour.
4. **Remote Diagnostics & OTA Updates** — manufacturer monitors health and pushes firmware.
5. **Stolen Vehicle Recovery** — covert TCU reports location to law enforcement.
6. **Infotainment & Connected Navigation** — real-time traffic, weather, points of interest.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of GNSS in a telematics control unit. Why is a minimum of four satellites required for a 3D position fix?**

**Model Answer**:
> [!NOTE]
> **Role of GNSS (1.5 marks):** The Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS — which includes GPS, GLONASS, Galileo, and India's IRNSS/NavIC) provides the TCU with absolute geolocation. The TCU computes pseudoranges from the travel time of satellite signals and applies trilateration to obtain latitude, longitude, altitude, and time. This location is then transmitted to the cloud for tracking, routing, emergency dispatch, and geofencing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 4 satellites (1.5 marks):** A 3D position fix involves 4 unknowns — three spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$ plus the receiver's clock bias $b$ (since consumer-grade GNSS receivers do not carry an atomic clock and drift unpredictably). Each visible satellite provides one equation of the form $\rho_i = \sqrt{(x-x_i)^2+(y-y_i)^2+(z-z_i)^2} + c\cdot b$, so 4 satellites are mathematically the minimum required to solve for 4 unknowns.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Module in the End-Semester Examination carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the four-layer architecture of an automotive telematics system. Identify the key hardware components in each layer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A TCU operates on a 4G LTE link at a carrier frequency of **1800 MHz**. The line-of-sight distance to the nearest base station is **5 km**. The TCU transmits at **+20 dBm** with an antenna gain of **+1 dBi**, and the base-station antenna gain is **+17 dBi**. Calculate (i) the free-space path loss, and (ii) the received power at the base-station receiver. Comment on the link margin if the receiver sensitivity is **−103 dBm**.

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture of Automotive Telematics [7 Marks]**

| Layer | Function | Key Hardware |
|-------|----------|--------------|
| **1. Perception / Sensor Layer** | Collects vehicle + environment data | GNSS receiver, IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope), CAN/OBD-II interface, crash sensor (high-G ≥ 24 g), microphone, fuel-level sensor, gyroscope |
| **2. Edge / Processing Layer** | Data fusion, event detection, OTA verification | Telematics Control Unit (TCU) with 32-bit automotive MCU (NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850), real-time OS (AUTOSAR), FRAM/NAND Flash, hardware security module (HSM) for cryptography |
| **3. Communication / Network Layer** | Wireless transmission of data | 4G/5G cellular modem, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth/BLE, C-V2X/DSRC modem, satellite modem, in-vehicle Ethernet (100BASE-T1) |
| **4. Cloud / Application Layer** | Storage, analytics, user interface | Telematics Service Provider servers, big-data pipelines (Kafka, Spark), driver-scoring engines, fleet dashboards, mobile apps |

*Marking scheme: 1 mark for naming the layers, 4 marks for hardware per layer (1 each), 2 marks for neat diagram.*

**(b) Link Budget Calculation [7 Marks]**

Given: $f = 1800$ MHz, $d = 5$ km, $P_t = +20$ dBm, $G_t = +1$ dBi, $G_r = +17$ dBi, receiver sensitivity $S = -103$ dBm.

**(i) Free-space path loss** [3 marks]:

$$
FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(1800)
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(5) = 20 \times 0.6990 = 13.98\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(1800) = 65.11\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{FSPL = 32.44 + 13.98 + 65.11 = 111.53\ \text{dB}}
$$

**(ii) Received power** [2 marks]:

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL
$$

$$
P_r = 20 + 1 + 17 - 111.53
$$

$$
\boxed{P_r = -73.53\ \text{dBm}}
$$

**Link margin comment** [2 marks]:

$$
M = P_r - S = -73.53 - (-103) = +29.47\ \text{dB}
$$

A margin of nearly **30 dB** is excellent and can absorb vehicle body attenuation (8–12 dB for a metal roof), rain fade (1–3 dB at 1800 MHz), and multipath fading (5–10 dB). The link is robust for reliable operation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the **eCall system** mandated by the European Union. Discuss the Minimum Set of Data (MSD) transmitted during an eCall event and explain why GNSS and cellular connectivity are both essential for its operation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A fleet-management telematics server needs to store position updates from **5,000 vehicles**. Each vehicle transmits a **200-byte packet** every **10 seconds**. Calculate: (i) the average data rate in **packets per second**, (ii) the average bandwidth in **bytes per second**, and (iii) the daily storage requirement in **gigabytes** (assume 30% overhead for protocol headers, replication, and indexing).

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) eCall System Working [7 Marks]**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **eCall (Emergency Call)** is an EU-mandated (Regulation (EU) 2015/758, in force since **31 March 2018**) automatic emergency-call system fitted to all new M1 (passenger car) and N1 (light commercial vehicle) category vehicles sold in the European Union.

**Working sequence** [4 marks]:
1. **Crash detection** — A high-G accelerometer (typically ≥ ±24 g range, sampling at ≥ 1 kHz) detects a sudden deceleration event.
2. **TCU activation** — The TCU immediately powers the cellular modem and GNSS receiver.
3. **Automatic 112 dialing** — The TCU places a voice + data call to the European emergency number **112**.
4. **MSD transmission** — A 140-byte (post-2017 standard) Minimum Set of Data packet is transmitted over the voice channel using in-band modem technology (4-level FSK at 300 baud, as per CEN TS 16454).
5. **Voice channel opening** — Occupants can speak to the PSAP (Public Safety Answering Point) operator.
6. **Manual SOS button** — Drivers can also press an SOS button to trigger eCall manually (e.g., for medical emergencies without a crash).

**MSD contents** [2 marks]: GPS coordinates (latitude, longitude), position time stamp, vehicle VIN, direction of travel, number of occupants (if available), service activation type (automatic vs. manual), recent crash-related data (Δv, belt status).

**Why both GNSS and cellular are essential** [1 mark]: GNSS provides the *where* (geographic location of the crash) — without it, the ambulance cannot find the vehicle on a remote highway. Cellular provides the *how to tell* (communication channel to the PSAP) — without it, the data and voice never leave the vehicle. Both are non-negotiable.

**(b) Telematics Bandwidth & Storage Calculation [7 Marks]**

Given: $N = 5{,}000$ vehicles, packet size $P = 200$ bytes, update interval $T = 10$ s, overhead $H = 30\%$.

**(i) Packets per second** [2 marks]:

$$
\text{pps} = \frac{N}{T} = \frac{5{,}000}{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{pps} = 500\ \text{packets per second}}
$$

**(ii) Bandwidth in bytes per second** [2 marks]:

$$
B = \text{pps} \times P = 500 \times 200
$$

$$
\boxed{B = 100{,}000\ \text{bytes/s} \approx 100\ \text{kB/s}}
$$

Converting to bits: $100{,}000 \times 8 = 800\ \text{kbps} = 0.8\ \text{Mbps}$ — easily handled by a single 4G LTE backhaul link.

**(iii) Daily storage with 30% overhead** [3 marks]:

Raw data per day:

$$
D_{raw} = 100{,}000\ \text{bytes/s} \times 86{,}400\ \text{s/day}
$$

$$
D_{raw} = 8.64 \times 10^9\ \text{bytes/day} = 8.64\ \text{GB/day}
$$

With 30% overhead:

$$
D_{stored} = D_{raw} \times (1 + H) = 8.64 \times 1.30
$$

$$
\boxed{D_{stored} = 11.232\ \text{GB/day}}
$$

Annual storage ≈ **4.1 TB** — easily within the capacity of modern cloud object storage (e.g., AWS S3, Azure Blob).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Telematics Questions (2024 pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing telematics with infotainment** — A surprising number of students write about touchscreens, Apple CarPlay, and Android Auto when asked about telematics. Telematics is the *data and communication* layer, not the *entertainment* layer. This alone can cost **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark answer.
>
> 2. **Wrong GNSS satellite count** — Some students write "3 satellites are enough for 3D positioning." This is incorrect because the unknown clock bias requires a **fourth** satellite. Writing "3 satellites for 2D + time" is also wrong — 2D positioning still requires the clock-bias equation, so **4 satellites are minimum for any 3D fix**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units in FSPL** — KTU deducts **0.5–1 mark** if you use $d$ in metres or $f$ in Hz in the 32.44 formula. The constant 32.44 only works when $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**. Get this wrong and your answer is numerically wrong.
>
> 4. **Skipping the link-margin comment** — In link-budget problems, KTU expects you to **compute the margin** and comment on its adequacy. Just calculating $P_r$ without a margin statement loses **1–2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Drawing flowcharts instead of block diagrams** — KTU examiners want **labelled functional block diagrams** with arrows showing signal/data flow. Hand-drawn "boxes" in an answer sheet are acceptable; a flowchart with decision diamonds where they are not required looks unprofessional.
>
> 6. **Missing the MSD byte count** — The MSD is exactly **140 bytes** (post-2017 eCall standard). Writing "few hundred bytes" or "around 1 KB" loses marks. Commit the number **140 bytes** to memory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read this on the morning of your exam:**
>
> - **Telematics = Telecommunications + Informatics.** Integrated wireless data exchange between vehicle ↔ cloud ↔ user.
> - **Four layers**: Sensor → Edge (TCU) → Communication → Cloud/Application.
> - **GNSS minimum satellites for 3D fix = 4** (three for position, one for clock bias).
> - **GNSS systems to remember**: GPS (USA), GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), IRNSS/NavIC (India), BeiDou (China).
> - **TCU core hardware**: Automotive MCU + cellular modem + GNSS receiver + HSM + non-volatile memory.
> - **FSPL formula** (dB): $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$.
> - **Shannon capacity**: $C = B \log_2(1 + \mathrm{SNR})$ — practical throughput is 30–40% of theoretical.
> - **eCall** is mandatory in EU since 31 March 2018; emergency number **112**; MSD is **140 bytes**; uses in-band modem over voice channel.
> - **AIS-140** mandates telematics for Indian public transport vehicles (GPS + emergency button + 4G connectivity to government backend).
> - **V2X modes**: V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle), V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure), V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian), V2N (Vehicle-to-Network), V2G (Vehicle-to-Grid).
> - **Communication techs**: 4G/5G cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth/BLE, DSRC (IEEE 802.11p), C-V2X (3GPP Rel 14+).
> - **In-vehicle bus**: CAN (Controller Area Network) is the dominant telematics-data source from ECUs.
> - **OTA updates** require cryptographic signature verification (RSA-2048 or ECDSA) in the TCU's HSM.
> - **Driver Behaviour Scoring** uses harsh-braking, rapid-acceleration, speeding, and cornering events.
> - **UBI** = Usage-Based Insurance; premium is dynamically linked to driver score.
> - **Fleet KPIs** monitored: fuel consumption, idling time, route deviation, mileage, geofence breaches.
> - **Security threats** in telematics: GPS spoofing, cellular man-in-the-middle, OTA tampering, CAN bus injection — mitigated via HSM, TLS/DTLS, and intrusion-detection systems.
> - **Key brands/products**: OnStar (GM), eCall (EU), Sync (Ford), Connected Car (BMW), BlueLink (Hyundai), iRA (Tata Motors).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Fuel Control, Engine Control Sequence, Open Loop and Closed Control, Cold Starting, Acceleration and Full Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Electronic Fuel Control (EFC)

**Electronic Fuel Control (EFC)** is the closed-regulation subsystem of a port-fuel-injected (PFI) or gasoline-direct-injected (GDI) spark-ignition engine in which an **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** calculates the precise mass of fuel to be injected per combustion cycle for every cylinder, taking into account the prevailing thermodynamic, ambient, and transient operating conditions of the engine.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, EFC is the firmware and hardware strategy that:

1. Acquires real-time inputs from sensors (MAP/MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT, O₂/λ, CKP/CMP, knock).
2. Computes a **base injection pulse width** $T_{b}$ from the sensed air mass per cylinder per cycle.
3. Applies multiplicative and additive **enrichment / enleanment correction factors** for temperature, cold start, acceleration, deceleration, full load, idle, and post-start warm-up.
4. Issues a high-side / low-side driver pulse to the electromagnetic solenoid of each injector whose **on-time (injector pulse width)** $T_{i}$ is the corrected injection duration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The examiner expects the student to articulate *why* the air-fuel ratio must be regulated to a stoichiometric value of **14.7 : 1** (mass basis) for a three-way catalytic converter to operate at its peak simultaneous efficiency for CO, HC, and NOₓ — this is the central pillar around which all EFC strategies revolve.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The ECU as a Chef"

Imagine a fine-dining chef (the **ECU**) who is preparing identical servings of soup every minute, but the kitchen conditions keep changing:

| Kitchen Variable | Engine Variable | Chef's Adjustment |
|---|---|---|
| Outside humidity (changes soup evaporation) | Ambient air density, IAT | Adjusts stock (fuel) added |
| Customer suddenly says "I'm starving, hurry!" | Driver floors accelerator (acceleration) | Adds extra seasoning right now |
| First customer at dawn — kitchen cold | Cold-start | Pre-heats and adds extra stock |
| Lunch-rush — many hungry diners | Full-load / WOT | Adds extra stock to sustain richness |
| Customer says "I'm full, stop" | Closed-loop λ correction | Tastes and trims until perfect |

The chef never adds a *fixed* amount; he **tastes (λ-sensor feedback), anticipates (feed-forward maps), and corrects (PID loop)** every serving. That is exactly what EFC does at thousands of times per second.

---

## 1.3 The Engine Control Sequence — High-Level View

The complete engine control sequence executed by the ECU in **every CPU cycle (typically every 5–10 ms for the main loop, and 1–2 ms for crank-synchronous events)** proceeds as follows:

1. **Sensory Acquisition:** Read crankshaft position (CKP), camshaft position (CMP), MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT, O₂/λ, battery voltage.
2. **Engine State Determination:** Cold/hot, cranking/running, idle/part-load/WOT, closed-loop/ open-loop.
3. **Air- mass Computation:** Compute the mass of air inducted per cylinder per cycle, $m_{air,c}$.
4. **Base Pulse Width Calculation:** Convert $m_{air,c}$ to a stoichiometric fuel mass, divide by injector static flow rate $Q_{stat}$, obtain **base injection time** $T_{b}$.
5. **Apply Corrections:** Multiply by **Cold-Start, Warm-Up, Acceleration (Accel), Full-Load, Battery-Voltage, Idle, and Decel Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)** correction factors.
6. **Closed-Loop Trim:** If in closed loop, blend a long-term (LTFT) and short-term (STFT) trim from the upstream O₂ sensor.
7. **Driver Stage Output:** Trigger the injector driver for the calculated $T_i$ microseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> The above loop is commonly called the **Engine Control Strategy** and forms the backbone of every modern gasoline ECU (Bosch Motronic, Denso, Continental, Delphi, Magneti Marelli). KTU examiners reward students who can name **sensor → computation → actuator** cleanly.

---

## 1.4 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop Control — The Foundational Dichotomy

**Open-Loop Control** is the *feed-forward* strategy where the ECU calculates injector pulse width **purely from pre-calibrated maps and sensor inputs**, without using any feedback from the exhaust oxygen sensor. It is used when:

- Engine is **cold** (O₂ sensor < ~300 °C, no reliable signal).
- Engine is **cranking** or within a few seconds of start.
- Wide-open-throttle / **full-load** (intentionally rich for cooling and knock margin).
- **Deceleration fuel cut-off** (DFCO, fuel fully cut).
- **O₂ sensor failure** (limp-home mode).

**Closed-Loop Control** is the *feedback-corrected* strategy where the upstream **wide-band or switching O₂ (lambda) sensor** continuously reports the actual exhaust air-fuel ratio. The ECU drives a **PI / PID controller** so that $\lambda$ is held at **1.000 ± 0.01** (stoichiometric).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The closed-loop band is **typically active between 1200 RPM and 2500 RPM, and between 15 % and 50 % engine load** in many production ECUs. Outside this window the ECU falls back to open-loop — this is a frequently asked 3-mark KTU question.

---

## 1.5 Cold Starting — What & Why

A cold engine requires **substantial fuel enrichment** because:

- **Poor fuel atomization and vaporization** — fuel droplets cling to cold port walls.
- **Lower volumetric efficiency** and colder intake air increase air density but fuel pool formation on intake walls is severe.
- **Crankcase oil is thick**, raising mechanical friction, dropping cranking RPM, and lengthening the in-cylinder air-pulse duration.
- **Catalyst light-off is incomplete**, so HC and CO emissions must be controlled by richer mixture.

The enrichment factor is denoted $f_{cold}$ or **CHOKE factor** and is a function of **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** reading $T_{cool}$.

---

## 1.6 Acceleration Enrichment — What & Why

When the driver depresses the accelerator pedal rapidly, the **in-rush of air** through the throttle body arrives at the intake port *faster* than the fuel puddle on the port wall can vaporize. The result is a momentary **lean excursion**, which would cause a stumble or hesitation. The ECU compensates by adding a transient **acceleration enrichment pulse** $T_{acc}$, computed from the rate of change of either:

- **TPS voltage** $\dfrac{dV_{TPS}}{dt}$, or
- **MAP pressure** $\dfrac{dP_{map}}{dt}$, or
- **MAF signal** $\dfrac{d\dot{m}_{air}}{dt}$.

---

## 1.7 Full-Load (Wide-Open-Throttle) Enrichment

At WOT, the engine demands **maximum torque**. The ECU intentionally targets a **lambda of approximately 0.78–0.85** (rich mixture) for the following reasons:

- **Knock suppression** — rich mixtures have lower peak in-cylinder temperatures and slower flame propagation, providing a wider knock margin.
- **Internal cooling** — excess fuel vaporizes and absorbs latent heat, reducing cylinder-head and exhaust-valve temperatures.
- **Maximum power** — the chemically correct (λ = 1.0) mixture is not always the *maximum-power* mixture; on most engines λ ≈ 0.85–0.90 yields slightly more torque.
- **Exhaust-valve seat lubrication** — rich mixture protects exhaust valves in engines without hardened valve seats.

The enrichment is implemented as a function of **MAP/Boost** and **engine speed**, lookup-table driven.

---

## 1.8 Core Symbols & Units Reference

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this block — it appears verbatim in KTU numericals.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\lambda$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (equivalence ratio inverse) | dimensionless |
| $m_{air,c}$ | Air mass per cylinder per cycle | kg |
| $T_b$ | Base injection pulse width | ms or µs |
| $T_i$ | Final injection pulse width | ms or µs |
| $Q_{stat}$ | Static injector flow rate | cm³/min or g/s |
| $f_c$ | Cold / warm-up enrichment factor | dimensionless |
| $f_{acc}$ | Acceleration enrichment factor | dimensionless |
| $f_{FL}$ | Full-load enrichment factor | dimensionless |
| $f_{BL}$ | Battery-voltage correction | dimensionless |
| $T_{dead}$ | Injector dead time (opening delay) | ms |
| $P_{man}$ | Manifold absolute pressure | kPa |
| $V_{TPS}$ | Throttle position sensor voltage | V |
| $T_{cool}$ | Coolant temperature | °C |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of EFC

Modern electronic fuel control is best understood as a **layered control architecture** — the ECU computes the injection duration in **five cascaded layers**:

### Layer 1 — Air-Charge Estimation (Mass Air Flow)
The ECU estimates the **mass of air inducted per cylinder per cycle**:

$$m_{air,c} \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\,\rho_{air}\,V_d}{N_{cyl}\,n_{rev}/2}$$

where $\eta_{vol}$ is volumetric efficiency, $\rho_{air}$ is the density of intake air, $V_d$ is the **total displacement volume**, $N_{cyl}$ is the number of cylinders, and $n_{rev}$ is the engine speed in revolutions per second.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Speed-Density** systems (MAP-based), $\rho_{air}$ is inferred from manifold pressure and IAT using the ideal gas law $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P_{man}}{R_{air}\,T_{man}}$. In **MAF-based** systems, the air mass flow rate is measured directly.

### Layer 2 — Base Stoichiometric Fuel Calculation
The target stoichiometric fuel mass per cylinder per cycle:

$$m_{fuel,stoich} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}}$$

with $AFR_{stoich} = \mathbf{14.7}$ for gasoline.

### Layer 3 — Base Pulse Width Conversion
The injector is a fixed-orifice solenoid. To deliver $m_{fuel,stoich}$ in one shot per cycle, the ECU holds the injector open for:

$$T_{b} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{fuel,stoich}}{Q_{stat}\,\rho_{fuel}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\,Q_{stat}\,\rho_{fuel}}$$

where $Q_{stat}$ is the manufacturer's rated static flow rate (cm³/min or g/s) and $\rho_{fuel}$ is fuel density.

### Layer 4 — Multiplicative & Additive Corrections
The corrected pulse width is built as:

$$T_i \;=\; T_{b}\,\cdot\,f_c\,\cdot\,f_{acc}\,\cdot\,f_{FL}\,\cdot\,f_{BL} \;+\; T_{acc,puddle} \;+\; T_{dead}(V_{bat})$$

Where each factor is bounded (typically 0.7 – 1.5 for warm corrections, 1.5 – 3.0 for cold enrichment).

### Layer 5 — Closed-Loop Trim (when active)
The final commanded pulse width is multiplied by a trim factor $f_{\lambda}$ driven by the upstream O₂ sensor through a PI controller:

$$f_{\lambda}(k) \;=\; f_{\lambda}(k-1) \;+\; K_p\,e(k) \;+\; K_i\,\sum_{j=0}^{k}e(j)$$

with $e(k) = \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{measured}(k)$.

---

## 2.2 Engine Control Sequence — Detailed Steps

| Step # | ECU Task | Sensors Used | Typical Timing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Determine crank position (TDC #1) | CKP, CMP | Every crank tooth (~6°) |
| 2 | Compute engine speed $n_{rev}$ | CKP | 5–10 ms |
| 3 | Measure intake air mass / MAP | MAF, MAP, IAT | 5–10 ms |
| 4 | Measure coolant & air temps | CTS, IAT | 50–200 ms |
| 5 | Measure throttle position | TPS | 5–10 ms |
| 6 | Determine engine state (cold/crank/idle/WOT/closed-loop) | All above | 10 ms |
| 7 | Look up base pulse width $T_b$ | MAP-RPM 2-D map | 5 ms |
| 8 | Apply all corrections | — | < 1 ms |
| 9 | Add injector dead time | Battery voltage | < 1 ms |
| 10 | Issue injector trigger | — | Sync to cam |
| 11 | Read O₂ sensor & update trim | LSU 4.9 / switching | 10–100 ms |

---

## 2.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback sensor used | None | Upstream λ sensor |
| AFR accuracy | ± 10 % | ± 1 % |
| Lambda target | λ = 0.78 to 1.05 (varies with mode) | λ = 1.000 ± 0.01 |
| Used when | Cold start, crank, WOT, DFCO, sensor fail | Steady cruise, idle, light load |
| Catalytic converter efficiency | Reduced | Maximum (3-way cat sweet spot) |
| Computation complexity | Low (table lookup) | Higher (PI/PID + integrator) |
| Response to disturbance | Slow, map dependent | Fast, real-time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students write that "closed loop is always better". This is **wrong**. Closed-loop λ = 1.0 control is *incompatible* with WOT (full-load) where λ must be ~0.85 to prevent knock. The ECU must **deactivate closed-loop** and revert to open-loop whenever MAP > threshold.

---

## 2.4 Cold-Starting Enrichment — Mathematical Model

The cold-start enrichment factor $f_c$ is empirically a strong function of $T_{cool}$:

$$f_{c}(T_{cool}) \;=\; 1 \;+\; K_c\,e^{-\alpha_c\,(T_{cool}-T_{ref})}$$

Common production values:

| $T_{cool}$ | $f_c$ (typical) |
|---|---|
| −20 °C | **2.5 – 3.0** |
| 0 °C | **1.8 – 2.0** |
| 20 °C | **1.3 – 1.4** |
| 40 °C | **1.1** |
| 80 °C | **1.0** (no enrichment) |
| 90 °C+ | **0.95 – 1.0** (slight enleanment for emissions) |

Cold-start enrichment is **gradually tapered** during the **after-start warm-up phase** using a time-based decay function so that by the time $T_{cool} \approx 70$ °C, $f_c \to 1$.

### Cold-Start Auxiliary Devices
- **Electric Fuel Pump** runs at maximum duty.
- **Cold-Start Injecttor** (in older CIS-E and early Motronic systems) — a single extra injector upstream of the intake manifold.
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** opens more to raise idle RPM (fast-idle) compensating for higher friction.
- **Ignition timing retarded** slightly during cold start to prevent stalling.

---

## 2.5 Acceleration Enrichment — Mathematical Model

The acceleration enrichment pulse width has two components:

### (a) Wall-Wet (Puddle) Compensation — Additive
This compensates for fuel deposited on the intake port walls:

$$T_{acc,puddle} \;=\; K_{pw}\,\cdot\,\dfrac{d\dot{m}_{air}}{dt} \;+\; K_{pw2}\,\cdot\,\dfrac{dP_{man}}{dt}$$

### (b) Synchronous Multiplicative Wall-Wetting
This compensates during the next full engine cycle:

$$f_{acc} \;=\; 1 \;+\; \tau_{v}\,\dfrac{d\theta_{throttle}}{dt}\,\cdot\,\dfrac{1}{n_{rev}}$$

where $\tau_{v}$ is the **wall-wetting time constant** (typically 0.3 – 1.5 s) and $\theta_{throttle}$ is the throttle angle in degrees.

The **total** acceleration correction is then:

$$T_{i,accel} \;=\; T_{b}\,\cdot\,f_{acc} \;+\; T_{acc,puddle}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In production ECUs (e.g., Bosch ME 9 / MED 17), a **two-state wall-wetting model** (Hiroyasu model) is used: one fraction $x$ of injected fuel is "wall-wet" with time constant $\tau_{1}$, the remaining $(1-x)$ is "wall-dry" with time constant $\tau_{2}$. This is the gold-standard model.

---

## 2.6 Full-Load Enrichment — Mathematical Model

Full-load enrichment is purely map-based (open-loop). A typical implementation:

$$f_{FL} \;=\; \begin{cases} 1.0 & \text{if } P_{man} < P_{FL,threshold} \\ 1 + K_{FL}\left(\dfrac{P_{man}-P_{FL,threshold}}{P_{atm}-P_{FL,threshold}}\right) & \text{if } P_{man} \ge P_{FL,threshold} \end{cases}$$

For naturally aspirated engines the rich-target is approximately:

| Operating Mode | Target λ | Approximate AFR |
|---|---|---|
| Idle / cruise / light load (closed loop) | 1.000 | 14.7 |
| Part load (open loop) | 1.00 – 1.03 | 14.7 – 15.1 |
| Wide-open-throttle | 0.80 – 0.88 | 11.8 – 12.9 |
| Cold start / warming | 0.65 – 0.90 | 9.5 – 13.2 |
| Overrun fuel cut | — (no fuel) | — |

For **turbocharged / GDI engines** the same λ targets hold, but the AFR is also a function of boost pressure, knock-sensor activity, and EGR fraction.

---

## 2.7 Battery Voltage Correction $f_{BL}$

The injector solenoid's mechanical opening time depends on the **pull-in voltage**. At low $V_{bat}$ the injector opens more slowly, so the actual fuel delivered is less than commanded. The ECU compensates by adding a **dead-time** $T_{dead}$ that increases as $V_{bat}$ drops:

$$f_{BL} \;=\; 1 \quad\text{(multiplicative, usually near unity)}$$

$$T_{dead}(V_{bat}) \;=\; T_{dead,nom}\left(\dfrac{V_{bat,nom}}{V_{bat}}\right)^{k_v}$$

with $k_v \approx 1.5$ to $2$ empirically.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Range / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equivalence ratio | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| 3 | $m_{air,c} = \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\rho_{air}V_d}{N_{cyl}\,(n_{rev}/2)}$ | Air mass per cyl per cycle | kg |
| 4 | $m_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\cdot\lambda}$ | Fuel mass per cyl per cycle | kg |
| 5 | $T_b = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{Q_{stat}\rho_{fuel}}$ | Base injection pulse width | ms |
| 6 | $T_i = T_b f_c f_{acc} f_{FL} f_{BL} + T_{dead}$ | Final pulse width | ms |
| 7 | $f_c = 1 + K_c e^{-\alpha_c(T_{cool}-T_{ref})}$ | Cold enrichment | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| 8 | $f_{FL} = 1 + K_{FL}\dfrac{P_{man}-P_{th}}{P_{atm}-P_{th}}$ | Full-load enrichment | 1.0 – 1.25 |
| 9 | $f_{\lambda}(k) = f_{\lambda}(k-1) + K_p e(k) + K_i \sum e(j)$ | PI closed-loop trim | ± 25 % |
| 10 | $T_{acc} = K_{pw}\dfrac{d\dot{m}_{air}}{dt} + K_{pw2}\dfrac{dP_{man}}{dt}$ | Acceleration enrichment | ms |
| 11 | $T_{dead}(V) = T_{dead,nom}\left(\dfrac{V_{bat,nom}}{V_{bat}}\right)^{k_v}$ | Injector opening delay | 0.5 – 4 ms |
| 12 | $\rho_{air} = \dfrac{P_{man}}{R_{air}T_{man}}$ | Air density (speed-density) | kg/m³ |
| 13 | $\eta_{vol} \approx c_1 + c_2 n_{rev} + c_3 P_{man}/P_{atm}$ | Volumetric efficiency model | 0.6 – 1.0 |
| 14 | $N_{inj,events/sec} = N_{cyl} \cdot \dfrac{n_{rev}}{2}$ | Injection event rate | events/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all table rows above, the absolute-value notation $\vert x \vert$ has been deliberately written as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser — never use bare pipes inside table cells.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Domain | Application of EFC Theory |
|---|---|
| Aftermarket tuning (e.g., COBB, EcuTek) | Re-mapping $T_b$, $f_c$, $f_{acc}$ for race fuel, E85 flex-fuel |
| Emissions compliance (Euro 6/BS-VI) | Maintaining λ = 1.000 ± 0.005 over WLTP drive cycles |
| Hybrid powertrains | EFC integrated with energy-management strategy |
| Heavy-duty diesel | Borrowed strategy adapted for pilot/main injections |
| Autonomous vehicles | EFC is one of several low-level actuators in the longitudinal controller |
