# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Base Injection Pulse Width $T_b$

We derive $T_b$ from first principles so the student sees every step.

### Starting Premise
The injector is modelled as a **constant-orifice, fixed-pressure differential solenoid valve**. During the commanded on-time $T_b$, the volumetric flow rate is approximately constant and equal to the **static flow rate** $Q_{stat}$ specified by the manufacturer at a reference pressure differential $\Delta P_{ref}$.

### Step 1 — Volume of Fuel to Inject per Cycle per Cylinder

We want to inject the **stoichiometric fuel mass** $m_{fuel,stoich}$ corresponding to one cylinder's air charge $m_{air,c}$.

$$V_{fuel,stoich} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{fuel,stoich}}{\rho_{fuel}}$$

Substituting the stoichiometric fuel mass:

$$V_{fuel,stoich} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\cdot\rho_{fuel}}$$

### Step 2 — Relate Volume to Injector On-Time

The volumetric flow rate during steady on-state is $Q_{stat}$. The volume delivered equals flow rate times on-time (neglecting dead-time for now):

$$V_{fuel,stoich} \;=\; Q_{stat}\,T_{b}$$

### Step 3 — Equate and Solve

$$\dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\,\rho_{fuel}} \;=\; Q_{stat}\,T_{b}$$

Solving for $T_b$:

$$\boxed{\,T_{b} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\,\rho_{fuel}\,Q_{stat}}\,}$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **14-mark KTU numerical style**. Practice writing every step.

**Given:**
- $m_{air,c} = 0.00045$ kg per cyl per cycle
- $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$
- $\rho_{fuel} = 750$ kg/m³
- $Q_{stat} = 250$ cm³/min $= 250 \times 10^{-6}/60$ m³/s $= 4.167 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s

**Find:** $T_b$ in **milliseconds**.

**Step (i):** Calculate required fuel volume per cycle:

$$V_{fuel} \;=\; \dfrac{0.00045}{14.7 \times 750} \;=\; \dfrac{4.5\times 10^{-4}}{11025} \;\approx\; 4.082\times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step (ii):** Convert to cm³ for cross-check:

$$V_{fuel} \;=\; 4.082\times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{3} \times 10^{6}\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{m}^{3} \;=\; 0.04082\ \text{cm}^{3}$$

**Step (iii):** Divide by injector static flow rate:

$$T_{b} \;=\; \dfrac{4.082\times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{3}}{4.167\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}} \;=\; 9.79\times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$$

**Step (iv):** Express in milliseconds:

$$\boxed{\,T_{b} \;\approx\; 9.79\ \text{ms}\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Cold-Start Enrichment Factor

### Assumption
Empirically, the additional fuel needed relative to a hot engine decays approximately **exponentially** with coolant temperature.

### Step 1 — Define Enrichment Ratio

At any coolant temperature $T_{cool}$, define:

$$f_{c}(T_{cool}) \;=\; \dfrac{m_{fuel,cold}(T_{cool})}{m_{fuel,hot}}$$

We require $f_c \to f_{max}$ as $T_{cool} \to -40$ °C and $f_c \to 1$ as $T_{cool} \to T_{hot}$ (≈ 80 °C).

### Step 2 — Propose Exponential Form

$$f_{c}(T_{cool}) \;=\; 1 \;+\; (f_{max}-1)\,e^{-\alpha_c\,(T_{cool}-T_{cold,ref})}$$

### Step 3 — Choose Constants

A common production calibration:

$$f_{c}(T_{cool}) \;=\; 1 \;+\; 1.5\,e^{-0.06\,(T_{cool}+20)}$$

This yields:

| $T_{cool}$ (°C) | $f_c$ |
|---|---|
| −20 | 2.50 |
| 0 | 1.88 |
| 20 | 1.50 |
| 40 | 1.21 |
| 60 | 1.05 |
| 80 | 1.00 |

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation at $T_{cool} = 10$ °C

$$f_{c}(10) \;=\; 1 + 1.5\,e^{-0.06\times 30} \;=\; 1 + 1.5\,e^{-1.8} \;=\; 1 + 1.5 \times 0.1653 \;=\; 1.248$$

So at 10 °C coolant, **25 % additional fuel** is commanded.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Acceleration Enrichment (Two-State Wall-Wetting Model)

The **Hiroyasu two-state model** treats the port wall as having two fuel fractions:

- $x$ — fraction that deposits on the wall (slow release).
- $(1-x)$ — fraction that remains airborne (instant delivery).

Let $\tau_1$ be the wall-fueling time constant and $\tau_2$ the wall-dewetting time constant. The mass of fuel in the wall puddle at time $k$:

$$m_{wall}(k) \;=\; m_{wall}(k-1)\,e^{-\Delta t/\tau_1} \;+\; x\,m_{inj}(k-1)$$

The **delivered fuel** to the cylinder is:

$$m_{cyl}(k) \;=\; (1-x)\,m_{inj}(k) \;+\; m_{wall}(k)\,e^{-\Delta t/\tau_2}$$

For steady-state ($k \to \infty$), $m_{cyl}^{ss} = m_{inj}^{ss}$ as expected (the wall puddle stabilizes).

For a **step change** in commanded fuel from $m_{inj}^{old}$ to $m_{inj}^{new}$ at $k=0$, the transient shortfall over the first few cycles is compensated by the **acceleration enrichment adder**:

$$T_{acc,puddle} \;=\; \tau_{v}\,\dfrac{x\,(m_{inj}^{new} - m_{inj}^{old})}{Q_{stat}\rho_{fuel}}$$

A simpler **first-order** approximation commonly seen in KTU exams:

$$T_{acc,puddle} \;=\; K_{pw}\,\dfrac{d\dot{m}_{air}}{dt}\quad\text{(in ms)}$$

with $K_{pw} \approx 0.5$ to $2.0$ ms per (g/s)/s — a tuning parameter.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Closed-Loop Lambda Control

### Step 1 — Plant Model

Let the controlled variable be $\lambda$. The ECU commands a trim factor $f_{\lambda}(k)$ such that effective injection time is $T_{eff} = f_{\lambda}\,T_i$. The exhaust gas composition (and hence the O₂ sensor voltage) responds with:

$$\lambda(k) \;=\; \lambda_0 \;+\; G_p\,f_{\lambda}(k-d) \;+\; \text{disturbance}$$

where $G_p$ is the plant gain (negative — increasing fuel decreases $\lambda$), $d$ is the transport delay (≈ 0.5–2 engine cycles due to exhaust transit time), and disturbance includes intake humidity, EGR, fuel-comp variation.

### Step 2 — PI Controller Design

Define error: $e(k) = \lambda_{target} - \lambda(k)$.

The trim command:

$$f_{\lambda}(k) \;=\; K_p\,e(k) \;+\; K_i\,\sum_{j=0}^{k}e(j)$$

The integral term eliminates steady-state offset; the proportional term provides response speed.

### Step 3 — Stability Criterion (Barkhausen, simplified)

For discrete PI control with sampling period $T_s$ and transport delay $d\,T_s$:

$$0 \;<\; K_p \,<\; \dfrac{1}{G_p\,\left(1 + K_i\,T_s\,d\right)}$$

Production ECUs further split trim into **STFT (Short-Term Fuel Trim)** that swings ± 25 % around the current map value, and **LTFT (Long-Term Fuel Trim)** that learns and updates the **base map** slowly to bring STFT back toward zero — compensating for **fuel-comp variation, injector aging, air-leaks, and MAF drift**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Full Cold-Start Calculation

**Given:**
- Engine at $T_{cool} = -10$ °C
- $n_{rev} = 1200$ RPM (idle after first second)
- $\eta_{vol} = 0.75$
- $\rho_{air} = 1.30$ kg/m³
- $V_d = 1.6$ L $= 1.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³
- $N_{cyl} = 4$
- $Q_{stat} = 250$ cm³/min
- $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$
- $f_c(-10°C) = 2.10$ (from the formula in 3.2)
- $f_{acc} = 1.05$ (mild throttle opening during idle settling)
- $f_{FL} = 1.0$ (not WOT)
- $f_{BL} = 1.0$, $T_{dead} = 1.2$ ms

**Find:** Final injection pulse width $T_i$.

**Step (i):** Air mass per cylinder per cycle:

$$m_{air,c} = \dfrac{0.75 \times 1.30 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times (1200/60)/2}$$

Compute numerator: $0.75 \times 1.30 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.56 \times 10^{-3}$.

Compute denominator: $4 \times 10 = 40$.

$$m_{air,c} = \dfrac{1.56 \times 10^{-3}}{40} = 3.9 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/cyl/cycle}$$

**Step (ii):** Base pulse width (fuel volume and on-time):

$$V_{fuel,stoich} = \dfrac{3.9 \times 10^{-5}}{14.7 \times 750} = 3.537 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

$$T_b = \dfrac{3.537 \times 10^{-9}}{4.167 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.488 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 0.849\ \text{ms}$$

**Step (iii):** Apply all corrections:

$$T_i = T_b f_c f_{acc} f_{FL} f_{BL} + T_{dead}$$

$$T_i = 0.849 \times 2.10 \times 1.05 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 + 1.2$$

$$T_i = 1.872 + 1.2 = 3.07\ \text{ms}$$

**Result:** Under cold idle at −10 °C, the injector must fire for **≈ 3.07 ms per cylinder per cycle** — about **3.6× the hot-engine value** (0.849 ms).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Engine Control Mode

| ECU Diagnostic | CTS < 40 °C ? | MAP > $P_{FL,th}$ ? | TPS rate > $K_{acc}$ ? | O₂ sensor warm & valid ? | Engine Mode Selected |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Yes | Yes | No | No | No | **Cold-Start (Open Loop)** |
| Yes | Yes | No | Yes | No | **Cold-Start + Accel Enrichment** |
| No | No | No | No | Yes | **Closed Loop (λ = 1.0)** |
| No | No | Yes | — | — | **Full-Load Open Loop (λ ≈ 0.85)** |
| No | No | No | Negative spike | — | **Decel Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)** |
| O₂ fail | — | — | — | No | **Limp-Home Open Loop** |
| Cranking | Yes (cold) | — | — | No | **Crank-Pulse Map** |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Engine Control Sequence — Master Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On ECU] --> B[Read All Sensors]
    B --> C{Crank Engaged?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Crank Mode: Open Loop]
    C -- No --> E{Engine Running?}
    E -- No --> A
    E -- Yes --> F{Coolant Temp Low?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Cold Start Open Loop with Cold Enrichment]
    F -- No --> H{Throttle Wide Open?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Full Load Open Loop Rich lambda approx 0.85]
    H -- No --> J{O2 Sensor Ready?}
    J -- No --> K[Part Load Open Loop lambda near 1]
    J -- Yes --> L[Closed Loop PI Control lambda equals 1]
    G --> M[Compute Base Pulse Width Tb]
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Apply Cold Accel FullLoad Battery Corrections]
    N --> O[Add Injector Dead Time]
    O --> P[Trigger Injector Driver]
    P --> Q[Update O2 Trim Learning]
    Q --> A
```

## 4.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Side-by-Side Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OL[Open Loop Path]
        OL1[MAP MAF TPS CTS IAT] --> OL2[Map Lookup in ECU]
        OL2 --> OL3[Base Pulse Width Tb]
        OL3 --> OL4[Correction Factors]
        OL4 --> OL5[Injector Driver]
    end
    subgraph CL[Closed Loop Path]
        CL1[O2 Lambda Sensor] --> CL2[Lambda Measurement]
        CL2 --> CL3[Error Calculation]
        CL3 --> CL4[PI Controller]
        CL4 --> CL5[Trim Factor Multiplier]
        CL5 --> OL5
    end
    OL5 --> Engine[(Engine Cylinder)]
    Engine --> Exhaust[Exhaust Gas]
    Exhaust --> CL1
```

```mermaid
gantt
```

## 4.4 Acceleration Enrichment — Two-State Wall-Wetting Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InjCmd[Injector Pulse Width T_i] --> Split{Split into x and 1-x}
    Split -- fraction x --> Wall[(Wall Puddle Mass)]
    Split -- fraction 1-x --> Cyl[Direct to Cylinder]
    Wall -->|release with tau_2| Cyl
    AccelSignal[d dot m_air over dt] --> Adder[Plus Adder]
    InjCmd --> Adder
    Adder --> Compensated[T_i Compensated Output]
```

## 4.5 Full-Load Lambda Targeting — Decision Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Read MAP Pressure] --> Comp{Compare to P_th}
    Comp -- Below --> CL[Lambda Target equals 1.000 Closed Loop]
    Comp -- Above --> FL[Full Load Open Loop]
    FL --> Knock[Monitor Knock Sensor]
    Knock -- Knock Detected --> Retard[Retard Ignition plus further enrich]
    Knock -- No Knock --> Hold[Hold Lambda equals 0.85]
    Retard --> Hold
    Hold --> Out[Commanded Injector Pulse]
    CL --> Out
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    Sensor[O2 Sensor Reading] --> Sum
    Sum -->|e of k| PI[PI Controller Kp plus Ki integrator]

    Trim --> Plant[Engine Combustion Plant]
    Plant --> Exhaust[Exhaust Gas]
    Exhaust --> Sensor
```

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] 
**"Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control in an Electronic Fuel Injection system. Mention two engine operating conditions under which open-loop control is mandatory."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open Loop | Closed Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | None — pre-calibrated maps | Upstream O₂ / wide-band λ sensor |
| AFR accuracy | ± 10 % | ± 1 % |
| Lambda target | Mode-dependent (0.78 – 1.05) | 1.000 ± 0.01 |

**Two conditions where open-loop is mandatory (any two of the following, ½ mark each):**

1. **Cold-start / engine cranking** — O₂ sensor below 300 °C, no reliable signal.
2. **Wide-Open-Throttle / Full-Load** — target λ ≈ 0.85 for knock suppression, deliberate rich mixture.
3. **Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off (DFCO)** — fuel fully cut, no combustion to regulate.
4. **O₂ sensor failure (limp-home mode)** — diagnostic trouble code P0130/P0131 forces open-loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often answer *only* "closed-loop is better than open-loop" without naming the **conditions** where open-loop is mandatory. KTU awards ½ mark for each condition explicitly stated — **2 marks vanish** if you merely say "open loop is used when feedback isn't available".

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Explain why cold-start enrichment is necessary in an EFI engine. Write a typical relationship between enrichment factor and coolant temperature."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Why enrichment is necessary (1.5 marks):**
- Poor fuel atomization and vaporization at low temperature — fuel condenses on cold intake-port walls.
- Higher engine friction (thick oil) → lower cranking RPM → reduced air-pulse duration → incomplete fuel transport.
- Catalyst not yet at light-off temperature → HC and CO emissions must be limited by richer mixture.
- Battery voltage drop at low temperature → slower injector opening → requires compensation.

**Typical relationship (1 mark):**
An exponentially decaying function, e.g.:

$$f_{c}(T_{cool}) = 1 + K_{c}\,e^{-\alpha_{c}(T_{cool}-T_{ref})}$$

with constants chosen such that $f_c \approx 2.5$ at −20 °C and $f_c \to 1$ above 80 °C.

**Sketch required:** A decaying curve from left (cold, high) to right (hot, unity) — examiners reward axis labels.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**  *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]*

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **complete engine control sequence** executed by the ECU in a modern port-fuel-injected gasoline engine. State the role of each major sensor and actuator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive, from first principles, an expression for the **base injection pulse width** $T_b$ of an electronically controlled injector in terms of the air mass per cylinder per cycle $m_{air,c}$, stoichiometric air-fuel ratio $AFR_{stoich}$, fuel density $\rho_{fuel}$, and injector static flow rate $Q_{stat}$. **A 4-cylinder, 1.6 L gasoline engine idles at 1200 RPM with 75 % volumetric efficiency, intake density 1.30 kg/m³, $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$, $\rho_{fuel} = 750$ kg/m³ and $Q_{stat} = 250$ cm³/min. Compute the corresponding hot-idle base pulse width in milliseconds.** **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks) — describe verbally here:**

| Block | Function | Key Sensors / Actuators |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Sensor Acquisition | Read engine state | CKP, CMP, MAP, MAF, TPS, CTS, IAT, O₂, battery voltage |
| 2. Air-Charge Estimation | Compute $m_{air,c}$ | Speed-density or MAF model |
| 3. Base Map Lookup | Determine $T_b$ | 2-D MAP × RPM table in ECU ROM |
| 4. Correction Layer | Adjust for transients | $f_c$, $f_{acc}$, $f_{FL}$, $f_{BL}$, $T_{dead}$ |
| 5. Closed-Loop Trim (when enabled) | PI control on λ | O₂ sensor, STFT/LTFT |
| 6. Driver Stage | Convert to solenoid current | Injector, ignition coil, IACV, fuel pump relay |

**Sequential narrative (2 marks):** The ECU begins every cycle by reading CKP to determine crank angle and engine speed. It then samples MAP/MAF to compute the air charge. Using a 2-D lookup table indexed by MAP and RPM, it retrieves the base pulse width $T_b$. The ECU then evaluates engine-state flags (cold, WOT, deceleration, closed-loop eligibility). All multiplicative and additive corrections are applied. The final value is compared against the closed-loop trim, blended, and loaded into the injector driver's compare register. On receiving the **synchronous trigger** from the CMP sensor at the correct cam angle, the driver energises the injector coil for exactly $T_i$ microseconds.

**Modes of operation (2 marks):** Mention briefly the five engine states — cranking, cold-start, warm-up, closed-loop cruise, full-load open-loop — and the conditions that transition between them.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

Start from volume of fuel required per cycle:

$$V_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\cdot\rho_{fuel}}$$

Injector's static flow rate $Q_{stat}$ delivers volume per second; hence on-time:

$$\boxed{\,T_{b} = \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\,\rho_{fuel}\,Q_{stat}}\,}$$

**Numerical evaluation (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Air mass per cylinder per cycle:

$$m_{air,c} = \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\cdot\rho_{air}\cdot V_d}{N_{cyl}\cdot (n_{rev}/2)} = \dfrac{0.75 \times 1.30 \times 1.6\times 10^{-3}}{4 \times (1200/60)/2}$$

$$= \dfrac{1.56\times 10^{-3}}{4\times 10} = 3.9\times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/cyl/cycle}$$

Step 2 — Required fuel volume:

$$V_{fuel} = \dfrac{3.9\times 10^{-5}}{14.7 \times 750} = 3.537\times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}^{3}$$

Step 3 — Convert $Q_{stat}$ to m³/s:

$$Q_{stat} = 250\ \text{cm}^{3}/\text{min} = \dfrac{250\times 10^{-6}}{60} = 4.167\times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

Step 4 — Compute $T_b$:

$$T_{b} = \dfrac{3.537\times 10^{-9}}{4.167\times 10^{-6}} = 8.488\times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = \mathbf{0.849\ \text{ms}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 1-mark Loss:** Forgetting the factor of **2 in the denominator** of $n_{rev}/2$ for a 4-stroke engine (one power stroke per **two** crank revolutions). KTU repeatedly penalises this.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**  *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]*

**(a)** Explain with appropriate mathematical expressions the **acceleration enrichment** strategy in a port-fuel-injected engine. Discuss the two-state wall-wetting model and its effect on transient air-fuel metering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A driver floors the accelerator from idle to WOT in 0.4 s. Discuss **cold-start enrichment and full-load enrichment** simultaneously — i.e. calculate the **total correction factor** when the engine is at $T_{cool} = 0$ °C, $P_{man} = 90$ kPa, $P_{atm} = 100$ kPa, full-load threshold $P_{FL,th} = 75$ kPa, $K_{FL} = 0.30$, and cold-start formula $f_c = 1 + 1.5\,e^{-0.06(T_{cool}+20)}$. Also state **why closed-loop control is disabled** during this transient. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)** (7 marks)

**Conceptual background (2 marks):**
Acceleration enrichment compensates for the **transient fuel-deposition deficit** on the cold intake port walls when the throttle is opened rapidly. Without enrichment, an additional air pulse enters the cylinder but the equivalent fuel mass has not yet evaporated off the wall → momentary lean excursion → torque dip and driver-perceived "hesitation".

**Additive component — wall-puddle compensation (2 marks):**

$$T_{acc,puddle} = K_{pw}\,\dfrac{d\dot{m}_{air}}{dt} + K_{pw2}\,\dfrac{dP_{man}}{dt}$$

with $K_{pw} \approx 0.5$ – $2$ ms per (g/s) per second.

**Two-state wall-wetting model (3 marks):**
The fraction $x$ of injected fuel adheres to the wall and is released with time constant $\tau_1$ (adsorption). The fraction $(1-x)$ stays airborne and reaches the cylinder directly. The wall puddle decays (releases back to the airstream) with time constant $\tau_2$. This is the **Hiroyasu model**:

$$m_{cyl}(k) = (1-x)\,m_{inj}(k) + m_{wall}(k)\,e^{-\Delta t/\tau_2}$$

$$m_{wall}(k) = m_{wall}(k-1)\,e^{-\Delta t/\tau_1} + x\,m_{inj}(k-1)$$

At steady state, $m_{cyl}^{ss} = m_{inj}^{ss}$. During a step increase in $\dot{m}_{inj}$, the cylinder initially sees a **shortfall** that the acceleration adder must make up.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)** (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Cold enrichment at 0 °C (2 marks):**

$$f_c = 1 + 1.5\,e^{-0.06(0+20)} = 1 + 1.5\,e^{-1.2} = 1 + 1.5 \times 0.3012 = 1.452$$

**Step 2 — Full-load enrichment (2 marks):**
Since $P_{man} = 90$ kPa > $P_{FL,th} = 75$ kPa, the ECU enters WOT enrichment:

$$f_{FL} = 1 + K_{FL}\,\dfrac{P_{man} - P_{FL,th}}{P_{atm} - P_{FL,th}} = 1 + 0.30 \times \dfrac{90-75}{100-75} = 1 + 0.30 \times 0.6 = 1.18$$

**Step 3 — Total correction factor (1 mark):**

$$f_{total} = f_c \times f_{FL} = 1.452 \times 1.18 = 1.713$$

**Step 4 — Why closed-loop is disabled (2 marks):**
Closed-loop control forces λ = 1.000 ± 0.01 using the O₂ sensor, but at WOT the desired λ is **deliberately ~0.85** for:
- Knock suppression (rich mixture lowers combustion temperature).
- Maximum torque and internal cylinder cooling.
- Exhaust-valve protection.

Allowing the O₂-sensor feedback to drive λ back to 1.0 would defeat the very purpose of full-load enrichment, so the ECU **switches to open-loop** by exiting the closed-loop window whenever MAP > $P_{FL,th}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget that **acceleration enrichment is also active** during a wide-open-throttle transition. In Question B's specific scenario the *additive* acceleration term $T_{acc,puddle}$ should also be mentioned (½ mark). If the examiner explicitly asked for "total correction factor" they usually mean **multiplicative product of corrections + mention of additive term**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — print this before every KTU exam!**

- [x] **EFC architecture**: Sensor → Air-mass estimation → Base map lookup → Corrections → Driver → Actuator.
- [x] **Base pulse width** $T_b = \dfrac{m_{air,c}}{AFR_{stoich}\,\rho_{fuel}\,Q_{stat}}$ — derived, know every symbol.
- [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7**; for diesel ≈ 14.5; for methanol ≈ 6.4; for hydrogen ≈ 34.3; for LPG ≈ 15.5.
- [x] **λ = 1** → stoichiometric; **λ < 1** → rich; **λ > 1** → lean.
- [x] **Open-loop** is mandatory during: **cold-start, cranking, WOT, DFCO, O₂ failure**.
- [x] **Closed-loop** band: typically **15 %–50 % load, 1200–2500 RPM** — exact numbers vary by calibration.
- [x] **Cold enrichment** is **exponentially decaying** with coolant temperature; values 1.0 → 2.5 typical.
- [x] **Acceleration enrichment** has both **multiplicative** (next-cycle wall release) and **additive** (immediate puddle compensation) components.
- [x] **Full-load λ target ≈ 0.85**, not 1.0; this is to suppress knock and protect exhaust valves.
- [x] **Battery voltage correction** adds **dead-time** $T_{dead}(V_{bat}) = T_{dead,nom}\left(\dfrac{V_{nom}}{V_{bat}}\right)^{k_v}$ because injector opening is slower at low voltage.
- [x] **Two-state wall-wetting model**: fractions $x$ and $(1-x)$, time constants $\tau_1$ (adsorption) and $\tau_2$ (desorption).
- [x] **PI controller** for closed-loop λ — $K_p$ provides speed, $K_i$ eliminates steady-state offset.
- [x] **STFT** = short-term fuel trim (± 25 %, fast); **LTFT** = long-term fuel trim (learns base map correction).
- [x] **DFCO** (Deceleration Fuel Cut-Off) — fuel fully cut when TPS closed AND RPM > threshold to save fuel.
- [x] **Knock sensor** allows ECU to **retard ignition AND enrich mixture** in WOT regions.
- [x] **GDI engines** use stratified charge at light load (very lean, λ = 2 to 4) — *not* asked at KTU level but worth mentioning as future-readiness.
- [x] Always include the **factor of 2** in 4-stroke air-charge formula: $m_{air,c} = \dfrac{\eta_{vol}\rho_{air}V_d}{N_{cyl}(n_{rev}/2)}$.
- [x] **Units audit**: $Q_{stat}$ in cm³/min → convert to m³/s; $T_b$ in seconds → convert to ms for final answer.
- [x] Know the **five operating modes** — crank, cold-start, warm-up, closed-loop cruise, full-load open-loop — and the trigger conditions for each.

---

### Examiner's Closing Note

> [!NOTE]
> **For 100 % KTU score on this topic:**
> 1. Draw **one block diagram** for engine control sequence.
> 2. Write the **derivation** of $T_b$ — examiners *love* derivations from first principles.
> 3. Show the **5-layer correction architecture** ($T_b \rightarrow f_c \rightarrow f_{acc} \rightarrow f_{FL} \rightarrow f_{BL} \rightarrow T_{dead}$).
> 4. State **two cases** where closed-loop is intentionally disabled and **why**.
> 5. Provide one **work-out numerical** — partial-credit recovery happens only when every substitution is shown.

**Best of luck for your KTU Module-3 examination!** 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enrichment, Deceleration Leaning, Overrun Fuel Cut Off, Idle Speed Control and EGR Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Enrichment, Deceleration Leaning, Overrun Fuel Cut Off, Idle Speed Control and EGR Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Enrichment (Start-Up, Acceleration & Power Enrichment)

### Formal Definition
**Fuel Enrichment** in a multi-point or single-point Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI) system is the deliberate, ECU-commanded increase in the injector pulse width (or pulse duration) beyond the stoichiometric base value so that the delivered air–fuel ratio (AFR) becomes **richer than the chemically ideal 14.7:1** (λ < 1). It is invoked under three distinct operating regimes: **Cold-Start Enrichment (CSE)**, **Acceleration Enrichment (ASE)** and **Power / Full-Load Enrichment (PSE)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** Enrichment is *not* a malfunction — it is a programmed strategy. The ECU adds fuel in **mg/stroke** or **ms injection time** referenced from a 3-D or 4-D fuel map indexed by RPM and load (MAP/MAF).

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of pouring water from a tap onto a dry sponge versus a wet sponge. A dry, cold engine "drinks up" fuel — it condenses on cold intake walls, has poor atomisation and low evaporation. So the ECU literally **pours extra fuel** (richer mixture) until the engine warms up. During sudden acceleration (tip-in), fuel droplets lag behind the air rush, so the ECU pre-pumps extra fuel. During wide-open throttle (WOT), the engine demands power and tolerates a slightly rich mixture (λ ≈ 0.78–0.85) to keep exhaust temperatures safe and combustion stable.

| Enrichment Type | Trigger Condition | Typical Lambda (λ) |
|---|---|---|
| Cold-Start Enrichment (CSE) | Coolant temp < **40 °C** | 0.6 – 0.8 |
| Acceleration Enrichment (ASE) | ΔMAP/dt > threshold (rapid throttle opening) | 0.85 – 0.95 |
| Power / WOT Enrichment (PSE) | Throttle > 85 % AND high load | 0.78 – 0.85 |

---

## 1.2 Deceleration Leaning

### Formal Definition
**Deceleration Leaning** is the fuel-map strategy in which the ECU progressively *reduces* the injector pulse width (leaning the mixture toward λ → 1.0 or even slightly lean) when the driver lifts off the throttle but the engine speed is still moderately high — typically between **1200 rpm and 1800 rpm** — and the manifold pressure (MAP) drops below atmospheric (vacuum rises).

> [!NOTE]
> At very high RPM during deceleration, leaning is replaced entirely by **Overrun Fuel Cut-Off (FCO)**. Thus *leaning* is the *transition zone* and *cut-off* is the *terminal zone*.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine coasting downhill on a bicycle — you stop pedalling, but the wheels keep spinning due to inertia. Similarly, when you lift your foot off the accelerator, the car is still moving and the engine is being driven by the wheels. Very little fuel is needed — the ECU gives just enough to keep combustion smooth, but no excess.

---

## 1.3 Overrun Fuel Cut-Off (FCO)

### Formal Definition
**Overrun Fuel Cut-Off** is an emission-control and fuel-economy feature in which the ECU **completely inhibits the injector pulses** (pulse width = 0 ms) when the following four conditions are simultaneously satisfied:
1. Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) reads **0 %** (closed throttle).
2. Engine RPM is **above a calibrated threshold** (typically **1800–2500 rpm**, coolant-dependent).
3. Coolant temperature is **above ~ 60 °C** (warm engine — to avoid stalling a cold engine).
4. Vehicle speed > **0 km/h** (gear engaged / moving).

### Intuitive Analogy
It is the automotive equivalent of **shutting off a water tap when the bucket is already full**. The engine is being driven by the wheels — it does not need any fuel at all. Cutting fuel saves petrol and reduces HC/CO emissions dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When RPM drops below the *re-enable threshold* (≈ **1100–1300 rpm**), the ECU smoothly restores injection with a small extra pulse (clear-flood recovery) so the engine does not stumble.

---

## 1.4 Idle Speed Control (ISC)

### Formal Definition
**Idle Speed Control** is the closed-loop mechanism that maintains a target idle RPM (typically **650–900 rpm** for a warm passenger car, **700–800 rpm** for motorcycles) against disturbances such as accessory loads (AC compressor, alternator, power steering), gear engagement, coolant temperature transients, and aging. The two dominant hardware implementations are:
- **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** — a duty-cycle-controlled solenoid that bypasses air around the closed throttle plate (Ford, Honda).
- **Stepper Motor ISC** — a bi-directional permanent-magnet stepper motor that mechanically repositions an air-bypass screw (Bosch, Toyota, older EFI).

Modern Drive-by-Wire (DBW) vehicles achieve idle control **electronically** by commanding the throttle plate angle directly — no bypass valve is needed.

### Intuitive Analogy
Idle speed control is like a **thermostat in a room**. If the room gets cooler (a load is added, RPM dips), the system turns on the heater (opens more air bypass / advances throttle angle) to bring the temperature back to the setpoint. If the room gets warmer (load removed), it cools down (closes the bypass).

---

## 1.5 EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation) Control

### Formal Definition
**EGR** is an emissions-control strategy that recirculates a controlled portion of inert exhaust gas back into the intake manifold, lowering peak combustion temperatures and thus suppressing the formation of **NOx (oxides of nitrogen)**. The ECU modulates an **EGR valve** (vacuum-operated, solenoid-vacuum, or fully electronic stepper) to achieve a target **EGR rate** (typically **5 %–15 %** at part-throttle cruise, **0 %** at idle, idle stability, cold start and WOT).

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine blowing on a candle flame versus a campfire. The campfire is much harder to blow out because it is bigger and hotter — high combustion temperatures produce more NOx. EGR is like **diluting the campfire with steam** — it lowers the temperature, so less NOx is born, while the candle (small flame) doesn't need any dilution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** EGR is **disabled** at idle (would cause stalling), at cold start (emissions would spike), at WOT (would kill power), and during warm-up. It is **active only at part-throttle cruise/light load**.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall)
- Stoichiometric AFR for petrol/gasoline: **14.7 : 1**
- Stoichiometric AFR for diesel: **14.5 : 1**
- Lambda (λ) at stoichiometry: **1.00**
- Typical cold-start λ: **0.6 – 0.8**
- Typical idle λ: **1.00 ± 0.02**
- Typical WOT λ (power enrichment): **0.78 – 0.85**
- EGR activation band: **part-throttle cruise**
- NOx formation threshold temperature: **> 1370 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fuel Injection Pulse Width — The Master Equation

The fundamental equation the ECU computes every **10 ms** (100 Hz) on a typical engine is:

$$
T_{inj} \;=\; T_{base} \cdot F_{enrich} \cdot F_{warm} \cdot F_{accel} \cdot F_{batt} + T_{dead}
$$

where each factor is a multiplier > 0 applied to the **base pulse width** derived from a 3-D fuel map (RPM × Load).

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{base}$ | Base pulse width from 3-D fuel map | 1 – 6 ms |
| $F_{enrich}$ | Start-up enrichment multiplier | 1.0 – 3.0 |
| $F_{warm}$ | Warm-up enrichment multiplier | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| $F_{accel}$ | Acceleration enrichment multiplier | 1.0 – 1.4 |
| $F_{batt}$ | Battery-voltage correction (longer pulse at low V) | 0.8 – 1.3 |
| $T_{dead}$ | Injector dead time / opening latency | 0.5 – 1.5 ms |

### 2.1.1 Why Each Multiplier Exists — The "Why & How"

- **$T_{base}$** — The "skeleton". Looked up from a fuel map indexed by RPM and either MAP (speed-density) or mass air flow (MAF). It delivers *stoichiometric* fuel for a *warm* engine at *steady-state*.
- **$F_{enrich}$ (CSE)** — During cranking, fuel films condense on cold port walls. Extra fuel replaces these losses. Implemented as a function of **coolant temperature (CTS)** and **cranking RPM**.
- **$F_{warm}$** — Even after start-up, a cold engine still has poor atomisation. This multiplier tapers off as the engine warms.
- **$F_{accel}$ (ASE / Wall-Wetting Compensation)** — When throttle snaps open, the sudden manifold pressure rise causes fuel droplets to deposit on the walls ("wall wetting"). The ECU anticipates this and adds fuel based on **ΔMAP/Δt** or **d(MAF)/dt**.
- **$F_{batt}$** — Solenoid injectors open slower at low battery voltage, so the ECU extends the pulse so that the *effective open time* matches the commanded fuel quantity.
- **$T_{dead}$** — The mechanical latency of the solenoid between energisation and full needle lift.

---

## 2.2 Lambda (λ) and Air-Fuel Ratio Fundamentals

$$
\lambda \;=\; \frac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}
$$

$$
AFR_{actual} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}}
$$

| Mixture | λ | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Rich (Power) | 0.80 – 0.90 | WOT, cold start |
| Slightly rich | 0.95 – 1.00 | Cruise in power mode |
| Stoichiometric | 1.00 | Closed-loop cruise (catalyst window) |
| Slightly lean | 1.00 – 1.05 | Deceleration leaning |
| Lean burn | 1.05 – 1.20 | Some GDI stratified-charge engines |

---

## 2.3 Overrun Fuel Cut-Off — Decision Logic

The ECU executes the following Boolean decision every injection cycle:

$$
FCO_{active} \;=\; TPS_{<5\%} \;\wedge\; RPM_{>1800} \;\wedge\; CTS_{>60°C} \;\wedge\; V_{veh>0}
$$

When $FCO_{active} = \text{TRUE}$, **injector pulse width is set to zero**.

The *re-enable* logic uses a hysteresis band to prevent oscillation:

$$
FCO_{off} \;=\; RPM_{<1200} \;\vee\; CTS_{<55°C} \;\vee\; TPS_{>8\%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hysteresis in FCO:** The cut-in RPM (≈ 1800) and cut-out RPM (≈ 1200) differ by ~ 600 rpm to ensure smooth transitions. This is a classic *Schmitt-trigger* style debounce.

---

## 2.4 Idle Speed Control — Closed-Loop Mathematics

The ISC operates as a PI (Proportional-Integral) controller:

$$
u(t) \;=\; K_P \cdot e(t) \;+\; K_I \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

where the error is:

$$
e(t) \;=\; RPM_{target} - RPM_{actual}
$$

| Variable | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $RPM_{target}$ | ECU map-based target (depends on CTS, AC load, gear) | 700 – 850 rpm |
| $K_P$ | Proportional gain | 0.5 – 2.0 % duty / rpm |
| $K_I$ | Integral gain | 0.05 – 0.20 % duty / (rpm·s) |
| $u(t)$ | ISC valve duty cycle or stepper steps | 0 – 100 % or 0 – 125 steps |

The output $u(t)$ drives either:
- a **duty-cycle solenoid** (PWM at ~ 200 Hz), or
- a **stepper motor** (each step ≈ **0.025 mm** air-bypass movement, typically 0–125 steps total range).

### Real-World Utility
The ISC keeps the engine from stalling when:
- Headlights are turned on (alternator load ≈ +30 Nm).
- AC compressor kicks in (+10–15 Nm).
- Gear is shifted from N → D (automatic transmission torque reaction).
- Coolant fan engages.
- Power-steering pump load increases at parking speeds.

---

## 2.5 EGR — Mass-Flow and Temperature Relationship

The EGR rate (mass-based) is defined as:

$$
EGR_{\%} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{EGR}}{\dot{m}_{air}+\dot{m}_{EGR}} \times 100
$$

NOx formation follows the **Zeldovich mechanism**, which is exponentially sensitive to peak flame temperature:

$$
\left[NO_x\right] \;\propto\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{R\,T_{flame}}\right)
$$

where $E_a \approx 251 \; \text{kJ/mol}$, $R = 8.314\;\text{J/(mol·K)}$.

When EGR dilutes the charge, the specific heat capacity of the in-cylinder mixture rises (more triatomic CO₂ and H₂O), lowering $T_{flame}$ and dramatically suppressing NOx.

| EGR Rate | Approx. NOx Reduction | Penalty |
|---|---|---|
| 5 % | 25 % | Minimal |
| 10 % | 50 % | Slight BSFC rise |
| 15 % | 65 % | Noticeable roughness |
| 20 % | 75 % | Misfire risk |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = AFR_{act} / AFR_{stoich}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $AFR = \dot{m}_{air}/\dot{m}_{fuel}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio | kg/kg |
| 3 | $T_{inj} = T_{base}\cdot F_E\cdot F_W\cdot F_A\cdot F_V + T_d$ | Injection pulse width | ms |
| 4 | $FCO = TPS_{<5\%} \wedge RPM_{>1800} \wedge CTS_{>60°C}$ | Cut-off condition | Boolean |
| 5 | $e(t) = RPM_{target}-RPM_{actual}$ | ISC control error | rpm |
| 6 | $u(t)=K_P e + K_I\!\int e\,dt$ | ISC PI control law | % duty / steps |
| 7 | $EGR_{\%} = \dot{m}_{EGR}/(\dot{m}_{air}+\dot{m}_{EGR})$ | EGR mass fraction | % |
| 8 | $[NO_x]\propto \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | NOx formation rate | ppm |
| 9 | $ASE \propto \frac{d\,MAP}{dt}$ | Acceleration enrichment trigger | kPa/s |
| 10 | $CSE = f(CTS)$ | Cold-start enrichment multiplier | 1.0 – 3.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU 2024 exams:** Memorise formulas #1, #2, #3, #6, #7 — they appear every year. Formula #8 is often asked as a "write the expression and explain the physics" 7-mark question.

---

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Where Are These Used?

| Strategy | Production Vehicle Implementation | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Cold-Start Enrichment | Bosch Motronic, Denso, Continental EMS | Drivability and emissions during warm-up (HC spike without CSE) |
| Overrun FCO | All modern EFI cars (Maruti, Hyundai, Tata, Toyota) | 3–8 % fuel saving in urban driving |
| Idle Speed Control | DBW throttle-by-wire (Bosch E-gas), stepper ISC | Prevents stall, controls idle emissions |
| EGR | Diesel (heavy duty — cooled HP-EGR), petrol (LP-EGR) | NOx reduction 40–70 % — meets BS-VI / Euro 6 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Injection Pulse-Width Decomposition

### Goal
Show how a measured injector pulse width $T_{inj}$ (in ms) translates into a *delivered* fuel mass $m_f$ and how each enrichment factor mathematically layers onto the base.

### Step 1 — Injector Fuel Flow Equation

A saturated injector delivers fuel at a fixed **static flow rate** $q$ (e.g., $q = 200\;\text{cm}^3/\text{min}$ at **3.0 bar** reference pressure). The delivered fuel mass per injection event is:

$$
m_f \;=\; \rho_{fuel} \cdot q \cdot \frac{T_{open}}{60{,}000}
$$

**Logic:** $\rho_{fuel}$ converts volume to mass; the factor $1/60{,}000$ converts ms → minutes.

### Step 2 — Effective Open Time

The injector is energised for $T_{inj}$ but only *fully open* for $T_{inj} - T_{dead}$:

$$
T_{open} \;=\; T_{inj} - T_{dead}
$$

**Logic:** The solenoid takes time to overcome spring force and lift the needle — this is the dead time.

### Step 3 — Substituting the Master Pulse-Width Equation

Insert $T_{inj}$ from §2.1:

$$
T_{open} \;=\; T_{base} \cdot F_E \cdot F_W \cdot F_A \cdot F_V + T_{dead} - T_{dead}
$$

$$
T_{open} \;=\; T_{base} \cdot F_E \cdot F_W \cdot F_A \cdot F_V
$$

> The dead time **cancels perfectly**! This is why the ECU injects *raw* pulse width = $T_{base}\cdot\prod F + T_{dead}$.

### Step 4 — Final Delivered Fuel Mass

$$
m_f \;=\; \rho_{fuel} \cdot q \cdot \frac{T_{base} \cdot F_E \cdot F_W \cdot F_A \cdot F_V}{60{,}000}
$$

### Step 5 — Required Air Mass for Target Lambda

$$
\dot{m}_{air,\,req} \;=\; \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel}
$$

This is the equation the ECU solves backward: given $\dot{m}_{air}$ from the MAF sensor and a *desired* $\lambda$, the required $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ is:

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel,\,req} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (typical KTU 14-mark setup)

> A 4-cylinder petrol engine runs at **3000 rpm**, MAF reads **0.040 kg/s**, target λ = **0.85** (WOT power enrichment). Injector flow $q = 200\;\text{cm}^3/\text{min}$, $\rho_{fuel} = 0.745\;\text{g/cm}^3$. Compute the per-cylinder fuel mass per cycle and the pulse width. Take $F_E = F_W = F_A = F_V = 1$ for simplicity.

**Step A — Total fuel flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{fuel} \;=\; \frac{0.040}{0.85 \cdot 14.7} \;=\; 3.20 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step B — Fuel per cycle** (4-stroke, so one injection every **2 revolutions** → cycles/sec = RPM/120):

$$
\text{Cycles/s} \;=\; \frac{3000}{120} \;=\; 25\;\text{cycles/s}
$$

$$
m_{f,\,cycle,\,total} \;=\; \frac{3.20\times10^{-3}}{25} \;=\; 1.28\times10^{-4}\;\text{kg/cycle}
$$

**Step C — Per cylinder (4 injectors, simultaneous firing bank):**

$$
m_{f,\,cyl} \;=\; \frac{1.28\times10^{-4}}{4} \;=\; 3.2\times10^{-5}\;\text{kg} \;=\; 32\;\text{mg}
$$

**Step D — Pulse width:**

$$
T_{open} \;=\; \frac{m_f}{\rho_{fuel}\cdot q}\cdot 60{,}000 \;=\; \frac{32}{0.745 \cdot 200}\cdot 60{,}000 \cdot \frac{1}{1000}
$$

Let us simplify step by step:

$$
T_{open} \;=\; \frac{32 \times 60{,}000}{0.745 \cdot 200 \cdot 1000}\;\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{open} \;=\; \frac{1{,}920{,}000}{149{,}000}\;\text{ms} \;\approx\; 12.88\;\text{ms}
$$

**Result:** Pulse width ≈ **12.9 ms** per injector per cycle — well within the typical 1–15 ms range of production ECUs.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Trigger vs Action vs Sensor Map

| Strategy | Primary Trigger Sensors | ECU Action | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold-Start Enrichment | Coolant Temp Sensor (CTS), Crank RPM | Inject extra fuel per CTS lookup | Every cycle during cold cranking |
| Warm-Up Enrichment | CTS, Air Temp Sensor (IAT) | Tapered extra fuel | 10 Hz |
| Acceleration Enrichment | ΔMAP/dt, ΔTPS/dt, MAF-rate-of-change | Wall-wetting fuel pulse | 10–100 Hz |
| Power Enrichment (WOT) | TPS > 85 %, MAP > 90 kPa | Pre-stored power enrichment map | 10 Hz |
| Deceleration Leaning | TPS = 0 %, MAP < 30 kPa, RPM 1200–1800 | Reduce fuel (lean toward λ=1) | 10 Hz |
| Overrun FCO | TPS=0, RPM>1800, CTS>60°C, V>0 | $T_{inj} = 0$ ms | Every cycle |
| Idle Speed Control | RPM sensor, CTS, AC switch, Gear signal | Adjust IACV duty / stepper steps | 50–200 Hz (PI loop) |
| EGR Control | MAP, RPM, CTS, MAF, vehicle speed | Modulate EGR valve to target rate | 10–50 Hz |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Closed-Loop Components Used in Each Strategy

| Strategy | Open-Loop? | Closed-Loop? | Feedback Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSE | ✓ | ✗ | — |
| ASE | ✓ (predictive) | Optional | Front O2 (post-event trim) |
| PSE | ✓ | Optional | Front O2 |
| FCO | ✓ | ✗ | — |
| Deceleration Leaning | ✓ | ✗ | — |
| ISC | Initial map lookup | ✓ (PI) | Crankshaft position sensor (RPM) |
| EGR | Initial map lookup | ✓ (PID) | MAP sensor (ΔP across EGR) / EGR valve position sensor |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master ECU Fuel & Air Management — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> B[ECU Processor]
    C[MAF or MAP Sensor] --> B
    D[Throttle Position Sensor] --> B
    E[Coolant Temp Sensor] --> B
    F[IAT Sensor] --> B
    G[Front O2 Sensor] --> B
    H[Crankshaft RPM] --> B
    I[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> B
    J[AC Switch / Load Signals] --> B

    B --> K{Operating Mode Decoder}
    K --> L[Cold Start Strategy]
    K --> M[Warm-Up Enrichment]
    K --> N[Acceleration Wall-Wetting]
    K --> O[Power Enrichment]
    K --> P[Deceleration Leaning]
    K --> Q[Overrun Fuel Cut-Off]
    K --> R[Idle Speed Control Loop]
    K --> S[EGR Control Loop]

    L --> T[Injector Driver Stage]
    M --> T
    N --> T
    O --> T
    P --> T
    Q --> T

    R --> U[IACV Solenoid or Stepper Motor]
    S --> V[EGR Valve Actuator]
    T --> W[Fuel Injectors]

    B --> X[Closed-Loop Trim from O2]
    X --> K
```

## 4.2 Cold-Start Enrichment — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ignition ON] --> B[ECU Power-On Self Test]
    B --> C[Read CTS and IAT]
    C --> D{CTS less than 40 C?}
    D -- YES --> E[Apply CSE Multiplier 1.5 to 3.0]
    D -- NO --> F[Stoichiometric Base Pulse]
    E --> G[Add Asynchronous Cranking Pulses]
    G --> H[Schedule Injector Firing]
    F --> H
    H --> I{Engine Started?}
    I -- NO --> J[Continue CSE and Re-Crank]
    I -- YES --> K[Begin Warm-Up Enrichment Taper]
    K --> L[O2 Sensor Warm and Closed-Loop Engage]
```

## 4.3 Overrun Fuel Cut-Off Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Each Injection Cycle Tick] --> B[Read TPS]
    B --> C{TPS less than 5 percent}
    C -- NO --> X1[Normal Fuel Map Operation]
    C -- YES --> D[Read RPM]
    D --> E{RPM greater than 1800}
    E -- NO --> F[Apply Deceleration Leaning Map]
    E -- YES --> G[Read CTS]
    G --> H{CTS greater than 60 C}
    H -- NO --> F
    H -- YES --> I[Read Vehicle Speed]
    I --> J{Vehicle Speed greater than 0}
    J -- NO --> F
    J -- YES --> K[Set Injector Pulse Width to 0 ms]
    K --> L{Monitor RPM Drop}
    L --> M{RPM dropped below 1200}
    M -- NO --> K
    M -- YES --> N[Restore Injection with Small Lead Pulse]
    F --> X1
```

## 4.4 Idle Speed Control — Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Target RPM Map Lookup] --> B[Subtract Actual RPM]
    C[Crankshaft RPM Sensor] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal e of t]
    D --> E[Proportional Gain Kp times e]
    D --> F[Integral Term Ki times Integral e dt]
    E --> G[Sum Block]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Control Output u of t]
    H --> I[IACV Solenoid PWM or Stepper Motor Driver]
    I --> J[Idle Air Bypass Passage]
    J --> K[Intake Manifold Air Quantity Changes]
    K --> L[Engine Torque Output Changes]
    L --> C
```

## 4.5 EGR Control — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ECU Reads MAP RPM CTS and MAF] --> B[Compute Target EGR Rate from Map]
    B --> C{EGR Allowed Region}
    C -- Idle Cold Start or WOT --> D[Command EGR Valve Closed 0 percent]
    C -- Part Throttle Cruise --> E[Command EGR Valve to Target Position]
    E --> F[EGR Stepper or Solenoid Vacuum Modulator]
    F --> G[EGR Valve Opens]
    G --> H[Exhaust Gas Flows into Intake]
    H --> I[Combustion Temperature Drops]
    I --> J[NOx Formation Rate Falls]
    J --> K[Front O2 Sensor Long Term Fuel Trim Adjustment]
    K --> A
```

## 4.6 Lambda Control Band — Visual Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rich Power Lambda 0.78] --> B[Slightly Rich 0.90]
    B --> C[Stoichiometric Lambda 1.00]
    C --> D[Lean Decel Lambda 1.05]
    D --> E[Lean Burn Lambda 1.20]

    A -.WOT Power.-> A
    B -.Hot Idle.-> B
    C -.Closed Loop Cruise.-> C
    D -.Deceleration.-> D
    E -.GDI Stratified.-> E
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024, Set B]**
**Explain with a neat sketch the Overrun Fuel Cut-Off strategy in a modern EFI engine. List the four conditions that must be simultaneously satisfied.**

**Model Answer:**

Overrun Fuel Cut-Off (FCO) is an ECU strategy in which the fuel injectors are completely de-energised (pulse width = 0 ms) when the engine is being driven by the vehicle's kinetic energy with no driver demand for torque.

**Four mandatory conditions:**

1. **TPS < 5 %** — driver foot off accelerator (closed throttle).
2. **RPM > 1800 rpm** (calibrated threshold, coolant-dependent).
3. **Coolant temperature > 60 °C** — engine fully warm (to avoid cold-engine stall).
4. **Vehicle speed > 0 km/h** — gear engaged or rolling.

**Behaviour:** When RPM drops back below ≈ 1200 rpm (hysteresis band), fuel injection is restored with a small lead pulse to prevent engine stumble.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "engine is OFF" — this is wrong. The engine is still rotating (driven by wheels); only the injectors are silenced.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, KTU Model Paper]**
**Define EGR. Why is EGR activated only at part-throttle and not at idle or WOT?**

**Model Answer:**

**EGR (Exhaust Gas Recirculation)** is the controlled re-introduction of inert exhaust gas into the intake manifold to lower peak combustion temperature and thereby suppress thermal NOx formation.

| Operating Regime | EGR Status | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Idle | **OFF** | Would cause misfire and unstable idle; very low temperature anyway — no NOx |
| Cold Start | **OFF** | Catalyst not yet warm; cold combustion makes negligible NOx |
| Part-Throttle Cruise | **ON (5–15 %)** | Highest NOx zone — moderate load, high temperature |
| WOT | **OFF** | Driver demands max power — diluting charge would reduce torque |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the various **fuel enrichment strategies** used by the ECU in a multi-point fuel injection system. Discuss cold-start, warm-up, acceleration (wall-wetting) and power enrichment in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **Deceleration Leaning** and **Overrun Fuel Cut-Off** with their decision logic, hysteresis band and engine benefits. Draw the FCO decision tree. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**1. Introduction (1 mark)**
Modern MPFI ECUs compute injector pulse width each cycle as:
$$ T_{inj} = T_{base} \cdot F_{enrich} \cdot F_{warm} \cdot F_{accel} \cdot F_{batt} + T_{dead} $$
Enrichment = increasing fuel above stoichiometric (λ < 1).

**2. Cold-Start Enrichment (CSE) (2 marks)**
Trigger: CTS < 40 °C during cranking.
Cause: Fuel condenses on cold port walls; poor atomisation; slow evaporation.
Action: ECU multiplies base pulse by **1.5–3.0×** depending on CTS. Adds **asynchronous cranking pulses** to wet the manifold walls before first piston stroke.
Lambda target: **0.6–0.8**.

**3. Warm-Up Enrichment (1.5 marks)**
Trigger: CTS between 40 °C and 80 °C.
Action: Enrichment tapers linearly (1.5 → 1.0) as CTS rises. Some ECUs use **time-after-start** and **CTS** in a 2-D map.

**4. Acceleration Enrichment / Wall-Wetting Compensation (1.5 marks)**
Trigger: ΔMAP/Δt > threshold (≈ 5 kPa/s) OR ΔTPS/Δt > 4 %/100 ms.
Cause: Sudden manifold pressure rise sends fuel droplets to walls.
Action: Inject **predictive fuel burst** (open-loop), then decay over ~ 1 s. Some ECUs use **d(MAF)/dt** to estimate in real-time.

**5. Power Enrichment (PSE) (1 mark)**
Trigger: TPS > 85 % AND MAP > 90 kPa.
Action: Enriches to **λ = 0.78–0.85** to cool exhaust valves (protects cat. converter) and provide slight detonation margin.

**Diagram:** Refer to §4.1 and §4.2.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Deceleration Leaning (3.5 marks)**

When the throttle is closed but RPM is still moderate (1200–1800), manifold vacuum is high (> 60 kPa). The ECU progressively reduces the injector pulse width so the mixture **leans toward λ = 1.0** (or slightly lean). This:
- Reduces HC and CO emissions during engine braking.
- Improves fuel economy marginally.
- Smooths the transition between closed throttle and FCO.

**Overrun Fuel Cut-Off (3.5 marks)**

Decision logic:
$$ FCO_{active} = TPS_{<5\%} \;\wedge\; RPM_{>1800} \;\wedge\; CTS_{>60°C} \;\wedge\; V_{veh>0} $$
Pulse width set to **0 ms**.

**Hysteresis:** Cut-in RPM = 1800, Cut-out RPM = 1200. This prevents the FCO from rapidly toggling on/off near the threshold (Schmitt-trigger style).

**Benefits:**
- 3–8 % fuel saving in urban driving.
- Near-zero HC/CO during cut.
- Reduced pump work (less fuel pumped → less parasitic loss).

**Diagram:** Refer to §4.3.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *leaning* with *cut-off*. Leaning = reduced pulse width; Cut-off = zero pulse width. Both occur during deceleration but at *different RPM bands*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the **Idle Speed Control (ISC)** system in a modern passenger car. Compare **IACV solenoid** and **stepper-motor ISC**, and explain the closed-loop PI control strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **EGR Control system** with reference to its types (internal vs external, HP vs LP, cooled vs uncooled). Discuss the EGR map and why EGR is disabled at idle, cold-start and WOT. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**1. Purpose of ISC (1 mark)**
Maintain a target idle RPM (typically 700–850 rpm for a warm petrol engine) regardless of accessory loads (AC, alternator, PS) or gear engagement. Also controls idle during warm-up — higher target RPM when cold.

**2. Hardware Implementations (3 marks)**

| Feature | IACV Solenoid (Duty-Cycle) | Stepper-Motor ISC |
|---|---|---|
| Actuator | PWM solenoid | Bi-directional permanent-magnet stepper |
| Output | Duty cycle 0–100 % | Step count 0–125 (typically) |
| Response | Fast (10 ms) | Slower (~ 50 ms) |
| Air Path | Bypass around throttle plate | Bypass screw or pintle |
| Modern Use | Honda, older Ford | Toyota, Bosch L-Jetronic, Tata |
| Failure Mode | Stuck open → high idle; stuck closed → stall | Lost steps → re-learn on every key-on |

**3. Closed-Loop PI Control (2 marks)**

The ECU compares actual RPM to target:
$$ e(t) = RPM_{target} - RPM_{actual} $$
The controller output:
$$ u(t) = K_P\, e(t) + K_I \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau $$
where $K_P \approx 0.5\text{–}2.0\;\%\text{/rpm}$ and $K_I \approx 0.05\text{–}0.2\;\%\text{/(rpm·s)}$.
The output $u(t)$ modulates the IACV duty cycle or stepper step count.

**4. DBW Variant (1 mark)**
In Drive-by-Wire vehicles, there is **no separate ISC valve**. The ECU directly commands the throttle plate angle to a small opening (~ 2–5°) to control idle air. A "learning" routine stores the learned position in NVM.

**Diagram:** Refer to §4.4.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**1. Definition and Purpose (1 mark)**
EGR = Exhaust Gas Recirculation — dilutes intake charge with inert exhaust gas (mainly CO₂ and H₂O vapour) to lower flame temperature and suppress **thermal NOx** (Zeldovich mechanism).

**2. Types of EGR (3 marks)**

| Type | Path | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| Internal EGR | Re-breathing via valve overlap (no external pipe) | Older engines, some Honda |
| External EGR | External pipe from exhaust manifold to intake | Most modern engines |
| HP-EGR (High Pressure) | Exhaust manifold → intake (before throttle) | Petrol engines |
| LP-EGR (Low Pressure) | After catalyst → before throttle | Petrol engines (cleaner gas) |
| Cooled EGR | EGR passes through a heat exchanger cooler | Diesel BS-VI / Euro 6 (mandatory) |
| Uncooled EGR | No cooler | Older petrol engines |

**3. EGR Map (1.5 marks)**
EGR rate is a 3-D map indexed by RPM and Load. Typical values:
- Idle, cold start, WOT: **0 %**
- Light cruise (1500–2500 rpm, MAP 30–60 kPa): **5–15 %**
- Moderately loaded cruise: **8–12 %**

**4. Why EGR is Disabled in Specific Modes (1.5 marks)**

| Mode | Why EGR is OFF |
|---|---|
| Idle | Would cause misfire (low flame speed); unstable idle |
| Cold Start | Catalyst not warm; low cylinder temp → little NOx anyway |
| WOT | Driver wants max power; dilution reduces torque |
| High RPM | Combustion becomes less temperature-sensitive; risk of misfire |

**Diagram:** Refer to §4.5.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often say "EGR reduces CO₂ emissions" — **wrong**. EGR reduces **NOx**, and may *slightly increase* CO₂ due to reduced efficiency. The greenhouse gas targeted is CO₂, but EGR's role is purely NOx.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Enrichment** = adding fuel (richer than stoich). Three types: **CSE**, **ASE**, **PSE**.
- [ ] Cold-Start Enrichment → λ = **0.6 – 0.8**; multiplier **1.5 – 3.0×**.
- [ ] Acceleration Enrichment → wall-wetting compensation; trigger = **ΔMAP/Δt**.
- [ ] Power Enrichment → λ = **0.78 – 0.85**; protects cat. converter from overheating.
- [ ] **Deceleration Leaning** = leaning toward λ=1 during closed-throttle coasting (1200–1800 rpm).
- [ ] **Overrun FCO** = 0 ms pulse when **TPS=0, RPM>1800, CTS>60°C, V>0**.
- [ ] FCO has **hysteresis** — cut-in RPM ≠ cut-out RPM (≈ 1800 vs 1200).
- [ ] FCO saves **3–8 %** fuel in urban driving; emissions drop to near zero during cut.
- [ ] **ISC** maintains idle RPM via IACV / stepper / DBW throttle plate.
- [ ] ISC uses **PI control**: $u = K_P e + K_I \int e\,dt$.
- [ ] Typical target idle: **700–850 rpm** (warm petrol car).
- [ ] Stepper ISC has **0–125 steps**; ≈ **0.025 mm** air bypass per step.
- [ ] **EGR** lowers peak flame temp → reduces **NOx** (Zeldovich mechanism).
- [ ] EGR is **active only at part-throttle cruise** (5–15 %).
- [ ] EGR is **OFF** at idle, cold-start, WOT, high RPM.
- [ ] **HP-EGR** = high pressure; **LP-EGR** = low pressure (after cat.).
- [ ] **Cooled EGR** is mandatory for **BS-VI diesel** engines.
- [ ] Lambda (λ) = AFR_actual / AFR_stoich; stoich = **14.7 : 1** (petrol).
- [ ] Closed-loop trim happens via **front (upstream) O2 sensor** in the catalyst window.
- [ ] NOx ∝ exp(-E_a/RT); temperature is the **key driver**.
- [ ] **Wall-wetting** = fuel film on intake port walls during tip-in.
- [ ] **Hysteresis** in FCO and ISC prevents oscillation (Schmitt-trigger principle).
- [ ] **Dead time** of injector (0.5–1.5 ms) is compensated by adding $T_{dead}$ to pulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always state the *trigger condition*, the *ECU action* and the *reason/benefit* when describing any of the five strategies. Examiners award 3 marks per sub-section when all three are present.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Injection System Parameters Affecting Combustion, Noise and Emissions in CI Engines

### Formal Academic Definition

In a **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine**, combustion is initiated by the auto-ignition of fuel injected into highly compressed hot air inside the combustion chamber. Because there is **no spark plug**, every characteristic of the **fuel injection event** directly dictates the quality of combustion, the level of combustion noise (diesel knock), and the formation of regulated emissions (NOx, soot, HC, CO, PM).

The **governing injection parameters** recognized by the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Injection Pressure** ($P_{inj}$) — pressure at which fuel leaves the injector nozzle.
2. **Injection Timing** ($\theta_{inj}$) — crank angle position (BTDC or ATDC) at which injection begins.
3. **Injection Duration** ($\Delta\theta_{inj}$) — total crank-angle span during which the injector is energized.
4. **Number of Injections per Cycle** ($n_{inj}$) — pilot + main + post injections (multi-pulse strategy).
5. **Spray Pattern & Atomization Quality** — Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD), spray cone angle.
6. **Nozzle Geometry** — number of holes, hole diameter, sac/no-sac volume.
7. **Fuel Physical Properties** — density ($\rho_f$), viscosity ($\mu_f$), cetane number.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The parameter set above is the **exact list** expected in 14-mark descriptive answers. Memorize the table in Section 2 — these are the seven "must-know" variables examiners test.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuition

Think of a CI engine cylinder as a **pressure cooker** full of scorching air (≈800 K). When you squirt fuel inside, it behaves like **spraying water onto a hot pan**:

- If you **spray hard and early** (high pressure, advanced timing): the droplets vaporize fast, mix quickly with air, and burn smoothly with low noise — but **high peak temperatures generate NOx**.
- If you **spray weakly and late** (low pressure, retarded timing): fuel puddles on the piston crown, mixes poorly, and explodes with a sharp **"DIESEL KNOCK"** — low NOx but high soot, HC, and noise.
- **Pilot injection** is like "preheating the pan with a tiny mist" — a small dose prepares the chamber, so the main charge burns gradually instead of explosively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multi-Pulse Injection?** Modern Common-Rail systems fire fuel **3–7 times** per cycle, not once. Each pulse is tuned to control a specific pollutant or noise phenomenon. This is the heart of modern Euro-VI/BS-VI emission compliance.

---

## 1.2 Pilot, Main, Advanced Post Injection and Retarded Post Injection

### Formal Definition — Multi-Pulse Injection Strategy

Modern electronically controlled **Common-Rail Diesel Injection (CRDI)** systems use a **multi-event injection schedule** within a single combustion cycle. The standard nomenclature is:

| Pulse Name | Crank-Angle Position | Quantity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pilot Injection** | 10°–30° BTDC (early) | 1.5–3 mm³/stroke | Reduce combustion noise & pressure rise rate |
| **Main Injection** | 0°–10° BTDC (primary) | Bulk of fuel | Generate torque / power |
| **Advanced Post Injection** | Shortly after main (≈5°–25° ATDC) | Small dose | After-burn soot → reduce PM emissions |
| **Retarded Post Injection** | Far after main (≈40°–120° ATDC) | Small dose | Regenerate DPF, raise exhaust temperature |

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine lighting a campfire:

- **Pilot** = crumpling small dry twigs first (gentle warming flame).
- **Main** = placing the big logs on top (big steady fire).
- **Advanced Post** = adding a few more sticks while logs still burn (cleans up smoke/soot).
- **Retarded Post** = blowing extra air after the fire is mostly out (to burn charcoal residue in a filter).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU:** **P-M-A-R = "Please Make A Roaring fire"** — Pilot, Main, Advanced-post, Retarded-post.

---

## 1.3 Electronic Ignition Control (SI Engines)

### Formal Definition

**Electronic Ignition Control (EIC)** is an ECU-based system that determines and triggers the **optimum spark timing** for each combustion cycle in a **Spark Ignition (SI) engine**. It replaces mechanical centrifugal and vacuum advance mechanisms with a software-controlled **ignition timing map** stored in the Engine Control Unit (ECU) memory.

Inputs to the EIC:
- Crankshaft position (CKP sensor) — for **rpm**
- camshaft position (CMP sensor) — for **synchronization**
- Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) or Mass Air Flow (MAF) — for **load**
- Coolant Temperature (CTS), Intake Air Temperature (IATS)
- Knock Sensor (KS) signal
- Throttle Position Sensor (TPS)

### Intuition

A car's "spark moment" is like **popping popcorn** — pop too early, kernels fly out unburned; pop too late, they burn. The ECU watches engine speed (rpm) and load (MAP) and decides *exactly* when to fire the spark plug, in **real time**, every cycle.

---

## 1.4 Ignition Timing Map & Spark Advance Correction

### Formal Definition

The **Ignition Timing Map** is a **two-dimensional lookup table** stored in the ECU's read-only memory, with:

- **X-axis:** Engine speed (rpm), typically 600 – 7000 rpm
- **Y-axis:** Engine load (MAP in kPa, or MAF in g/s, or throttle %)
- **Z-value (cell content):** Optimum **basic spark advance angle** in degrees BTDC

The **Spark Advance Correction Scheme** applies **real-time corrections** on top of the basic map value for ambient and operating conditions.

### Intuitive Analogy

The ignition timing map is like a **thermostat dial**: hotter weather → less cooling needed → slightly different setting. The map gives the "default schedule"; the corrections are "fine-tuning dials" for cold start, knock, idle, etc.

---

## 1.5 Ignition Timing Calculation — Composite Equation

The **total spark advance angle** delivered by the ECU in any cycle is:

$$\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{basic}(\text{rpm}, \text{load}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_{correction,i}$$

Where:
- $\theta_{initial}$ = **Initial (base/idle) timing** — fixed by manufacturer (e.g., **10° BTDC**).
- $\theta_{basic}$ = **Basic Advance Angle** — read from the 3D ignition timing map.
- $\theta_{correction,i}$ = **Corrective Advance Angles** — knock, coolant temp, idle stability, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandate:** Every 14-mark question on this topic must include the composite timing equation. Marks are split: 4 marks for naming three components, 4 marks for the equation, 6 marks for explaining correction schemes.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CI Engine Injection Parameter — Effect Mechanics

Each injection parameter influences combustion through a clearly traceable physical chain. The KTU board examiners expect students to articulate the **cause → mechanism → effect** chain.

### 2.1.1 Injection Pressure ($P_{inj}$)

- **Mechanism:** Higher pressure atomizes fuel into **smaller droplets**, reducing Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD). Smaller droplets vaporize faster → better air–fuel mixing.
- **Effect on Combustion:** Shorter ignition delay, smoother premixed combustion phase.
- **Effect on Noise:** Reduces diesel knock → **lower peak pressure rise rate** ($dP/d\theta$).
- **Effect on Emissions:**
  - **Soot ↓** (better mixing, more complete combustion)
  - **NOx ↑** (higher in-cylinder temperatures from fast burning)
  - **HC ↓, CO ↓**

$$P_{inj} \uparrow \Rightarrow \text{SMD} \downarrow \Rightarrow dP/d\theta \downarrow \Rightarrow \text{Noise} \downarrow$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values:** Common-Rail pressure ranges from **250 bar (idle)** to **2000+ bar (full load)** in modern Bosch / Denso systems.

### 2.1.2 Injection Timing ($\theta_{inj}$)

- **Mechanism:** Earlier injection gives fuel more time to mix → longer ignition delay → more fuel accumulates → abrupt combustion.
- **Effect on Combustion:**
  - Early injection → higher peak pressure, longer premixed burn → high thermal efficiency
  - Late injection → less mixing time → diffusion-controlled burn → lower efficiency
- **Effect on Noise:**
  - Early → **higher noise** (more premixed combustion)
  - Late → **lower noise** but higher soot & smoke
- **Effect on Emissions:**
  - Early → **NOx ↑** (high peak temp), **Soot ↓**
  - Late → **NOx ↓, Soot ↑**

### 2.1.3 Number of Injections (Multi-Pulse)

- **Single pulse** → high noise, high NOx/PM trade-off
- **Pilot + Main** → smoother pressure rise, **noise ↓ 3–5 dB**, NOx ↓
- **Post Injection** → burns soot during expansion stroke → **PM ↓ 30–50%**
- **Retarded Post** → regenerates DPF (Diesel Particulate Filter)

### 2.1.4 Spray Pattern & Atomization

- Spray cone angle ($\alpha_{spray}$) affects jet penetration depth.
- Excess penetration → fuel hits cylinder wall → **wall-wetting → HC ↑**
- Insufficient penetration → poor air utilization → **soot ↑**

### 2.1.5 Nozzle Geometry

- More holes, smaller diameter → finer atomization → NOx/Soot trade-off.
- **Sac-less (VCO) nozzles** eliminate nozzle sac volume → reduce HC emissions at cold start.

### 2.1.6 Fuel Properties

- **Cetane Number (CN):** Higher CN = shorter ignition delay = smoother combustion = less noise. Modern diesel CN ≈ **45–55**.
- **Density & Viscosity:** Affect spray momentum and atomization quality.

---

## 2.2 Electronic Ignition Control — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Why Spark Timing Matters

In an SI engine, the **flame kernel** propagates from the spark plug at finite speed (≈20–40 m/s). For **MBT (Minimum Spark advance for Best Torque)**, combustion must end a few degrees ATDC so peak pressure occurs near 15°–20° ATDC for optimum work output.

### 2.2.2 ECU Decision Logic

The ECU executes this sequence every 10 ms (or every crankshaft revolution):

1. **Read inputs:** CKP (rpm), MAP/MAF (load), CTS, IAT, KS, TPS
2. **Look up basic advance:** $\theta_{basic} = f(\text{rpm}, \text{load})$ from the **ignition map**
3. **Apply corrections:** $\theta_{correction,i}$ from sub-maps
4. **Compute total advance:** $\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{basic} + \sum \theta_{correction}$
5. **Schedule spark:** Trigger ignition coil primary at the **correct crank angle** before TDC

### 2.2.3 Components of the Correction Scheme

| Correction Type | Trigger | Magnitude | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cold-start enrichment & advance** | CTS < 40 °C | +5° to +25° | Aids starting, prevents stalling |
| **Warm-up correction** | 40 °C < CTS < 80 °C | +2° to +8° | Smooth idle, low emissions when cold |
| **Knock correction** | Knock sensor vibration | Retard **−1° to −10°** | Protects engine from detonation |
| **Idle stability correction** | rpm deviation from target | ±1° to ±3° | Smooth idle |
| **Overheat correction** | CTS > 105 °C | Retard −2° to −6° | Reduces coolant load |
| **Altitude / barometric** | Low atmospheric pressure | Retard −1° to −4° | Reduces knock at altitude |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Symbol | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{basic} + \sum \theta_{correction,i}$ | Composite spark advance equation | degrees BTDC |
| 2 | $dP/d\theta$ | Pressure rise rate (noise indicator) | bar / °CA |
| 3 | $SMD = \frac{6 \cdot V_{drop}}{A_{drop}}$ | Sauter Mean Diameter | $\mu m$ |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{inj} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_f \Delta P}$ | Injector mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 5 | $\theta_{ign.delay} \propto \dfrac{1}{P_{inj} \cdot CN}$ | Ignition delay in CI engine | ms |
| 6 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | Combustion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Q_{inj} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \dot{m}_{inj}(t) \, dt$ | Total injected quantity | mm³ / cycle |
| 8 | $\tau_{inj} = \dfrac{Q_{inj}}{\dot{m}_{inj}}$ | Injection duration | ms or °CA |
| 9 | $\theta_{spray.pen} = K \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot d_n}{\rho_{air}}}$ | Spray penetration depth | mm |
| 10 | $T_{max} \approx T_{comp} + \Delta T_{comb}$ | Peak cycle temperature | K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Exam:**
> - Spark advance is always expressed in **degrees BTDC** (Before Top Dead Centre).
> - Retard values are **negative** (e.g., −5° BTDC = 5° ATDC).
> - **MBT timing** is the optimum; deviations produce either knock (too advanced) or backfire/overheating (too retarded).

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CI Engines:** Multi-pulse injection is the cornerstone of **BS-VI / Euro-VI** emission norms. Without pilot + post injection, diesel cars cannot legally be sold.
- **SI Engines:** Electronic spark control enables **knock-free operation** of **turbocharged, high-compression-ratio (>12:1)** engines running on regular octane fuel, gaining ≈ 5–10 % thermal efficiency.
- Both systems directly feed into **OBD-II diagnostics** — fault codes P0300 (random misfire), P0335 (CKP), P0325 (knock sensor) all relate to these modules.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spark Advance Angle Composition

The composite timing equation is **not derived** from first principles — it is a **definitional superposition** of three orthogonal timing contributions. The justification comes from engine-cycle thermodynamics.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1 — Define the total angular duration before TDC available for spark scheduling.**

The crankshaft rotates by 360° in one engine cycle (2 revolutions for 4-stroke). The spark must occur **before** TDC because combustion itself takes ~30°–40° of crank rotation. Let:

$$\theta_{total} = \text{Total spark advance measured in degrees BTDC}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose into base contribution.**

A **fixed base/idle timing** $\theta_{initial}$ is set at the assembly line (typically 5°–15° BTDC). This ensures the engine starts and idles correctly even if the map fails:

$$\theta_{total,1} = \theta_{initial}$$

**Step 3 — Add the speed–load dependent contribution.**

The ECU reads the **3-D map value** $\theta_{basic}(\text{rpm}, \text{load})$ — a pre-calibrated optimum from dynamometer testing at MBT:

$$\theta_{total,2} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{basic}$$

**Step 4 — Add environmental/operating-condition corrections.**

Real engines deviate from MBT due to cold start, knock, altitude, etc. These are summed as corrections $\theta_{corr,i}$:

$$\boxed{\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{basic}(\text{rpm}, \text{load}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_{corr,i}}$$

**Step 5 — Demonstrate the numerical workflow.**

Consider: initial = 10°, rpm = 3000, MAP = 60 kPa → basic = 28° from map, coolant cold → +6°, knock detected → −4°.

$$\theta_{total} = 10° + 28° + 6° - 4° = 40° \text{ BTDC}$$

This is the **exact crank angle** at which the ECU will energize the ignition coil.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Pulse Injection Effect on Emissions & Noise

The table below is a **high-yield KTU summary** explicitly tying each injection strategy to its combustion, noise, and emission consequences.

| Injection Event | Crank Position | Injected Quantity | Combustion Phase Affected | Noise Effect | NOx Effect | Soot/PM Effect | HC/CO Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pilot** | 10°–30° BTDC | 1.5–3 mm³ | Pre-mixed "warm-up" flame | **↓ 3–5 dB** (smoother pressure rise) | Slight ↓ | Slight ↑ | Negligible |
| **Main** | 0°–10° BTDC | Bulk fuel (≈80–90 %) | Premixed + diffusion burn | Reference baseline | Reference | Reference | Reference |
| **Advanced Post** | 5°–25° ATDC | 1–3 mm³ | Late oxidation of soot precursors | Marginal | ↑ slightly | **↓ 30–50 %** | Negligible |
| **Retarded Post** | 40°–120° ATDC | 1–2 mm³ | Expansion-stroke burn — heats exhaust | — | Slight ↑ | **↓ 70 % (with DPF)** | ↑ slight (unburned HC) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Effect of Retarding Injection Timing on NOx

A 4-stroke CI engine operates at stoichiometric air–fuel ratio with excess air factor $\lambda = 1.5$. Original injection timing $\theta_{1} = 8°$ BTDC. The ECU retards to $\theta_{2} = 4°$ BTDC. Peak in-cylinder temperature drops by **8 %**. Estimate the NOx reduction using the simplified Zeldovich mechanism proportional factor.

### Given Data
- $T_{peak,1}$ from baseline = **2200 K**
- Drop after retardation = **8 %**
- Zeldovich NOx formation rate $\propto \exp(-E_a / RT)$ with $E_a / R = 38000$ K

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute new peak temperature.**

$$\Delta T = 0.08 \times 2200 = 176 \text{ K}$$

$$T_{peak,2} = 2200 - 176 = 2024 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute exponential ratio of NOx formation rate.**

$$\frac{R_{NOx,2}}{R_{NOx,1}} = \exp\left[-\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]$$

Substituting values:

$$\frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{2200} = 4.545 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{2024} = 4.941 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta(1/T) = (4.941 - 4.545) \times 10^{-4} = 3.96 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Plug into Zeldovich factor.**

$$\frac{R_{NOx,2}}{R_{NOx,1}} = \exp(-38000 \times 3.96 \times 10^{-5})$$

$$= \exp(-1.505) \approx 0.222$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage reduction.**

$$\% \text{NOx reduction} = (1 - 0.222) \times 100\% = \boxed{77.8\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A **4° retardation** of injection timing alone reduces peak temperature by 8 %, which — through the Arrhenius Zeldovich mechanism — cuts NOx by **≈ 78 %**. This is why **retarded post-injection** is so powerful for emission compliance, but it costs fuel economy and increases soot. Hence the need for DPF.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Total Spark Advance Calculation

A 1.6 L gasoline engine runs at **3200 rpm** under **partial load (MAP = 55 kPa)**. The ignition map cell reads **24° BTDC**. Active corrections:

- Cold coolant (45 °C) → **+5°**
- Mild knock detected → **−3°**
- Initial timing = **8°**

Compute $\theta_{total}$.

$$\theta_{total} = 8° + 24° + 5° - 3°$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{total} = 34° \text{ BTDC}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Correction Scheme Behaviour

| Operating Condition | Sensor Input | Correction Direction | Typical Magnitude | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cold start (CTS < 0 °C) | CTS | Advance | +10° to +25° | Reduce wall-quenching HC, aid vaporization |
| Warm-up (40–80 °C) | CTS | Advance | +2° to +8° | Improve cold idle quality |
| Normal operating (80–100 °C) | CTS | None | 0° | Map value directly used |
| Knock detected | Knock sensor | **Retard** | −1° to −10° | Prevent detonation damage |
| Idle speed deviation | CKP (rpm) | Advance or retard | ±1° to ±3° | Maintain target idle |
| Overheat (CTS > 105 °C) | CTS | **Retard** | −2° to −6° | Reduce thermal load |
| High altitude (low baro P) | BARO / MAF | **Retard** | −1° to −4° | Lower effective compression, less knock |
| Wide-open throttle | TPS = 100 % | Advance (slight) | +1° to +3° | Higher volumetric efficiency |

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Ignition Timing Calculation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Engine Start / Every Cycle] --> READ[Read Sensors CKP, MAP, CTS, KS]
    READ --> RPM[Compute Engine Speed rpm]
    READ --> LOAD[Compute Engine Load MAP]
    RPM --> MAPLK[Lookup Basic Advance from 3D Map]
    LOAD --> MAPLK
    MAPLK --> BASE[Basic Advance theta_basic]
    BASE --> ADD[Sum with Initial Timing theta_initial]
    ADD --> CHK1{Cold Coolant?}

    CHK2 -- Yes --> CORR2[Subtract Knock Correction minus 1 to minus 10 deg]

    CORR2 --> SUM[Total Advance]
    CORR3 --> SUM
    SUM --> SCHED[Schedule Coil Trigger at theta_total BTDC]
    SCHED --> FIRE[Fire Spark Plug]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**List the four stages of a multi-pulse common-rail injection strategy in a CI engine and state the primary purpose of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 per stage):**

1. **Pilot Injection** — a small fuel dose (1.5–3 mm³) injected ≈ 10°–30° BTDC; creates a small pre-mixed flame that raises local temperature and pressure, **reducing the pressure rise rate and diesel knock** during the main event.
2. **Main Injection** — the bulk fuel dose injected at ≈ 0°–10° BTDC; **generates the torque** through combined premixed and diffusion combustion.
3. **Advanced Post Injection** — small dose at ≈ 10°–25° ATDC; promotes **oxidation of soot precursors** in the expansion stroke, lowering PM emissions.
4. **Retarded Post Injection** — small dose at ≈ 40°–120° ATDC; the unburnt fuel reaches the exhaust and **heats the Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** to regenerate it.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Write the composite equation for total spark advance in an electronic ignition system and explain each term in one line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$\theta_{total} = \theta_{initial} + \theta_{basic}(\text{rpm}, \text{load}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \theta_{correction,i}$$

- **$\theta_{initial}$** — fixed base/idle timing preset by manufacturer; ensures fail-safe starting (≈ **5°–15° BTDC**).
- **$\theta_{basic}$** — value read from the **3-D ignition timing map** indexed by engine rpm and load; represents the **MBT advance**.
- **$\theta_{correction,i}$** — additive (advance) or subtractive (retard) corrections for **knock, coolant temp, altitude, idle stability**, etc.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]**

(a) **Explain with a neat sketch how the fuel injection parameters affect combustion, noise, and emissions in a CI engine. Discuss the role of pilot and post injection strategies in reducing noise and particulate emissions.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **Explain in detail the Electronic Ignition Control system of an SI engine. With a block diagram, describe the working of the Spark Advance Correction Scheme.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q A (a) [7 marks]

**(i) Injection Parameters affecting CI performance (3 marks):**

The principal fuel injection parameters governing combustion, noise and emissions in a CI engine are:

| Parameter | Effect on Combustion | Effect on Noise | Effect on Emissions |
|---|---|---|---|
| Injection pressure ↑ | Better atomization, faster mixing | **Noise ↓** | NOx ↑, Soot ↓ |
| Injection timing advanced | Longer premixed burn | **Noise ↑** | NOx ↑, Soot ↓ |
| Injection timing retarded | Diffusion-controlled burn | **Noise ↓** | NOx ↓, Soot ↑ |
