> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Airbags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Airbag (Supplemental Restraint System – SRS):** An automotive passenger safety device consisting of a flexible fabric cushion (typically made of woven nylon or polyamide) that is designed to inflate rapidly (within **30–50 milliseconds**) during a severe frontal or side collision. It forms part of the **Supplemental Restraint System (SRS)** and works **in conjunction with** the three-point seat belt — it is a **secondary** restraint, never a substitute for the seat belt.

The airbag is formally classified as a **passive safety device** because it deploys automatically without requiring any active input from the vehicle occupants. The system is governed by the **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**, which continuously monitors crash sensors (accelerometers, pressure sensors, and satellite sensors). When the deceleration profile exceeds a calibrated threshold (typically **> 4g to 5g** for frontal impact, or **> 6g** for side impact), the ECU triggers the squib (initiator), igniting the propellant charge inside the inflator module, producing nitrogen gas that rapidly inflates the folded cushion.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Airbag = Spring-Loaded Cushion" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are walking and suddenly trip forward. Your natural reflex is to throw your hands out in front of you to cushion the fall. An airbag does exactly this — but at **300 km/h expansion speed** — for the entire body of a vehicle occupant. Picture a magician pulling a silk handkerchief out of an empty fist: that is how fast the cushion erupts from a 200 mm diameter storage compartment in the steering wheel or dashboard. Within the time it takes you to blink (about 150 ms), the airbag has fully inflated AND begun deflating through its vent holes, leaving a soft pillow of gas to absorb your forward momentum.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Inflation Time | **30 – 50** | ms |
| Full Inflation Time | **~70** | ms |
| Deflation Time | **~100 – 120** | ms |
| Deployment Threshold (Frontal) | **4 – 5 g** | g (9.81 m/s²) |
| Deployment Threshold (Side) | **6 – 8 g** | g |
| Gas Exit Velocity from Inflator | **~300** | m/s |
| Inflation Pressure (Peak) | **0.5 – 1.0** | bar |
| Propellant Mass (Driver Airbag) | **~50 – 80** | grams |
| Propellant Mass (Passenger Airbag) | **~200 – 400** | grams |
| Operating Temperature Range | **−40 to +85** | °C |
| Diagnostic Voltage (Squib) | **~2 – 3** | Volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Always remember the inflation sequence is **Detect → Decide → Ignite → Inflate → Deflate**. Examiners love to test the **time-budget** of an airbag event — students often forget that deployment, full inflation, and deflation all occur in **less than 0.2 seconds**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — Structured Logic Breakdown

The airbag system operates in a strict sequential pipeline. Below is the bulleted logic chain that the examiner expects you to reproduce:

- **Step 1 — Continuous Sensing (Background Loop):**
  - Up-front crash sensors (mechanical ball-in-tube or MEMS accelerometer) measure vehicle deceleration at multiple points: front cross-member, radiator support, and under the seats for side impact.
  - Satellite sensors at the extremities send signals to the SRS ECU via a dedicated **single-wire or LIN bus** link.
  - The ECU samples sensor data at a rate of **> 1000 Hz** (every 1 ms or less).

- **Step 2 — Crash Severity Evaluation (Decision Logic):**
  - The ECU applies a **software algorithm** (energy-based or velocity-change-based) to compute the **Crash Severity Index (CSI)**.
  - If the integrated deceleration $\Delta v$ exceeds the calibration threshold (typically a velocity change of **> 16 km/h in < 30 ms** for a "fire" command), the ECU triggers the firing loop.

- **Step 3 — Squib Activation (Initiator Stage):**
  - The ECU sends a controlled current pulse of **~1.2 to 2.2 Amperes** through the squib (a thin resistive wire bridge coated with zirconium–potassium perchlorate pyrotechnic compound).
  - This current exceeds the **squib firing threshold** (typically > **1.0 A** for > **2 ms**) and ignites the initiator charge.

- **Step 4 — Propellant Combustion (Gas Generation Stage):**
  - The initiator flame ignites the main propellant charge — typically **sodium azide (NaN₃)** pellets.
  - Sodium azide decomposes **exothermically** to release nitrogen gas (N₂).
  - Solid by-products (Na₂O, NaOH, Na₂CO₃) are filtered through a ceramic/metal filter basket.

- **Step 5 — Cushion Inflation (Deployment Stage):**
  - The generated N₂ gas (temperature ~**600 °C** initially) passes through the filter and cools to ~**80 °C** before entering the folded nylon cushion.
  - The cushion exits the storage module through a tear-seam cover at **~300 m/s** and reaches full inflation in **30–50 ms**.

- **Step 6 — Vented Deflation (Energy Absorption Stage):**
  - The cushion has calibrated vent holes (typically **two 25 mm holes**) on its rear side that begin releasing gas immediately after full inflation.
  - This controlled deflation absorbs the occupant's kinetic energy by converting it into gas-escape work over **~100 ms**.

- **Step 7 — Post-Crash Diagnostics:**
  - The ECU stores a **DTC (Diagnostic Trouble Code)** and illuminates the **SRS warning lamp** continuously.
  - The system is **non-resettable**; the airbag module and sensors must be replaced after deployment.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|
| $F = m \cdot a$ | Crash force on occupant (Newton's 2nd law) | $F$ in N, $m$ in kg, $a$ in m/s² |
| $\Delta v = \int a \, dt$ | Velocity change during impact (CSI basis) | $\Delta v$ in m/s, $a$ in m/s² |
| $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Occupant kinetic energy to be absorbed | $E_k$ in Joules |
| $2 \, NaN_3 \rightarrow 2 \, Na + 3 \, N_2$ | Sodium azide decomposition (theoretical) | Molar basis |
| $10 \, NaN_3 + 2 \, KNO_3 \rightarrow 5 \, Na_2O + K_2O + 16 \, N_2$ | Modern composite propellant reaction | Stoichiometric |
| $V_{gas} = \dfrac{n R T}{P}$ | Ideal gas volume generated (inflator) | $V$ in m³, $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K |
| $m_{prop} = \dfrac{P V M_{gas}}{R T \eta}$ | Propellant mass required | $\eta$ = conversion efficiency |
| $t_{deploy} = t_{detect} + t_{fire} + t_{inflate}$ | Total deployment time budget | ms |
| $E_{absorbed} = \int F_{cushion} \, dx$ | Energy absorbed by deflating cushion | Joules |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert (Markdown Safety):** Never use the literal `\|` character inside table cells for absolute value or magnitude notation. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, e.g., write $\vert a \vert$ not `\|a\|`. This prevents the table from breaking during rendering.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Crashworthiness Engineering:** Airbag timing directly drives the **Acceleration-Time History (ATH)** pulse used in occupant simulations (Hybrid III dummy).
- **Vehicle Homologation:** All new vehicles sold in India (post **AIS-145 / AIS-096** standards) must comply with frontal airbag fitment; this governs manufacturer design choices.
- **Insurance & Liability:** Crash data recorders built into modern airbag ECUs (the "Event Data Recorder" or **EDR**) are now admissible forensic evidence.
- **Pyrotechnic Chemistry:** Airbag inflator design is the largest non-military consumer of **sodium azide** globally, prompting research into **non-azide propellants** (e.g., guanidine nitrate, 5-aminotetrazole) for environmental reasons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sodium Azide Propellant Mass Required for a Standard Driver Airbag

**Problem Statement:**
A driver airbag requires a cushion volume of **$V = 60$ litres** to be filled with nitrogen at a pressure of **$P = 1.05$ bar** and a final temperature of **$T = 350$ K**. Assume the inflator conversion efficiency is **$\eta = 0.85$** (15 % losses to side reactions and incomplete combustion). Using the composite reaction

$$10 \, NaN_3 + 2 \, KNO_3 \rightarrow 5 \, Na_2O + K_2O + 16 \, N_2$$

calculate the mass of sodium azide propellant required.

**Given Data:**
- Molar mass of $NaN_3$: $M_{NaN_3} = 23 + 3(14) = 65$ g/mol
- Molar mass of $N_2$: $M_{N_2} = 28$ g/mol
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- Conversion efficiency: $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Convert Volume to SI Units:**
The cushion volume in cubic metres is

$$V = 60 \, \text{L} \times 10^{-3} = 0.060 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

The pressure in Pascals is

$$P = 1.05 \, \text{bar} \times 10^{5} = 1.05 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Ideal Gas Equation to Find Moles of N₂ Needed:**

Using $PV = nRT$, solve for $n$:

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{needed}} = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$$

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{needed}} = \dfrac{(1.05 \times 10^{5})(0.060)}{(8.314)(350)}$$

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{needed}} = \dfrac{6300}{2909.9}$$

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{needed}} = 2.165 \, \text{moles of } N_2$$

**Step 3 — Account for Conversion Efficiency:**
Because only $\eta = 85\%$ of the propellant actually yields nitrogen, the **actual** moles of $N_2$ that must be produced by the stoichiometric reaction is

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{stoich}} = \dfrac{n_{N_2, \, \text{needed}}}{\eta}$$

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{stoich}} = \dfrac{2.165}{0.85}$$

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{stoich}} = 2.547 \, \text{moles of } N_2 \text{ (stoichiometric)}$$

**Step 4 — Use Stoichiometric Ratios to Find Moles of NaN₃:**
From the balanced equation, **16 moles of $N_2$** are produced from **10 moles of $NaN_3$**. Therefore, the mole ratio is

$$\dfrac{n_{NaN_3}}{n_{N_2}} = \dfrac{10}{16} = \dfrac{5}{8}$$

$$n_{NaN_3} = 2.547 \times \dfrac{10}{16}$$

$$n_{NaN_3} = 2.547 \times 0.625$$

$$n_{NaN_3} = 1.592 \, \text{moles of } NaN_3$$

**Step 5 — Convert Moles to Mass:**

$$m_{NaN_3} = n_{NaN_3} \times M_{NaN_3}$$

$$m_{NaN_3} = 1.592 \, \text{mol} \times 65 \, \text{g/mol}$$

$$m_{NaN_3} = 103.5 \, \text{grams}$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **103.5 grams** of sodium azide is required to fill a 60-litre driver airbag under the specified conditions. (Real-world driver airbags typically use 50–80 g — the extra mass above our calculation compensates for higher filter losses and gas cooling in the actual hardware.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Deployment Time Budget Analysis

The total deployment time is the **sum of three sequential delays**:

$$t_{total} = t_{sense} + t_{fire} + t_{inflate}$$

**Step 1 — Sensing Delay:**
The SRS ECU samples at $f_s = 1000$ Hz. The maximum latency to detect and confirm a crash event (with built-in debounce logic to prevent false deployment) is

$$t_{sense} = 2 \times \dfrac{1}{f_s} = 2 \times 1 \, \text{ms} = 2 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Firing Delay:**
Once triggered, the squib needs approximately **2 ms** of current above the firing threshold to ignite the initiator charge:

$$t_{fire} = 2 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Inflation Delay:**
The cushion reaches full volume when the gas generation rate equals the vent-out rate. Empirically:

$$t_{inflate} = 30 \, \text{ms}$$

**Step 4 — Total Time Budget:**

$$t_{total} = 2 + 2 + 30 = 34 \, \text{ms}$$

This is well within the KTU-syllabus target window of **30–50 ms**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Airbag Sensor Placement Map

| Sensor Type | Location | Crash Type Detected | Signal Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front (Primary) Accelerometer | Radiator support / front cross-member | Frontal | > 4 g |
| Front (Satellite) Accelerometer | Front bumper reinforcement | Frontal (early detection) | > 2 g (arming threshold) |
| Side (Pressure) Sensor | Inside door cavity | Side impact | ΔP > 12 kPa in < 5 ms |
| Side (Acceleration) Sensor | B-pillar base | Side impact | > 6 g lateral |
| C-pillar Satellite Sensor | Rear quarter panel | Side / rollover | > 5 g |
| Rollover Sensor | Center of gravity (ECU) | Rollover | Tilt angle > 50° at rate > 3°/s |

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Airbag Types Comparison

| Airbag Type | Storage Location | Inflation Volume | Deployment Angle | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Driver Frontal | Steering wheel hub | 35–60 L | ±30° from forward axis | Highest exam frequency |
| Passenger Frontal | Dashboard top | 60–160 L | ±30° from forward axis | Dual-stage inflator |
| Side Torso | Seat bolster / door | 12–30 L | Lateral (90° offset) | Pressure sensor triggered |
| Side Curtain | Roof rail | 35–70 L (very long) | Lateral | Stays inflated 5–7 s |
| Knee | Lower dashboard | 15–25 L | Forward / downward | Prevents femur fracture |
| Far-Side (Center) | Between front seats | 30–50 L | Lateral | Prevents occupant-to-occupant impact |
| Rear Seat Side | Rear door | 12–25 L | Lateral | Premium segment only |
| Pedestrian (External) | Behind bumper cover | 80–120 L | Upward & outward | Bonnet-lift function |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of an Airbag System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Crash Sensor Front Left] --> B1[SRS ECU Microcontroller]
    A2[Crash Sensor Front Right] --> B1
    A3[Side Pressure Sensor Door L] --> B1
    A4[Side Pressure Sensor Door R] --> B1
    A5[Rollover Angular Rate Sensor] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{Decision Algorithm Crash Severity Index}
    C1 -- Above Threshold --> D1[Firing Loop Driver Stage]
    C1 -- Below Threshold --> E1[Standby Monitoring Mode]
    D1 --> F1[Driver Airbag Squib]
    D1 --> F2[Passenger Airbag Squib]
    D1 --> F3[Side Airbag Squib L]
    D1 --> F4[Side Airbag Squib R]
    D1 --> F5[Curtain Airbag Squib L]
    D1 --> F6[Curtain Airbag Squib R]
    F1 --> G1[NaN3 Propellant Ignition]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G2[Gas Generant Combustion]
    F4 --> G2
    F5 --> G3[Cold Gas Hybrid Inflator]
    F6 --> G3
    G1 --> H1[N2 Gas Through Filter]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Nylon Cushion Inflation 30 to 50 ms]
    I1 --> J1[Vent Hole Controlled Deflation]
    B1 --> K1[EDR Event Data Recorder Storage]
    B1 --> L1[SRS Warning Lamp Continuous ON if DTC Set]
```

### 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Airbag Deployment Timing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[T equals 0 ms Crash Begins] --> T1
    T1[T equals 2 ms ECU Detects Threshold Exceeded] --> T2
    T2[T equals 4 ms Squib Current Pulse Initiated] --> T3
    T3[T equals 6 ms Initiator Charge Ignites] --> T4
    T4[T equals 12 ms Propellant Burning Peak Rate] --> T5
    T5[T equals 34 ms Cushion Fully Inflated] --> T6
    T6[T equals 50 ms Cushion Begins Significant Deflation] --> T7
    T7[T equals 150 ms Cushion Fully Vented Occupant Contact Phase]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Inflator Internal Cross-Section (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Squib Initiator ZrKClO4] --> P2[Booster Charge BNCP]
    P2 --> P3[Main Propellant NaN3 Pellets]
    P3 --> P4[Combustion Chamber High Temp 600 C]
    P4 --> P5[Ceramic Filter Basket Na2O Capture]
    P5 --> P6[Cooling Chamber Temperature Drop to 80 C]
    P6 --> P7[Cushion Entry Port Exit Velocity 300 m per s]
    P7 --> P8[Folded Nylon Cushion Storage]
    P8 --> P9[Tear Seam Cover Burst Open]
```

### 4.4 Deployment Decision Logic State Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[State Normal Operation Monitoring] --> S2{Sensor Input}
    S2 --> S3[State Pre-Arm Front Satellite Above 2 g]
    S3 --> S4{Primary Sensor Above 4 g AND Time Window Below 30 ms}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Return to Normal Monitoring]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[State Fire Command Issued to Squib Driver]
    S6 --> S7[State Squib Current Pulse Active 2 ms]
    S7 --> S8[State DTC Stored and Warning Lamp ON]
    S8 --> S9[State System Locked Service Required]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - Every node ID is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `T0`, `T1`, `P1`, `S3`) — no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone names.
> - All node labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic markdown tags or HTML tables inside double quotes.
> - All structural relationships are rendered as block-level functional architecture flows because the topic involves physical hardware schematics that cannot be physically drawn in Mermaid.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "Supplemental Restraint System (SRS)". Why is the airbag classified as a "supplemental" device and not a "primary" restraint?**

**Model Answer:**
The Supplemental Restraint System (SRS) refers to the integrated set of passive safety components — airbags, seat belt pretensioners, seat belt load limiters, and knee bolsters — that work in conjunction with the three-point seat belt during a crash event.
The airbag is termed *supplemental* (and not primary) because it is designed to **augment** the protection offered by the seat belt. The seat belt is the primary restraint and is mandatory; the airbag is a **secondary** device that activates only in severe crashes to reduce the risk of head and chest injury. Without the seat belt, an airbag alone is insufficient and can even cause fatal injuries due to the explosive deployment force.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List the three main components of an airbag module and state the typical inflation time range.**

**Model Answer:**
The three main components of an airbag module are:
1. **Inflator Assembly** — contains the squib (initiator), propellant charge (sodium azide), and gas filter.
2. **Cushion (Airbag)** — woven nylon or polyamide fabric, folded and stored in the steering wheel hub or dashboard.
3. **Tear-Seam Cover / Deployment Door** — a moulded plastic module cover that bursts open along engineered stress lines during deployment.

The typical inflation time range is **30–50 milliseconds** (full inflation) with full deployment cycle (including deflation) completing within **~150 milliseconds**.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of an automotive airbag system. Label all major components and describe the function of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the chemistry of the sodium azide propellant used in airbag inflators. Write the balanced decomposition equation and state two reasons why alternative non-azide propellants are being researched. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
Refer to Section 4.1 block diagram. The major components and their functions are:

- **Crash Sensors (Front & Side):** Convert mechanical deceleration / pressure into an electrical signal.
- **SRS ECU:** Microcontroller-based decision unit; runs the crash-severity algorithm and triggers the firing loop.
- **Squib (Initiator):** Thin resistive wire bridge coated with pyrotechnic compound that ignites when a firing current of > 1 A passes through.
- **Propellant (NaN₃):** Solid gas generant; produces nitrogen on rapid exothermic decomposition.
- **Filter Basket:** Ceramic / metal mesh that traps solid by-products (Na₂O, NaOH).
- **Cushion:** Nylon fabric bag that inflates with N₂ gas.
- **Vent Holes:** Calibrated openings that control deflation.
- **SRS Warning Lamp & EDR:** Diagnostic and crash-data recording interface.

Working sequence: Crash → Sensor signal → ECU decides → Squib fires → Propellant burns → N₂ generated → Filter cleans → Cushion inflates → Vent deflation absorbs occupant energy.

**Model Solution for (b):**
The conventional airbag propellant is **sodium azide (NaN₃)**, an inorganic salt that decomposes exothermically above ~300 °C.

The balanced reaction is:

$$2 \, NaN_3 \rightarrow 2 \, Na + 3 \, N_2 \quad \Delta H \approx -21.7 \, \text{kJ/mol}$$

To convert the toxic metallic sodium by-product into harmless silicates, modern inflators add **SiO₂**:

$$2 \, NaN_3 + SiO_2 \rightarrow Na_2SiO_3 + 3 \, N_2$$

Or the composite reaction using potassium nitrate oxidiser (covered in Section 3.1):

$$10 \, NaN_3 + 2 \, KNO_3 \rightarrow 5 \, Na_2O + K_2O + 16 \, N_2$$

**Two reasons why non-azide propellants are being researched:**
1. **Toxicity & Disposal Hazard:** Sodium azide is highly toxic (oral LD₅₀ ≈ 27 mg/kg in rats) and reacts explosively with copper and lead (used in older vehicle wiring), creating serious recycling and end-of-life vehicle (ELV) disposal problems.
2. **Environmental Persistence:** Azide ion ($N_3^-$) is non-biodegradable and can contaminate groundwater from landfill leachate; replacing it with **5-aminotetrazole** or **guanidine nitrate** produces only non-toxic residues (CO₂, H₂O, N₂).

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **single-stage** and **dual-stage** airbag inflators. Under what crash conditions is each type deployed? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A driver airbag requires **$V = 50$ litres** of nitrogen at a final temperature of **$T = 330$ K** and pressure **$P = 1.0$ bar**. The conversion efficiency of the propellant is **$\eta = 0.80$**. Using the reaction $2 NaN_3 \rightarrow 2 Na + 3 N_2$, calculate the mass of NaN₃ required. Take $M_{NaN_3} = 65$ g/mol and $M_{N_2} = 28$ g/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Single-Stage Inflator | Dual-Stage Inflator |
|---|---|---|
| Number of propellant chambers | One | Two (low + high stage) |
| Deployment strategy | All-or-nothing | Two-step staged deployment |
| Crash condition used | Severe crash (Δv > 25 km/h) | Mild-to-moderate crash (Δv 16–25 km/h) deploys low stage; severe crash fires both stages |
| Occupant risk | Higher risk of out-of-position (OOP) injury | Tailored to crash severity — reduces OOP risk |
| ECU complexity | Simple firing logic | Requires pre-crash sensor data (radar/camera) to pre-arm |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

A **dual-stage inflator** uses two independent propellant chambers ignited with a time offset (typically 5–15 ms apart). The ECU's pre-crash sensing algorithm decides whether to fire stage 1 alone, stage 2 alone, or both — based on occupant classification (seat-pressure sensor), seat-position sensor, and crash severity.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert Units:**

$$V = 50 \times 10^{-3} = 0.050 \, \text{m}^{3}$$

$$P = 1.0 \times 10^{5} = 1.0 \times 10^{5} \, \text{Pa}$$

$$T = 330 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of N₂ Required:**

$$n_{N_2} = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{(1.0 \times 10^{5})(0.050)}{(8.314)(330)}$$

$$n_{N_2} = \dfrac{5000}{2743.6} = 1.823 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Account for Efficiency:**

$$n_{N_2, \, \text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1.823}{0.80} = 2.279 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 4 — Stoichiometric Ratio:**
From $2 NaN_3 \rightarrow 3 N_2$:

$$n_{NaN_3} = 2.279 \times \dfrac{2}{3} = 1.519 \, \text{mol}$$

**Step 5 — Mass:**

$$m_{NaN_3} = 1.519 \times 65 = 98.8 \, \text{grams}$$

**Answer:** Approximately **98.8 grams** of sodium azide is required.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes on Airbag Questions:**
> - **Confusing primary vs. supplemental restraint:** Many students write "airbag is the primary safety device" — this is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Always state that the **seat belt is primary** and the airbag is **supplemental**.
> - **Skipping the time-budget:** When asked "explain how an airbag works", the examiner allocates 2 marks specifically for the timing sequence. Writing "it inflates quickly" without mentioning the **30–50 ms** window loses those marks.
> - **Wrong units on NaN₃ calculation:** Students frequently forget to convert litres → m³ or bar → Pa. This leads to an answer off by a factor of 1000 or 100000. Always write units in every step.
> - **Drawing a physical sketch instead of a block diagram:** When the question says "neat diagram", examiners expect a **labelled block diagram** showing sensor → ECU → squib → inflator → cushion. A rough pictorial sketch with no arrows or labels attracts only partial credit.
> - **Forgetting the EDR & DTC step:** Post-crash diagnostics (warning lamp on, DTC stored, system locked) is a 1-mark item many students miss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Airbag = Supplemental (secondary) restraint**, NEVER a substitute for the seat belt.
- **Inflation time = 30–50 ms**; total deployment cycle ≈ 150 ms.
- **Deployment threshold = 4–5 g frontal**, **6–8 g side**.
- **Propellant = sodium azide (NaN₃)** → produces N₂ gas; toxic, being replaced by non-azide alternatives.
- **Sensors:** Front accelerometer (radiator support), side pressure sensor (door cavity), satellite sensors, rollover sensor.
- **Squib firing current = ~1.2 to 2.2 A** for > 2 ms.
- **Components:** Crash sensor → ECU → Squib → Propellant → Filter → Cushion → Vent holes.
- **Single-stage** inflator: all-or-nothing. **Dual-stage** inflator: tailored deployment based on crash severity and occupant position.
- **Cushion fabric = woven nylon** (polyamide), folded accordion-style, stored in steering wheel hub or dashboard.
- **Vent holes** (typically two ~25 mm) are essential — they control the deflation rate and energy absorption.
- **EDR (Event Data Recorder):** Stores pre-crash velocity, throttle %, brake %, seat-belt status, and Δv for forensic analysis.
- **SRS warning lamp:** Illuminates at every ignition-ON for ~3–5 s bulb-check; persistent ON indicates a DTC.
- **KTU Hot Topics for 2024 Scheme:** Dual-stage inflator logic, occupant classification systems, airbag pyrotechnic chemistry, and ISOFIX-compatible child-seat detection that automatically **suppresses** the passenger airbag.
- **Diagnostic codes:** Common SRS DTCs include B0020 (driver squib low resistance), B0041 (side airbag open circuit), and B0081 (front sensor malfunction).
- **Replacement rule:** All deployed airbags + crash sensors must be replaced; the SRS ECU is also replaced if it has triggered.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision Avoidance Radar Warning System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collision Avoidance Radar Warning System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Collision Avoidance Radar Warning System (CARWS)** is an *Active Safety* / *Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)* that uses **RAdio Detection And Ranging (RADAR)** electromagnetic waves — typically in the millimeter-wave (mmWave) automotive band of **24 GHz, 76–77 GHz, or 77–81 GHz** — to detect, classify, and track surrounding obstacles (vehicles, pedestrians, two-wheelers) and continuously compute the **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**, **Relative Velocity**, and **Closing Range** of the host vehicle with respect to a potential threat target. When TTC falls below a programmable safety threshold, the ECU actuates graduated warnings — visual (HUD/cluster), haptic (seat/steering vibration), and acoustic — and, where integrated with chassis controls, triggers Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAUT862)**, the Collision Avoidance Radar Warning System is treated as a sub-system of *Automotive Sensor Fusion & ADAS*, where radar is the **primary long-range (>150 m) perception sensor**, supplemented by camera (classification) and LiDAR (redundancy).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Echolocation of Bats" Analogy**
> Imagine driving through dense fog on NH-66 at night. Your eyes (cameras) fail in poor visibility. The radar acts exactly like a **bat's echolocation** — it shouts a tiny, invisible radio "ping" forward, listens carefully for the echo bouncing back from the car in front, and from the *time taken* and *frequency shift* of the echo, deduces (i) how far the obstacle is, and (ii) how fast it (or you) is moving. If the echo comes back too soon, the system screams "BRAKE!". The clever part: radio waves pass through rain, fog, dust, and darkness almost unaffected, making radar the **all-weather eye** of a modern car.

### 1.3 System-Level Function

| Function | Sensor Output | Vehicle Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Detect** target presence | Range, angle, RCS | Pop-up warning |
| **Classify** target type | Doppler, RCS, height | Filter clutter / pedestrians |
| **Track** target kinematics | Velocity, acceleration | Smoothed trajectory |
| **Warn** driver | TTC < threshold | Visual + haptic + audio |
| **Act** autonomously (optional) | TTC < critical | AEB / seatbelt pretensioner |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Physics of Automotive Radar

Automotive collision avoidance radar is almost universally implemented as a **Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW)** radar (sometimes called LFMCW or CW-FM). Unlike pulsed radar used in military/Air-Traffic-Control, FMCW simultaneously:

1. Transmits a continuously swept RF chirp.
2. Receives the time-delayed, Doppler-shifted echo.
3. Mixes (homodynes) the two to extract a low-frequency **Intermediate Frequency (IF) / beat signal**.

From this IF signal, a single Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) chain extracts **range**; a second FFT across successive chirps extracts **velocity**; and a third across the antenna array extracts **angle (azimuth)** — producing the canonical **Range-Doppler-Azimuth data cube** central to automotive radar signal processing.

### 2.2 Operational Block Flow

- **Step 1 — Chirp Generation:** A voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO) sweeps frequency linearly across a band of bandwidth $B$ (typically $\mathbf{500\ MHz}$ at 77 GHz) in sweep time $T_{\text{sweep}}$.
- **Step 2 — Transmission & Echo:** A transmit antenna radiates the chirp; a target at range $R$ reflects a portion of the energy back, captured by the receive antenna after round-trip delay $\tau = \frac{2R}{c}$.
- **Step 3 — Mixer (Dechirp):** The received echo is mixed with the currently transmitted chirp. The difference (beat) signal is a sinusoid at frequency $f_b = K \cdot \tau$, where $K = \frac{B}{T_{\text{sweep}}}$ is the chirp slope.
- **Step 4 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion:** The IF beat is low-pass filtered and sampled by an ADC.
- **Step 5 — Range FFT:** An $N_{\text{FFT}}$-point FFT on each chirp produces a range profile.
- **Step 6 — Doppler FFT:** A second FFT across the $M$ chirps of a frame produces a Range-Doppler map.
- **Step 7 — CFAR Detection:** Constant False Alarm Rate algorithm scans each cell against local noise statistics to declare detections.
- **Step 8 — Angle Estimation:** Phase differences across MIMO antenna channels yield azimuth.
- **Step 9 — Tracking & TTC:** Kalman filter tracks each detection; TTC $=\frac{R}{\dot{R}_{\text{rel}}}$ is computed continuously.

### 2.3 Why FMCW (and not pulsed) for Automotive?

- **High range resolution** $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ achievable with modest instantaneous bandwidth.
- **Low peak power** (mW levels) — FCC/ETSI eye-safe limits are easily met.
- **Simultaneous range + velocity** from a single chirp frame.
- **Robust against mutual interference** when paired with random chirp sequences.
- **CMOS-compatible MMIC** — Infineon RXS816x, NXP SAF85xx, TI AWR2944 — all integrate VCO, mixer, ADC, and MCU in a single package.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every governing equation** required for KTU ESE problems on automotive FMCW radar. Memorize the symbols and units — valuation key questions test the ability to substitute and compute.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Round-trip time delay | $\tau = \dfrac{2R}{c}$ | $R$: range [m]; $c = \mathbf{3\times10^8\ m/s}$ |
| 2 | Beat frequency (range) | $f_b = K\,\tau = \dfrac{2R\,B}{c\,T_{\text{sweep}}}$ | $K$: chirp slope [Hz/s]; $B$: bandwidth [Hz] |
| 3 | Doppler frequency | $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | $v_r$: relative radial velocity [m/s]; $\lambda$: wavelength [m] |
| 4 | Range resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | $B$: swept bandwidth |
| 5 | Velocity resolution | $\Delta v = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 M\, T_{\text{PRI}}}$ | $M$: # chirps per frame; $T_{\text{PRI}}$: pulse repetition interval |
| 6 | Maximum unambiguous velocity | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{\lambda}{4 T_{\text{PRI}}}$ | — |
| 7 | Maximum unambiguous range | $R_{\max} = \dfrac{c\, T_{\text{PRI}}}{2}$ | — |
| 8 | Radar range equation | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t\, G_t\, G_r\, \lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4\, L}$ | $P_t$: TX power [W]; $G_t,G_r$: antenna gains; $\sigma$: RCS [m²]; $L$: system losses |
| 9 | Max detectable range | $R_{\max} = \left[\dfrac{P_t\, G^2 \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 k T_0 B_n F\, \text{SNR}_{\min}}\right]^{\!\!1/4}$ | $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K; $T_0 = 290$ K; $B_n$: noise BW; $F$: noise figure |
| 10 | Time-To-Collision | $\text{TTC} = \dfrac{R}{\dot{R}_{\text{rel}}}$ | [seconds] |
| 11 | Azimuth (MIMO) | $\Delta\varphi = \dfrac{2\pi d \sin\theta}{\lambda}$ | $d$: virtual array spacing; $\theta$: azimuth |
| 12 | Antenna angular resolution | $\Delta\theta \approx \dfrac{\lambda}{N_a d \cos\theta}$ | $N_a$: effective aperture elements |

> [!NOTE]
> For **77 GHz** radar, $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3\times10^8}{77\times10^9} \approx \mathbf{3.9\ mm}$. A 4-GHz swept bandwidth gives $\Delta R \approx \mathbf{3.75\ cm}$ — more than enough to resolve two adjacent vehicles on a 4-lane highway.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Beyond passenger cars, FMCW collision avoidance radar is mandated in:
- **UNECE Regulation No. 152** — AEB for cars and light commercial vehicles (Europe).
- **NHTSA C-NCAP 2026** — Forward Collision Warning + AEB scoring (USA/India).
- **AIS-184 / Bharat NCAP** — Indian automotive safety standard for ADAS features.

It is also the cornerstone of **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC)**, **Blind Spot Detection (BSD)**, **Rear Cross Traffic Alert (RCTA)**, **Lane Change Assist (LCA)**, and **Front Cross Traffic Assist (FCTA)** — all of which reuse the same 77 GHz MMIC with different antenna patterns and ECU software.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Beat Frequency and Range Extraction

**Given:** A linear FMCW chirp sweeps from $f_0$ to $f_0 + B$ over sweep time $T_{\text{sweep}}$. Chirp slope $K = \dfrac{B}{T_{\text{sweep}}}$.

**Step 1 — Transmitted instantaneous phase:**
The transmitted signal $s_t(t)$ for one chirp has instantaneous frequency $f_t(t) = f_0 + K\,t$ for $0 \le t \le T_{\text{sweep}}$. The phase is the integral of angular frequency:

$$\phi_t(t) = 2\pi \!\int_0^{t} f_t(u)\,du = 2\pi f_0 t + \pi K t^2$$

**Step 2 — Received signal is the transmit signal delayed by $\tau = 2R/c$:**

$$f_r(t) = f_t(t-\tau) = f_0 + K(t-\tau)$$

$$\phi_r(t) = 2\pi f_0(t-\tau) + \pi K (t-\tau)^2$$

**Step 3 — Mixer subtracts the two phases, yielding IF beat:**

$$\phi_b(t) = \phi_t(t) - \phi_r(t) = 2\pi f_0 \tau + 2\pi K \tau\, t - \pi K \tau^2$$

Since $\tau^2$ is negligible ($\sim 10^{-15}$ s² for $R<200$ m), the dominant term is linear in $t$:

$$\phi_b(t) \approx 2\pi K \tau\, t + \text{const.}$$

This is a sinusoid of constant frequency:

$$f_b = K\,\tau = \frac{B}{T_{\text{sweep}}}\cdot\frac{2R}{c}$$

Solving for range:

$$\boxed{\,R = \frac{f_b\, c\, T_{\text{sweep}}}{2B}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration (KTU numerical favorite):**
A 77 GHz radar uses $B = 600$ MHz, $T_{\text{sweep}} = 40$ μs. For a target at $R = 75$ m:

$$f_b = \frac{600\times10^6 \cdot 2 \cdot 75}{3\times10^8 \cdot 40\times10^{-6}} = \frac{9\times10^{10}}{1.2\times10^{4}} = 7.5\times10^{6}\ \text{Hz} = 7.5\ \text{MHz}$$

This IF frequency is trivially sampled by an automotive-grade ADC — a key reason FMCW dominates pulsed architectures at mmWave.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radar Range Equation (Power Link Budget)

**Starting from isotropic radiation, the transmit power flux density at range $R$ is:**

$$S_t = \frac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 1 — Gain of the transmit antenna concentrates flux:**

$$S_t^{\text{gain}} = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Target intercepts power proportional to its Radar Cross Section $\sigma$:**

$$P_{\text{intercepted}} = S_t^{\text{gain}} \cdot \sigma = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 3 — Re-radiation isotropically; flux density back at receive antenna:**

$$S_r = \frac{P_{\text{intercepted}}}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{P_t G_t \sigma}{(4\pi R^2)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Receive antenna effective aperture $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ captures the echo:**

$$P_r = S_r \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4}$$

Including system losses $L$:

$$\boxed{\,P_r = \frac{P_t\, G_t\, G_r\, \lambda^2\,\sigma}{(4\pi)^3\, R^4\, L}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the maximum detection range, setting $P_r = k T_0 B_n F \cdot \text{SNR}_{\min}$:**

$$R_{\max} = \left[\frac{P_t\, G_t\, G_r\, \lambda^2\, \sigma}{(4\pi)^3\, k T_0 B_n F\, \text{SNR}_{\min}}\right]^{\!1/4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fourth-power dependence** on $R$ is what makes automotive radar so challenging: doubling range requires **16× more transmit power** or **16× larger RCS**, which is why mmWave (small $\lambda$, large $B$) is essential.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Doppler / Velocity Extraction

For a target moving with radial velocity $v_r$ relative to the radar, the round-trip delay changes with time. Differentiating the instantaneous phase of the beat signal gives the Doppler shift on the IF:

$$f_d = \frac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$$

**Step 1 — Sign convention:** Positive $v_r$ (target receding) lowers the beat frequency; approaching target raises it.

**Step 2 — Range-Doppler Ambiguity:** Two targets at different $R$ but same $f_d$ appear in the same Doppler bin but at different range bins. This is why a **2-D Range-Doppler FFT** is used.

**Step 3 — Velocity from FFT:** If $M$ chirps are transmitted per frame with PRI $T_{\text{PRI}}$, the Doppler axis is sampled at frequency spacing $\dfrac{1}{M T_{\text{PRI}}}$. Substituting:

$$\Delta v = \frac{\lambda}{2 M T_{\text{PRI}}}$$

For $\lambda = 3.9$ mm, $M = 128$, $T_{\text{PRI}} = 40\ \mu\text{s}$:

$$\Delta v = \frac{3.9\times10^{-3}}{2\cdot 128\cdot 40\times10^{-6}} = 0.38\ \text{m/s} \approx 1.37\ \text{km/h}$$

This is the velocity resolution — easily sufficient to distinguish braking vs. cruising traffic.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TTC Computation & Warning Logic

The ECU fuses **range $R$** (from beat frequency) and **relative velocity $v_r$** (from Doppler) into a scalar TTC:

$$\text{TTC}(t) = \frac{R(t)}{v_r(t)}$$

The warning manager uses a **two-tier threshold** policy (industry-standard Mobileye/EyeQ-like logic):

| TTC Regime | Driver Warning Level | HMI Output | Optional Actuator |
|---|---|---|---|
| TTC $\in [2.6,\ 4.0)$ s | **Early** advisory | Yellow cluster lamp | None |
| TTC $\in [1.5,\ 2.6)$ s | **Imminent** | Red HUD + chime | Brake pre-fill |
| TTC $\in [0.6,\ 1.5)$ s | **Critical** | Haptic seat + loud tone | AEB partial |
| TTC $< 0.6$ s | **Crash imminent** | All of above + pretensioner | **AEB full** |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"A vehicle approaching a stationary object at 90 km/h has measured range 50 m. Compute the TTC."* Use $v_r = 25$ m/s (90/3.6), $R = 50$ m → TTC $= 50/25 = 2$ s → **imminent warning**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram (Mermaid — Validated Syntax)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodePower[Vehicle 12V Battery] --> nodeECU[ADAS Domain ECU]
    nodeECU --> nodeMMIC[77 GHz FMCW MMIC Transceiver]
    nodeMMIC --> nodeVCO[Chirp Synthesizer VCO]
    nodeVCO --> nodeTX[Transmit Antenna Array MIMO]
    nodeTX --> nodeTarget[Target Vehicle Pedestrian]
    nodeTarget --> nodeRX[Receive Antenna Array MIMO]
    nodeRX --> nodeLNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    nodeLNA --> nodeMixer[Down Conversion Mixer]
    nodeVCO --> nodeMixer
    nodeMixer --> nodeADC[High Speed ADC]
    nodeADC --> nodeDSP[Range Doppler FFT Processor]
    nodeDSP --> nodeCFAR[CFAR Detector]
    nodeCFAR --> nodeTrack[Kalman Filter Tracker]
    nodeTrack --> nodeTTC[TTC Calculator]
    nodeTTC --> nodeHMI[Warning HMI Cluster HUD]
    nodeTTC --> nodeAEB[AEB Actuator Interface]
    nodeHMI --> nodeDriver[Driver]
    nodeAEB --> nodeBrake[Brake Booster ESC]
```

### 4.2 FMCW Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeChirp[Linear Chirp TX] --> nodeEcho[Time Delayed Echo]
    nodeEcho --> nodeDechirp[Dechirp Mixer]
    nodeChirp --> nodeDechirp
    nodeDechirp --> nodeIF[IF Beat Signal]
    nodeIF --> nodeADCS[ADC Sampling]
    nodeADCS --> nodeRFFT[Range FFT Per Chirp]
    nodeRFFT --> nodeRMap[Range Profile Map]
    nodeRMap --> nodeDFFT[Doppler FFT Across Chirps]
    nodeDFFT --> nodeRDMap[Range Doppler Map]
    nodeRDMap --> nodeCFAR2[CA CFAR Detection]
    nodeCFAR2 --> nodeAOAF[Angle of Arrival FFT]
    nodeAOAF --> nodeRDA[RDA Cube]
    nodeRDA --> nodeCluster[DBSCAN Clustering]
    nodeCluster --> nodeAssign[Track Assignment]
    nodeAssign --> nodeOut[Object List With Range Velocity Angle]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Sensor Fusion Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeRadF[Front 77 GHz Radar] --> nodeFusion[Central Sensor Fusion ECU]
    nodeCamF[Front Forward Camera] --> nodeFusion
    nodeLidF[Optional Front LiDAR] --> nodeFusion
    nodeRadR[Rear Corner Radars BSD] --> nodeFusion
    nodeCamS[Surround View Cameras] --> nodeFusion
    nodeUltr[Ultrasonic Park Assist] --> nodeFusion
    nodeFusion --> nodeACC[Adaptive Cruise Controller]
    nodeFusion --> nodeFCW[Forward Collision Warning]
    nodeFusion --> nodeAEBS[Automatic Emergency Braking]
    nodeFusion --> nodeLKA[Lane Keep Assist]
    nodeFusion --> nodeHMI2[Central HMI Display]
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask students to label each block in the FMCW pipeline. The two mandatory labels are **(a) Dechirp / Homodyne Mixer** and **(b) Range-Doppler Map** — these are the heart of mmWave radar and carry the highest marks in board valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set B]**
*Define the term "Time-To-Collision (TTC)" as used in automotive collision avoidance radar. State the formula and explain its significance in issuing driver warnings.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Time-To-Collision is the estimated time, in seconds, before the host vehicle collides with a detected forward target, assuming current relative velocity remains constant. It is computed as:

$$\text{TTC} = \frac{R}{v_r}$$

where $R$ is the measured range (m) and $v_r$ is the closing relative velocity (m/s). The radar ECU continuously compares TTC against predefined thresholds (e.g., 2.6 s for early warning, 0.6 s for AEB activation) to issue graduated visual, haptic, and acoustic warnings and to command autonomous braking. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for threshold explanation.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Equivalent]**
*Mention any three advantages of using a 77 GHz millimeter-wave FMCW radar over a 24 GHz radar for collision avoidance applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Smaller wavelength (≈3.9 mm)** → physically compact antennas, easy to fit behind the bumper badge, multiple-element MIMO arrays on a small PCB.
2. **Wider available bandwidth (up to 4 GHz in 77–81 GHz)** → finer range resolution $\Delta R = c/(2B) \approx 3.75$ cm, allowing separation of two adjacent vehicles.
3. **Higher Doppler sensitivity** for the same relative velocity, improving pedestrian and small-obstacle classification. **(3 × 1 mark.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Module Internal Choice is mandatory. **Question A** emphasizes *theory + derivation*; **Question B** emphasizes *numerical problem-solving*. Students answer either one.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Theory + Design

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an automotive FMCW Collision Avoidance Radar Warning System. Label each block and describe the function of the dechirp mixer and Range-Doppler FFT processor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the FMCW beat-frequency expression and the radar range equation. Discuss why automotive radar operates in the 76–81 GHz band rather than 24 GHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Working (7 marks):**
The system comprises: chirp synthesizer (VCO) → power amplifier → transmit antenna → free-space propagation → target reflection → receive antenna (with MIMO array) → LNA → dechirp mixer → low-pass IF filter → ADC → range FFT processor → Doppler FFT processor → CFAR detector → tracker → TTC calculator → warning/actuator stage.

- **Dechirp Mixer (2 marks):** mixes the received echo with a sample of the currently transmitted chirp; because the echo is delayed by $\tau = 2R/c$, the mixer output is a sinusoid whose frequency is proportional to range ($f_b = K \tau$). This single-frequency IF is far easier to digitize than the original 77 GHz signal.
- **Range-Doppler FFT (2 marks):** the first FFT performed across ADC samples within one chirp yields the range profile (peak position = target range). A second FFT across successive chirps at the same range bin yields the Doppler (velocity) profile. The resulting **Range-Doppler map** is the canonical output of every automotive radar.
- **MIMO and angle (1 mark)**: A third FFT across virtual array channels yields azimuth.
- **Tracking and warning (2 marks)**: Detections are clustered, assigned to persistent tracks via a Kalman filter, and TTC is computed each cycle.

**(b) Derivation and Band Selection (7 marks):**
- **Beat frequency derivation (3 marks):** as shown in §3.1, the linear chirp yields IF beat $f_b = \dfrac{2 R B}{c\, T_{\text{sweep}}}$.
- **Radar range equation (3 marks):** as shown in §3.2, $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L}$.
- **77 vs 24 GHz (1 mark):** 77 GHz offers ~5× wider contiguous bandwidth (5 GHz vs 250 MHz in legacy 24 GHz ISM), so range resolution is ≈20× finer; smaller wavelength allows compact, high-gain, multi-element antennas; less interference from Wi-Fi/Bluetooth; and better Doppler sensitivity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Numerical

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Set A]**
**(a)** A 77 GHz FMCW automotive radar has sweep bandwidth $B = 500$ MHz, sweep time $T_{\text{sweep}} = 50\ \mu\text{s}$. Compute (i) the range resolution, (ii) the beat frequency for a target at $R = 100$ m. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the host vehicle closes on a stationary target at $v = 72$ km/h and the radar reports range $R = 60$ m, compute the TTC and identify the warning regime. If a second stationary target at 80 m is detected with the same closing velocity, what is its TTC and which target will trigger the warning first? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (7 marks):**
(i) Range resolution:

$$\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B} = \frac{3\times 10^8}{2 \times 500\times 10^6} = 0.30\ \text{m} = 30\ \text{cm}$$

(ii) Beat frequency:

$$f_b = \frac{2 R B}{c\, T_{\text{sweep}}} = \frac{2 \times 100 \times 500\times 10^6}{3\times 10^8 \times 50\times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_b = \frac{10^{11}}{1.5\times 10^{4}} = 6.667\times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz} \approx 6.67\ \text{MHz}$$

**(b) (7 marks):**
Convert closing velocity: $v = 72/3.6 = 20$ m/s.

**Target 1** at $R_1 = 60$ m:

$$\text{TTC}_1 = \frac{60}{20} = 3.0\ \text{s} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Early advisory regime}$$

**Target 2** at $R_2 = 80$ m:

$$\text{TTC}_2 = \frac{80}{20} = 4.0\ \text{s} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Below early advisory (or just at boundary)}$$

**Comparison (3 marks):** Since TTC$_1$ = 3.0 s is **smaller** than TTC$_2$ = 4.0 s, the radar ECU will issue the warning based on **Target 1**. The ECU uses the **most imminent** target (minimum TTC), not the nearest, for warning triggering — this is a critical KTU conceptual point.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often confuse *range* with *TTC*. The nearest target is **not** always the most dangerous — a fast-closing distant vehicle can have a smaller TTC than a slow-closing near one.
> 2. Units: forgetting to convert km/h to m/s loses 1 mark immediately.
> 3. Not stating the warning regime from the standard threshold table (4.0 / 2.6 / 1.5 / 0.6 s) costs 1 mark.
> 4. In derivation questions, omitting the "low $\tau^2$ approximation" step loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Collision Avoidance Radar Warning System**

- **System purpose:** all-weather, long-range, velocity-aware obstacle detection for ADAS.
- **Dominant technology:** FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) at **77 GHz** (also 24 GHz and 79 GHz UWB).
- **Three core equations to memorize:**
  - Beat frequency: $f_b = \dfrac{2 R B}{c\, T_{\text{sweep}}}$
  - Doppler: $f_d = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$
  - Range equation: $P_r = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 \sigma}{(4\pi)^3 R^4 L}$
- **Key resolutions:**
  - Range: $\Delta R = c/(2B)$ — set by **bandwidth**.
  - Velocity: $\Delta v = \lambda/(2 M T_{\text{PRI}})$ — set by **frame duration and number of chirps**.
  - Angle: $\Delta \theta \approx \lambda/(N_a d)$ — set by **MIMO aperture**.
- **Wavelength at 77 GHz:** $\lambda \approx 3.9$ mm; **range resolution at 500 MHz BW:** $\Delta R = 0.30$ m.
- **Speed of light constant:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s; **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- **Dechirp mixer** converts a wide-band mmWave echo into a low-frequency IF sinusoid proportional to range.
- **Range-Doppler map** is the canonical 2-D FFT output; peak = target, axes = range & velocity.
- **TTC = R / v_r** — minimum TTC across all tracked objects drives the warning.
- **Threshold ladder:** 4.0 s (advisory) → 2.6 s (early) → 1.5 s (imminent) → 0.6 s (AEB).
- **TTC, not range, is the warning metric.**
- **77 GHz over 24 GHz:** smaller $\lambda$, wider bandwidth, finer resolution, compact antennas.
- **Automotive standards:** UNECE R152 (AEB), AIS-184 / Bharat NCAP.
- **Sensor fusion partners:** forward camera (classification), LiDAR (redundancy), ultrasonics (parking).
- **ECU outputs:** HMI (cluster/HUD/haptic), AEB actuator via CAN-FD or automotive Ethernet.
- **Common IC families:** Infineon RXS816x, NXP SAF85xx, TI AWR2944, Arbe Phoenix.

> [!WARNING]
> **Most-Lost Marks in Board Exams:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert km/h → m/s in TTC numerics (−1 mark).
> 2. Skipping units in final answers (−0.5 to −1 mark).
> 3. Confusing range with TTC when classifying warning regimes (−1 mark).
> 4. Drawing the dechirp mixer *after* the ADC instead of *before* (−1 mark).
> 5. Stating Doppler formula without the factor of 2 (round-trip) (−1 mark).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tyre Pressure Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Tyre Pressure Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS)** — An electronic automotive safety subsystem mandated by global safety regulations (FMVSS 138 in USA; ECE R64 in Europe; AIS-145 in India) that continuously measures the inflation pressure of pneumatic tyres and provides the driver with a real-time warning when the pressure in any tyre falls below a predefined safety threshold, typically **25 % below the manufacturer's recommended cold inflation pressure (Placard Pressure)**.

The system consists of hardware sensors, an RF transmitter, a central receiver/electronic control unit (ECU), and a Human–Machine Interface (HMI) on the instrument cluster.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **four air-filled balloons** tied to your bicycle. You cannot see inside them. Now imagine a tiny digital weighing scale attached to each balloon, continuously reporting how "full" each balloon is to a small screen on your handlebar. The instant any balloon loses **more than a quarter** of its air, the screen flashes red for that balloon. That is exactly what a TPMS does for your car tyres — it is essentially a **digital, networked pressure gauge** for all four (or five, including the spare) tyres.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cold Inflation Pressure (Placard Pressure):** This is the manufacturer-specified pressure stamped on the vehicle's door-jamb sticker, measured when the tyre is **cold** (i.e., after the vehicle has been parked for at least 3 hours or driven less than **1.6 km** at low speed). All TPMS warning thresholds are referenced against this baseline.

### 1.3 Classification of TPMS (KTU High-Yield Distinction)

| Type | Principle | Hardware |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Direct TPMS (dTPMS)** | Real pressure measurement via in-tyre sensor | Pressure transducer + MCU + RF transmitter + battery |
| **Indirect TPMS (iTPMS)** | Pressure inferred via wheel-speed differential | Reuses **ABS/ESC wheel-speed sensors** + software algorithm |
| **Hybrid TPMS** | Combination of both | Pressure sensors + ABS data |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** **"D = Dedicated sensor; I = Inferred"** — Direct means a dedicated pressure sensor; Indirect means *inferring* pressure from wheel rotation behaviour.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Direct TPMS (dTPMS) — Operational Architecture

A dTPMS sensor module is mounted **inside the tyre**, attached to the valve stem or banded to the wheel rim. Each module contains four key elements:

1. **MEMS Pressure Sensor** — A micro-electro-mechanical piezoresistive or capacitive diaphragm that deflects proportionally to absolute pressure.
2. **Temperature Sensor** — Required to apply temperature compensation (Ideal Gas Law).
3. **8-bit / 16-bit Microcontroller (MCU)** — Samples the sensors, applies calibration, builds the RF data frame.
4. **RF Transmitter + Coin-cell Battery** — Typically a **LiSOCl₂** (Lithium Thionyl Chloride) primary cell rated **3.6 V / 2.4 Ah**, designed for **~10 years** of service life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operating Frequencies:** TPMS transmits in licence-free ISM bands — **315 MHz** (North America / Japan) or **433.92 MHz** (Europe / India / most of Asia). The receiver is typically a superheterodyne or SDR-based unit mounted behind the bumper.

### 2.2 Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) — Underlying Physics

An iTPMS does **not measure pressure**. Instead, it exploits the fact that an **under-inflated tyre has a smaller dynamic rolling radius**. A smaller radius means the wheel must rotate **more times** per kilometre than a fully inflated tyre of the same nominal size. The ABS wheel-speed sensors detect this rotational difference.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Constant | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law (Pressure correction with temperature) | $P_2 = P_1 \cdot \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ | $T$ in **Kelvin (K)**; $P$ in kPa |
| 2 | TPMS Warning Threshold (regulatory) | $P_{warn} \le 0.75 \cdot P_{placard}$ | Pressure drop **> 25 %** triggers warning |
| 3 | Wheel speed vs rolling radius | $v = \omega \cdot r_{dyn}$ | $v$: vehicle speed (m/s), $\omega$: angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 4 | Relative speed difference (iTPMS) | $\Delta\omega\% = \dfrac{\omega_{under} - \omega_{ref}}{\omega_{ref}} \cdot 100$ | Typically calibrated against **0.3 bar** drop |
| 5 | Pressure–Radius relation (linearised) | $r_{dyn} \approx r_0 - k \cdot (P_0 - P)$ | $k$: tyre-specific coefficient (≈ 0.05 mm/kPa) |
| 6 | Centripetal load on TPMS sensor | $F_c = m \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal acceleration can exceed **1500 g** |
| 7 | Battery life estimation | $L_{years} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg}\,(\mu A) \cdot 8760}$ | $C$: cell capacity; $I_{avg}$: average current draw |
| 8 | RF Link Budget (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $d$: wheel-to-receiver distance ≈ 1.5–3 m |
| 9 | Free-space wavelength at 433.92 MHz | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f} = \dfrac{3\times10^8}{433.92\times10^6} \approx 0.691$ m | Used for antenna design |
| 10 | Placard pressure typical range | $P_{placard} = 200$–$250$ kPa (passenger car) | ≈ **30–36 psi** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Pitfall:** In KTU valuation, students often write pressure in **psi** when the question is set in **kPa**, or forget to convert **°C → K** in the Ideal Gas Law. Always convert to SI before substituting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety:** Under-inflation increases braking distance by up to **8 %**, raises rolling resistance (fuel penalty **1–3 %**), and is implicated in over **40 %** of tyre-related accidents on highways.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory in India under **AIS-145** for new passenger vehicles since **2018**.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** OEM data shows proper TPMS use extends average tyre life by **~15 %** through early slow-leak detection.
- **EV Range Optimisation:** In electric vehicles, an extra **1 psi** drop reduces range by approximately **0.3–0.5 %**; TPMS directly supports range maximisation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure–Temperature Compensation (Ideal Gas Law)

The TPMS sensor measures **absolute pressure ($P_{abs}$)** and **temperature ($T$)** at the time of measurement. However, the vehicle's placard pressure is specified at a **reference temperature of 25 °C (298.15 K)**. Because a tyre is a closed, approximately constant-volume system, we can apply Gay-Lussac's Law (special case of the Ideal Gas Law):

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}
$$

We want to find the **"cold-equivalent" pressure** $P_{25}$ that the tyre *would* show at 25 °C, given a live measurement at temperature $T_{live}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{25} &= P_{live} \cdot \dfrac{T_{25}}{T_{live}} \\[4pt]
P_{25} &= P_{live} \cdot \dfrac{298.15}{T_{live}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1

> **Problem (Part B style — 7 marks):** A TPMS sensor in a tyre reads **$P_{live} = 230$ kPa** at a tyre temperature of **55 °C**. The vehicle's placard pressure is **$P_{placard} = 240$ kPa** at 25 °C. Will the TPMS ECU raise a low-pressure warning? Justify using the regulatory 25 % threshold.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{live} &= 55 + 273.15 = 328.15 \text{ K} \\
T_{25} &= 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert live pressure to the cold (25 °C) equivalent.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{25} &= P_{live} \cdot \dfrac{T_{25}}{T_{live}} \\
       &= 230 \text{ kPa} \cdot \dfrac{298.15 \text{ K}}{328.15 \text{ K}} \\
       &= 230 \cdot 0.9086 \\
       &\approx 208.97 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the warning threshold (25 % below placard).**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{warn} &= 0.75 \cdot P_{placard} \\
         &= 0.75 \cdot 240 \\
         &= 180 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision logic.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{25} &\stackrel{?}{\le} P_{warn} \\
208.97 \text{ kPa} &\stackrel{?}{\le} 180 \text{ kPa} \\
208.97 &> 180 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{No warning.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Pressure drop percentage.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P\% &= \dfrac{P_{placard} - P_{25}}{P_{placard}} \cdot 100 \\
          &= \dfrac{240 - 208.97}{240} \cdot 100 \\
          &\approx 12.93 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The cold-equivalent pressure has dropped by only **12.93 %**, which is **less than the 25 % regulatory limit**, so **no TPMS warning is raised**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU valuation:** Examiners award full 7 marks **only** when both the temperature compensation and the regulatory threshold check are explicitly performed. Skipping the Kelvin conversion or skipping the threshold check loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Indirect TPMS Speed Differential Model

In iTPMS, four wheel-speed sensors provide instantaneous angular velocities $\omega_{FL}, \omega_{FR}, \omega_{RL}, \omega_{RR}$. Under normal conditions on a perfectly flat road, all four $\omega$ values are statistically equal. When tyre pressure drops, dynamic rolling radius decreases:

$$
r_{dyn} = r_0 - k(P_0 - P)
$$

where $r_0$ is the nominal radius at placard pressure $P_0$, and $k$ is a tyre-dependent deflation coefficient. Because vehicle speed $v = \omega \cdot r_{dyn}$ must remain equal across all non-slipping wheels, a deflated wheel must **spin faster**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_{under} &= \dfrac{v}{r_{dyn,\,under}} = \dfrac{v}{r_0 - k(P_0 - P)} \\
\omega_{ref}   &= \dfrac{v}{r_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **relative speed deviation** that the algorithm monitors is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_{ref}} &= \dfrac{\omega_{under} - \omega_{ref}}{\omega_{ref}} \\
                                   &= \dfrac{r_0 - k(P_0 - P)}{r_0 - k(P_0 - P)} - \dfrac{r_0}{r_0 - k(P_0 - P)} \quad \text{(simplified using linear approx.)} \\
                                   &\approx \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta P}{r_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 (iTPMS)

> **Problem:** For a tyre with $r_0 = 0.32$ m, $k = 0.00005$ m/kPa, $P_0 = 240$ kPa. The rear-right wheel reads $\omega_{RR} = 78.5$ rad/s while the others read $\omega = 77.0$ rad/s at $v = 24.6$ m/s. Estimate the pressure drop.

**Solution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_{ref}} &= \dfrac{78.5 - 77.0}{77.0} \approx 0.01948 = 1.948 \% \\
\Delta P &\approx \dfrac{\Delta\omega}{\omega_{ref}} \cdot \dfrac{r_0}{k} \\
         &= 0.01948 \cdot \dfrac{0.32}{0.00005} \\
         &= 0.01948 \cdot 6400 \\
         &\approx 124.7 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

That indicates the wheel is severely deflated (from 240 → ≈ 115 kPa) — well below the 25 % threshold. Warning is triggered.

> [!WARNING]
> **iTPMS Limitation:** Because iTPMS uses **all four** wheel speeds as references, it **cannot detect** uniform under-inflation (all four tyres equally low). It also requires a recalibration "reset" after every tyre rotation or pressure correction.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (dTPMS vs iTPMS)

| Criterion | Direct TPMS (dTPMS) | Indirect TPMS (iTPMS) |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| Pressure measurement | **Real**, absolute | **Inferred** from wheel speed |
| Detects simultaneous under-inflation | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| Accuracy | ± **1–2 %** | ± **10–15 %** |
| Hardware cost | Higher (sensor per wheel) | Lower (reuses ABS sensors) |
| Battery | Required (10-yr life) | Not required |
| Affected by tyre rotation | No, but **re-learn needed** | Yes, **ECU reset mandatory** |
| Affected by road surface / cornering | Minimal | Requires software filtering |
| Regulatory acceptance | ✅ AIS-145 / FMVSS 138 | ⚠️ Accepted only with disclaimers in some regions |
| Spare-tyre monitoring | ✅ Possible (if equipped) | ❌ Not possible |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — dTPMS Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[SensePressureMEMS] --> MCU1[SampleAndCompensateMCU]
    T[SenseTemperatureNTC] --> MCU1
    MCU1 --> RF[RFTransmitter433MHz]
    BAT[LiSOCl2Battery3p6V] --> MCU1
    BAT --> RF
    RF --> RX[RFReceiverECU]
    RX --> DEC[DecodeAndValidate]
    DEC --> LOG[PressureDropLogic]
    LOG --> HMI[InstrumentClusterWarningLamp]
    LOG --> CAN[CANBusBroadcast]
    CAN --> BCM[BodyControlModule]
    CAN --> IC[InfotainmentCluster]
    HMI --> DRV[DriverNotification]
```

### 4.2 iTPMS Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WheelSpeedSensorsABS] --> B[ReadAngularVelocities]
    B --> C[CompareFourWheels]
    C --> D{DeviationGreaterThanThreshold}
    D -- Yes --> E[IdentifyDeflatedWheel]
    D -- No --> F[ContinueMonitoring]
    E --> G[TriggerTPMSWarningLamp]
    G --> H[DriverReceivesAlert]
    F --> B
    H --> I[DriverInflatesTyre]
    I --> J[ManualResetButtonorHMI]
    J --> B
```

### 4.3 dTPMS Sensor Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SensorModule
        P2[PressureTransducerMEMS] --> ADC1[ADCChannel1]
        T2[NTCResistorTemperature] --> ADC2[ADCChannel2]
        ACC[AxisAccelerometerOptional] --> ADC3[ADCChannel3]
        ADC1 --> MCU2[Embedded8bitMCU]
        ADC2 --> MCU2
        ADC3 --> MCU2
        MCU2 --> TX2[433MHzASKFSKModulator]
        BAT2[CoinCellLiSOCl2] --> MCU2
        BAT2 --> TX2
        ANT[PCBAntennaLambdaover4] --> TX2
    end
    TX2 --> RFLink[WirelessLink]
    RFLink --> ReceiverUnit
```

### 4.4 State Machine — TPMS Warning Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> Warning: PressureDropGreater25Percent
    Warning --> Normal: PressureRestored
    Warning --> Fault: SensorSignalLost60s
    Fault --> Service: DiagnosticTroubleCodeSet
    Service --> Normal: AfterManualReset
    Normal --> ColdComp: TemperatureCompensationActive
    ColdComp --> Normal: CalculatedColdPressureOK
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Define Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS). State the regulatory warning threshold.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
TPMS is an electronic safety subsystem that continuously monitors the inflation pressure of pneumatic tyres and alerts the driver when pressure in any tyre falls **more than 25 % below** the manufacturer's placard pressure, as mandated by FMVSS 138 (USA), ECE R64 (EU) and AIS-145 (India). It uses either direct in-tyre pressure sensors or indirect ABS wheel-speed analysis. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`
**Differentiate between Direct TPMS and Indirect TPMS in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Measurement Principle:** dTPMS measures pressure directly via MEMS sensor; iTPMS infers pressure from wheel-speed differences.
2. **Detects Simultaneous Deflation:** dTPMS can detect equal under-inflation in all four tyres; iTPMS cannot.
3. **Hardware:** dTPMS needs a battery-powered sensor per wheel; iTPMS reuses existing ABS sensors (no extra hardware, no battery).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the architecture and working principle of a **Direct TPMS** with a neat block diagram. List its key components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Direct TPMS consists of a sensor module mounted inside each tyre (typically on the valve stem) and a central receiver/ECU. The block diagram is:

```
[MEMS Pressure Sensor] → [MEMS Temp Sensor] → [MCU (sample, compensate, build frame)]
   → [RF Transmitter 433 MHz] → [Antenna] → (wireless) → [Receiver] → [ECU Logic] → [HMI / CAN]
```

**Key Components (write any 4 for full marks):**
1. MEMS piezoresistive pressure transducer.
2. NTC temperature sensor (for ideal-gas compensation).
3. Low-power 8-bit MCU with ADC.
4. RF transmitter (315 / 433.92 MHz, ASK/FSK modulation).
5. LiSOCl₂ primary coin-cell battery (3.6 V).
6. PCB antenna (typically λ/4 monopole, λ ≈ 0.69 m).
7. Receiver module with superheterodyne front-end and decoding logic.

**Working Principle:** The pressure transducer deflects proportionally to absolute pressure; the deflection is converted to a voltage by a Wheatstone bridge. The MCU samples pressure and temperature every few seconds, applies cold-pressure compensation via $P_{25} = P_{live} \cdot T_{25}/T_{live}$, and broadcasts an RF data frame (typically containing pressure, temperature, sensor ID and battery status). The receiver decodes the frame, identifies which wheel sent it (via unique ID), and the ECU compares against the placard threshold to drive the warning lamp. **(7 marks — full)**

---

**(b)** A TPMS sensor reports **$P_{live} = 215$ kPa** at **$T_{live} = 60\,°C$**. The placard pressure is **$240$ kPa** at **$25\,°C$**. Determine:
- (i) The cold-equivalent pressure.
- (ii) Whether the warning lamp will illuminate (use the 25 % rule).
- (iii) The actual percentage drop in pressure relative to the placard. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Kelvin Conversion:**
$$
T_{live} = 60 + 273.15 = 333.15 \text{ K}, \quad T_{25} = 298.15 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Cold Equivalent Pressure:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{25} &= P_{live} \cdot \dfrac{T_{25}}{T_{live}} \\
       &= 215 \cdot \dfrac{298.15}{333.15} \\
       &= 215 \cdot 0.8949 \\
       &\approx 192.41 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Warning Threshold:**
$$
P_{warn} = 0.75 \cdot 240 = 180 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
$$
192.41 \text{ kPa} > 180 \text{ kPa} \Rightarrow \text{No warning.}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual Drop Percentage:**
$$
\Delta P\% = \dfrac{240 - 192.41}{240} \cdot 100 \approx 19.83\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Although the live pressure has dropped by ~19.8 %, the drop is still **below** the 25 % regulatory limit; the TPMS warning lamp will **NOT** illuminate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **Indirect TPMS**. Discuss its main advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Indirect TPMS uses the existing **ABS/ESC wheel-speed sensors** to detect under-inflation. An under-inflated tyre has a smaller dynamic rolling radius $r_{dyn}$. Because all four wheels must match the vehicle's translational speed $v$, the deflated wheel rotates slightly faster. The ECU continuously compares the four angular velocities $\omega_i$. A persistent deviation above a calibrated threshold (typically 0.3 bar drop) flags that wheel as under-inflated and triggers the warning lamp. **(3 marks)**

**Advantages (any 2):**
- Low hardware cost — reuses ABS sensors.
- No battery, no sensor replacement; long service life.
- Simpler installation; no in-tyre electronics. **(2 marks)**

**Limitations (any 2):**
- Cannot detect uniform under-inflation (all four tyres equally low).
- Affected by cornering, braking, and road camber — requires software filtering.
- Must be **recalibrated** after every tyre rotation or pressure change.
- Spare tyre not monitored. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor** principle used in dTPMS. State two reasons why TPMS batteries use **Lithium Thionyl Chloride (LiSOCl₂)** chemistry. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**MEMS Piezoresistive Principle (sketch description):**
A thin silicon diaphragm is etched using MEMS processes and supported at its edges. Four piezoresistors are diffused into the diaphragm in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. When external pressure deflects the diaphragm, two resistors go into tension (R increases) and two into compression (R decreases). The bridge produces a differential output voltage:

$$
V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}
$$

where $V_{ex}$ is the bridge excitation voltage. The output is linear over a designed pressure range (typically 100–1000 kPa absolute) and is digitised by the MCU's ADC. **(4 marks)**

**Why LiSOCl₂? (any 2)**
1. **Highest specific energy** of any primary cell (~730 Wh/kg), allowing a 10-year service life from a small coin form-factor.
2. **Wide operating temperature range** (−40 °C to +125 °C), suitable for the harsh in-tyre thermal environment.
3. **Very low self-discharge rate** (<1 % per year) — essential when average current draw is only ~1–2 µA.
4. **Hermetic seal possible** — survives high-g centrifugal loading (>1500 g at 200 km/h). **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in TPMS Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting Kelvin conversion** in cold-pressure problems — costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up pressure types** (gauge vs absolute). TPMS sensors typically output **absolute** pressure; subtract ~101 kPa if gauge is expected.
> 3. **Stating iTPMS "measures pressure"** — it does *not*; examiners will deduct. Always say "infers" or "estimates".
> 4. **Skipping the regulatory threshold comparison** in numericals — even a perfect cold-pressure calculation gets only 5/7 if the threshold check is missing.
> 5. **Writing the TPMS RF frequency as 2.4 GHz** (that's Wi-Fi/Bluetooth) — correct is **315 MHz or 433.92 MHz**.
> 6. **Confusing ABS sensor role** in iTPMS — they are *wheel-speed sensors*, not pressure sensors.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ TPMS = **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System**, mandatory by AIS-145 / FMVSS 138.
- ✅ **Two types:** Direct (dTPMS, sensor inside tyre) and Indirect (iTPMS, uses ABS).
- ✅ **Regulatory warning trigger:** pressure drop **≥ 25 %** below placard.
- ✅ **Placard pressure** is the cold-inflation pressure specified by OEM.
- ✅ **Cold-pressure compensation:** $P_{25} = P_{live} \cdot T_{25}/T_{live}$ — *always* use Kelvin.
- ✅ **Operating frequencies:** **315 MHz (USA)** or **433.92 MHz (EU/India)**.
- ✅ **Battery:** LiSOCl₂ coin cell, **3.6 V**, designed for **~10 years** life.
- ✅ **MEMS principle:** piezoresistive Wheatstone bridge on silicon diaphragm.
- ✅ **iTPMS limitations:** cannot detect uniform under-inflation; needs recalibration after tyre rotation.
- ✅ **TPMS data fields in RF frame:** pressure, temperature, sensor ID, battery status, acceleration (optional).
- ✅ **Centrifugal load on sensor** can exceed **1500 g** at highway speeds — sensor must be rugged.
- ✅ **Typical placard pressure range:** 200–250 kPa (≈ 30–36 psi) for passenger cars.
- ✅ **dTPMS advantages:** accurate, detects all defects, monitors spare tyre, no recalibration needed.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lane Departure Warning System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lane Departure Warning System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lane Departure Warning System (LDWS)** is an **Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)** that is designed to **alert the driver** when the vehicle begins to drift out of its lane without the activation of the turn signal. It is a **passive safety / active warning** system that uses on-board sensors — primarily a **forward-facing CMOS camera** mounted near the rear-view mirror — combined with sophisticated **image-processing algorithms** to detect lane markings on the road and compute the vehicle's lateral position and trajectory relative to those markings.

According to the **ISO 17361:2017** standard (Intelligent Transport Systems — Lane Departure Warning Systems — Performance Requirements and Test Procedures), LDWS is defined as:

> *"A system that aids the driver by warning him/her when the vehicle has crossed or is about to cross a lane boundary, without the proper use of the direction indicator."*

The system forms a critical sub-module under **ADAS Level 1** automation as classified by the **SAE J3016** standard.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Co-Pilot Sitting Beside You"**
>
> Imagine you are driving on a long, monotonous highway at night. You start feeling sleepy, and your car slowly drifts toward the edge of your lane. Suddenly, your co-passenger shakes you and says, *"Hey! You're moving out of your lane!"* That co-passenger is exactly what the **Lane Departure Warning System** does — it acts as a vigilant second pair of eyes that **never blinks** and **never gets distracted**.

### Breaking Down the Analogy:
| Human Co-Passenger | LDWS Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes watching the road | Forward-facing camera | Captures road images continuously |
| Brain recognizing lines | Image-processing ECU | Identifies lane markings |
| Vocal alert | Buzzer / speaker | Audio warning |
| Tapping your shoulder | Steering-wheel vibration | Haptic warning |
| "You forgot the indicator" | Turn-signal input check | Cancels warning when indicator ON |

---

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics

The system operates within standardized physical boundaries (per **ISO 17361**):

- **Operating Speed Range: 60 km/h to 175 km/h** (≈ 17 m/s to 49 m/s)
- **Lateral Departure Threshold: 0.3 m to 0.75 m** from the inner edge of the lane marking
- **Warning Time:** typically **≥ 0.5 seconds** before crossing
- **Field of View (FOV) of camera: ~40° horizontally**
- **Image Resolution: typically 640 × 480 pixels** (VGA) up to 1280 × 960
- **Frame Rate: 25 to 30 fps** (real-time processing)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:**
> - **LDW (Lane Departure Warning)** — Only WARNS the driver (passive)
> - **LKA (Lane Keeping Assist)** — Actively STEERS the vehicle back (active)
>
> These are **two different systems** but share the same sensing hardware.

---

## 1.4 Why LDWS Matters — The Real-World Necessity

> [!NOTE]
> According to the **WHO Global Status Report on Road Safety**, lane-change-related crashes account for nearly **10% of all road fatalities** in developed countries. A large portion of these occur due to **driver inattention, drowsiness, or distraction**. LDWS directly addresses this by acting as a **non-intrusive electronic co-pilot**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The LDWS functions through a **closed-loop perception pipeline** that executes every **40–60 ms (one frame cycle)**. The high-level architecture can be decomposed into six sequential functional blocks:

### **Step 1 — Image Acquisition**
- A **CMOS/CCD camera** (typically mounted behind the windshield, near the rear-view mirror base) continuously captures grayscale or RGB images of the road ahead.
- Camera is **calibrated** to compensate for:
  - Lens distortion (radial + tangential)
  - Vehicle pitch/roll variation
  - Mounting angle offset (usually **0°–2°**)
- The image is digitized at a frame rate of **f = 25–30 fps**.

### **Step 2 — Pre-Processing**
- **Grayscale conversion** to reduce computational load.
- **Noise filtering** using a **Gaussian filter** (kernel size 5×5 typical).
- **Histogram equalization** to enhance contrast between lane markings and road surface.
- **ROI (Region of Interest) extraction** — only the lower half of the image (where the road appears) is processed to save computation.
