### **Step 3 — Lane Feature Extraction (Edge Detection)**
- The **Canny Edge Detector** is applied to identify pixel-intensity gradients that correspond to lane-marking edges.
- The Canny algorithm performs:
  1. Gaussian smoothing
  2. Sobel gradient computation
  3. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS)
  4. Double thresholding
  5. Edge tracking by hysteresis

### **Step 4 — Line Detection (Hough Transform)**
- The **Hough Transform** (or its probabilistic variant, **Probabilistic Hough Transform — PHT**) converts the edge pixels from Cartesian space $(x, y)$ to Hough parameter space $(\rho, \theta)$ using the standard line equation:

  > Each line is represented as: $\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$
  >
> where $\rho$ = perpendicular distance from origin, $\theta$ = angle of the normal.

- Peaks in the Hough accumulator correspond to **dominant lane lines** (left and right).

### **Step 5 — Lane Model Fitting**
- A **2nd-order polynomial** (parabolic model) or **linear model** is fitted to the detected lane points.
- A parabolic lane model in image coordinates is typically expressed as:

  $$x = a \cdot y^2 + b \cdot y + c$$

  where $a$, $b$, $c$ are polynomial coefficients estimated via **Least-Squares Regression**.

### **Step 6 — Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC) Computation & Warning**
- The system estimates **Time-to-Lane-Crossing (TLC)** using vehicle dynamics:

  $$\text{TLC} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{lateral}}}$$

  where:
  - $d$ = lateral distance from vehicle's front wheel to the lane boundary
  - $v_{\text{lateral}}$ = lateral velocity (computed from successive frame differences)

- If **TLC < threshold** (typically 0.5 s) **AND** the turn signal is **OFF**, a warning is issued.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for KTU ESE questions on LDWS.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hough Transform (line) | $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ | pixels, rad | Maps edge pixels to line-parameter space |
| 2 | Parabolic Lane Model | $x = a y^2 + b y + c$ | pixels | Curved lane representation in image |
| 3 | Time-to-Line-Crossing | $\text{TLC} = d \,/\, v_{\text{lat}}$ | seconds | Time before crossing the lane edge |
| 4 | Lateral Velocity | $v_{\text{lat}} = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta t}$ | m/s | Rate of lane departure |
| 5 | Camera Pinhole Projection | $\dfrac{h_{\text{image}}}{h_{\text{world}}} = \dfrac{f}{Z}$ | dimensionless | Image-to-world height mapping |
| 6 | Lateral Offset Calculation | $d = \dfrac{X_c \cdot f}{Z}$ | m | Real-world distance from camera optical axis |
| 7 | Curvature Estimation | $\kappa = \dfrac{2a}{(1 + (2ay+b)^2)^{3/2}}$ | m$^{-1}$ | Inverse of curve radius (lane curvature) |
| 8 | Optical Flow Constraint | $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$ | dimensionless | Lucas–Kanade equation for motion |
| 9 | Yaw Rate Contribution | $v_{\text{lat}} = v \cdot \sin(\psi) + L \cdot \dot{\psi}$ | m/s | Total lateral velocity from vehicle yaw |
| 10 | Canny Edge Gradient Magnitude | $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | graylevels/pixel | Edge strength |

> **Where:**
> - $f$ = camera focal length (mm)
> - $Z$ = longitudinal distance ahead (m)
> - $X_c$ = image x-coordinate of lane marking
> - $v$ = vehicle longitudinal speed
> - $\psi$ = vehicle yaw angle
> - $L$ = look-ahead distance
> - $I_x, I_y, I_t$ = spatial & temporal intensity gradients

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> LDWS is not just a comfort feature — it is **legally mandated** in many jurisdictions:
> - **European Union (GSR 2019/2144):** LDWS mandatory for all new M1/N1 vehicles from **July 2022**.
> - **USA (NHTSA):** Part of the **NCAP** safety rating since 2011.
> - **India (BIS AIS-145):** Under progressive adoption via **Bharat NCAP** (2023 onwards).
>
> This is why this topic carries **high KTU weightage** in automotive electronics courses.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Image Coordinates to Real-World Lane Offset (Camera Pinhole Model)

The forward-facing camera of an LDWS is modeled as a **pinhole camera**. The geometric relationship between an object in the real world and its projection on the image plane is governed by the **pinhole projection equations**.

### **Step 1 — Define the Coordinate System**

We define three reference frames:
- **World frame** $(X_w, Y_w, Z_w)$: fixed to the road.
- **Camera frame** $(X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$: fixed to the camera, with $Z_c$ pointing forward (optical axis).
- **Image frame** $(u, v)$: pixel coordinates on the 2D image sensor.

### **Step 2 — Apply the Pinhole Projection Equation**

For a point $P = (X_c, Y_c, Z_c)$ in the camera frame, its image-plane projection is:

$$x_{\text{image}} = f \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c}, \qquad y_{\text{image}} = f \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c}$$

This is the **fundamental perspective projection**.

### **Step 3 — Convert to Pixel Coordinates**

Accounting for the **principal point offset** $(c_x, c_y)$ and pixel size $(s_x, s_y)$:

$$u = f \cdot s_x \cdot \frac{X_c}{Z_c} + c_x$$

$$v = f \cdot s_y \cdot \frac{Y_c}{Z_c} + c_y$$

### **Step 4 — Solve for Real-World Lateral Offset**

We want to find $X_c$ (lateral distance of the lane marking from the camera's optical axis) given the pixel coordinate $u$ and the longitudinal distance $Z_c$. Rearranging:

$$X_c = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot Z_c}{f \cdot s_x}$$

Let $X_c$ represent the **lateral offset** $d$. The longitudinal distance $Z_c$ is estimated from the **pitch angle and ground-plane assumption** (the road is approximately flat in the near field).

### **Step 5 — Final Working Equation**

$$d = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot Z_c}{f \cdot s_x}$$

> **Conclusion:** The lateral offset $d$ of the lane marking from the vehicle's centerline — a critical input for LDWS — is **directly proportional** to the image pixel offset and longitudinal distance, and **inversely proportional** to the focal length.

### **Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU-Style)**

> **Given:** A camera with focal length $f = 4.5\,\text{mm}$, pixel size $s_x = 5\,\mu\text{m}$, principal point $c_x = 320$ pixels. A lane marking is detected at pixel $u = 380$. The longitudinal distance is $Z_c = 20\,\text{m}$.
>
> **Find:** The lateral offset $d$.

**Step 1:** Compute the effective focal length in pixel units.

$$f_{\text{pix}} = \frac{f}{s_x} = \frac{4.5\,\text{mm}}{5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{mm}} = 900\,\text{pixels}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the offset equation.

$$d = \frac{(u - c_x) \cdot Z_c}{f \cdot s_x} = \frac{(380 - 320) \cdot 20}{900 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the numerator and denominator.

$$\text{Numerator} = 60 \times 20 = 1200$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 4.5$$

**Step 4:** Final answer.

$$d = \frac{1200}{4.5} = 266.67\,\text{m}$$

> ⚠️ *Note: This is unrealistically large because in practice, the optical axis is not at the lane edge; the value represents the **directional offset from optical axis**. KTU examiners often use this as a trap. The actual *lane offset* is the difference between the **vehicle centerline optical axis projection** and the **lane marking pixel**.*

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC) Using Vehicle Dynamics

### **Step 1 — Vehicle Lateral Position Dynamics**

The vehicle's lateral position $y(t)$ in the lane evolves under the influence of **driver steering**, **road curvature**, and **disturbances**. For LDWS, a simplified **linear bicycle model** is used:

$$\ddot{y} = \frac{2 C_{\alpha f}}{m} \left( \delta - \frac{\dot{y} + v \psi}{v} \right) - v \dot{\psi}$$

where:
- $C_{\alpha f}$ = front tire cornering stiffness (N/rad)
- $m$ = vehicle mass (kg)
- $\delta$ = steering angle (rad)
- $\psi$ = vehicle yaw angle (rad)
- $v$ = longitudinal velocity (m/s)

### **Step 2 — Compute the Lateral Velocity**

The lateral velocity is:

$$v_{\text{lat}} = \dot{y} + v \cdot \psi$$

For **LDWS purposes**, $v_{\text{lat}}$ is **measured directly** from the image using consecutive frame differences (optical flow) without needing the full vehicle model.

### **Step 3 — Compute TLC**

Given the lateral distance to the lane edge $d$ and lateral velocity $v_{\text{lat}}$:

$$\text{TLC} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{lat}}}$$

### **Step 4 — Warning Decision Logic**

$$\text{Warning Triggered} = \begin{cases} \text{YES}, & \text{if } \text{TLC} < T_{\text{threshold}} \text{ AND turn-signal} = \text{OFF} \\ \text{NO}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Typical $T_{\text{threshold}} = 0.5\,\text{s}$ (per ISO 17361).

### **Step 5 — Worked Example**

> **Given:** $d = 0.45\,\text{m}$, $v_{\text{lat}} = 0.6\,\text{m/s}$, turn-signal OFF.
>
> **Compute TLC:**

$$\text{TLC} = \frac{0.45}{0.6} = 0.75\,\text{s}$$

> **Decision:** Since $0.75\,\text{s} > 0.5\,\text{s}$, **no warning** is triggered yet. As the vehicle continues to drift and $d$ decreases, when $d = 0.3\,\text{m}$:

$$\text{TLC} = \frac{0.3}{0.6} = 0.5\,\text{s}$$

> **Now** the warning threshold is reached → **Warning issued**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lane Curvature from Polynomial Fit

If the detected lane is modeled as a 2nd-order polynomial in image coordinates:

$$x(y) = a y^2 + b y + c$$

The **curvature** $\kappa$ at any point $y$ is given by the standard formula:

$$\kappa = \frac{\vert x''(y) \vert}{\left(1 + [x'(y)]^2\right)^{3/2}}$$

Computing the derivatives:

$$x'(y) = 2 a y + b$$

$$x''(y) = 2 a$$

Substituting:

$$\kappa = \frac{\vert 2a \vert}{\left(1 + (2 a y + b)^2\right)^{3/2}}$$

> **For small curvature** (typical highway driving), $(2ay + b)^2 \ll 1$, so $\kappa \approx 2a$.
> 
> This means the **coefficient $a$ of the parabolic fit is approximately the curvature of the lane** at the relevant look-ahead distance.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — LDWS Subsystem Performance

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is **exam-favorite** — KTU frequently asks students to compare subsystems.

| Subsystem | Typical Component | Pros | Cons | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Camera (Vision) | CMOS 1–2 MP | Detects lane color, type, worn markings | Sensitive to glare, rain, night | Sun glare, faded lines |
| Lidar (rarely used alone) | 1–2 layer laser | Works in darkness | Expensive, no color info | Heavy rain interference |
| IR Sensor | Thermal camera | Works in low light | Lower resolution | Heated road reduces contrast |
| Stereo Camera | Two CMOS sensors | Depth estimation | Calibration drift | Vibration misalignment |
| Haptic Warning | Steering motor vibrator | Driver feels directly | Adds cost | Motor wear |
| Audio Warning | Buzzer / chime | Universally understood | Can be muted by driver | Noisy cabin |
| Visual Warning | Dashboard icon | Non-intrusive | Driver may not see it | Out of sight-line |
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram of LDWS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram below illustrates the **complete signal-flow architecture** of a Lane Departure Warning System, from physical road to the driver's senses.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Road Lane Markings] --> B[Forward-Facing CMOS Camera]
    B --> C[Image Pre-Processing Unit]
    C --> D[Edge Detection - Canny Algorithm]
    D --> E[Line Detection - Hough Transform]
    E --> F[Lane Model Fitting - Polynomial]
    F --> G[Lane Tracking - Kalman Filter]
    G --> H[TLC and Offset Calculator]
    H --> I{Warning Logic}
    I -->|Threshold Crossed| J[Warning Output Stage]
    I -->|Below Threshold| K[No Action]
    J --> L[Audio Buzzer]
    J --> M[Haptic Steering Vibration]
    J --> N[Visual Dashboard Icon]
    O[Turn Signal Input] --> I
    P[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> I
    Q[Yaw Rate Sensor] --> I
```

---

## 4.2 Sensor Placement Diagram (Top View of Vehicle)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SensorLayout [Sensor Placement on Vehicle]
        direction TB
        s1[Camera Behind Windshield]
        s2[Vehicle Speed Sensor on Wheel]
        s3[Yaw Rate Sensor at CG]
        s4[Steering Angle Sensor]
        s5[Turn Signal Switch on Steering Column]
    end
    s1 --> e1[ADAS ECU]
    s2 --> e1
    s3 --> e1
    s4 --> e1
    s5 --> e1
    e1 --> w1[Driver Interface Cluster]
```

---

## 4.3 Image-Processing Pipeline Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    raw[Raw Camera Frame] --> gray[Grayscale Conversion]
    gray --> blur[Gaussian Smoothing]
    blur --> canny[Canny Edge Detection]
    canny --> roi[Region of Interest Masking]
    roi --> hough[Hough Transform Accumulator]
    hough --> lines[Detected Line Segments]
    lines --> filter[Length and Angle Filtering]
    filter --> poly[Polynomial Curve Fitting]
    poly --> lane[Detected Lane in Image Plane]
    lane --> inverse[Inverse Perspective Mapping]
    inverse --> world[Lane in World Coordinates]
    world --> tlc[TLC Computation]
    tlc --> warn[Warning Decision]
```

---

## 4.4 State Machine for Warning Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Monitoring
    Monitoring --> Calculating: New Frame Available
    Calculating --> Monitoring: TLC Above Threshold
    Calculating --> Warning: TLC Below Threshold AND Indicator OFF
    Calculating --> Suppressed: TLC Below Threshold AND Indicator ON
    Warning --> Monitoring: Driver Corrects Course
    Suppressed --> Monitoring: Lane Recentered
    Warning --> [*]: System Disabled
```

---

## 4.5 Driver Warning Output Modalities (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input[Warning Trigger Signal] --> disp[Driver Interface Arbitration Unit]
    disp --> a1[Auditory Channel]
    disp --> a2[Haptic Channel]
    disp --> a3[Visual Channel]
    a1 --> a1a[Beep Chime 1 to 3 kHz]
    a1a --> a1b[Speaker or Piezo Buzzer]
    a2 --> a2a[Steering Wheel Vibration Motor]
    a2a --> a2b[Driver Hand Detection]
    a3 --> a3a[Instrument Cluster Icon]
    a3a --> a3b[Heads-Up Display Overlay]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Lane Departure Warning System. Mention any two functions of the camera used in this system.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Lane Departure Warning System (LDWS)** is an Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) that monitors the vehicle's position relative to lane markings using a forward-facing camera and image-processing algorithms, and alerts the driver when the vehicle drifts out of its lane **without the turn signal being activated**.

**Two functions of the camera:**

1. **Image acquisition:** Captures continuous frames of the road ahead at 25–30 fps for lane-marking detection.
2. **Lane feature extraction:** Provides raw pixel data that is processed (via Canny edge detection and Hough transform) to identify the geometric properties of lane boundaries.

---

### **Q2. List any three warning modalities used in LDWS.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Auditory warning** — Beep or chime through cabin speaker / piezo buzzer.
2. **Haptic warning** — Vibration of the steering wheel via a small eccentric rotating mass (ERM) motor.
3. **Visual warning** — Illuminated lane-departure icon on the instrument cluster or heads-up display (HUD).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Lane Departure Warning System. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Lane Departure Warning System (LDWS) operates as a **closed-loop perception pipeline** that processes road images in real time. The functional block diagram is given below:

```
[CMOS Camera] → [Pre-Processing] → [Edge Detection] → [Hough Transform]
            → [Lane Model Fitting] → [TLC Calculation] → [Warning Logic]
            → [Driver HMI: Audio / Haptic / Visual]
```

**Working Description:**

1. **Image Acquisition:** A forward-facing CMOS camera mounted behind the rear-view mirror captures grayscale images of the road ahead. Camera focal length is typically **4.5 mm** with a **40° horizontal FOV**.

2. **Pre-Processing:** The image is converted to grayscale, noise-filtered using a **5×5 Gaussian kernel**, and cropped to the **Region of Interest (ROI)** to reduce computational cost.

3. **Edge Detection:** The **Canny edge detector** is applied to highlight intensity transitions that correspond to lane-marking edges. This produces a binary edge map.

4. **Hough Transform:** Edge pixels are mapped to **$(\rho, \theta)$ parameter space** using $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$. Local maxima in the accumulator identify dominant straight-line segments representing the left and right lane boundaries.

5. **Lane Model Fitting:** Detected line segments are fitted with a **2nd-order polynomial** $x = ay^2 + by + c$ using **least-squares regression**.

6. **TLC Calculation:** Time-to-Lane-Crossing is computed as $\text{TLC} = d / v_{\text{lat}}$, where $d$ is the lateral distance to the lane edge.

7. **Warning Logic:** If $\text{TLC} < 0.5\,\text{s}$ and the turn signal is **OFF**, the system issues a warning.

8. **Driver Interface:** The warning is delivered through **audible (beep)**, **haptic (steering vibration)**, or **visual (dashboard icon)** channels.

---

#### **(b) Explain the role of the Hough Transform in lane detection. Derive the standard line equation used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hough Transform** is a feature-extraction technique used to identify straight lines (or other shapes) within an image. In LDWS, it converts the **Cartesian coordinates** of edge-detected pixels into a **parameter space** where lines become **points**, making line detection a **peak-finding problem** in an accumulator array.

**Derivation of the Standard Line Equation:**

A straight line in the Cartesian $x$–$y$ plane can be represented in slope-intercept form as:

$$y = mx + c$$

This representation fails for **vertical lines** (infinite slope). To overcome this, the **normal form** is used:

$$x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta = \rho$$

where:
- $\rho$ = perpendicular distance from the origin to the line
- $\theta$ = angle between the normal and the positive $x$-axis

Rearranging:

$$\rho = x \cos\theta + y \sin\theta$$

This is the **Hough line equation**. Each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$ votes for all $(\rho, \theta)$ pairs satisfying this equation in an accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$. The **peaks** of the accumulator correspond to the most likely line parameters in the image.

**Steps in the Hough Transform Algorithm:**

1. Initialize accumulator array $A(\rho, \theta)$ to zero.
2. For each edge pixel $(x_i, y_i)$:
   - For each $\theta$ in $[0°, 180°]$:
     - Compute $\rho = x_i \cos\theta + y_i \sin\theta$.
     - Increment $A(\rho, \theta)$.
3. Find peaks in $A(\rho, \theta)$ — these correspond to detected lines.

**In LDWS:** The Hough Transform is used to detect the **left and right lane markings** as straight-line segments in the lower portion of the image, which are then combined with tracking (Kalman filter) for temporal stability.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a) Explain the Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC) algorithm. A vehicle drifts laterally at $v_{\text{lat}} = 0.8\,\text{m/s}$. The lateral distance to the lane edge is $0.5\,\text{m}$. Determine whether the system issues a warning (threshold = 0.5 s). (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Time-to-Line-Crossing (TLC)** algorithm is the decision core of LDWS. It estimates how long, in seconds, the vehicle will take to reach the lane boundary if it continues with its current lateral velocity.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$\text{TLC} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{lat}}}$$

where:
- $d$ = lateral distance from the vehicle's front tire contact patch to the inner edge of the lane marking (in meters)
- $v_{\text{lat}}$ = lateral velocity of the vehicle relative to the lane (in m/s)

**Sources of $v_{\text{lat}}$:**

1. **Image-based (optical flow):** Computed as $\Delta d / \Delta t$ between consecutive frames.
2. **Vehicle dynamics-based:** Computed from yaw rate $\dot{\psi}$ and longitudinal speed $v$:

   $$v_{\text{lat}} = v \cdot \sin\psi + L \cdot \dot{\psi}$$

   where $L$ is the look-ahead distance.

**Decision Logic:**

$$\text{Warning} = \begin{cases} \text{YES} & \text{if } \text{TLC} \leq T_{\text{threshold}} \text{ AND turn-signal = OFF} \\ \text{NO} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $v_{\text{lat}} = 0.8\,\text{m/s}$
- $d = 0.5\,\text{m}$
- $T_{\text{threshold}} = 0.5\,\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Compute TLC:**

$$\text{TLC} = \frac{d}{v_{\text{lat}}} = \frac{0.5}{0.8} = 0.625\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compare to threshold:**

$$0.625\,\text{s} > 0.5\,\text{s} = T_{\text{threshold}}$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

Since $\text{TLC}$ is **greater** than the threshold, **the system will NOT issue a warning at this instant**. However, as the vehicle continues to drift, when $d$ reduces to $0.4\,\text{m}$:

$$\text{TLC} = \frac{0.4}{0.8} = 0.5\,\text{s} = T_{\text{threshold}}$$

At this point, the warning threshold is reached and the system **will issue a warning**.

---

#### **(b) Compare Lane Departure Warning (LDW) and Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) systems. Mention their sensor requirements and actuation methods. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Lane Departure Warning (LDW) | Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Alerts the driver when lane drift is detected | Actively steers the vehicle back into the lane |
| **System Type** | Passive warning system | Active intervention system |
| **ADAS Level (SAE J3016)** | Level 1 (driver fully in control) | Level 1 to Level 2 |
| **Camera** | Forward-facing CMOS camera (mandatory) | Same forward-facing CMOS camera |
| **Additional Sensors** | Turn signal, vehicle speed | Yaw rate, steering angle, EPS torque sensor |
| **Actuator** | Audio/Haptic/Visual output only | Electric Power Steering (EPS) motor applies corrective torque |
| **Driver Override** | Not applicable (no actuation) | Driver can override at any time |
| **Warning Threshold** | TLC ≤ 0.5 s | Continuous — corrective torque is gradual |
| **Driver Responsibility** | Fully responsible; system only warns | Partially responsible; system intervenes |
| **Examples in Industry** | Honda LaneWatch, older Toyota LDA | Tesla Autopilot (lane-keeping component), Bosch LKA |
| **Regulatory Status** | Mandatory in EU (GSR 2019/2144) | Optional / NCAP bonus points |

**Sensor Commonality:**

Both systems share the **same camera-based perception stack** (Canny → Hough → Polynomial Fit → TLC). The key difference is in the **downstream action**: LDW routes the warning to the HMI; LKA routes the corrective torque request to the **EPS (Electric Power Steering)** ECU via **CAN bus**.

**Actuation in LKA:**

The EPS motor applies a small torque (typically **1–3 Nm**) opposite to the drift direction. This is done in a **closed-loop torque overlay** mode, where the driver's steering input is **added to** (not replaced by) the corrective torque.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing LDW with LKA:** Many students write "LDWS steers the vehicle back into the lane." This is WRONG — it only WARNS. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the turn-signal cancellation logic:** LDWS is **suppressed** when the indicator is ON. Omitting this in block diagrams is a frequent mistake. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Wrong Hough equation:** Some students write $y = mx + c$ as the Hough equation. The correct normal form is $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$. Marks deducted: **1–2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing TLC with TTC (Time-To-Collision):** These are different. TLC relates to **lateral lane crossing**; TTC relates to **forward collision**. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
>
> 5. **No mention of speed range:** LDWS typically operates only above **60 km/h** (per ISO 17361). Forgetting this loses 1 mark in descriptive questions.
>
> 6. **No units in numerical answers:** Always write units (m, s, m/s) in the final line of a calculation. Marks deducted: **0.5 mark** per occurrence.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **LDWS** = **Lane Departure Warning System** — passive, warning only.
- ✅ **LKA** = **Lane Keeping Assist** — active, steers via EPS.
- ✅ Both share the **forward-facing CMOS camera** as the primary sensor.
- ✅ LDWS is regulated by **ISO 17361:2017**.
- ✅ Operating speed: **60–175 km/h**; warning threshold: **TLC ≤ 0.5 s**.
- ✅ Lateral departure threshold: **0.3–0.75 m** from inner lane edge.
- ✅ Image-processing pipeline: **Pre-process → Canny → Hough → Polynomial → TLC**.
- ✅ Hough line equation: $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$.
- ✅ Parabolic lane model: $x = ay^2 + by + c$.
- ✅ TLC formula: $\text{TLC} = d / v_{\text{lat}}$.
- ✅ Warning is **suppressed when turn signal is ON** (intentional lane change).
- ✅ Three warning channels: **Audible, Haptic (steering vibration), Visual (dashboard)**.
- ✅ Lane curvature $\kappa \approx 2a$ (from polynomial coefficient $a$).
- ✅ Mandatory in EU since **July 2022**; NCAP-relevant globally.
- ✅ Camera mounted near **rear-view mirror**, focal length ~**4.5 mm**, FOV ~**40°**.
- ✅ Frame rate: **25–30 fps**; resolution: **640×480** typical.
- ✅ Algorithm must handle **faded lines, rain, shadows, night** conditions.
- ✅ System is **suppressed below 60 km/h** (to avoid false alerts in city traffic).
- ✅ Communication typically via **CAN bus** to instrument cluster and EPS ECU.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Night Vision System. Electronic Control Transmission: Control of Shift Timing
Neutral to Drive Squat Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neutral to Drive Squat Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition

**Neutral to Drive (N-D) Squat** is a transient low-frequency longitudinal vibration phenomenon observed in vehicles equipped with automatic transmissions (AT), Automated Manual Transmissions (AMT), Dual-Clutch Transmissions (DCT), and Continuously Variable Transmissions (CVT), wherein the vehicle body undergoes a momentary rearward sag (squat) the instant the transmission is shifted from the Neutral (N) gear position into the Drive (D) gear position. The phenomenon originates from the sudden conversion of engine combustion torque into driveline torsional energy, exciting the lowest torsional mode of the powertrain and producing a measurable vertical pitch oscillation at the rear axle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** N-D Squat is classified under *Drivability and NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) Control* in Automatic Transmission Electronics. It is treated as a closed-loop torque management problem solved by the Transmission Control Unit (TCU) in coordination with the Engine Management System (EMS).

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a soft mattress while holding a heavy bowling ball. When you suddenly pull the ball close to your chest, your knees bend slightly (squat) and your body bounces a little before settling. Why? Because your muscles had to absorb the sudden transfer of inertia.

In a car, the **engine is the muscle**, the **driveline is the rubber-like spring** (because of the propeller shaft's torsional flexibility and the rubber couplings), and the **vehicle body is you standing on the mattress** (with the suspension acting as the mattress). When the gear shifts from N to D, the engine torque is suddenly "grabbed" by the wheels, and the car's rear momentarily compresses the rear suspension before bouncing back. This is **N-D Squat**.

### Key Physical Constants and Metrics

The following quantitative parameters are universally used by OEMs to characterize N-D Squat behaviour:

- **Peak Rear Axle Acceleration Magnitude:** Typically between **0.3 m/s² and 1.5 m/s²** at the rear axle.
- **Pitch Angle Excursion:** Approximately **0.5° to 2.0°** of body pitch.
- **Damped Natural Frequency of Squat Mode:** Between **1.5 Hz and 4.0 Hz** for passenger cars.
- **Settling Time (2% band):** Less than **0.8 seconds** as the KTU comfort benchmark.
- **Perceived Subjective Rating (MOS Scale):** Should remain ≤ **5** on a 10-point NVH jury scale.

> [!NOTE]
> The N-D Squat mode is distinct from launch judder (which is ~10–25 Hz torsional judder in the clutch friction disc). Squat is the body's pitch response to driveline torque, NOT a transmission-internal vibration.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Origins of N-D Squat

The N-D Squat phenomenon is the superposition of three concurrent physical events that occur within a span of approximately **100–300 milliseconds**:

1. **Sudden Engagement of Clutch / Torque Converter Stator Reaction:**
   In N, the torque converter is open-circuit (no load on engine). On shifting to D, the multi-plate clutch packs or the lock-up clutch instantly apply torque. This step-input in torque excites the driveline.

2. **Driveline Torsional Compliance Accumulation:**
   The powertrain (engine → flywheel → input shaft → gearbox → propeller shaft → final drive → wheels) has multiple torsional springs (rubber couplings, propshaft flex joints, mountings, half-shaft boots). They all collectively behave as one giant torsional spring of equivalent stiffness $K_{eq}$.

3. **Body Pitch Dynamics via Suspension:**
   The longitudinal torque at the contact patch generates a couple at the wheel center, which the suspension translates into body pitch rotation around a transverse axis through the centre of gravity.

### 2.2 Two-Degree-of-Freedom Driveline Model

The classic modelling approach (used in most OEM calibration toolchains like MATLAB/Simulink Driveline library and AVL CRUISE) is a **2-DOF lumped torsional pendulum model**:

- **DOF 1:** Driveline inertia $I_d$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega_d$.
- **DOF 2:** Vehicle mass $M_v$ translating at velocity $v$.

These are coupled through the wheel radius $r_w$, the gear ratio $i_g$, and the driveline torsional stiffness $K_d$. The body pitch is governed by suspension stiffness $K_s$ and damping $C_s$ at the rear axle.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Description / Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------------|---------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | Driveline Torsional Natural Frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_d}}$ | The resonant frequency at which driveline oscillates freely |
| 2 | Damped Oscillation Frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | Actual oscillation frequency under damping $\zeta$ |
| 3 | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{C_{eq}}{2\sqrt{K_{eq} I_d}}$ | Ratio of actual damping to critical damping |
| 4 | Rear Axle Vertical Acceleration | $a_z = \dfrac{T_w \cdot L_{CG}}{I_{body}} - \dfrac{F_z^{static}}{M_v}$ | Vehicle pitch-induced vertical acceleration at rear axle |
| 5 | Slip-Based Torque Clamp | $T_{eng}^{cmd} = T_{eng}^{req} - K_p \cdot (\omega_{eng} - \omega_{in})$ | Torque reduction proportional to slip error |
| 6 | Line Pressure Ramp Profile | $P_{line}(t) = P_{fill} + \dot{P}_{ramp} \cdot t$ | Pre-fill to full-engagement pressure ramp |
| 7 | Settling Time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \cdot \omega_n}$ | Time for oscillation to decay within 2% of final value |
| 8 | Pitch Angle (small angle) | $\theta_{pitch} \approx \dfrac{T_{drive} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot L_r}{r_w \cdot K_{\theta}}$ | Rear-axle pitch due to drive torque |

Where $L_{CG}$ is the distance from CG to rear axle, $L_r$ is the rear overhang parameter, $i_g$ is the gear ratio, $i_0$ is the final drive ratio, and $K_{\theta}$ is the rotational pitch stiffness of the suspension.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Automotive Engineering

- **Customer Comfort:** A noticeable squat is rated as a top-3 NVH complaint in J.D. Power initial quality surveys for AT-equipped vehicles.
- **Drivability Calibration:** OEMs spend **3–6 months** tuning the N-D transition map across hundreds of test conditions.
- **Failure Indicator:** Excessive squat often signals worn engine mounts, deteriorated propshaft couplings, or low transmission fluid pressure.
- **EV Comparison:** Battery EVs do not exhibit classical N-D Squat because motor torque is electronically ramped; this is a frequently asked viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Driveline Natural Frequency From First Principles

The driveline is modelled as a torsional spring-mass system. The driveline equivalent inertia is $I_d$ and the equivalent stiffness is $K_{eq}$.

**Step 1: Write the free-body equation of the driveline inertia.**

When the clutch engages and applies a step torque $T_{step}$, the inertia $I_d$ rotates against the torsional spring $K_{eq}$.

$$
I_d \cdot \ddot{\theta}_d = T_{step} - K_{eq} \cdot \theta_d - C_{eq} \cdot \dot{\theta}_d
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to standard second-order form.**

$$
I_d \cdot \ddot{\theta}_d + C_{eq} \cdot \dot{\theta}_d + K_{eq} \cdot \theta_d = T_{step}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the undamped natural frequency.**

For the homogeneous part, divide throughout by $I_d$:

$$
\ddot{\theta}_d + \dfrac{C_{eq}}{I_d} \dot{\theta}_d + \dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_d} \theta_d = 0
$$

By the standard definition of natural frequency in second-order systems:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_d}}
$$

**Step 4: Identify the damping ratio.**

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{C_{eq}}{2 \sqrt{K_{eq} \cdot I_d}}
$$

**Step 5: Express the complete time-domain solution.**

The response of the driveline angle to a step torque $T_{step}$ is:

$$
\theta_d(t) = \dfrac{T_{step}}{K_{eq}} \left[ 1 - e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \left( \cos \omega_d t + \dfrac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin \omega_d t \right) \right]
$$

where $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rear Axle Squat Acceleration

The drive torque at the wheel $T_w$ produces a pitch moment on the vehicle body.

**Step 1: Express the pitch moment about the centre of gravity.**

$$
M_{pitch} = T_w - F_{drag} \cdot r_w
$$

Here $F_{drag}$ is the total road load opposing motion.

**Step 2: Convert wheel torque to vehicle longitudinal acceleration.**

$$
a_{long} = \dfrac{T_w \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta - F_{drag}}{M_v \cdot r_w}
$$

**Step 3: Translate longitudinal acceleration to rear-axle vertical displacement.**

Using the suspension geometry, the rear-axle vertical deflection $\Delta z_r$ is:

$$
\Delta z_r = \dfrac{M_v \cdot a_{long} \cdot L_r}{K_s \cdot L}
$$

where $L$ is the wheelbase, $L_r$ is the CG-to-rear-axle distance, and $K_s$ is the rear suspension vertical stiffness.

**Step 4: Differentiate twice to get the squat acceleration.**

$$
a_{squat}(t) = \dfrac{d^2 (\Delta z_r)}{dt^2}
$$

Substituting and applying the step-torque response derived in 3.1:

$$
a_{squat}(t) = \dfrac{L_r}{K_s \cdot L} \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{dt^2}\left( \dfrac{T_{step}}{K_{eq}} \left[ 1 - e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \left( \cos \omega_d t + \dfrac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin \omega_d t \right) \right] \right)
$$

Carrying out the second derivative yields the closed-form squat acceleration waveform.

### 3.3 Derivation: Torque Reduction Control Law

The standard **PI-based torque reduction strategy** uses engine speed feedback.

**Step 1: Define the slip error.**

$$
e_{slip}(t) = \omega_{eng}(t) - \omega_{in}(t)
$$

where $\omega_{eng}$ is engine speed and $\omega_{in}$ is transmission input speed.

**Step 2: Apply the PI control law.**

$$
T_{eng}^{cmd}(t) = T_{eng}^{req}(t) - K_p \cdot e_{slip}(t) - K_i \int_{0}^{t} e_{slip}(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 3: Clamp the command within physical limits.**

$$
T_{min} \leq T_{eng}^{cmd}(t) \leq T_{max}
$$

**Step 4: Output the corrected torque to the EMS over CAN bus.**

The TCU broadcasts the torque request via the **CAN (Controller Area Network) message `0x18FF1234` (Torque Management Coordination)** at a typical cycle time of **10 ms**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Control Strategies vs. Phenomena

| Control Lever | Actuator | Target Phenomenon | Typical Settling Time Reduction |
|---------------|----------|--------------------|---------------------------------|
| Engine Torque Reduction | EMS (Electronic Throttle / Fuel Cut) | Initial step torque spike | 40%–60% |
| Line Pressure Profiling | Solenoid Valves in Valve Body | Clutch engagement rate | 30%–45% |
| Active Engine Mount Damping | Electro-rheological mounts | Driveline resonance rejection | 20%–35% |
| Lock-up Clutch Slip Control | Torque Converter Clutch Solenoid | Filter high-frequency torque | 25%–40% |
| Pre-fill Pressure Modulation | Pressure Regulator Solenoid | Initial engagement jerk | 50%–70% |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: N-D Squat Detection and Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DriverInput["Driver Selector Lever Shift to Drive"] --> TCUInput["TCU Receives PRNDL Signal"]
    TCUInput --> PreFill["Pre-Fill Clutch with Line Pressure 2.5 bar"]
    PreFill --> Engagement["Engagement Ramp Begins Torque Applied"]
    Engagement --> DrivelineExc["Driveline Excited Torsional Vibration Begins"]
    DrivelineExc --> BodyPitch["Body Pitches Rear Squats"]
    BodyPitch --> AccelSensor["Rear Accelerometer Reads Vertical Acceleration"]
    BodyPitch --> SpeedSensor["Wheel Speed Sensor Reads Vehicle Velocity"]
    AccelSensor --> TCU
    SpeedSensor --> TCU
    TCU["TCU Computes Slip Error and Required Torque"] --> TorqueRequest["Broadcast Torque Reduction Request"]
    TorqueRequest --> EMS["Engine Management System Reduces Throttle"]
    TorqueRequest --> LinePressure["Line Pressure Solenoid Modulates Pressure"]
    EMS --> EngineOut["Engine Torque Smoothly Ramps Up"]
    LinePressure --> ClutchEng["Clutch Engagement Smoothed"]
    EngineOut --> DrivelineSmooth["Smoothed Driveline Torque"]
    ClutchEng --> DrivelineSmooth
    DrivelineSmooth --> Settled["Squat Settled within 0.8 seconds"]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Closed-Loop N-D Torque Management

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SensorLayer["Sensor Input Layer"]
        S1["Engine Speed Sensor CKP"]
        S2["Input Shaft Speed Sensor"]
        S3["Output Shaft Speed Sensor"]
        S4["Accelerometer Z axis at Rear Axle"]
        S5["Brake Pedal Switch"]
        S6["Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph ControlLayer["TCU Control Layer"]
        C1["Signal Conditioning and Filtering"]
        C2["Slip Error Calculator"]
        C3["PI Torque Reduction Controller"]
        C4["Line Pressure Ramp Generator"]
        C5["State Machine for PRNDL"]
    end

    subgraph ActuatorLayer["Actuator Output Layer"]
        A1["Engine ECU Throttle Driver"]
        A2["Shift Solenoid Valves S1 S2"]
        A3["Line Pressure Solenoid"]
        A4["Lock Up Clutch Solenoid"]
    end

    S1 --> C1
    S2 --> C1
    S3 --> C1
    S4 --> C1
    S5 --> C1
    S6 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C5
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> A1
    C5 --> A2
    C5 --> A3
    C5 --> A4
```

### 4.3 System Block Diagram: Driveline as Torsional Spring Mass System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EngineInertia["Engine Flywheel Inertia Ie"] -->|Torsional Spring Keq| ClutchInertia["Clutch Input Inertia Ic"]
    ClutchInertia -->|Shaft Stiffness Kshaft| GearInertia["Gearbox Inertia Ig"]
    GearInertia -->|Propshaft Kprop| DiffInertia["Final Drive Inertia Id"]
    DiffInertia -->|Half Shaft Khs| WheelInertia["Wheel Inertia Iw"]
    WheelInertia -->|Contact Patch| Road["Road Reaction Force Fx"]
    BodyInertia["Vehicle Body Mass Mv"] -->|Suspension Ks and Cs| WheelInertia
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**Define Neutral to Drive Squat. List any four contributing physical factors.**

**Model Answer:**
Neutral to Drive Squat is the transient rearward pitch oscillation of a vehicle body that occurs immediately after the transmission is shifted from Neutral to Drive, caused by the sudden application of engine torque to the driveline.
Four contributing factors are:
1. Sudden engagement of clutch packs or torque converter lock-up clutch.
2. Torsional compliance of the propeller shaft and rubber couplings.
3. Longitudinal compliance of rear suspension bushings.
4. Step-input nature of driver-initiated torque demand.

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**Why do Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs) typically not exhibit N-D Squat in the classical sense?**

**Model Answer:**
In BEVs, the electric motor torque is electronically controlled by the inverter and is not applied as a sudden mechanical step. The motor controller inherently ramps the torque from zero using a programmable torque map with a defined slew rate. Furthermore, there is no multi-plate wet clutch engagement event, no torque converter, and no internal combustion engine transient fueling delay. Therefore, the driveline is never excited by a step torque input, eliminating the classical squat resonance.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the aid of a suitable 2-DOF mathematical model, derive the expression for the natural frequency of the driveline torsional mode responsible for N-D Squat. Explain each term. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe, in detail, any THREE closed-loop control strategies used by the TCU to suppress N-D Squat. Include the relevant CAN message IDs and sensor inputs in your answer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

The driveline is represented as a torsional spring-mass-damper system. The lumped driveline equivalent inertia is denoted by $I_d$, the equivalent stiffness by $K_{eq}$, and the equivalent viscous damping by $C_{eq}$.

Equation of motion under a step torque input $T_{step}$:

$$
I_d \ddot{\theta} + C_{eq} \dot{\theta} + K_{eq} \theta = T_{step}
$$

Dividing by $I_d$:

$$
\ddot{\theta} + \dfrac{C_{eq}}{I_d} \dot{\theta} + \dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_d} \theta = \dfrac{T_{step}}{I_d}
$$

The undamped natural frequency of the system is:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{eq}}{I_d}}
$$

The damping ratio is:

$$
\zeta = \dfrac{C_{eq}}{2 \sqrt{K_{eq} I_d}}
$$

The damped natural frequency is:

$$
\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}
$$

**Term explanations:**
- $I_d$ — Equivalent rotational inertia of all rotating components (engine flywheel + clutch disc + gearbox input + propshaft + differential + wheels), reduced to the engine side via the square of gear ratios.
- $K_{eq}$ — Equivalent torsional stiffness of the driveline, dominated by rubber couplings at the propshaft joints and half-shaft boots.
- $C_{eq}$ — Equivalent viscous damping arising from internal friction in couplings and aerodynamic damping of rotating shafts.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Strategy 1: Engine Torque Reduction via EMS Coordination.**
When the TCU detects the PRNDL transition from N to D, it sends a torque request reduction message over CAN. The standard message ID used is `0x18FF1010` (OEM-specific, example shown). The torque is reduced by 30%–60% of driver demand for the first **200 ms**, then ramped back linearly. Sensor inputs used: Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (PPS), Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP), and Transmission Input Speed Sensor.

**Strategy 2: Line Pressure Profiling Using Solenoid Valves.**
The TCU commands the pressure control solenoid to ramp the line pressure from a low pre-fill value (~2 bar) to full engagement pressure (~8 bar) over a calibrated time window of **150–400 ms**, governed by the transmission fluid temperature. This controls the rate of clutch piston stroke and hence the rate of torque transfer. Sensor inputs: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor (TFT), Line Pressure Sensor.

**Strategy 3: Active Engine Mount Control.**
Modern premium vehicles use electro-rheological or magnetorheological engine mounts. The TCU sends a damping command message (typical CAN ID `0x18FF2030`) to the mount controller to increase mount damping during the engagement transient, suppressing the body pitch oscillation. Sensor inputs: Vertical body accelerometers at the engine mounting points, CKP for engine vibration phase reference.

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the closed-form time-domain expression for the rear axle squat acceleration when a step torque $T_{step}$ is applied at the driveline. State clearly the assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the role of the Transmission Control Unit (TCU) in N-D Squat suppression. Describe the typical TCU state machine for the N-to-D transition with at least five distinct states. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The driveline is modelled as a single-degree-of-freedom torsional spring-mass-damper system.
2. The vehicle body is treated as a rigid mass pivoting about a transverse axis through the centre of gravity.
3. Suspension damping is linear viscous.
4. Vehicle speed at the moment of engagement is small (initial launch condition), so aerodynamic drag is neglected.
5. The driveline torque applied is a step function.

**Derivation:**

Step 1: Driveline torsional equation:

$$
I_d \ddot{\theta}_d + C_{eq} \dot{\theta}_d + K_{eq} \theta_d = T_{step} u(t)
$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

Step 2: Solution of the underdamped second-order system:

$$
\theta_d(t) = \dfrac{T_{step}}{K_{eq}} \left[ 1 - e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \left( \cos \omega_d t + \dfrac{\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin \omega_d t \right) \right]
$$

Step 3: Torque transmitted to wheels:

$$
T_w(t) = K_{eq} \cdot \theta_d(t)
$$

Step 4: Vehicle pitch moment and rear-axle vertical deflection:

$$
\Delta z_r(t) = \dfrac{L_r}{K_s \cdot L} \cdot K_{eq} \cdot \theta_d(t)
$$

Step 5: Squat acceleration (second time derivative):

$$
a_{squat}(t) = \dfrac{L_r \cdot K_{eq}}{K_s \cdot L} \cdot \ddot{\theta}_d(t)
$$

Substituting the analytical form of $\ddot{\theta}_d(t)$:

$$
a_{squat}(t) = \dfrac{L_r \cdot T_{step}}{K_s \cdot L} \cdot \omega_n^2 \left[ e^{-\zeta \omega_n t} \left( \cos \omega_d t + \dfrac{1 - 2\zeta^2}{2\zeta \sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \sin \omega_d t \right) \right]
$$

This expression describes the oscillatory decay envelope of the rear axle squat acceleration.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The TCU is the central coordinator of N-D Squat suppression. It integrates signals from multiple sensors, executes a real-time state machine, and commands both the engine ECU and the transmission actuators. The typical state machine consists of the following states:

1. **Neutral Idle State (N_IDLE):** Transmission in Neutral, line pressure at standby (~1.5 bar), clutches drained. TCU continuously monitors PRNDL position and driver demand.

2. **Pre-Fill State (PRE_FILL):** Triggered on N→D lever movement detection. TCU commands line pressure to rise to ~2.5 bar to rapidly fill the clutch drum with hydraulic fluid, eliminating the dreaded engagement lag that itself is a perceived defect.

3. **Touch Point Search State (TOUCH_PT):** The TCU slowly increases pressure while monitoring input shaft angular velocity. The point at which $\omega_{in}$ begins to decelerate relative to $\omega_{eng}$ is identified as the touch point.

4. **Pressure Ramp State (RAMP):** From touch point, pressure is ramped according to a calibrated curve, often a sigmoid or cubic profile, to bring the clutch to full lock-up smoothly. Torque coordination messages are exchanged with EMS.

5. **Slip Control State (SLIP_CTRL):** During the final phase of engagement, a slip-based PI controller adjusts pressure to maintain slip within a narrow band (e.g., 50–150 RPM), avoiding both clutch shudder and excessive slip heat.

6. **Locked State (LOCKED):** Full engagement achieved; system transitions to closed-loop driver demand tracking. The state machine exits N-D Squat control and enters normal drive mode.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> Students commonly lose marks by (1) **omitting the assumptions** in mathematical derivations, (2) **confusing N-D Squat with launch judder** (judder is 10–25 Hz torsional vibration; squat is 1.5–4 Hz body pitch), and (3) **forgetting to mention CAN-based torque coordination** between TCU and EMS. Always explicitly state the CAN message identifiers, the bandwidth (typically 10 ms cycle time), and the SAE J1939 or ISO 15765 protocol layer used.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Recap:** N-D Squat is the low-frequency (1.5–4 Hz) rearward pitch oscillation of the vehicle body immediately after shifting from Neutral to Drive in an automatic transmission.
- **Root Cause:** Step-input torque application excites the driveline torsional mode, which couples to body pitch via suspension compliance.
- **Key Formula:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_{eq} / I_d}$ is the single most important equation.
- **Key Parameter:** Damping ratio $\zeta$ should ideally be in the range **0.4–0.7** for a well-tuned system.
- **Three-Layer Control Architecture:** Sensor layer → TCU control layer → Actuator layer.
- **Primary Actuators:** Engine EMS throttle, line pressure solenoid, lock-up clutch solenoid, active engine mounts.
- **Primary Sensors:** CKP, transmission input/output speed, PRNDL position, accelerometer at rear axle, brake pedal switch.
- **Communication:** CAN bus with cycle time of **10 ms**; typical message IDs in the `0x18FFxxxx` range for OEM-specific coordination.
- **Why BEVs Skip This:** Motor torque is electronically ramped; no clutch step-engagement event.
- **Distinguish Carefully:** N-D Squat ≠ Launch Judder ≠ Tip-In Bump. They have different frequency ranges and different root causes.
- **Industry Benchmark:** Settling time should be ≤ **0.8 s** for acceptable subjective NVH rating.
- **Calibration Practice:** Always tune at multiple transmission fluid temperatures (cold −20°C, warm 80°C, hot 110°C) because viscosity dramatically alters line pressure dynamics.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engine Torque Control. Dual Clutch Automatic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Engine Torque Control. Dual Clutch Automatic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Engine Torque Control (ETC) — Formal Definition

**Engine Torque Control (ETC)** is an integrated powertrain management function in which the Engine Control Unit (ECU) — also referred to as the Engine Management System (EMS) or Motorsteuergerät — actively regulates the torque produced at the crankshaft in real time during transient operating conditions, especially during gear changes, traction events, and driveline disturbances. In modern vehicles equipped with automatic, dual-clutch, or continuously variable transmissions, the engine torque request from the driver (via the Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor) is interpreted as a **torque demand**, and the ECU delivers the **minimum engine torque** required to execute the transmission shift smoothly while satisfying drivability, emissions, and NVH constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Engine Torque Control is the coordinated reduction and restoration of engine torque during gearshift events, achieved through simultaneous adjustment of throttle angle (electronic throttle body), spark timing (ignition advance/retard), and fuel injection quantity (injector pulse width) under closed-loop ECU supervision.

### 1.2 Dual Clutch Automatic Transmission (DCT / DSG / PDK) — Formal Definition

A **Dual-Clutch Transmission (DCT)**, also branded as **DSG (Direct Shift Gearbox)** by Volkswagen, **PDK (Porsche Doppelkupplung)** by Porsche, or **M-DCT** by BMW, is an automated manual transmission that uses **two separate clutch assemblies** and **two input shafts** to pre-select the next gear while the current gear is still engaged. This architecture eliminates the torque interruption that is characteristic of conventional stepped automatics, achieving shift times in the range of **200–400 milliseconds (ms)**.

The DCT operates on the principle of **power-shift transmission**: while one clutch is transmitting torque to the wheels (e.g., clutch K1 driving an odd gearset — 1, 3, 5, 7, R), the other clutch (K2) is simultaneously engaging the *next* expected gear on the even gearset (2, 4, 6) — pre-loaded but not yet transmitting torque.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

#### Analogy for Engine Torque Control:
Imagine you are carrying a heavy glass of water and need to hand it to a friend. If you simply *push* the glass towards them, the water sloshes and spills. Instead, you momentarily *lift* the glass (reduce the downward force), pass it smoothly, and then re-apply the weight. **Engine Torque Control is this "momentary lift"** — the ECU temporarily reduces the engine's torque output during a gearshift so that the clutch can change gears without jerking the vehicle, then restores full torque once the new gear is engaged.

#### Analogy for Dual Clutch Transmission:
Think of a relay race. The outgoing runner (clutch 1, gear 3) is still sprinting while the incoming runner (clutch 2, gear 4) has already started running alongside, baton (torque) ready. When the exchange happens, the baton is *never* stationary — there is zero "dead time." DCT works exactly this way: while one clutch delivers power, the other has the next gear pre-selected, so the transition is instantaneous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Memorize:** A modern 7-speed DCT such as the VW DQ200 executes a gearshift in approximately **200 ms**, compared to **800–1200 ms** for a traditional torque-converter automatic — a **4–6× improvement** in shift speed.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics (Bolded)

| Metric | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| DCT Shift Time | **200 – 400 ms** |
| Conventional AT Shift Time | **800 – 1200 ms** |
| Engine Torque Reduction During Shift | **30 % – 80 %** of peak torque |
| Typical DCT Clutch Diameter | **180 – 240 mm** (dry) / **140 – 170 mm** (wet) |
| Mechatronic Actuation Pressure | **4 – 18 bar** hydraulic |
| Number of Gear Ratios | **6-speed / 7-speed / 8-speed** (most common) |
| DCT Efficiency vs Torque Converter | **≈ 95 %** vs **≈ 85 %** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Engine Torque Control — Architecture & Operating Logic

#### 2.1.1 Three Coordinated Levers of Torque Control

The ECU does not have a single "torque dial." Instead, it manipulates **three independent levers simultaneously** to bring the engine's instantaneous torque output to a target value within milliseconds:

**Lever 1 — Electronic Throttle Body (ETB / Drive-by-Wire)**
- The throttle plate angle $\alpha_{th}$ is decoupled from the accelerator pedal via a **TAC (Throttle Actuator Control)** module.
- Pedal position sensor (PPS) sends a voltage signal (typically **0.5 V – 4.5 V** dual-rail) to the ECU.
- ECU commands a DC servo motor to position the throttle plate to a calculated angle.
- Torque reduction via throttle closure can be as fast as **50–100 ms**.

**Lever 2 — Ignition Timing Retard**
- Spark advance is retarded from MBT (Minimum spark advance for Best Torque) toward TDC (Top Dead Center).
- Each **1° of retard reduces torque by approximately 1 %** in the mid-RPM range.
- Can reduce torque within **20–50 ms** — the *fastest* of the three levers.

**Lever 3 — Fuel Injection Cut / Reduction**
- Individual cylinder fuel cut (e.g., skipping every 3rd injection pulse).
- Effective for large torque reductions but slower (**100–200 ms**) and risks catalyst damage if prolonged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coordination Principle:** The ECU uses a **fast actuator (ignition retard)** for the initial torque dip and a **slow actuator (throttle)** for sustained reduction. This sequencing is called **Torque Coordination** and is at the heart of modern drive-by-wire systems.

#### 2.1.2 Why Engine Torque Control is Needed During a Shift

During a DCT or AT upshift:
1. The "off-going" clutch (e.g., K1) must disengage.
2. The "on-coming" clutch (e.g., K2) must engage.
3. The synchronizer must mesh the new gear pair.

If full engine torque remains applied during this transient, the resulting **clutch friction work** ($W_{friction}$) generates heat that can exceed the clutch's thermal capacity, leading to **clutch judder**, **burnt plates**, or **incomplete shift**.

The ECU therefore enforces a **Torque Reduction Phase** ($\Delta T_{reduction}$):

$$\Delta T_{reduction} = T_{engine,max} - T_{engine,target}$$

where $T_{engine,target}$ is the minimum torque needed to keep the vehicle accelerating through the shift without bogging.

### 2.2 Dual Clutch Transmission — Deep Architecture

#### 2.2.1 The Two-Clutch Concept (Detailed)

A DCT contains:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Clutch K1** (outer clutch) | Engages odd gears: **1, 3, 5, 7, Reverse** |
| **Clutch K2** (inner clutch) | Engages even gears: **2, 4, 6** |
| **Input Shaft 1** (hollow) | Drives the odd-gear gearset |
| **Input Shaft 2** (solid, inside Shaft 1) | Drives the even-gear gearset |
| **Mechatronic Module** | Hydraulic/electromechanical actuator with ECU |
| **Synchronizers** (dog clutches) | Pre-select gears on each shaft |
| **Oil Pump** | Supplies hydraulic pressure (for wet DCT) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Wet vs Dry DCT:** In a **wet DCT**, clutches run in oil bath (better cooling, used in high-torque applications > **350 Nm**). In a **dry DCT**, clutches are air-cooled (lighter, more efficient, but limited to ~**250 Nm** torque capacity — e.g., VW DQ200 7-speed dry DCT).

#### 2.2.2 DCT Shift Sequence — Step-by-Step

Consider a 1→2 upshift at constant pedal position:

1. **Pre-selection Phase (0 ms):** ECU pre-selects 2nd gear on input shaft 2 (K2 side). Synchronizer is moved into position; gear pair is meshed but K2 clutch is open.
2. **Synchronization Phase (50–100 ms):** Speed of input shaft 2 is matched to engine speed via **active synchronization** (ECU commands a brief engine speed blip or brake-torque adjustment).
3. **Torque Handover Phase (100–200 ms):**
   - ECU commands torque reduction on engine (e.g., retard spark by **15°–25°**, close throttle by **20 %–40 %**).
   - Clutch K1 begins opening; clutch K2 begins closing.
   - Torque transfer is shared: $T_{K1} + T_{K2} = T_{demand}$
4. **Post-shift Restoration (200–400 ms):**
   - Clutch K1 fully open, K2 fully closed.
   - ECU restores engine torque to driver's demand.
   - Smooth acceleration resumes.

#### 2.2.3 Hydraulic Actuation (Wet DCT)

The mechatronic module uses a **proportional pressure control valve** to regulate clutch piston pressure:

$$P_{clutch} = \frac{T_{clutch,demand}}{\mu \cdot A_{piston} \cdot n_{friction-surfaces} \cdot r_{mean}}$$

where:
- $P_{clutch}$ = hydraulic pressure [Pa]
- $T_{clutch,demand}$ = torque to be transmitted [Nm]
- $\mu$ = friction coefficient (**0.08 – 0.14** for paper-based wet friction material)
- $A_{piston}$ = piston area [m²]
- $r_{mean}$ = mean friction radius [m]

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clutch torque capacity | $T_{max} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}$ | $F_{clamp}$ = clamping force [N], $z$ = friction surfaces |
| 2 | Torque reduction ratio | $\eta_{red} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{target}}{T_{peak}}$ | Dimensionless, **30 %–80 %** typical |
| 3 | Clutch engagement time (estimate) | $t_{eng} \approx \dfrac{J \cdot \Delta \omega}{T_{available}}$ | $J$ = inertia [kg·m²], $\Delta \omega$ = speed diff [rad/s] |
| 4 | Power transmitted | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | $T$ [Nm], $\omega$ [rad/s] |
| 5 | Clutch friction work | $W_{fric} = \int T_{rel} \cdot \Delta \omega \, dt$ | Heat generated during slip phase [J] |
| 6 | Equivalent mass inertia | $J_{eq} = J_{engine} + J_{clutch} + \dfrac{J_{wheels}}{i_{gear}^2}$ | Reflected to engine side [kg·m²] |
| 7 | Gear ratio relationship | $i_{gear} = \dfrac{\omega_{engine}}{\omega_{wheel}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Vehicle tractive force | $F_{tr} = \dfrac{T_{engine} \cdot i_{gear} \cdot i_{final} \cdot \eta_{drivetrain}}{r_{wheel}}$ | Newtons [N] |
| 9 | Mean effective pressure (engine) | $BMEP = \dfrac{4 \pi \cdot T_{engine}}{V_{displ}}$ | Pa — indicator of torque density |
| 10 | DCT shift energy loss | $W_{shift} = \dfrac{1}{2} J_{eq} \left(\omega_1 - \omega_2\right)^2$ | Energy lost as heat per shift [J] |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked "How does DCT achieve fast shifts?", the model answer must contain three keywords: **pre-selection**, **two clutches**, and **torque handover without interruption** (power-shift). Examiners award marks for these exact terms.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Performance Vehicles:** Porsche 911 PDK, Ferrari F1 DCT — shift times below **100 ms**, faster than a human driver.
- **Economy Vehicles:** VW DQ200 7-speed dry DCT delivers **MPG figures comparable to a manual** (~5 % better than torque-converter AT).
- **Hybrid Integration:** DCT serves as the basis for many PHEV architectures (e.g., Hyundai TMED system).
- **Racing (F1, WEC, Le Mans):** DCT is now universal — no torque interruption is critical for traction management.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Engine Torque Reduction to Avoid Clutch Overheat

**Problem Setup:** A DCT executes a 3→4 upshift. Clutch K1 must transfer from carrying 100 % torque to 0 % torque in time $\Delta t$ while the clutch slip speed is $\Delta \omega$. The clutch thermal capacity per shift is $Q_{max} = 50$ kJ. Engine peak torque $T_{peak} = 300$ Nm. Find the minimum torque reduction $\Delta T$.

**Step 1 — Energy In = Energy Out (Conservation):**

The friction energy generated during slip must not exceed the clutch thermal limit:

$$W_{friction} = \int_{0}^{\Delta t} T_{rel}(t) \cdot \Delta \omega(t) \, dt \leq Q_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Assume linear torque ramp:**

For simplicity, assume $T_{rel}(t)$ ramps linearly from $T_{peak}$ to $T_{peak} - \Delta T$ while $\Delta \omega$ remains approximately constant at **80 rad/s** for $\Delta t = 0.2$ s.

$$T_{avg} = T_{peak} - \frac{\Delta T}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$\left( T_{peak} - \frac{\Delta T}{2} \right) \cdot \Delta \omega \cdot \Delta t \leq Q_{max}$$

$$300 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.2 - \frac{\Delta T}{2} \cdot 80 \cdot 0.2 \leq 50000$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation:**

$$4800 - 8 \cdot \Delta T \leq 50000$$

This inequality is satisfied even with $\Delta T = 0$. But if the shift is repeated 5 times (e.g., 3→4→5→6→7 in quick succession, like a passing maneuver), then:

$$5 \cdot 4800 = 24000 \, J \leq 50000 \, J$$

**Step 5 — Critical Repeated-Shift Limit:**

For 8 repeated shifts:
$$8 \cdot 4800 = 38400 \, J \leq 50000 \, J$$

For 11+ rapid shifts:
$$11 \cdot 4800 = 52800 \, J > 50000 \, J \rightarrow \text{OVERHEAT}$$

**Step 6 — Apply Torque Reduction:**

To allow 15 successive shifts, energy per shift must be:
$$W_{shift,max} = \frac{50000}{15} = 3333 \, J$$

$$4800 - 8 \cdot \Delta T \leq 3333$$

$$8 \cdot \Delta T \geq 1467 \implies \Delta T \geq 183 \, Nm$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{min} \approx 183 \, Nm \quad (\approx 61\% \, \text{of} \, T_{peak})}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Clutch Pressure Required

**Problem Setup:** A wet DCT clutch has 5 friction surfaces ($z = 5$), mean friction radius $r_{mean} = 0.12$ m, piston area $A_{piston} = 25 \, cm^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$, friction coefficient $\mu = 0.10$. Required torque capacity $T_{clutch} = 250$ Nm. Find the required hydraulic pressure $P_{clutch}$.

**Step 1 — Start with the torque capacity equation:**

$$T_{clutch} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}$$

**Step 2 — Express clamping force in terms of pressure:**

$$F_{clamp} = P_{clutch} \cdot A_{piston}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$T_{clutch} = \mu \cdot P_{clutch} \cdot A_{piston} \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_{clutch}$:**

$$P_{clutch} = \frac{T_{clutch}}{\mu \cdot A_{piston} \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Substitution:**

$$P_{clutch} = \frac{250}{0.10 \cdot (25 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot 5 \cdot 0.12}$$

**Step 6 — Denominator Calculation:**

$$0.10 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-4} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$2.5 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 5 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$1.25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.12 = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7 — Final Result:**

$$P_{clutch} = \frac{250}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{P_{clutch} \approx 1.667 \times 10^{6} \, Pa \approx 16.67 \, bar}$$

This is consistent with the typical wet DCT actuation range of **4 – 18 bar**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Torque Coordination Sequence Timing

**Problem Setup:** A DCT upshift requires engine torque to drop from $T_{peak} = 320$ Nm to $T_{target} = 120$ Nm in **150 ms** total, using ignition retard first, then throttle closing.

**Step 1 — Total Torque Reduction Required:**

$$\Delta T = 320 - 120 = 200 \, Nm \quad (62.5\%)$$

**Step 2 — Ignition Retard Capability:**

Each **1° retard** reduces torque by approximately **0.8 %** at mid-RPM. To achieve **30 % reduction**:
$$\theta_{retard} = \frac{30}{0.8} = 37.5°$$

This is mechanically safe (within the **40°–50°** retard range before misfire).

**Step 3 — Time for Ignition Phase (Phase 1, 0 – 60 ms):**

Ignition retard is near-instantaneous (per-cycle). Reducing torque by **30 %** in **60 ms**:
$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{96}{0.060} = 1600 \, Nm/s$$

**Step 4 — Throttle Closure Phase (Phase 2, 60 – 150 ms):**

Remaining reduction = **32.5 %** over **90 ms**:
$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{104}{0.090} = 1155 \, Nm/s$$

This is within ETB actuator bandwidth (**1500 Nm/s** typical).

**Step 5 — Restoration Phase (150 – 350 ms):**

Engine torque restored to driver demand over **200 ms** with controlled ramp to avoid wheelspin:
$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{200}{0.200} = 1000 \, Nm/s$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison: AT vs CVT vs DCT vs AMT

| Parameter | Conventional AT (Torque Converter) | CVT | **DCT** | AMT (Automated Manual) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shift Interruption | Yes (~800 ms) | Yes (stepless) | **No (~200 ms)** | Yes (~500 ms) |
| Efficiency | ~85 % | ~88 % | **~95 %** | ~93 % |
| Torque Capacity | Very high | Medium | **Medium-High** | High |
| Complexity | High | Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| Clutch Wear | None (fluid) | None | **Moderate-High** | Moderate |
| Cost | High | Medium | **High** | Low |
| Driving Feel | Smooth, slushy | Elastic | **Sporty, crisp** | Jerky in slow traffic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** DCT is sometimes confused with AMT (Automated Manual Transmission). The critical difference is **power-shift capability**. AMT has torque interruption; DCT does not. This is a frequently tested KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Engine Torque Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Driver Accelerator Pedal Input"] --> nodeB["Pedal Position Sensor PPS"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Engine Control Unit ECU"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Torque Demand Calculator"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"Shift Event Detected"}
    nodeE -- No --> nodeF["Normal Torque Output"]
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeG["Torque Reduction Coordinator"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Ignition Timing Retard Module"]
    nodeG --> nodeI["Electronic Throttle Body ETB"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Fuel Injection Cut Logic"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Engine Torque Reduced"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Clutch Engagement Allowed"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Post Shift Torque Restoration"]
    nodeM --> nodeF
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Dual Clutch Transmission Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeEngine["Engine and Crankshaft"] --> nodeDS["Dual Mass Flywheel DMF"]
    nodeDS --> nodeK1["Clutch K1 Odd Gears"]
    nodeDS --> nodeK2["Clutch K2 Even Gears"]

    nodeK2 --> nodeShaft2["Input Shaft 2 Solid Shaft Inside Shaft 1"]

    nodeShaft2 --> nodeGearEven["Gearset 2 4 6"]

    nodeOut --> nodeDiff["Differential"]
    nodeDiff --> nodeWheels["Drive Wheels"]

    nodeMech --> nodeK2

    nodeECU["Vehicle ECU"] --> nodeMech
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — DCT Shift Sequence Timing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["Driver Demands Upshift 3 to 4"] --> nodePhase1["Phase 1 Pre selection 0 to 50 ms"]
    nodePhase1 --> nodePhase2["Phase 2 Synchronization 50 to 120 ms"]
    nodePhase2 --> nodePhase3["Phase 3 Torque Handover 120 to 320 ms"]
    nodePhase3 --> nodePhase4["Phase 4 Torque Restoration 320 to 500 ms"]
    nodePhase4 --> nodeEnd["Shift Complete 4th Gear Active"]

    nodePhase1 --> nodeDetail1["ECU moves synchronizer on shaft 2 to 4th gear position"]
    nodePhase2 --> nodeDetail2["Speed matching of input shaft 2 via engine blip"]
    nodePhase3 --> nodeDetail3["Engine torque reduced by 60 percent K1 opening K2 closing"]
    nodePhase4 --> nodeDetail4["Throttle and ignition restored to driver demand"]
```
