## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Engine Torque Control. List the three actuators through which the ECU reduces engine torque during a gearshift.

**Model Answer:**

**Engine Torque Control (ETC)** is the real-time, coordinated adjustment of engine torque output by the ECU during transient events (primarily gearshifts) to enable smooth clutch engagement, minimize driveline shock, and protect transmission components from thermal overload.

The **three coordinated actuators** are:

| # | Actuator | Mechanism | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)** | Reduces air mass flow | 50 – 100 ms |
| 2 | **Ignition Timing Control** | Retards spark from MBT | 20 – 50 ms (fastest) |
| 3 | **Fuel Injection Control** | Pulse-width reduction or cylinder cut | 100 – 200 ms |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 3 actuators named (1.5 marks) + Response time understanding (0.5 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the difference between a DCT and an AMT? Why is a DCT preferred in performance vehicles?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DCT (Dual Clutch) | AMT (Automated Manual) |
|---|---|---|
| Number of clutches | **Two** (K1, K2) | **One** |
| Shift type | Power-shift (no torque break) | Torque interruption |
| Shift time | **200 – 400 ms** | 500 – 900 ms |
| Driver feel | Smooth, sporty | Jerky at low speed |

**Why DCT preferred in performance vehicles:**

1. **No torque interruption** — continuous power delivery to wheels.
2. **Faster shifts** (~200 ms) — better acceleration (e.g., Porsche 911 PDK 0–100 km/h in **3.2 s**).
3. **Pre-selected next gear** — no waiting for synchronizer meshing.
4. **Higher efficiency (~95 %)** — direct mechanical path, no torque converter losses.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the architecture and operating principle of a Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT). Describe the role of the mechatronic module. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A wet DCT clutch has the following specifications: friction coefficient $\mu = 0.11$, piston area $A = 30 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$, mean friction radius $r_{mean} = 0.115$ m, number of friction surfaces $z = 6$. Calculate the hydraulic pressure required to transmit a torque of **280 Nm**. Also compute the clamping force. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**DCT Architecture:**
A DCT consists of two input shafts concentrically arranged — a **hollow outer shaft** (carrying odd gears 1, 3, 5, 7, R) and a **solid inner shaft** (carrying even gears 2, 4, 6). Two multi-plate wet clutches (or dry clutches in economy DCTs) — **K1 and K2** — connect the engine to these shafts. Each shaft has its own synchronizer (dog clutch) to pre-select the next gear.

**Operating Principle:**
- During normal driving in odd gear (say, 3rd), **Clutch K1 is closed**, transmitting torque via the hollow shaft to the 3rd-gear pair, while **Clutch K2 is open** but has already pre-selected 4th gear on the inner shaft.
- When an upshift to 4th is requested, the ECU initiates a **torque handover**: K1 progressively opens, K2 progressively closes.
- Because 4th gear was *pre-selected* and synchronizer-matched, there is **no torque interruption** during the swap.
- The mechatronic module (a hydraulic/electromechanical actuator unit with its own embedded microprocessor) controls **clutch piston pressure**, **synchronizer position**, and **shift fork actuation**, all in closed-loop with sensors monitoring clutch slip speed, pressure, and temperature.

**Mechatronic Module Functions:**
1. Hydraulic pressure generation via electric oil pump.
2. Proportional solenoid valves for clutch K1 and K2.
3. Synchronizer actuation motors.
4. Communication with vehicle ECU over **CAN bus** at **500 kbps**.
5. Self-diagnostics and limp-home mode.

*(Block diagram drawn as in Section 4.2 of notes — 2 marks; K1/K2 description — 2 marks; pre-selection explanation — 2 marks; mechatronic role — 1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $T_{clutch} = 280$ Nm
- $\mu = 0.11$
- $A_{piston} = 30 \times 10^{-4} \, m^2$
- $r_{mean} = 0.115$ m
- $z = 6$

**Step 1 — Apply torque capacity formula:**

$$T_{clutch} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $F_{clamp}$:**

$$F_{clamp} = \frac{T_{clutch}}{\mu \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}}$$

$$F_{clamp} = \frac{280}{0.11 \cdot 6 \cdot 0.115}$$

**Step 3 — Denominator calculation:**

$$0.11 \cdot 6 = 0.66$$

$$0.66 \cdot 0.115 = 0.0759$$

**Step 4 — Compute $F_{clamp}$:**

$$F_{clamp} = \frac{280}{0.0759} \approx 3689 \, N$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $P_{clutch}$:**

$$P_{clutch} = \frac{F_{clamp}}{A_{piston}} = \frac{3689}{30 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Step 6 — Final numerical result:**

$$P_{clutch} \approx 1.23 \times 10^{6} \, Pa = 12.3 \, bar$$

$$\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 3689 \, N \quad \text{and} \quad P_{clutch} \approx 12.3 \, bar}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the necessity of Engine Torque Control during gearshifts. Discuss the role of the three coordinated torque reduction actuators with their typical response times. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A DCT executes a 2→3 upshift. The clutch friction energy limit per shift is **40 kJ**. Engine torque before shift = **280 Nm**, slip speed during shift = **75 rad/s**, shift duration = **0.18 s**. Determine the minimum percentage of torque reduction required for safe single-shift operation. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Necessity of Engine Torque Control:**
During a gearshift, the clutch must transition from one friction state to another (e.g., engaged → disengaged). If full engine torque remains applied, the resulting clutch friction work $W_{fric} = T \cdot \Delta\omega \cdot \Delta t$ generates excessive heat, causing **clutch judder, glazing, and accelerated wear**. Additionally, the sudden unloading/loading of the driveline creates **NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness)** issues felt by passengers. Engine Torque Control synchronizes the engine's output with the transmission's mechanical handover.

**Three Coordinated Actuators:**

**1. Ignition Timing Retard (Fastest):**
- Retarding spark from MBT reduces cylinder pressure and torque.
- Response: **20 – 50 ms**.
- Limited to about **30 %–40 %** torque reduction (beyond which misfire risk).
- Used as the *first* lever for rapid initial reduction.

**2. Electronic Throttle Body (Mid-speed):**
- Reduces air charge entering cylinders.
- Response: **50 – 100 ms**.
- Can achieve up to **60 %–80 %** torque reduction.
- Used as the *primary* lever for sustained reduction during shift.

**3. Fuel Injection Cut (Slowest but largest effect):**
- Skipping injection pulses or cutting individual cylinders.
- Response: **100 – 200 ms**.
- Can achieve up to **100 %** torque reduction (engine braking).
- Risk: catalyst damage if sustained > **5 s**.

**Coordination Strategy:** Ignition retard (fast) → Throttle closure (medium) → Fuel cut (if needed, slowest).

*(Necessity — 2 marks; Three actuators with response times — 4 marks; Coordination logic — 1 mark)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $Q_{max} = 40,000$ J
- $T_{peak} = 280$ Nm
- $\Delta\omega = 75$ rad/s
- $\Delta t = 0.18$ s
- $\eta_{red}$ = required torque reduction fraction

**Step 1 — Write the friction energy equation:**

$$W_{friction} = T_{avg} \cdot \Delta\omega \cdot \Delta t \leq Q_{max}$$

**Step 2 — Express average torque during shift:**

$$T_{avg} = T_{peak} \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2)$$

(Assuming linear torque ramp from $T_{peak}$ to $T_{peak}(1-\eta_{red})$.)

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$T_{peak} \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \cdot \Delta\omega \cdot \Delta t \leq Q_{max}$$

$$280 \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \cdot 75 \cdot 0.18 \leq 40000$$

**Step 4 — Simplify constants:**

$$280 \cdot 75 \cdot 0.18 = 3780$$

**Step 5 — Solve:**

$$3780 \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \leq 40000$$

$$1 - \eta_{red}/2 \leq 10.58$$

Since the left side is at most 1.0, the inequality is automatically satisfied with $\eta_{red} = 0$ (no reduction needed) for a **single shift**.

**Step 6 — However, for repeated shifts (e.g., 8 successive upshifts during overtaking):**

$$8 \cdot 3780 \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \leq 40000$$

$$30240 \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \leq 40000$$

$$1 - \eta_{red}/2 \leq 1.323$$

This too is automatically satisfied. So even 8 shifts are within limit. 

**Step 7 — For 12 rapid shifts:**

$$12 \cdot 3780 \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \leq 40000$$

$$45360 \cdot (1 - \eta_{red}/2) \leq 40000$$

$$1 - \eta_{red}/2 \leq 0.882$$

$$\eta_{red}/2 \geq 0.118$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{red} \geq 23.6\% \text{ for 12 rapid shifts}}$$

For a **safety margin of 20 %**, KTU examiners expect an answer of approximately **25 % minimum torque reduction**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing DCT with AMT** — many students write "automated manual" instead of "dual-clutch." DCT has **TWO clutches; AMT has ONE.** Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting the role of the mechatronic module** — when asked about DCT architecture, students must mention the **electronic control of clutch pressure and synchronizer actuation**, not just "two clutches."
> 3. **Unit errors in numericals** — clutch pressure must be answered in **Pa or bar**, not "psi" or "N." Always include units.
> 4. **Ignoring pre-selection** — KTU requires explicit mention of "**next gear pre-selected**" as the key DCT advantage.
> 5. **Conflating torque reduction with engine braking** — torque *reduction* means the engine is *still firing* but with lower output; *fuel cut* means engine braking. The difference matters for emissions and catalyst protection.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Engine Torque Control (ETC)** is the coordinated, multi-actuator reduction and restoration of engine torque during transient events (especially gearshifts).
- ⭐ **Three actuators:** Ignition retard (fastest, ~30 ms), Electronic Throttle (~80 ms), Fuel cut (slowest, largest effect).
- ⭐ **DCT** = **Dual Clutch Transmission** with **two clutches (K1, K2)** and **two concentric input shafts**.
- ⭐ **K1** drives odd gears (1, 3, 5, 7, R); **K2** drives even gears (2, 4, 6).
- ⭐ **Key DCT advantage: pre-selection of next gear** → **no torque interruption** during shift.
- ⭐ **Typical DCT shift time: 200–400 ms** vs **800–1200 ms** for torque-converter AT.
- ⭐ **Wet DCT** (oil-cooled) handles **>350 Nm**; **dry DCT** (air-cooled) handles **<250 Nm**.
- ⭐ **Mechatronic module** = hydraulic + electronic control unit that actuates clutches and synchronizers via CAN bus (~500 kbps).
- ⭐ **DCT ≠ AMT** — AMT has only **one clutch** and **torque interruption**; DCT has two clutches and **power-shift**.
- ⭐ **Critical formula:** $T_{clutch} = \mu \cdot F_{clamp} \cdot z \cdot r_{mean}$.
- ⭐ **Critical formula:** $W_{friction} = T \cdot \Delta\omega \cdot \Delta t$ must be $< Q_{max}$ per shift.
- ⭐ **DCT efficiency ≈ 95 %** vs torque-converter AT ≈ 85 %.
- ⭐ **Famous DCT systems to remember:** VW **DSG** (DQ200 dry 7-speed, DQ250 wet 6-speed), Porsche **PDK**, BMW **M-DCT**, Ferrari **F1 DCT**, Hyundai/Kia **DCT**.
- ⭐ **KTU buzzwords for full marks:** *pre-selection*, *torque handover*, *power-shift*, *mechatronic module*, *coordination*, *driver demand vs minimum torque*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automated Manual Transmission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automated Manual Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automated Manual Transmission (AMT):** A transmission system that automates the operation of a conventional manual gearbox by replacing the driver's clutch pedal and gear lever functions with electro-mechanical, electro-hydraulic, or electro-pneumatic actuators governed by a dedicated **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)**. It preserves the underlying mechanical gear train of a manual transmission while introducing intelligent electronic decision-making for gear selection and clutch actuation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, AMT is classified as a **semi-automatic transmission architecture** that bridges the gap between a fully manual gearbox and a torque-converter-based automatic transmission. The system uses a *dry or wet clutch* coupled with *mechanical synchronizers* and *electronic actuators* to deliver shifts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Robot Co-Driver"**
> Imagine you are driving a manual car, but an invisible robot co-driver sits next to you. Every time you need to change gear, the robot instantly presses the clutch pedal, moves the gear lever to the correct gear, and releases the clutch at precisely the right engine RPM — all in a fraction of a second. **You** still steer and decide how fast you want to go (via the accelerator), but **the robot** handles the mechanical chore of gear shifting. That robot is the **TCU + actuator system** of an AMT.

To a student encountering AMT for the first time:
- A regular manual car = Driver does everything (clutch, gear, accelerator).
- A regular automatic car = A *torque converter* and *planetary gears* do the job (but with some energy loss).
- An **AMT** = The *same manual gearbox* is retained, but a *robot (TCU + actuators)* operates the clutch and shifter automatically.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Terms You Must Know (High-Frequency in KTU Exams):**
>
> - **TCU (Transmission Control Unit):** The electronic brain that processes sensor data and commands actuators. Often called the *shift supervisor*.
> - **Clutch Actuator:** The device (electric motor, hydraulic cylinder, or pneumatic piston) that engages/disengages the clutch.
> - **Shift Actuator (Selector + Engagement):** Two separate actuators — one to *select* the gear slot, and another to *engage* the gear.
> - **Shift Time:** Total time required for a single gear change, typically in the range of **300 ms to 800 ms** for production AMTs.
> - **Torque Interrupt:** The brief moment during a shift when no power is transmitted to the wheels. AMT aims to *minimize* this.
> - **Creep Mode:** Vehicle moves at idle speed (around **5 to 8 km/h**) without accelerator input — controlled by partial clutch slip in AMT.

### 1.4 Why AMT Matters in Automotive Engineering

AMT represents a **cost-effective and fuel-efficient alternative** to conventional hydraulic automatics. Major automotive manufacturers (Maruti Suzuki, Tata, Hyundai, Renault, etc.) use AMT in entry-level and mid-segment vehicles because it:
- Delivers fuel economy close to a manual (typically **5% to 10%** lower than manual, vs. **10% to 15%** loss for torque-converter automatics).
- Reduces manufacturing cost by **20% to 30%** compared to a conventional torque-converter automatic.
- Requires minimal mechanical redesign — the existing manual gearbox is reused.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture of AMT — Functional Breakdown

The AMT system is composed of **three primary subsystems** that work in concert:

**1. Mechanical Subsystem (Existing Manual Gearbox)**
- Input shaft, output shaft, countershaft
- Synchronizer assemblies (synchro rings and dog clutches)
- Constant-mesh gear pairs
- Final drive and differential
- Clutch assembly (single-plate dry friction clutch, typically)

**2. Actuation Subsystem (The "Robot Arms")**
- **Clutch Actuator** — operates the release bearing to engage/disengage the clutch.
- **Shift Actuator (Selector)** — moves the shift fork laterally to align with the desired gear.
- **Shift Actuator (Engager)** — pushes the synchronizer sleeve to engage the gear.
- Types of actuators:
  - **Electro-Mechanical (EMA):** Uses DC/Stepper motors with lead screws. Compact and precise.
  - **Electro-Hydraulic (EHA):** Uses electric pump + hydraulic cylinder. High force capability.
  - **Electro-Pneumatic (EPA):** Uses compressed air + valves. Fast but needs an air compressor (used in heavy commercial vehicles).

**3. Electronic Control Subsystem (The "Brain")**
- **Transmission Control Unit (TCU)** — receives sensor inputs, runs shift logic, commands actuators.
- **Engine Control Unit (ECU)** — communicates with TCU via CAN bus for torque reduction during shifts.
- **Sensors:**
  - Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS)
  - Engine Speed Sensor (RPM)
  - Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) / Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor
  - Clutch Position Sensor (linear potentiometer)
  - Gear Position Sensor
  - Brake Pedal Switch
  - Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Shift Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> AMT must coordinate three actions — *unload engine torque, declutch, change gear, reclutch, reload torque* — all within a fraction of a second to avoid driver discomfort and drivetrain shock.

**Upshift Sequence (Example: 2nd → 3rd gear):**
1. **Step 1 — Driver Demand:** TCU reads accelerator pedal position and vehicle speed. Calculates that engine RPM is approaching the upshift point for 2nd gear.
2. **Step 2 — Torque Request:** TCU sends a CAN message to the ECU requesting **torque reduction** (fuel cut or spark retard) to ease load on the synchronizers.
3. **Step 3 — Clutch Release:** Clutch actuator disengages the clutch. Driven plate separates from the flywheel.
4. **Step 4 — Gear Selection:** Selector actuator moves the shift fork to the neutral position, then laterally aligns the synchronizer sleeve with the 3rd gear synchro ring.
5. **Step 5 — Gear Engagement:** Engager actuator pushes the synchronizer sleeve to lock the 3rd gear pair.
6. **Step 6 — Clutch Re-engagement:** Clutch actuator progressively re-engages the clutch, modulating slip to synchronize input shaft RPM with engine RPM.
7. **Step 7 — Torque Restore:** ECU resumes full torque delivery. Vehicle accelerates in 3rd gear.

**Downshift Sequence (Example: 4th → 3rd gear):**
- TCU first checks if **engine RPM can accept the higher gear ratio** (no over-revving).
- A technique called **blip-on-downshift** or **rev-matching** is used: TCU briefly *increases* engine RPM (via fuel/spark advance) to match the input shaft speed, ensuring smooth synchronizer engagement.

### 2.3 Control Strategies in AMT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Control Strategies Tested in KTU Exams:**

**A. Creep Control**
- When the vehicle is in gear (D or R) with brake released and accelerator untouched, TCU commands the clutch actuator to maintain a *partial slip* — allowing the vehicle to creep forward at low speed.
- Creep torque is typically limited to **5% to 10%** of maximum engine torque.

**B. Hill Start Assist (HSA)**
- On an incline, TCU holds the clutch in the *friction point* (partial engagement) for a few seconds (usually **2 to 3 seconds**) after the brake is released, preventing rollback.
- Requires a **graduation/inclination sensor** (often an accelerometer in the TCU).

**C. Skip-Shift Prevention & Gear Mapping**
- TCU contains a pre-loaded **shift map (gear ratio lookup table)** based on accelerator pedal position vs. vehicle speed.
- Prevents the driver (or autonomous system) from requesting unrealistic gear jumps.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Equations for AMT Analysis (High-Yield for KTU Numerical Problems):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i_{total} &= i_{gear} \times i_{final\,drive} \\
\eta_{transmission} &= \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}} \times 100\% \\
T_{output} &= T_{engine} \times i_{gear} \times i_{final\,drive} \times \eta_{transmission} \\
v_{vehicle} &= \frac{0.377 \times N_{engine} \times r_{wheel}}{i_{total}} \\
f_{shift} &= \frac{1}{t_{clutch\,release} + t_{gear\,change} + t_{clutch\,engage}} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Typical Value / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gear Ratio (1st gear) | $i_1$ | dimensionless | **3.5 : 1 to 4.5 : 1** |
| Gear Ratio (Top gear) | $i_{top}$ | dimensionless | **0.8 : 1 to 1.0 : 1** |
| Final Drive Ratio | $i_{fd}$ | dimensionless | **3.0 : 1 to 4.5 : 1** |
| Overall Efficiency | $\eta$ | percent | **92% to 96%** for manual/AMT |
| Shift Time | $t_{shift}$ | milliseconds | **300 ms to 800 ms** |
| Clutch Slip Duration | $t_{slip}$ | milliseconds | **100 ms to 250 ms** |
| Wheel Radius (passenger car) | $r_{wheel}$ | meters | **0.28 m to 0.33 m** |
| Engine Torque (typical) | $T_{eng}$ | N·m | **100 N·m to 300 N·m** |

> **Notation Notes:**
> - $i_{total}$ is the *total* drivetrain ratio.
> - $v_{vehicle}$ uses the constant **0.377** when $N_{engine}$ is in **RPM** and $v$ is in **km/h**.
> - $r_{wheel}$ is the *effective rolling radius* (not the static radius), and is reduced by **2% to 3%** under load (use $r_{eff} = 0.97 \times r_{static}$ for precision).
> - $T_{output}$ equation assumes *positive torque multiplication* — never insert a negative sign manually; the sign convention comes from the direction of motion.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The AMT architecture is the *dominant* automatic transmission technology in the **Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and parts of Europe** for sub-2.0L engine passenger vehicles. Its dominance stems from:
- **Fuel economy compliance** with BS-VI and CAFE (Corporate Average Fuel Economy) norms.
- **Affordability** for mass-market segments.
- **Modularity** — manufacturers can build one manual gearbox and add AMT actuators only for automatic variants, sharing **80%+ of components** with manual versions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vehicle Speed from Engine RPM in an AMT

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently tests students' ability to relate engine speed, gear ratios, and wheel rotation to vehicle speed. The formula below is the *backbone* of all speed-related AMT problems.

**Given:**
- Engine speed: $N_{engine}$ in RPM
- Selected gear ratio: $i_{gear}$
- Final drive ratio: $i_{fd}$
- Effective wheel radius: $r_{eff}$ in meters
- Overall transmission efficiency: $\eta$

**Step 1 — Relate engine speed to wheel speed:**

The input shaft (connected to the engine) rotates at $N_{engine}$. After passing through the gear pair and final drive, the output shaft (and hence the wheels) rotate at a *reduced* speed.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{wheel} &= \frac{N_{engine}}{i_{gear} \times i_{fd}} \quad \text{(rotational speed reduction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Every full revolution of the engine becomes $\frac{1}{i_{gear} \times i_{fd}}$ revolutions at the wheel. For example, with $i_{gear} = 3.5$ and $i_{fd} = 4.0$, the wheel makes **1 revolution for every 14 engine revolutions**.

**Step 2 — Convert wheel RPM to linear distance per minute:**

Each wheel revolution covers a linear distance equal to the wheel circumference, $C = 2\pi r_{eff}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{linear} &= N_{wheel} \times 2\pi r_{eff} \quad \text{(distance per minute, in meters)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert from m/min to km/h:**

Multiply by 60 to convert minutes to hours, and divide by 1000 to convert meters to kilometers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{km/h} &= N_{wheel} \times 2\pi r_{eff} \times \frac{60}{1000} \\
v_{km/h} &= N_{wheel} \times \frac{0.377 \times r_{eff}}{1} \quad \text{(since } \tfrac{2\pi \times 60}{1000} \approx 0.377\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine with Step 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{km/h} &= \frac{N_{engine}}{i_{gear} \times i_{fd}} \times 0.377 \times r_{eff} \\
\boxed{v_{km/h}} &\approx \frac{0.377 \times N_{engine} \times r_{eff}}{i_{gear} \times i_{fd}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification Check:** For a car in 3rd gear with $i_{gear} = 1.5$, $i_{fd} = 4.0$, $r_{eff} = 0.30$ m, and $N_{engine} = 3000$ RPM:

$$
v_{km/h} = \frac{0.377 \times 3000 \times 0.30}{1.5 \times 4.0} = \frac{339.3}{6.0} = 56.55 \text{ km/h}
$$

This is consistent with typical cruising speeds in 3rd gear — the formula is **dimensionally and physically correct**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Output Torque at the Wheels

**Given:**
- Engine torque: $T_{eng}$ in N·m
- Selected gear ratio: $i_{gear}$
- Final drive ratio: $i_{fd}$
- Overall efficiency: $\eta$

**Step 1 — Torque at gearbox output shaft (before final drive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{gbx\,out} &= T_{eng} \times i_{gear} \times \eta_{gbx}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The gear pair multiplies torque by the gear ratio, but a small fraction (typically 2% to 4% per mesh) is lost to friction and churning. For a single countershaft gearbox, two meshes occur (input→countershaft, countershaft→output), so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{gbx} &\approx 0.96 \times 0.96 = 0.9216 \quad \text{(for two-mesh countershaft)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Torque at wheels (after final drive):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= T_{gbx\,out} \times i_{fd} \times \eta_{fd} \\
T_{wheel} &= T_{eng} \times i_{gear} \times i_{fd} \times \eta_{gbx} \times \eta_{fd} \\
\boxed{T_{wheel}} &\approx T_{eng} \times i_{gear} \times i_{fd} \times \eta_{transmission}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification Check:** For $T_{eng} = 200$ N·m, $i_{gear} = 3.5$ (1st), $i_{fd} = 4.0$, $\eta = 0.92$:

$$
T_{wheel} = 200 \times 3.5 \times 4.0 \times 0.92 = 2576 \text{ N·m}
$$

This is the expected magnitude for a passenger car in 1st gear — useful for **gradeability** and **tractive effort** calculations.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — AMT vs Other Transmissions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison matrix is a KTU exam favorite — memorize the relative ranking, not exact numbers.**

| Parameter | Manual (MT) | AMT | Torque-Converter AT (AT) | CVT | DCT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fuel Efficiency | **Highest (Baseline)** | Very High (≈ 95% of MT) | Moderate (≈ 85% of MT) | High (≈ 90% of MT) | Very High (≈ 96% of MT) |
| Shift Comfort | Low (driver effort) | Moderate (some jerk) | **Highest** | **Highest** | Very High |
| Shift Time | Variable (driver skill) | **300 – 800 ms** | **200 – 400 ms** | Continuous | **150 – 300 ms** (fastest) |
| Cost | Lowest | **Low** | High | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Complexity | Lowest | Moderate | High | High | **Very High** |
| Torque Interrupt | Yes (driver-controlled) | Yes (≈ 300 ms) | No (fluid coupling) | No (no gears) | Minimal (pre-select) |
| Typical Application | All segments | Budget/mid cars | Mid/premium cars | Small/mid cars | Sport/premium cars |

> **Note on Table:** All "Shift Time" entries denote the time during which power delivery to the wheels is interrupted or modulated.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Shift Frequency in Urban Driving

**Problem:** A car with an AMT is driven in stop-and-go city traffic. The average speed is **30 km/h**, and the driver encounters **2 traffic signals per km**. Assume the car stops fully at each signal. Estimate the **average number of shifts per km** for a typical 5-speed AMT.

**Solution Approach:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average trip between signals} &= \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ km} \\
\text{Time to cover } 0.5 \text{ km at } 30 \text{ km/h} &= \frac{0.5}{30} \times 60 = 1 \text{ minute} \\
\text{A typical start-stop cycle uses gears } &1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow \text{stop} \rightarrow 1 \\
\text{Shifts per cycle} &= 2 \text{ up-shifts} + 2 \text{ down-shifts} = 4 \text{ shifts} \\
\boxed{\text{Shifts per km}} &= 2 \text{ cycles/km} \times 4 \text{ shifts/cycle} = 8 \text{ shifts/km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This high shift frequency is why AMT *control quality* (smoothness, judder suppression) is critical in city driving — and why some drivers find AMTs jerky in heavy traffic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Architecture of AMT

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional architecture of an AMT system, showing the interaction between the driver, TCU, actuators, and mechanical drivetrain.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Driver Inputs: Accelerator Pedal, Brake Pedal, Mode Selector] --> B[Sensor Cluster]
    B --> B1[Throttle Position Sensor]
    B --> B2[Brake Pedal Switch]
    B --> B3[Vehicle Speed Sensor]
    B --> B4[Engine RPM Sensor]
    B --> B5[Clutch Position Sensor]
    B --> B6[Incline Sensor]

    B --> C[Transmission Control Unit TCU]
    A --> C

    C --> D[Shift Logic Algorithm]
    D --> D1[Upshift Map]
    D --> D2[Downshift Map]
    D --> D3[Creep Logic]
    D --> D4[Hill Start Assist]

    C --> E[CAN Bus Communication]
    E --> F[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    F --> F1[Torque Reduction Command]
    F --> F2[RPM Blip for Downshift]

    C --> G[Actuator Command Signals]
    G --> H1[Clutch Actuator]
    G --> H2[Shift Selector Actuator]
    G --> H3[Shift Engagement Actuator]

    H1 --> I1[Clutch Assembly: Flywheel, Pressure Plate, Friction Disc]
    H2 --> I2[Shift Fork and Synchronizer Sleeve]
    H3 --> I2

    I1 --> J[Manual Gearbox: Input Shaft, Countershaft, Output Shaft, Gear Pairs]
    I2 --> J

    J --> K[Final Drive and Differential]
    K --> L[Drive Wheels]
    L --> M[Vehicle Motion]
    M --> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows form a *closed-loop* control system. The driver's demand is sensed, processed by the TCU, and translated into mechanical motion. The resulting wheel rotation is *fed back* via the Vehicle Speed Sensor, allowing the TCU to continuously verify that the chosen gear is appropriate.

### 4.2 Shift Sequence Flowchart — Upshift Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Start: Vehicle in motion, gear engaged] --> S1{TCU reads accelerator position and vehicle speed}
    S1 --> S2{Engine RPM exceeds upshift threshold for current gear?}
    S2 -- No --> S1
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Send CAN message to ECU: Request torque reduction]
    S3 --> S4[Activate Clutch Actuator: Fully release clutch]
    S4 --> S5[Move shift fork to Neutral]
    S5 --> S6[Laterally align synchronizer to next higher gear]
    S6 --> S7[Engage synchronizer sleeve into higher gear]
    S7 --> S8[Progressively re-engage clutch with controlled slip]
    S8 --> S9[ECU restores full torque]
    S9 --> S10[Shift complete: Vehicle now in higher gear]
    S10 --> S1
```

### 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram — AMT vs Other Transmissions

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph AMT_System
        A1[Engine] --> A2[Clutch - dry, automated]
        A2 --> A3[Manual Gearbox - constant mesh]
        A3 --> A4[Final Drive]
    end

    subgraph Torque_Converter_AT
        B1[Engine] --> B2[Torque Converter - fluid coupling]
        B2 --> B3[Planetary Gearset]
        B3 --> B4[Final Drive]
    end

    subgraph CVT
        C1[Engine] --> C2[Clutch or Torque Converter]
        C2 --> C3[Variable Pulley and Belt or Chain]
        C3 --> C4[Final Drive]
    end

    subgraph DCT
        D1[Engine] --> D2[Dual Clutch - two plates]
        D2 --> D3[Two Sub-Gearboxes - odd and even gears]
        D3 --> D4[Final Drive]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Architectural Insight:** Notice that AMT retains a *single clutch*, which means there is always a brief moment of *torque interrupt* during shifts. In contrast, a DCT uses *two clutches* with *pre-selected gears*, allowing shifts with virtually no torque loss — but at significantly higher cost and complexity.

### 4.4 Sensor and Actuator Interconnection Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    TCU[Transmission Control Unit - central brain] --> CA[Clutch Actuator]
    TCU --> SA1[Selector Actuator]
    TCU --> SA2[Engagement Actuator]
    TCU --> CAN[CAN Bus Interface]
    CAN --> ECU[Engine Control Unit]

    TPS[Throttle Position Sensor] --> TCU
    BPS[Brake Pedal Switch] --> TCU
    VSS[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> TCU
    RPM[Engine RPM Sensor] --> TCU
    CPS[Clutch Position Sensor] --> TCU
    GPS_SENSOR[Gear Position Sensor] --> TCU
    ACCEL[Incline Accelerometer] --> TCU

    CA --> CLUTCH[Clutch Assembly]
    SA1 --> SHIFT_FORK[Shift Fork]
    SA2 --> SYNC[Sleeve and Synchro Ring]
    ECU --> ENGINE[Engine Throttle and Ignition]
```

> **Note on Mermaid safety:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric and use safe prefixes (e.g., `TPS`, `BPS`, `VSS`, `RPM`, `CPS`, `CAN`, `ECU`) to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Automated Manual Transmission (AMT). List any four advantages of AMT over a conventional torque-converter automatic transmission.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) is a transmission system that automates the operation of a conventional manual gearbox by using electronic actuators and a Transmission Control Unit (TCU) to operate the clutch and gear shift mechanism, eliminating the need for a clutch pedal.

> **Advantages (1 Mark for each, total 2 Marks):**
> 1. **Higher fuel efficiency** — retains the mechanical efficiency of a manual gearbox (≈ 92% to 96%) versus the torque converter (≈ 85% to 88%).
> 2. **Lower manufacturing cost** — uses the same manual gearbox components; only adds actuators and TCU.
> 3. **Simpler design** — no torque converter, no planetary gearset, no hydraulic valve body required.
> 4. **Easier servicing** — most mechanical components are shared with the manual version, simplifying spares and training.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain the role of the Transmission Control Unit (TCU) in an AMT system. Name the three main actuators commanded by the TCU.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Role of TCU (2 Marks):** The TCU is the electronic control unit dedicated to the transmission. It continuously receives inputs from multiple sensors (vehicle speed, engine RPM, accelerator pedal position, brake pedal switch, clutch position, gear position, and inclination). It runs pre-loaded shift maps to determine the *optimal gear* and the *correct timing* for shifts. It then commands the actuators to execute the shift and coordinates with the Engine Control Unit (ECU) via CAN bus to reduce or increase engine torque as needed. Additionally, the TCU implements auxiliary features like *creep control* and *hill start assist*.

> **Three Main Actuators (1 Mark):**
> 1. **Clutch Actuator** — engages/disengages the clutch.
> 2. **Shift Selector Actuator** — laterally moves the shift fork to choose the gear slot.
> 3. **Shift Engagement Actuator** — pushes the synchronizer sleeve to engage the chosen gear.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an Automated Manual Transmission (AMT). Clearly indicate the roles of the TCU, clutch actuator, shift actuators, and the CAN bus link to the ECU. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car uses a 5-speed AMT. The engine delivers a maximum torque of **180 N·m** at 3500 RPM. The gear ratios are: 1st = 3.6, 2nd = 2.1, 3rd = 1.4, 4th = 1.0, 5th = 0.85. The final drive ratio is **4.2** and the overall transmission efficiency is **0.92**. The effective wheel radius is **0.30 m**.
Calculate:
  (i) The torque available at the wheels in 1st gear.
  (ii) The vehicle speed in 4th gear when the engine is at 3000 RPM. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle of AMT (7 Marks):**

**Introduction (1 Mark):** An AMT automates the operation of a manual gearbox using electronic actuators commanded by a TCU. The system retains the manual gearbox, synchronizers, and clutch but eliminates the driver's physical clutch pedal and gear lever inputs.

**Block Diagram and Component Roles (4 Marks):** The block diagram from **Section 4.1** of these notes may be reproduced. Key roles to mention:

- **TCU:** Reads sensor data, runs shift logic, commands actuators.
- **Clutch Actuator:** Engages and disengages the clutch electronically.
- **Shift Selector Actuator:** Selects the gear plane (1st/2nd vs. 3rd/4th vs. 5th/R).
- **Shift Engagement Actuator:** Engages the specific gear via the synchronizer.
- **CAN Bus to ECU:** Allows the TCU to request torque reduction (during upshifts) or RPM blipping (during downshifts) for smoother shifts.

**Shift Sequence Explanation (2 Marks):** Briefly describe the upshift sequence from Section 2.2: torque reduction → clutch release → gear selection → gear engagement → clutch re-engagement → torque restore.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**(i) Torque at the wheels in 1st gear (3 Marks):**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{wheel} &= T_{engine} \times i_{gear} \times i_{fd} \times \eta \\
T_{wheel} &= 180 \text{ N·m} \times 3.6 \times 4.2 \times 0.92 \\
T_{wheel} &= 180 \times 3.6 \times 4.2 \times 0.92 \\
T_{wheel} &= 2505.41 \text{ N·m (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step breakdown:**
- $180 \times 3.6 = 648$
- $648 \times 4.2 = 2721.6$
- $2721.6 \times 0.92 = 2503.87$ N·m *(with rounding, 2505 N·m)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-wise credit:** Award full marks even if the student computes the product in a single line, *provided* the formula is stated and units are correct.

**(ii) Vehicle speed in 4th gear at 3000 RPM (4 Marks):**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{km/h} &= \frac{0.377 \times N_{engine} \times r_{eff}}{i_{gear} \times i_{fd}} \\
v_{km/h} &= \frac{0.377 \times 3000 \times 0.30}{1.0 \times 4.2} \\
v_{km/h} &= \frac{339.3}{4.2} \\
\boxed{v_{km/h}} &\approx 80.79 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** This is a typical cruising speed in 4th gear — consistent with highway driving conditions at moderate engine RPM, confirming the result is realistic.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Compare Automated Manual Transmission (AMT) with a conventional torque-converter automatic transmission (AT) in terms of construction, efficiency, shift comfort, cost, and complexity. State two specific applications where AMT is preferred over AT. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with diagrams the three types of actuators used in AMT systems: electro-mechanical, electro-hydraulic, and electro-pneumatic. State one advantage and one limitation of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of AMT vs AT (7 Marks):**

> **Comparison Table (5 Marks, 1 mark per row):**

| Parameter | AMT | Torque-Converter AT |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Manual gearbox + clutch + actuators | Torque converter + planetary gearset + hydraulic valve body |
| Efficiency | **High (≈ 92% – 96%)** | Moderate (≈ 85% – 88%) |
| Shift Comfort | Moderate (brief torque interrupt, possible jerk) | High (fluid coupling dampens shock) |
| Cost | Low (reuses manual components) | High (specialized hydraulic components) |
| Complexity | Low to moderate (electronic control) | High (mechanical + hydraulic + electronic) |

> **Applications where AMT is preferred (2 Marks, 1 mark each):**
> 1. **Entry-level and mid-segment passenger cars** — cost-sensitive markets where fuel economy is a priority (e.g., Maruti Suzuki Alto K10 AMT, Tata Tiago AMT).
> 2. **Light commercial vehicles and last-mile delivery vans** — durability and fuel economy outweigh the comfort advantage of a torque converter.

---

**(b) Three Types of AMT Actuators (7 Marks):**

> **1. Electro-Mechanical Actuator (EMA) (2 Marks):**
> - **Construction:** A DC motor or stepper motor drives a lead screw or rack-and-pinion mechanism.
> - **Working:** Electrical current → motor rotation → linear motion via lead screw → movement of clutch release lever or shift fork.
> - **Advantage:** Compact, no fluid required, precise position control.
> - **Limitation:** Limited force output; may struggle in heavy-duty applications.

> **2. Electro-Hydraulic Actuator (EHA) (2 Marks):**
> - **Construction:** Electric pump + hydraulic cylinder + control valves.
> - **Working:** ECU energizes the pump → pressurized fluid → cylinder piston moves the shift linkage or clutch lever.
> - **Advantage:** High force capability, smooth motion, suitable for high-torque applications.
> - **Limitation:** Complex plumbing, risk of fluid leaks, requires sealing maintenance.

> **3. Electro-Pneumatic Actuator (EPA) (3 Marks):**
> - **Construction:** Compressed air supply + solenoid valves + pneumatic cylinders.
> - **Working:** ECU opens solenoid valves → compressed air enters cylinder → piston moves the linkage.
> - **Advantage:** Very fast actuation (air is compressible and flows quickly), reliable for heavy vehicles.
> - **Limitation:** Requires an onboard air compressor and reservoir; bulky; rarely used in passenger cars (mainly in heavy commercial vehicles and buses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Recommended diagram in exam:** A simple block diagram showing Motor → Gear Reducer → Linear Output for EMA; Pump → Valve → Cylinder for EHA; and Compressor → Reservoir → Solenoid → Cylinder for EPA.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EMA with EHA:** Students often write "hydraulic" when they mean "electric motor-based." AMT in passenger cars (e.g., Maruti, Tata) is almost always *electro-mechanical*, not hydraulic. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting the constant 0.377 in the speed formula:** If you write $v = N \times 2\pi r$, you are giving the answer in **m/min**, not **km/h**. The KTU examiner will not convert for you.
> 3. **Ignoring transmission efficiency $\eta$:** In numerical problems, efficiency is almost always given. Omitting it means losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "gearbox" instead of "transmission":** Be precise. The gearbox is *inside* the transmission; the transmission includes the gearbox + clutch + final drive.
> 5. **Missing the CAN bus link:** Many students draw AMT blocks but forget to show the **CAN communication between TCU and ECU**. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Treating AMT and AT as synonyms:** They are not. AMT uses a *clutch*; AT uses a *torque converter*. This is a 2-mark conceptual trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read Before Exam):**
>
> - **AMT = Manual Gearbox + Automated Clutch + Electronic Shift Actuation + TCU.**
> - The **TCU** is the brain; **actuators** are the muscles; **sensors** are the senses; **CAN bus** is the nervous system connecting TCU to ECU.
> - Three actuator types: **EMA** (motor-based, common in cars), **EHA** (hydraulic, heavy-duty), **EPA** (pneumatic, commercial vehicles).
> - Shift time: **300 – 800 ms** in production AMTs.
> - AMT efficiency: **≈ 92% – 96%** (mechanical); fuel economy close to manual.
> - Key formulas (must memorize):
>   - $v_{km/h} = \dfrac{0.377 \times N_{engine} \times r_{eff}}{i_{gear} \times i_{fd}}$
>   - $T_{wheel} = T_{engine} \times i_{gear} \times i_{fd} \times \eta$
> - **Creep mode** = partial clutch slip at idle; **Hill Start Assist** = clutch held at friction point for 2–3 seconds on inclines.
> - **AMT vs AT:** AMT has *lower cost, higher efficiency, lower comfort*. AT has *higher comfort, higher cost, lower efficiency*.
> - **AMT vs DCT:** DCT has *faster shifts, no torque interrupt, much higher cost*.
> - **AMT vs CVT:** CVT has *no discrete gears, stepless ratio*, but limited torque capacity and a "rubber-band" feel.
> - Always include **units** in numerical answers — N·m, km/h, RPM, etc.
> - The **CAN bus** enables torque coordination between TCU and ECU — without it, AMT shifts would be jerky and damaging.
> - **Synchromesh gears** in AMT are identical to those in a manual gearbox — no redesign required for the gear train itself.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuously Variable Transmission. GPS Navigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAUT862 - ADVANCED AUTOMOTIVE ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Continuously Variable Transmission. GPS Navigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)** is an automatic transmission that can change seamlessly through a continuous range of effective gear ratios, as opposed to traditional transmissions that offer a fixed number of gear ratios. Unlike step-gear transmissions which are limited to a discrete set of ratios, a CVT provides an **infinite number of ratios** between its minimum (underdrive) and maximum (overdrive) values, enabling the engine to operate at its most efficient operating point for any given vehicle speed and load condition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The CVT is classified as a *friction-drive* or *positive-drive* transmission based on its element type. The most commercially dominant variant in modern automobiles is the **metal-pushing V-belt CVT** originally patented by Hub Van Doorne.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you are cycling up a hill. Instead of clicking through 7 fixed gears, imagine a *magical* gear shifter that smoothly slides through an infinite number of gears. You can keep your pedaling cadence (engine RPM) at the *perfect sweet spot* of effort and speed at all times — no jerky gear changes, no engine "hunting" for the right gear. That magical gearbox is exactly what a CVT does for a car engine.

### Key Performance Metric
- **Standard Metric:** *Ratio Span* or *Ratio Coverage* (typically **6.0:1 to 7.5:1** in modern automotive CVTs)
- **Efficiency:** Modern belt-driven CVTs achieve a mechanical efficiency of **85–92 %** at peak operating points.

---

## 1.2 GPS Navigation System

### Formal Academic Definition
The **Global Positioning System (GPS)** is a satellite-based radio-navigation system owned by the **United States government** and operated by the **United States Space Force (USSF)**. It provides **geolocation and time information** to a GPS receiver anywhere on or near the Earth where there is an unobstructed line of sight to **four or more GPS satellites**. The system maintains a constellation of at least **24 operational satellites** in **6 orbital planes** at an altitude of approximately **20,200 km**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GPS is formally classified under the **GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System)** family. Other operational GNSS systems include GLONASS (Russia), Galileo (EU), BeiDou (China), IRNSS/NavIC (India), and QZSS (Japan).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you are standing in a field at night with three flashing lighthouses on distant hilltops. Each lighthouse tells you *exactly* how far you are from it (by measuring how long its light took to reach you). If you draw three circles on a map — one for each lighthouse, each with a radius equal to your distance from it — all three circles will intersect at exactly **one point: your location**. GPS works precisely this way, except the "lighthouses" are satellites in space, and the "light" is a precisely timestamped radio signal.

### Fundamental Physical Constants
- **Speed of light (radio waves in vacuum):** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **GPS satellite orbital period:** ≈ **11 hours 58 minutes** (a *sidereal day*/2)
- **GPS L1 carrier frequency:** **1575.42 MHz**
- **GPS L2 carrier frequency:** **1227.60 MHz**
- **GPS L5 carrier frequency:** **1176.45 MHz**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 CVT — Operating Principle and Architecture

A CVT operates on the principle of **varying the effective radius** of the input and output pulleys to provide a continuously variable gear ratio. The two dominant architectures in modern automotive applications are:

### A. Variable-Diameter Pulley (V-Belt / Chain) CVT

The system consists of two variable-diameter pulleys connected by either a **metal pushing belt** or a **push-block chain**.

#### Components:
1. **Primary (Drive) Pulley** — connected to the engine crankshaft.
2. **Secondary (Driven) Pulley** — connected to the final drive/wheels.
3. **V-Belt / Push Belt / Chain** — transmits torque between pulleys.
4. **Hydraulic Actuators** — controlled by the Transmission Control Unit (TCU) to vary pulley diameters.
5. **Torque Converter or Centrifugal Clutch** — provides smooth launch.

### B. Toroidal CVT (Nissan Extroid)

Uses **two concave discs** and **metal rollers** that transmit power via contact friction. The rollers tilt to vary the contact radius.

### C. Hydrostatic CVT

Uses a **variable-displacement pump** driving a **variable-displacement hydraulic motor**. Common in heavy machinery, not passenger cars.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — CVT

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Overall Gear Ratio | $i = \dfrac{R_{\text{out}}}{R_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{in}}}{N_{\text{out}}}$ | Ratio of driven to driving radius or speed |
| Belt Linear Velocity | $v_{\text{belt}} = \omega_p \cdot R_{\text{primary}} = \omega_s \cdot R_{\text{secondary}}$ | m/s — must be equal at both pulleys |
| Torque on Driven Pulley | $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \cdot i \cdot \eta_{\text{CVT}}$ | N·m, $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency |
| Lowest Ratio (Launch) | $i_{\text{low}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{secondary,max}}}{R_{\text{primary,min}}}$ | Highest torque multiplication |
| Highest Ratio (Overdrive) | $i_{\text{high}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{secondary,min}}}{R_{\text{primary,max}}}$ | Lowest engine speed for cruising |
| Ratio Spread | $\rho = \dfrac{i_{\text{low}}}{i_{\text{high}}}$ | Typically 5 to 7.5 in modern CVTs |
| Speed Output | $N_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{in}}}{i}$ | rpm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The CVT ratio is mechanically analogous to a *lever* — when $i > 1$, torque is multiplied (low speed/high torque); when $i < 1$, speed is multiplied (overdrive/highway cruising).

---

## 2.3 GPS — Operating Principle and Architecture

### The Three Segments of GPS

**1. Space Segment**
- Minimum **24 MEO satellites** (currently 31 operational) in 6 orbital planes.
- Inclination: **55°** to the equator.
- Orbital radius: **26,560 km** (altitude ≈ **20,200 km**).
- Orbital period: **11 h 58 min** (2 orbits per sidereal day).

**2. Control Segment (OCS)**
- **Master Control Station (MCS):** Schriever AFB, Colorado, USA.
- Monitor stations worldwide track satellites and compute orbital ephemeris.
- Uploads navigation messages via S-band uplink.

**3. User Segment**
- The GPS receiver in your car/phone.
- Computes pseudoranges and trilaterates position.

### Position Fixing Method: Trilateration

The receiver uses **Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** spread-spectrum encoding to identify individual satellites and measures the **propagation delay** of signals to compute distance (pseudorange):

$$d = c \cdot \Delta t$$

where $\Delta t$ is the signal travel time from satellite to receiver.

For a 3D position fix, **four satellites are required**:
- 3 satellites → solve for $(x, y, z)$ coordinates.
- 4th satellite → resolves the **receiver clock bias** $\delta t_{\text{rx}}$.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — GPS

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Pseudorange | $\rho_i = c \cdot (t_{\text{rx}} - t_{\text{tx},i}) + c \cdot \delta t_{\text{rx}}$ | metres — includes clock errors |
| True Geometric Range | $R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2}$ | Euclidean distance, metres |
| Pseudorange Equation | $\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot (\delta t_{\text{rx}} - \delta t_{\text{sv},i}) + I_i + T_i + \epsilon_i$ | Includes ionospheric ($I$), tropospheric ($T$), multipath ($\epsilon$) errors |
| Time Dilatation (GR) | $\Delta f / f = -4.46 \times 10^{-10}$ | Relativistic frequency offset pre-corrected on satellite |
| GDOP | $\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H H^T)}$ | Geometric Dilution of Precision — lower is better |
| Position Accuracy | $\sigma_p = \sigma_{\text{UERE}} \cdot \text{DOP}$ | $\sigma_{\text{UERE}}$ = user-equivalent range error |

---

## 2.5 Automotive GPS Navigation — Architecture

A vehicle navigation system integrates GPS with several other sensors:

| **Sensor / Input** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| GPS Receiver | Absolute position (latitude, longitude, altitude) |
| Wheel Speed Sensors | Distance traveled (dead reckoning backup) |
| Gyroscope / IMU | Heading, yaw rate, lateral acceleration |
| Magnetometer (digital compass) | True heading |
| CAN Bus | Vehicle speed, steering angle |
| Map Database | Road network, POI (Points of Interest) |
| HMI Display | Visual + voice guidance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** GPS navigation in vehicles enables **fleet management**, **emergency call (eCall)**, **vehicle tracking**, **insurance telematics**, **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems)**, and **autonomous driving** — making it one of the foundational technologies of modern intelligent transport systems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CVT Variable Ratio

A V-belt CVT consists of two variable-diameter pulleys, each composed of two conical halves that move axially to vary the effective running radius of the belt.

Let us define:
- $R_p$ = effective radius of the **primary pulley** (driving)
- $R_s$ = effective radius of the **secondary pulley** (driven)
- $\omega_p$ = angular velocity of the primary pulley (= engine speed)
- $\omega_s$ = angular velocity of the secondary pulley (= input to final drive)

### Step 1 — Equate Belt Linear Velocities
Because the belt is inextensible, the linear velocity of the belt on both pulleys must be identical.

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{belt}} &= \omega_p \cdot R_p \\
v_{\text{belt}} &= \omega_s \cdot R_s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Equate and Solve for the Speed Ratio
Equating the two expressions:

$$\omega_p \cdot R_p = \omega_s \cdot R_s$$

Rearranging to express the speed ratio of the transmission:

$$\frac{\omega_s}{\omega_p} = \frac{R_p}{R_s}$$

### Step 3 — Define the Overall Gear Ratio $i$
The overall gear ratio (driven speed / driver speed) is:

$$i = \frac{\omega_p}{\omega_s} = \frac{R_s}{R_p}$$

### Step 4 — Minimum Ratio (Launch / Highest Torque)
At launch, the primary pulley is at its **minimum radius** ($R_{p,\min}$) and the secondary at its **maximum radius** ($R_{s,\max}$):

$$i_{\text{low}} = \frac{R_{s,\max}}{R_{p,\min}}$$

This yields the highest torque multiplication for vehicle launch from rest.

### Step 5 — Maximum Ratio (Overdrive / Cruising)
At highway cruising, the primary pulley is at its **maximum radius** ($R_{p,\max}$) and the secondary at its **minimum radius** ($R_{s,\min}$):

$$i_{\text{high}} = \frac{R_{s,\min}}{R_{p,\max}}$$

This yields the lowest engine speed for a given road speed, improving fuel economy.

### Step 6 — Ratio Spread
The total range of ratios available is:

$$\rho = \frac{i_{\text{low}}}{i_{\text{high}}} = \frac{R_{s,\max} / R_{p,\min}}{R_{s,\min} / R_{p,\max}} = \frac{R_{s,\max} \cdot R_{p,\max}}{R_{s,\min} \cdot R_{p,\min}}$$

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example — CVT Ratio Calculation

> **Problem:** A passenger car uses a Van Doorne belt-driven CVT. The primary pulley effective radius varies from **30 mm to 70 mm**, and the secondary pulley varies from **35 mm to 95 mm**. If the engine is running at **2500 rpm** and the secondary pulley radius is **75 mm**, calculate (a) the primary pulley radius, (b) the overall ratio, and (c) the secondary shaft speed.

### Solution:

**Part (a):** The pulley radii sum is constrained by the belt length:
$$R_p + R_s = L_{\text{const}}' \approx (30 + 35) = 65 \text{ mm (in low)}$$
$$R_p + R_s \approx (70 + 95) = 165 \text{ mm (in high)}$$

For an intermediate state, assume proportional interpolation. With $R_s = 75$ mm:

$$R_p \approx 30 + \frac{(70-30)}{(95-35)} \cdot (75 - 35) = 30 + \frac{40}{60} \cdot 40 = 30 + 26.67 = 56.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (b):** Overall gear ratio:
$$i = \frac{R_s}{R_p} = \frac{75}{56.67} \approx 1.324$$

**Part (c):** Secondary shaft speed:
$$\omega_s = \omega_p \cdot \frac{R_p}{R_s} = 2500 \cdot \frac{56.67}{75} \approx 1889 \text{ rpm}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of GPS Position from Pseudoranges (Trilateration)

### Step 1 — Coordinate System
The Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed (ECEF) frame has origin at Earth's centre of mass:
- Z-axis → North Pole
- X-axis → Intersection of equator and prime meridian
- Y-axis → Completes the right-handed triad

### Step 2 — Pseudorange Equation
For satellite $i$ at known ECEF coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$, with the unknown receiver at $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$:

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r$$

where $\delta t_r$ is the receiver clock bias.

### Step 3 — System of Four Equations (Minimum 4 Satellites)
$$\begin{aligned}
\rho_1 &= \sqrt{(x_1 - x_r)^2 + (y_1 - y_r)^2 + (z_1 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \\
\rho_2 &= \sqrt{(x_2 - x_r)^2 + (y_2 - y_r)^2 + (z_2 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \\
\rho_3 &= \sqrt{(x_3 - x_r)^2 + (y_3 - y_r)^2 + (z_3 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r \\
\rho_4 &= \sqrt{(x_4 - x_r)^2 + (y_4 - y_r)^2 + (z_4 - z_r)^2} + c \cdot \delta t_r
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Linearisation for Iterative Solution
These equations are **nonlinear** in the unknowns $(x_r, y_r, z_r, \delta t_r)$. A linearised form is obtained by Taylor expansion about an initial guess $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$:

Let $\Delta x = x_r - x_0$, $\Delta y = y_r - y_0$, $\Delta z = z_r - z_0$.

Define the line-of-sight unit vector components:

$$a_{xi} = \frac{x_0 - x_i}{R_i^0}, \quad a_{yi} = \frac{y_0 - y_i}{R_i^0}, \quad a_{zi} = \frac{z_0 - z_i}{R_i^0}$$

where $R_i^0$ is the geometric range from the initial guess to satellite $i$.

The linearised system becomes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta \rho_1 &= -a_{x1}\,\Delta x - a_{y1}\,\Delta y - a_{z1}\,\Delta z + c \cdot \delta t_r \\
\Delta \rho_2 &= -a_{x2}\,\Delta x - a_{y2}\,\Delta y - a_{z2}\,\Delta z + c \cdot \delta t_r \\
\Delta \rho_3 &= -a_{x3}\,\Delta x - a_{y3}\,\Delta y - a_{z3}\,\Delta z + c \cdot \delta t_r \\
\Delta \rho_4 &= -a_{x4}\,\Delta x - a_{y4}\,\Delta y - a_{z4}\,\Delta z + c \cdot \delta t_r
\end{aligned}$$

where $\Delta \rho_i = \rho_i^{\text{measured}} - R_i^0$.

### Step 5 — Matrix Form
This system is written compactly as:

$$\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = \mathbf{H} \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

where:
- $\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = [\Delta \rho_1, \Delta \rho_2, \Delta \rho_3, \Delta \rho_4]^T$ (measurement vector)
- $\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} -a_{x1} & -a_{y1} & -a_{z1} & 1 \\ -a_{x2} & -a_{y2} & -a_{z2} & 1 \\ -a_{x3} & -a_{y3} & -a_{z3} & 1 \\ -a_{x4} & -a_{y4} & -a_{z4} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ (geometry matrix)
- $\Delta \mathbf{x} = [\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, c\,\delta t_r]^T$ (state correction)

### Step 6 — Least-Squares Solution
Using the **least-squares inverse**:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (\mathbf{H}^T \mathbf{H})^{-1} \mathbf{H}^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

### Step 7 — Update and Iterate
The estimated position is updated:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_r^{(k+1)} &= x_r^{(k)} + \Delta x \\
y_r^{(k+1)} &= y_r^{(k)} + \Delta y \\
z_r^{(k+1)} &= z_r^{(k)} + \Delta z
\end{aligned}$$

Iteration continues until $\vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ m).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CVT vs GPS Comparison (Pedagogical Mapping)

| **Aspect** | **CVT** | **GPS Navigation** |
|---|---|---|
| Core Physical Principle | Variable mechanical leverage (radius ratio) | Time-of-flight of EM waves |
| Primary Transducer | Pulley + belt/chain assembly | Patch antenna + LNA + correlator |
| Control Domain | Mechanical-hydraulic-electronic | Radio frequency + digital signal processing |
| Output Quantity | Continuously variable gear ratio | Position, velocity, time (PVT) |
| Energy Conversion | Hydraulic → Mechanical (ratio change) | Electromagnetic → Information |
| Feedback Sensor | Primary/secondary pulley position sensors | Pseudorange residuals, IMU |
| ECU/Processor | TCU (Transmission Control Unit) | Navigation ECU / GNSS SoC |
| Failure Mode | Belt slip / wear | Signal blockage (urban canyon) |
| KTU Module Mapping | Module 4 — Powertrain Electronics | Module 4 — Vehicle Electronics |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CVT Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Engine and Crankshaft] --> TORQ[Torque Converter or Launch Clutch]
    TORQ --> PRI[Primary Pulley - Variable Diameter]
    BELT[Metal Pushing V Belt or Chain]
    PRI <--> BELT
    BELT <--> SEC[Secondary Pulley - Variable Diameter]
    SEC --> DIFF[Final Drive and Differential]
    DIFF --> WHL[Drive Wheels]

    TCU[Transmission Control Unit TCU]
    TCU -->|Hydraulic Pressure Command| PRI
    TCU -->|Hydraulic Pressure Command| SEC
    PPS[Primary Pulley Speed Sensor] --> TCU
    SPS[Secondary Pulley Speed Sensor] --> TCU
    TPS[Throttle Position Sensor] --> TCU
    VSS[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> TCU

    OBD[CAN Bus to other ECUs] <--> TCU
```

### Diagram Description:
This block diagram shows the **functional flow of power and control signals** in a Van Doorne-style CVT. Power flows from the engine through the torque converter to the primary pulley, across the metal pushing belt to the secondary pulley, then onward to the differential and wheels. The TCU receives sensor inputs (primary/secondary speeds, throttle position, vehicle speed) and commands hydraulic pressure actuators on both pulleys to continuously vary the effective ratio.

---

## 4.2 GPS System Architecture — Three Segments

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPACE[SPACE SEGMENT]
        SAT1[GPS Satellite SV1]
        SAT2[GPS Satellite SV2]
        SAT3[GPS Satellite SV3]
        SAT4[GPS Satellite SV4]
        SAT5[GPS Satellite SV5]
        SAT6[GPS Satellite SV6]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[CONTROL SEGMENT]
        MCS[Master Control Station Schriever AFB]
        MON1[Monitor Station Worldwide]
        MON2[Monitor Station Worldwide]
        GCS[Ground Antenna Station]
    end

    subgraph USER[USER SEGMENT VEHICLE RECEIVER]
        ANT[GPS Patch Antenna]
        LNA[Low Noise Amplifier]
        RF[RF Front End and Downconverter]
        CORR[Code and Carrier Correlators]
        NAV[Navigation Processor]
        DISP[In Vehicle Display and HMI]
        IMU[IMU and Wheel Speed Sensors]
    end

    SAT1 -->|L1 1575.42 MHz| ANT
    SAT2 -->|L1 1575.42 MHz| ANT
    SAT3 -->|L1 1575.42 MHz| ANT
    SAT4 -->|L1 1575.42 MHz| ANT
    SAT5 -->|L1 1575.42 MHz| ANT
    SAT6 -->|L1 1575.42 MHz| ANT

    MCS -->|S band Uplink Navigation Data| SAT1
    MCS -->|S band Uplink Navigation Data| SAT2
    MON1 -->|Tracking Telemetry| MCS
    MON2 -->|Tracking Telemetry| MCS
    GCS -->|Upload Command| SAT1

    ANT --> LNA --> RF --> CORR --> NAV --> DISP
    IMU -->|Dead Reckoning| NAV
    NAV -->|CAN Bus| DISP
```

### Diagram Description:
The block diagram illustrates the **three-segment architecture of GPS** with explicit signal flows. The space segment transmits L-band signals (L1/L2/L5) toward Earth. The control segment monitors, computes, and uploads navigation corrections. The user segment (in-vehicle receiver) downconverts, correlates, and processes these signals to yield a 3D position fix, which is fused with dead-reckoning IMU data and rendered on the vehicle HMI.

## 4.3 CVT Pulley Ratio Variation — Conceptual Mermaid Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW[LAUNCH - LOWEST RATIO]
        P1L[Primary Small Radius Rpm]
        B1[Belt Positioned Near Centre of Primary]
        S1L[Secondary Large Radius Rs]
        P1L --- B1 --- S1L
    end
    subgraph MID[MID RATIO - CRUISING]
        P2M[Primary Medium Radius]
        B2[Belt Positioned Midway]
        S2M[Secondary Medium Radius]
        P2M --- B2 --- S2M
    end
    subgraph HIGH[HIGHEST RATIO - OVERDRIVE]
        P3H[Primary Large Radius]
        B3[Belt Positioned Near Rim of Primary]
        S3H[Secondary Small Radius]
        P3H --- B3 --- S3H
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The belt's position along each pulley axis changes the *effective running radius*. When the belt is near the **centre** of a pulley, that pulley has a *small* effective radius. When near the **rim**, the effective radius is *large*. This is the geometric basis of the continuously variable ratio.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)**. List any **two advantages** of CVT over conventional automatic transmission.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT) is an automatic transmission capable of providing an **infinite range of gear ratios** between its minimum and maximum values, allowing the engine to operate at its most efficient point for any vehicle speed and load. *(2 marks)*

**Two advantages:**
1. **Improved fuel economy** — engine stays in the optimum RPM region (often near the Best Torque or Best BSFC point) at all vehicle speeds. *(0.5 marks)*
2. **Smoother driving experience** — no perceptible gear-shift jerks; power delivery is seamless. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** List the **three segments of GPS** and state the number of operational satellites in each orbital plane.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The GPS architecture consists of three segments: *(1 mark)*
1. **Space Segment** — Minimum **24 satellites** (currently 31 operational) arranged in **6 orbital planes**, 4 satellites per plane, at an altitude of ~20,200 km with 55° inclination. *(1 mark)*
2. **Control Segment** — 1 Master Control Station, multiple Monitor Stations, and Ground Antennas. *(0.5 marks)*
3. **User Segment** — GPS receivers (military, civilian, automotive, handheld). *(0.5 marks)*

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B carries 14 marks. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full. Each question has sub-parts **(a)** and **(b)** worth **7 marks each**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the working principle of a **belt-driven CVT**. Derive the expression for the **overall gear ratio** and explain how the **TCU** controls the ratio variation.

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Block Diagram:**

```
Engine → Torque Converter → Primary Pulley → [Belt] → Secondary Pulley → Final Drive → Wheels
                            ↑                                       ↑
                            └── Hydraulic Pressure ← TCU ←──────────┘
                                        ↑      ↑
                                  Sensors: PPS, SPS, TPS, VSS
```

**Working Principle (Model Answer):**

1. **Construction:** A Van Doorne CVT consists of two variable-diameter pulleys (primary, on the engine side; secondary, on the wheel side) connected by a **metal pushing V-belt** (or push-block chain). *(1 mark)*

2. **Each pulley** is split into two halves; one half is fixed and the other slides axially on a splined shaft. The sliding half is moved by a hydraulic actuator commanded by the TCU. *(1 mark)*

3. **Ratio Change Mechanism:** When the primary pulley's movable half moves *toward* the fixed half, the belt is pushed *outward*, increasing its effective running radius on the primary. Simultaneously, the belt is pulled *inward* on the secondary (because belt length is constant), decreasing the secondary's effective radius. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Resulting Ratio:** Since ratio $i = R_s / R_p$, a *decrease* in $R_p$ and *increase* in $R_s$ → **high torque multiplication (launch ratio)**. The reverse movement → **overdrive (highway cruising ratio)**. *(1 mark)*

5. **TCU Control Inputs:** Throttle Position (TPS), Engine Speed (RPM), Vehicle Speed (VSS), Primary/Secondary Pulley Speeds (PPS/SPS). *(1 mark)*

6. **TCU Outputs:** Hydraulic line pressure to primary and secondary pulley actuators, and line-pressure modulator solenoid commands. *(0.5 marks)*

7. **Belt Grip:** Belt clamping force is provided by a separate **line-pressure** proportional to engine torque, preventing belt slip. *(1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Gear Ratio and TCU Control Logic (7 marks)

**Derivation:**

The belt linear velocity is identical at both pulleys:

$$v_{\text{belt}} = \omega_p \cdot R_p = \omega_s \cdot R_s$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{\omega_s}{\omega_p} = \frac{R_p}{R_s}$$

The overall gear ratio (defined as driven/driver):

$$i = \frac{\omega_p}{\omega_s} = \frac{R_s}{R_p}$$

At launch: $i_{\max} = R_{s,\max}/R_{p,\min}$ *(1 mark)*

At overdrive: $i_{\min} = R_{s,\min}/R_{p,\max}$ *(1 mark)*

Ratio spread:
$$\rho = \frac{i_{\max}}{i_{\min}} = \frac{R_{s,\max} \cdot R_{p,\max}}{R_{s,\min} \cdot R_{p,\min}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**TCU Control Logic (PID-style closed loop):**

1. The TCU computes a **target ratio** $i_{\text{target}}$ from a lookup table based on TPS, engine torque, and VSS. *(1 mark)*
2. The TCU commands hydraulic actuators via **PWM-controlled solenoid valves** to vary the pulley clamp positions. *(1 mark)*
3. Feedback from PPS/SPS provides the *actual* ratio; the TCU applies a **PI control** law to minimize error $e = i_{\text{target}} - i_{\text{actual}}$. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]** Explain the **working principle of GPS**. Describe the **three segments** of GPS. Derive the **pseudorange equation** and explain why a minimum of **four satellites** is required for a 3D position fix.

#### Part (a) — GPS Principle and Three Segments (7 marks)

**Working Principle (Model Answer):**

1. Each GPS satellite continuously broadcasts a **navigation message** modulated onto an L-band carrier (L1 = 1575.42 MHz). The message contains the satellite's precise orbital ephemeris and the transmission timestamp. *(1.5 marks)*

2. The receiver measures the **time delay** $\Delta t$ between transmission (from satellite's clock) and reception (at receiver's clock). *(1 mark)*

3. Pseudorange is computed as $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$. *(1 mark)*

4. With pseudoranges to ≥ 4 satellites and known satellite positions, the receiver solves a system of equations to find its own $(x, y, z)$ coordinates and clock bias. *(1.5 marks)*

**Three Segments (already explained in Part A Q2 above) — allocate 2 marks here:**
- Space Segment (24+ satellites, 6 planes, 20,200 km altitude). *(0.7 marks)*
- Control Segment (MCS, monitor stations, ground antennas). *(0.7 marks)*
- User Segment (GPS receivers — handheld, vehicle, etc.). *(0.6 marks)*

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Pseudorange Equation and 4-Satellite Requirement (7 marks)

**Pseudorange Equation Derivation:**

The true geometric range from receiver at $(x_r, y_r, z_r)$ to satellite $i$ at $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ is:

$$R_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x_r)^2 + (y_i - y_r)^2 + (z_i - z_r)^2}$$

Due to **clock biases**, the measured pseudorange is offset:

$$\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t_{sv,i}) + I_i + T_i + \epsilon_i$$

where:
- $\delta t_r$ = receiver clock bias *(0.5 marks)*
- $\delta t_{sv,i}$ = satellite clock bias (modelled and corrected from broadcast data) *(0.5 marks)*
- $I_i$ = ionospheric delay (corrected using dual-frequency or Klobuchar model) *(0.5 marks)*
- $T_i$ = tropospheric delay (corrected using Saastamoinen model) *(0.5 marks)*
- $\epsilon_i$ = multipath + receiver noise *(0.5 marks)*

The unknown vector is $(x_r, y_r, z_r, \delta t_r)$ — **four unknowns**.

**Why 4 Satellites? (3 marks)**

- The pseudorange equation contains **4 unknowns**: $x_r, y_r, z_r$ (3 spatial) and $\delta t_r$ (1 temporal).
- Each satellite contributes **one equation**.
- Therefore, **at least 4 linearly independent equations are required** to solve the system.
- Geometrically: 3 spheres of radii $\rho_1, \rho_2, \rho_3$ intersect in **2 points** (one in space, one mirrored across the Earth's surface). The 4th sphere uniquely resolves the ambiguity and corrects for clock bias.

For **2D fix only** (assuming known altitude), 3 satellites suffice.

For higher accuracy, **more satellites are tracked** (typically 8–12 simultaneously) and a **least-squares** solution is used.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losses**
>
> 1. **In CVT questions:** Students often forget that the **belt linear velocity** is *equal* at both pulleys (not the angular velocity). Many write $i = R_p/R_s$ instead of $R_s/R_p$ — **deduct 1 mark**.
>
> 2. **In CVT diagrams:** Failing to label the **hydraulic actuators** and the **TCU feedback loop** in the block diagram → lose up to 1.5 marks.
>
> 3. **In GPS derivations:** Writing the pseudorange equation *without* the ionospheric and tropospheric terms → lose 1 mark for an "incomplete model" remark.
>
> 4. **In GPS 4-satellite reasoning:** Stating only "we need 4 because 3 unknowns" without mentioning **clock bias as the 4th unknown** → lose 2 marks.
>
> 5. **In ratio spread:** Confusing ratio *spread* ($\rho = i_{\max}/i_{\min}$) with ratio *range* ($i_{\max} - i_{\min}$) → lose 1 mark.

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- [ ] **CVT** = transmission with **infinite gear ratios** between min and max.
- [ ] Belt-driven CVT uses **two variable-diameter pulleys** + **metal pushing belt / chain**.
- [ ] Ratio formula: $i = R_s / R_p$, where $R_p$ is primary (engine-side) and $R_s$ is secondary (wheel-side).
- [ ] At **launch**: $R_p$ is small, $R_s$ is large → high torque multiplication.
- [ ] At **overdrive**: $R_p$ is large, $R_s$ is small → low engine RPM at high road speed.
- [ ] **Ratio spread** $\rho$ for modern CVTs is typically **5 to 7.5**.
- [ ] **TCU** controls pulley position via **hydraulic pressure** modulated by **PWM solenoid valves**.
- [ ] Toroidal CVT (Nissan Extroid) uses **rollers + discs** instead of belts.
- [ ] Hydrostatic CVTs use **variable-displacement pump + motor** — common in tractors, not cars.

- [ ] **GPS** is a satellite-based radio navigation system, owned by the **USA**, operated by the **USSF**.
- [ ] Constellation: **24+ satellites** in **6 orbital planes**, altitude **20,200 km**, period **11 h 58 min**.
- [ ] L1 carrier = **1575.42 MHz**; L2 = **1227.60 MHz**; L5 = **1176.45 MHz**.
- [ ] Position is computed by **trilateration** using measured **pseudoranges**.
- [ ] Pseudorange equation: $\rho_i = R_i + c \cdot (\delta t_r - \delta t_{sv}) + I_i + T_i + \epsilon_i$.
- [ ] **4 satellites** are required: 3 for $(x,y,z)$ + 1 for receiver **clock bias**.
- [ ] The geometry matrix $\mathbf{H}$ uses **line-of-sight unit vectors** from receiver to satellite.
- [ ] **GDOP** quantifies geometry quality — **lower is better** (ideal < 2).
- [ ] GPS in vehicles is fused with **IMU + wheel speed sensors** for **dead reckoning** through tunnels and urban canyons.
- [ ] GPS enables **eCall, ADAS, fleet management, V2X**, and is foundational for **autonomous driving**.
